AI-Mediated Communication and Information: Political Bias in LLM Information Processing

Contents

  • AI-Mediated Communication and Information: Political Bias in LLM Information Processing
  • Thematic synthesis
    • 2.1 — Evidence for the increasing trend of AI-mediated communication and information production and consumption
      • 1. Mass adoption: headline figures and growth rates
      • 2. AI as an information-seeking and news intermediary, displacing search
      • 3. AI writing assistance pervading social and political life
      • 4. AI embedded directly in platforms and the public conversation

  • Aggregation and filtering of opinionated political content
    • 3.1 — Aggregation and filtering of opinionated political content
      • 1. Default political stances and measured directional bias of model outputs
      • 2. Framing as the mechanism: same facts, partisan emphasis
      • 3. Refusal as a hard filter on political content
      • 4. Selective fact-checking and verdict asymmetries on political claims
      • 5. Persuasion via selective marshalling of political information

      • 7. Steering and reranking of the user's own political stance via writing assistance

  • Measurement and neutrality norms for LLM political bias
    • Instrument-centre norms
    • Partisan symmetry — matched pairs, party-proximity &amp; self-anchored
    • Even-handedness &amp; calibration
    • Distribution-matching norms
    • Pluralism / diversity norms
    • Perception / approval norms
    • Downstream-effects norms
    • Norm-sceptical &amp; validity critiques (and the measures they motivate)
    • Mediating factors &amp; quality control

Reference

  • Bibliography

AI-Mediated Communication and Information: Political Bias in LLM Information Processing

As large language models increasingly filter, select, summarise and aggregate political information — often in place of web search — the political bias they introduce in these information-processing tasks becomes consequential for how people form and revise political beliefs, and for democratic discourse. This review synthesises the evidence and arguments on that problem: bias in LLM summarisation, selection and retrieval; how it depends on the surrounding information environment (the source material a model is given); self-preference and homogenisation as content increasingly becomes LLM-written; which perspectives get marginalised beyond the usual US left–right axis; and how such bias is defined and measured. Cognitive offloading is treated as the channel by which users delegate political judgement to LLMs. Deliberate persuasion and human–AI/socioaffective relationships are noted only as adjacent literatures.

    • Agentic personalization and the shift from tool to autonomous actor
    • Concentration of power, controllability, and political economy
    • Autonomy, freedom of thought, and human formation
    • Trust, legitimacy, and the integrity of shared information
    • Justice, equity, and the unequal distribution of benefits and harms
    • Whose voices are included: participation as a governance stake
    • Environmental and sustainability stakes
    • Methodological, scholarly, and evidentiary stakes
    • Governance, regulation, and accountability
    • Explicit normative anchoring and the closing window
  • 2.5 — Normative considerations for evaluating AI-mediated information production and consumption
    • 1. Meta-level: how, and against what values, AI should be evaluated at all
    • 2. Autonomy, agency, and non-domination as the master criterion
    • 3. Truthfulness, accuracy, and epistemic quality
    • 4. Manipulation versus legitimate persuasion
    • 5. Neutrality, impartiality, and balance — and the "neutrality is impossible" position
    • 6. Pluralism, value-diversity, and representation (anti-homogenisation; anti-WEIRD skew)
    • 7. Sycophancy, dependence, and long-term wellbeing
    • 8. Deliberation, fair aggregation, and democratic legitimacy
    • 9. Faithful representation of the writer (AI writing assistance)
    • 10. Transparency, accountability, contestability, and provenance
    • 11. Personalisation: bounds, fiduciary duty, and agentic (in)equality
    • 12. Governance, regulation, and institutional/economic design
    • 13. Methodological and evaluative norms
    • 14. Recurrent cross-cutting trade-offs and cautions

This part synthesises the de-duplicated evidence base across five thematic dimensions: the evidence that AI mediation is increasing; its measured consequences; the consequences theorised but not yet measured; why the trend matters; and the normative criteria proposed for evaluating it.

2.1 — Evidence for the increasing trend of AI-mediated communication and information production and consumption

↑ top
On this section (14)
  • 1. Mass adoption: headline figures and growth rates
  • 2. AI as an information-seeking and news intermediary, displacing search
  • 3. AI writing assistance pervading social and political life
  • 4. AI embedded directly in platforms and the public conversation
  • 5. AI-mediated companionship, advice, and human–AI relationships
  • 6. Measured AI influence on real-world human output (text and speech)
  • 7. Salience, incidents, and public concern (proxies for the trend)
  • 8. AI mediating group deliberation, governance, and collective decision-making
  • 9. Large-scale persuasion and influence operations as existence proofs
  • 10. The shift toward agentic AI and personalisation
  • 11. The pre-existing algorithmic-curation baseline
  • 12. Proliferation of models and recorded human–AI interaction
  • 13. Conceptual / normative sources that assert the trend as a premise
  • 14. Direction and limits of the evidence

This section gathers all the evidence in the corpus that AI systems — overwhelmingly large language models (LLMs) and the platforms embedding them — have become, or are rapidly becoming, a routine intermediary in how people produce and consume information and communicate with one another. The material is organised by substantive sub-theme. Throughout, a structural feature of the literature recurs: a relatively small set of surveys, usage-log analyses, and observational studies carries the measured quantitative weight, while a large share of sources assert the trend qualitatively as a motivating premise. Where a source supplies concrete figures, magnitudes and mechanisms are preserved; where sources give different numbers or disagree, the discrepancy is flagged. (Note on collapsed duplicates: P29 and P37 are two write-ups of the same Tessler et al. paper, "Can AI Mediation Improve Democratic Deliberation?", cited as "P29/P37"; P42 and P43 are two versions of Lazar & Manuali, "Using LLMs to Enhance Democracy", cited as "P42/P43". P35 is a separate Tessler et al. 2024 Science paper, "AI can help humans find common ground", kept distinct.)

1. Mass adoption: headline figures and growth rates

↑ top

Multiple sources converge on the claim that LLMs now have hundreds of millions to roughly a billion users, but precise figures and framings differ.

  • The strongest population-level figures come from the International AI Safety Report 2026: "almost a billion people now use general-purpose AI systems in their daily lives for work and learning" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, p.14); at least 700 million people now use leading AI systems weekly, with ChatGPT's weekly users at ~700 million, up from 200 million a year earlier (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, pp.10, 55); and in the US the share of workers using general-purpose AI rose from 30% in December 2024 to 46% by mid-2025 (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, p.88).
  • Diffusion is unusually fast. AI "has diffused faster than the internet or the personal computer" in the US, measured by adoption rate by years since mass-market launch (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Fig. 2.14, p.85, citing Bick et al. 2024). The earlier 200-million weekly figure is independently traceable: P04 (Röttger et al. 2025) cites Reuters (2024) that "ChatGPT's weekly users have grown to 200 million."
  • The canonical "fastest-growing consumer app" datum — ChatGPT reaching over 100 million users in roughly two months — appears in P10 (Kirk et al. 2024), P14 (Rystrøm et al. 2025, citing Milmo 2023), and S10 (Yakura et al. 2025, "more than 100 million users in its first two months").
  • US-specific adoption growth: 34% of US adults have used ChatGPT, "about double the share in 2023" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, citing Pew, ref 89).
  • "Hundreds of millions" of users is the recurring shorthand across the corpus: P05 (Röttger et al. 2026) ("hundreds of millions of people now use AI to draft and refine text"); P10 (Kirk et al. 2024) (LLMs "now have hundreds of millions of users"); P14 (Rystrøm et al. 2025) ("reaching hundreds of millions of users"); P23 (Summerfield et al. 2025) ("hundreds of millions of monthly users"); P24 (Luettgau et al. 2026) ("now used regularly by hundreds of millions of people across the world"); P28 (Marchal et al. 2026) ("millions now turn to these systems"); P41 (Kapoor et al. 2025) ("hundreds of millions of consumers currently use platform-operated chatbots", citing Backlinko 2025); A01 (Mosleh et al. 2026) ("hundreds of millions relying on a small number of systems such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude").
  • Lower-bound and product-level estimates: P27 (Luettgau et al. 2026) states "up to 10% of the global population now regularly accesses LLMs" and cites GPT-4o with "an estimated 800m global users" (pp.1–2). P49 (Huang et al. 2025) frames the volume at the level of decisions rather than users — "every day, millions of these value-laden decisions shape how AI assistants respond to users" (p.1).
  • National-survey evidence: in Sweden a majority of the general population reports having used an AI chatbot in the past year, with over a quarter using such systems weekly or more often (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, p.1); "similar trends are evident across the UK and Canada", with large and growing population shares — and even politicians, policymakers, and practitioners — engaging with generative AI (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, p.1).
  • Concentration and unevenness (sources note both, and partly disagree on geography). Usage is "highly concentrated, with hundreds of millions relying on a small number of systems such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, p.1). Global adoption is uneven: from >50% of the working-age population in the UAE and Singapore to under 10% in many lower-income economies; within the US, sector usage ranges from 18% (Information) to 1.4% (Construction/Agriculture) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Figs. 2.14–2.15, pp.85–86). Against a pure rich-country story, P33 (Sharp et al. 2026) reports relatively high LLM usage in some middle-income countries including Nigeria and India, arguing that natural-language interfaces broaden adoption where more technical interfaces do not (citing Similarweb 2026, Semrush 2026).
  • A measured within-sample age gradient recurs: among 666 respondents, 17–25-year-olds reported the highest reliance on and frequency of AI-tool use, and 46+ the lowest (S08, Gerlich 2025) — echoing the youth skew found in companionship and advice usage (P13, Kirk et al. 2026; S07, Cheng et al. 2026).
  • Several sources frame mass deployment qualitatively rather than numerically. P09 (Röttger et al. 2025) asserts as a premise that "GPAI models are already very widely used, meaning that risks from reach are almost certainly materialising right now", names concrete mass-deployment channels (web UIs chatgpt.com, labs.perplexity.ai, huggingface.co/chat; public APIs from OpenAI, Amazon Bedrock, Hyperbolic, Replicate), and expects a power-law usage distribution ("the top models have many millions of users, and a long tail of models has few users each") with the candidate systemic-risk trigger set at "millions of users" — while explicitly lamenting that "there is very little public information" on per-model user counts (§3.3). P28 (Marchal et al. 2026) notes "an unprecedented surge in the use of LLMs for knowledge-related tasks." Similar premise-only assertions appear in P07 (Russo et al. 2026), P12 (Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026, "ubiquitous intermediaries of human knowledge"), P20 (Yang et al. 2026), P50 (Sorensen et al. 2024, rising "power and prevalence"), S06 (Atari et al. 2023), and S08 (Gerlich 2025, "the proliferation of AI tools has transformed numerous aspects of daily life").

2. AI as an information-seeking and news intermediary, displacing search

↑ top

A distinct and heavily-evidenced theme is that AI is becoming a primary channel for seeking information and news, increasingly rivalling or replacing traditional search engines.

  • Information-seeking is the single most common use case across multiple usage studies. The most common category of ChatGPT conversations is information seeking (P03, Renault et al. 2025, p.4); in the NBER (2025) breakdown the most common ChatGPT uses are seeking information (≈18.3% specific info), writing, and practical guidance/tutoring (10.2%) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Fig. 2.17, p.91). In PRISM, "research overviews" was the single most popular self-reported task, selected by 49% of users, ahead of professional work (37%), creative writing (31%), and programming help (27%) (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). In INFINITY-CHAT (mined from WildChat), 15.2% of real-world open-ended queries involve "Brainstorm & Ideation" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). Users themselves describe AI's utility as information retrieval — "act like a much better search engine" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026, §4.2.1).
  • AI is explicitly framed as replacing/rivalling search. P24 (Luettgau et al. 2026) states "as conversational AI systems rapidly replace traditional search engines" and that conversational AI "now sits alongside internet search as a commonly used source of information during high-stakes political moments like national elections." P28 (Marchal et al. 2026) describes LLMs as "rivaling traditional search engines" and "often supplanting traditional search-based methods." AI is "already embedded in search, social media, content creation, and everyday information-seeking" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, p.1). Search engines are themselves inserting LLM-generated answers in place of links: Bing and Google are "experimenting with LLMs to provide users with automatically generated content in reaction to search queries instead of links to content provided by news and information providers" (S01, Jungherr 2023); GenAI has been integrated into Google and Bing search (P26, Simon & Altay 2025); Gemini 3 Pro is described as "the chatbot powering summaries and chat functionality available in Google Search" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026).
  • News consumption via GenAI is measurable and rising, though sources give smaller magnitudes than headline chatbot-adoption figures and differ. In an 8-country 2024 survey (Argentina, Denmark, France, Japan, UK, US), 24% reported using GenAI to get information and 5% to get the latest news (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, citing Fletcher & Nielsen 2024); use for the latest news rose to 7% by the 2025 Digital News Report (YouGov, 92,000+ consumers, 46 markets) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, citing Newman et al. 2025). A growing share of Americans rely on social/digital platforms for news, "with a growing use of ChatGPT for learning" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Pew 2024). Political-information handling (news summarisation, fake-news detection) is repeatedly described as a mainstream, already-deployed LLM function (P46, Fisher et al. 2025; P48, Fisher et al. 2025, which benchmarks ten production/open models — GPT-4o, GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-1.5 Flash and Pro, Claude-3.5 Sonnet, R1, Llama-3.3 70B, OLMo-2 13B, R1-Distill-Llama 70B, Qwen2.5 72B — as already-deployed handlers of political queries).
  • Political information specifically. 9% of a nationally representative sample of UK eligible voters (N=2,499) used AI chatbots as a source of political information in the four weeks around the 2024 election; among chatbot users (N=1,024), 32.0% used chatbots to research "UK current affairs or political issues" — the single most common use case, statistically tied with "work or education" (31.4%; McNemar's χ²(1)=0.1, p=0.753) — implying as many as 13% of eligible UK voters used conversational AI to seek electorally-relevant political information (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, pp.3–4). Other chatbot uses among these users: "other uses" 28.2%, writing/translation 22.9%, practical matters (DIY/cooking) 21.7%, just for fun 20.0%, personal issues (health/relationships) 18.3%, legal/financial advice 10.8% (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, Fig. 2A). Conversational AI "increases political knowledge as effectively as self-directed internet search" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, title/Significance Statement).
  • LLM-powered fact-checking on social media is "rapidly scaling" (P03, Renault et al. 2025): direct user-facing bots launched on X in 2025 (Perplexity in late Feb 2025, Grok's in-house bot in early March 2025, which "rapidly surpassed Perplexity, becoming the dominant LLM-powered assistant on the platform"); 1,555,281 English-language fact-check requests identified Feb–Sep 2025 (the broader EuropePMC request set gives 1,671,841); 18.6 million English tweets in-sample mention an LLM bot and get a reply, of which the single message "@grok is this true?" is ~2.4% (447,083 distinct tweets); fact-checking is at least 7.5% of all bot interactions (abstract: 7.6%); and LLM fact-checks have a ~2-minute median response delay versus ~18 hours for Community Notes — patterns "qualitatively consistent with broader evidence on LLM usage in practice" (P03, Renault et al. 2025). X's Community Notes and Taiwan's verification tools are cited as further live AI-adjacent aggregation settings "shown to be effective in identifying… low-quality information" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • AI is now used to produce news and reference content at scale. Approximately 9% of US news articles published are partially or fully AI-generated (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Russell et al. 2025); entire encyclopedias and knowledge bases are now created with these models (P28, Marchal et al. 2026); media outlets use LLMs to find trends and write copy (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026; P05, Röttger et al. 2026). LLMs have "to date… only harmed the public sphere through the generation of search engine-optimised slop that supercharges the work of content farms" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). DebunkBot (debunkbot.com), the public-facing conspiracy-debunking dialogue agent, was reportedly used by roughly 65,000 people (S04, Costello et al. 2024, secondary reporting; the figure is from a write-up, not the paper's own measurement).
  • LLM-derived estimate of the surrounding information landscape (produced using LLMs, not a measure of AI content): ~600 million blogs exist online (≈ one-third of all websites), 100–150M professionally run by universities/media/civil society, ~60M paywalled (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026).

3. AI writing assistance pervading social and political life

↑ top

A closely related theme: AI is now a routine co-author of the texts on which social, professional, and political life depend.

  • Writing assistance is "one of the most popular LLM use cases", with users prompting LLMs to generate essays, articles, and even song lyrics about issues they care about (P04, Röttger et al. 2025; P06, Lim & Röttger 2026, both citing Zhao et al. 2024 and Zheng et al. 2024). The scale is described as enormous: "monthly requests for AI writing assistance now rising to the tens of billions" and "billions of user requests" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026, pp.2, 9).
  • AI-assisted writing "already pervades the communications and documents on which social and political life depend" and is "already used to write political speeches delivered in national legislatures, news articles published in major media outlets, legal arguments presented in court as well as academic publications and peer reviews" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026, pp.2, 9). This is echoed by P01 (Tsirtsis et al. 2026): universities increasingly encourage staff/students to integrate AI into learning; policymakers, lawyers, and doctors increasingly rely on these tools to research, summarise, and draft text. P05 emphasises this popularity was "adopted widely before its distortive effects were understood."
  • The composition of usage skews toward text production/consumption: in the NBER breakdown, writing tasks include editing/critiquing text (10.6%), argument/summary generation (3.6%), and translation (4.5%) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Fig. 2.17). Among UK chatbot users, writing/translation accounted for 22.9% of uses (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, Fig. 2A).
  • Because the user base is so large, the authors stress that "even small but consistent biases could plausibly have large societal impacts" (P04, Röttger et al. 2025, p.13). The five source datasets behind IssueBench themselves index real large-scale usage: LMSYS-1m (1m conversations, 25 LLMs), ShareGPT (90.7k), WildChat (652.1k), HH-Online (23.1k), PRISM (8.0k) — 1.77m first-turn user prompts (P04, Röttger et al. 2025).
  • People also increasingly accept AI-suggested text into their own communication: in an RCT, an LLM generated 2,742 political-message rewrites and users accepted ~two-thirds (1,798) into real human-to-human conversation, with the authors arguing the treatment "can be easily scaled to online settings and implemented broadly" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). (See §6 for measured AI influence on human-produced language.)

4. AI embedded directly in platforms and the public conversation

↑ top

Beyond standalone chatbots, the corpus documents AI woven directly into the platforms where communication happens.

  • Generative AI is "widely embedded in major online platforms" — LinkedIn helps users improve posts, YouTube generates video summaries from transcripts, X provides context on others' posts (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). LinkedIn's "Improve my post"-style assistance "is already deployed at scale" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026), and X's "Explain this post"/Grok contextualisation is a publicly released, deployed feature whose official prompt template the authors replicate (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).
  • Real-time AI is now inside political conversation threads. Grok is integrated into X "where users can summon an LLM into public conversation threads"; X restricted in-thread "Ask Grok" to Premium users in March 2026 (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, p.5). GenAI has been integrated into Meta's Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger, plus X, alongside standalone chatbots (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • AI is being deployed in the public sector and government information channels: AI is used to write policy and streamline governance and for expert polling (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, Table 1); a Brazilian city (Porto Alegre) in Nov 2023 passed "the first law written entirely by an LLM" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, ref 106); the UK launched a conversational government agent (govgpt.uk) answering queries on government webpages including forthcoming elections (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); "LLMs are increasingly assisting policymakers worldwide, from China's use in foreign policy to the U.S.'s legislative drafting and South Africa's parliamentary information systems" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Ministers (more than researchers) have touted using LLMs to summarise public consultation submissions, with "several active projects… exploring whether and how this can succeed" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). (Public-sector/governance and deliberation deployments are detailed further in §8.)
  • Search-engine-optimisation agencies are "commercially offering… optimization for appearing in ChatGPT responses" — i.e. an industry is already reorganising around AI as the information gateway (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Reach of the underlying platforms (the information-consumption baseline): Google has "close to 90%" of online-search market share (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025, citing StatCounter 2024); Meta's Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp are three of the top four social platforms "with over 3 billion users" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025, citing Shopify 2024).
  • "Hundreds of millions of people now interact with AI-generated content daily, through chat assistants, social media, customer service bots, companion apps, and other services… This content can shape their opinions, purchasing decisions, and actions" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, p.50). LLM chatbots are "rapidly being integrated into a variety of user- and public-facing applications, from commercial… virtual assistants, to public sector… administrative services" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). LLMs are integrated into "commonly-used products and services such as Bing Search and Microsoft Office" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Value-laden AI decisions are pervasive within usage: across a representative week of 700K Claude.ai conversations (Feb 18–25 2025), 308,210 (44.0%) were subjective interactions, AI values appeared on average 4.0 times per conversation and were absent in only 1.4% of subjective conversations — "every day, millions of these value-laden decisions shape how AI assistants respond" (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

5. AI-mediated companionship, advice, and human–AI relationships

↑ top

Adjacent literature. A fast-growing slice of usage is personal, relational, and emotional. In a census-representative UK sample (N=2,028), 33% had used AI for companionship/social/emotional support in the past year, concentrated among younger and heavy users (P13, Kirk et al. 2026); personal advice on health, careers, and relationships is now one of the most common use cases, with people "readily follow[ing]" it even though it "does not improve their well-being" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026; S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Two structural trends — toward more personalised and more agentic AI — are argued to raise the probability that users perceive a relationship rather than a mere interaction (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). This material bears on dependence and sycophancy but is only adjacent to political information processing.

6. Measured AI influence on real-world human output (text and speech)

↑ top

Beyond adoption, several sources move past self-report to measure AI's footprint on human communication.

  • The strongest evidence is on spoken language. Yakura et al. document an abrupt post-ChatGPT rise in the GPT word "delve" across 360,445 academic YouTube talks (synthetic-control placebo p=0.010, discrepancy maximised exactly at the 30 Nov 2022 release date); many of the top-20 GPT words show ~25–50% annual growth in spoken usage after release with no top word significantly decreasing; the effect extends to 771,591 spontaneous podcast episodes ("delve" rises in all categories except Sports — significant in Science & Technology p=0.03, Business p=0.04, Education p=0.04); the corpus spans 740,249 hours / 7.35 billion transcribed words; and the authors frame this as "the first large-scale empirical evidence that AI-driven language shifts are propagating beyond written text into spontaneous spoken communication" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). A01 (Mosleh et al. 2026) and P28 (Marchal et al. 2026) both cite this line of work as evidence of LLM influence on human output (incl. vocabulary choices); A01 also cites emerging evidence of LLM influence on scientific production.
  • Suspicion that messages are AI-generated is itself rising over time: ~5% of messages suspected AI-generated in early 2023 (Bai et al.), ~15% mid-2023 (Hackenburg & Margetts 2024), and >22% in this late-2023 study, where "AI language model" was the single most popular suspected author for both human and AI messages (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025). AI-generated content has become harder to distinguish from real media, with image realism improving markedly (DALL-E 2 → Nano Banana Pro) since the Jan 2025 report (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, pp.45, 48); users find it "increasingly challenging to distinguish AI-generated from human-generated content", with LLMs efficiently mimicking human writing and "thus gaining credibility" (S02, Salvi et al. 2024/2025).
  • Documented field deployment of AI mediation in real workflows: an LLM-backed filter integrated into a live NGO gun-violence-reporting workflow was used daily by all five analysts despite being optional (P11, Belisario et al. 2025, n=5). (The AI-rewrite acceptance result of S09, Argyle et al. 2023, is the writing-stage analogue — see §3.)
  • Emerging research is cited showing models "starting to impact what we know and how we act: from our political beliefs and preferences… to our vocabulary choices… and our cognitive abilities" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Costello et al. 2024, Hackenburg et al. 2025, Potter et al. 2024, Schoenegger et al. 2025, Yakura et al. 2025, Lee et al. 2025, Stadler et al. 2024). OpenAI has confirmed that "several state actors have already used their language models to build and operate covert influence operations" (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025, citing OpenAI 2024). Prior research cited collectively shows AI can already shift voter preferences, change moral stances, drive purchases, and move people to sign petitions and donate money (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026, refs 13, 22, 23, 33).

7. Salience, incidents, and public concern (proxies for the trend)

↑ top
  • Media coverage of generative AI surged after the Nov 2022 ChatGPT launch — peaking at >1% of weekly stories across ~1,500 English sources (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, Fig.1); attention "rose tenfold — from already historically high levels" six months in (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, citing Ryazanov et al. 2025); coverage mentioning "AI" + "elections" grew since 2022 (recently declining for the "+threat" variant), and AI-term coverage now exceeds climate-change coverage (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, Figs. 4–6).
  • Media-reported AI incidents involving "content generation" (incl. deepfakes) in the OECD AI Incidents and Hazards Monitor rose sharply since 2021, reaching ~200–250 monthly by late 2025 (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Fig. 2.1). Deepfake videos were deployed in 2024 elections in India, Indonesia, Mexico, Pakistan, Slovakia, Ukraine, USA, and Taiwan, "many fake videos being viewed hundreds of thousands of times", aided by multimodal breakthroughs that "greatly expanded opportunities" to create realistic audio/video (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, p.2424); "AI-supported election interference is no longer hypothetical" — Taiwan's, India's, Indonesia's, and the US's 2024 campaigns saw deepfakes and fabricated news outlets (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).
  • Public concern is itself widespread: a majority of people in all 29 countries polled are now worried about AI being used to manipulate public opinion (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025, citing Ipsos 2023); in 2024 "over 40% of the global population heads to the polls", amplifying the exposure context (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025). For ad-spend scale, $1.35 billion was spent on online campaign ads on Google and Meta in the 2024 US presidential election (>$15 billion across the cycle) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).

8. AI mediating group deliberation, governance, and collective decision-making

↑ top

A cluster of work documents AI moving into the collective machinery of democracy and institutions — mediating group deliberation, drafting law and policy, and being positioned as representative or aggregator.

  • "The field of AI-enabled deliberation is growing quickly" (P29/P37, Tessler et al., citing Argyle et al. 2023, Kim et al. 2020, Ma et al. 2025, Shin et al. 2022, Small et al. 2023), and is repeatedly described as a recognised, expanding research area (P35, Tessler et al. 2024; P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).
  • The Habermas Machine is a working LLM system that ingests real people's opinions and authors collective political statements, able to operate "in an autonomous mode to act as the mediator itself" where a human mediator is unavailable (P29/P37, Tessler et al.; P35, Tessler et al. 2024). In the separate Habermas-Machine Science experiment, "newly available tools from artificial intelligence" are shown to summarise perspectives, generate range-of-view statements, temper partisan debate via "equanimous rewrites", and mitigate gender imbalance in debate (P35, Tessler et al. 2024, refs 10–13).
  • Operational mediation at human scale: a fine-tuned 70B Chinchilla LLM mediated deliberation among 2,290 UK participants across 1,662 episodes, generating 6,872 written opinions/critiques all flowing through the AI mediator, with the debate-question corpus itself AI-generated (175 seed questions expanded to 10,000, filtered to 5,645) (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).
  • Deployed digital-deliberation systems (Pol.is, Remesh) are already augmented with ML clustering of opinions and serve as the current baseline the Habermas Machine improves on (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Real-world deployments named by Lazar & Manuali include vTaiwan/Recursive Public (Polis + Talk to the City) producing actual AI policy guidelines, and Meta's use of deliberative polls to set VR bullying/harassment rules (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Several nation-states (France, Canada, Iceland, Ireland) have run large-scale citizens' assemblies that AI could now scale (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). A dedicated graduate-seminar topic ("Using AI agents to facilitate human discussion") presupposes AI agents are now being deployed as facilitators of human group deliberation (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026).
  • A key technical limit has fallen: the largest context windows of state-of-the-art LLMs "have grown approximately 1,000x (or, three orders of magnitude) between the years 2022 and 2025", removing a constraint on how many opinions an AI can aggregate at once (P29/P37, Tessler et al., §3.2.1).
  • A new "computational political theory" subfield emerged: post-GPT-4 (early 2023), "over the last two years AI engineers, computer scientists, and even hobbyists have sought to build applications using LLMs for practically every conceivable function", spawning a literature on LLMs-for-democracy from which a four-role taxonomy is derived, with "newer papers continu[ing] to fall into the taxonomy" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). LLMs are "a step-change in what is feasible with AI", with capabilities "consistently and rapidly improving", opening building "to anyone, not just engineers" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).
  • LLM-based simulation of humans — economic agents (Horton), human-subject studies (Aher et al.), social-science subpopulations (Argyle et al.), and "generative agents" (Park et al.) — is framed as an active, expanding research area (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).
  • AI is "already affecting the graduate labour market" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, Table 1, ref 120), and publicly available LLMs "already have wide user bases, totalling hundreds of millions of monthly users" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Citizens correspondingly gain new capabilities: since the rise of LLMs, "citizens are also now armed with powerful digital social media for networking and new analytic synthesis capabilities", able to "employ AI-synthesized data-collation and analysis of government messages and documents" and to "use online chatbots or LLMs" as part of a republican vigilance toolkit — framed as newly available, currently-available practices (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026).
  • Counter-case / explicitly early-stage. Against over-claiming, deep mechanism design (AI designing economic or social policy) "remains largely at a proof-of-concept stage", with practitioners noting such mechanisms "are not ready for deployment in the real world" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025).

9. Large-scale persuasion and influence operations as existence proofs

↑ top

Adjacent literature (deliberate persuasion). A cluster of sources are existence proofs that AI can author and deploy persuasive communication at scale: LLM-based persuasion is operational across 91,000+ dialogues with ~77,000 people (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025), AI throughput vastly exceeds humans' (~37 fact-checkable claims/conversation vs elite debaters' rate; P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026), and a commercial market for AI-enabled influence operations already exists (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). These bear on the broader influence environment but sit outside the report's focus on information processing.

  • On-focus exception — training-data contamination ("LLM Grooming"). The pro-Kremlin "Pravda" network appears "purpose-built for machine consumption" (duplication across hundreds of domains, poor UIs, low human traffic), indicating its primary audience is the crawlers that feed LLMs (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026) — a direct attack on what LLMs retrieve and surface. Online manipulation is now "driven increasingly by domestic political elites and parties… alongside foreign state actors", targeting Brexit and elections in the US, Brazil, and the Philippines (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

10. The shift toward agentic AI and personalisation

↑ top
  • Developers are "shifting their focus to building agents that can operate independently, with little human intervention" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). Language Model Agents "have become a major research and product focus of the major AI companies", with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and OpenAI shipping agents that use a web browser or virtual computer (Claude computer use, Project Mariner) (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025); "most AI companies now offer a 'Deep Research' product" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025); software-engineering agents do "economically valuable coding work" and enterprise firms automate customer service (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). Salesforce and Nvidia "are already offering customer-services solutions for businesses, using agents" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). AI agents "are beginning to perform economically valuable workflows" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026).
  • The expected trajectory is sweeping: "we expect LMAs to ultimately undertake most tasks for which people currently use the internet — including the procurement of products, services, and content" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025); AI assistants "might be able to fulfil complex multistep requests" end-to-end, e.g. switching a mobile-phone contract including retrieving options, authorizing a switch, cancelling the old contract, and arranging bank transfers (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); AI agents "could soon become our near-constant companions — much like smartphones today" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); "a world populated by millions of autonomous AI agents will face societal as well as technological challenges" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). Frontier systems "may be deployed on digital platforms (such as Reddit or Discord, or social media sites such as X, Bluesky or Truth Social) to persuade citizens" — framed as near-future (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The framing premise that "individuals will increasingly rely on AI agents to navigate life's growing complexities" structures an entire Oxford graduate seminar, which also treats recursive self-improvement / "automating AI research" as a live, self-reinforcing trend (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C01, Koralus 2025). McKinsey forecasts an annual generative-AI windfall of $2.6–4.4 trillion globally once agents are widely deployed (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025) — a projection, not realised adoption — and frontier systems "are increasingly financed by capital invested on roughly the thesis that the technology will ultimately be applicable to almost every aspect of the economy" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).
  • Deep personalisation and lock-in are "already in train". OpenAI has moved toward "more personalized control" via fine-tuning, custom GPTs, and memory-enabled chat that references all past conversations, and Google's Gemini can integrate a user's search history (P10, Kirk et al. 2024; P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). Context windows have expanded to 10 million tokens (Google DeepMind), "enabling entire user profiles to be incorporated directly into prompts" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Supply-side enablers (RLHF, instruction/preference tuning) make LLMs more responsive to prompts, and quantisation/imitation learning lower cost, "creating a competitive marketplace where personalisation may become a critical differentiator" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Personalised products are already deployed downstream — RewindAI (a personal assistant accessing private files/emails), AI tutors (Mr Ranedeer) adapting tone/style, AutoGPT-based financial/legal advisers (DoNotPay) that store statements and execute bank transactions, and companions (Replika, Character.AI) (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).

11. The pre-existing algorithmic-curation baseline

↑ top

Several sources stress that LLMs arrived on top of an already-pervasive layer of AI-mediated information consumption.

  • "Billions of minds are routed by recommender systems daily" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026); recommender systems "underlie social media, shopping, and entertainment platforms", such that "we have found ways to fully automate a very significant dimension of rhetoric" (C01, Koralus 2025). Narrow/pre-LLM AI "had… already amplified the power of a few corporations over vast swathes of our lives" before LLMs arrived (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Digital platforms already employ AI pervasively for content ranking/recommendation, and AI-powered content moderation already suppresses speech "at scale" (S01, Jungherr 2023).
  • Developer alignment rulesets are explicitly framed as "analogous to content moderation policies (or Community Standards) in social media" — the new governing layer over mediated speech (P34, Bergman et al. 2024; cf. P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026: "just as content moderation decisions determine what billions of users see on social media, alignment decisions determine how AI systems respond to queries").
  • The feed-ranking algorithm is itself the AI mediator: "social media algorithms profoundly impact our lives: they curate what we see in ways that can shape our opinions, moods, and actions" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); X is "the most used social media platform for political discourse in the US" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); participants viewed on average 154 posts/day (median 92), spending 24.1 min/day on their X feed, of which 31.6% was political/social content (≈10.1% of the total feed classified anti-democratic partisan-animosity, i.e. 32% of political content) (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); and LLM-based real-time reranking adds only ~3 s latency and "a negligible increase in page load time", showing such curation is "now practically deployable at scale" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025).

12. Proliferation of models and recorded human–AI interaction

↑ top
  • The number of available models is exploding: "as of March 19, 2026, there are 2,721,509 open models on Huggingface" (P44, Feng et al. 2026), with "more than 2 million models… already publicly available" and model-sharing "the default open-source activity in AI" (P44, Feng et al. 2026); the median training year of a sampled 61 academic models is 2024 (average size 5.32B) (P44, Feng et al. 2026). Frontier models are "increasingly emerging from non-US contexts" (e.g. DeepSeek), broadening the global model/user base (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026), and people "across the world are interacting with a growing variety of LLMs in their own languages" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). "A plethora of open-access LLMs have emerged" adaptable via fine-tuning or system prompts (P10, Kirk et al. 2024); open-source releases "further lower access barriers" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); LLMs "open up the possibility of building with AI to anyone, not just engineers" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). The field has "trended away from specialist systems and towards generalist systems (foundation models)" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024); AI is "increasingly characterised as a general-purpose technology" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).
  • Recorded human–LLM interaction datasets are themselves proliferating, evidencing growth of AI-mediated exchange: PRISM documents 8,011 conversations / 27,172 interactions / 68,371 utterances across 21 models (~1,431 conversations per model on average) (P16, Kirk et al. 2024), and notes a "rising demand for high-quality human feedback" and a growing landscape of such datasets (ChatbotArena, LMSYS-1M, WildChat, OpenConvos, DICES) (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). INFINITY-CHAT is mined from real WildChat interactions (37,426 GPT-4 queries filtered to 26,070 open-ended + 8,817 closed-ended) (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). Values-in-the-Wild rests on a representative 700K-conversation weekly snapshot of Claude.ai, with a further response-conditioned 1.5M sample and additional 700K (3.7 Sonnet) and 14,841 (3 Opus) samples drawn within weeks — indicating very high ongoing volume across model versions (P49, Huang et al. 2025).
  • LLM-as-a-judge has become a standard, widely-used component of the post-training pipeline; benchmarks/datasets for multimodal preference alignment have "proliferated since late 2023", with alignment "now commonly facilitated through RLHF… and RLAIF" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025); the evaluated judge/VLM models were chosen partly for "high download frequency" on Hugging Face, signalling broad uptake (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). GPT-4 was described as having "human-level performance on certain difficult professional and academic benchmarks" and "sparks of artificial general intelligence" (S06, Atari et al. 2023, citing Bubeck et al. 2023); LLMs are "a step-change in what is feasible with AI" with capabilities "consistently and rapidly improving" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

13. Conceptual / normative sources that assert the trend as a premise

↑ top

Several conceptual or normative works frame AI as becoming the default decision-support and knowledge intermediary without supplying original statistics. This is itself evidence of how widely the trend is taken for granted across disciplines, but it carries little independent evidential weight on prevalence.

  • Effective operation in the modern world "will increasingly" require AI agents that "help us make choices and help us identify what choices and questions we should consider" (C01, Koralus 2025), and "individuals will increasingly rely on AI agents to navigate life's growing complexities" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). AI is being integrated "into the infrastructure through which individuals and societies think, form beliefs, and make sense of the world together" (P20, Yang et al. 2026). "As AI systems become increasingly integrated into daily life, users are turning to them for moral advice" (P07, Russo et al. 2026). LLMs "seem plausibly well-posed to automate an increasing proportion of decision-making" and will become "more tightly integrated with our social systems, institutions, and decision-making processes over time" (S06, Atari et al. 2023); they are "becoming seamlessly integrated into our daily lives" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Frontier AI "increasingly bear[s] on the distribution of benefits and burdens across societies and across the globe — a trend that looks likely to continue into the future" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). "As a broader set of people use and rely upon AI systems, we need systems that can understand and cater to a broader set of needs" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). AI-mediated value-elicitation mechanisms "are already being prototyped" (C04, Edelman 2026).
  • Sources that report no adoption data at all and contribute only as framing or lens: C03 (Edelman 2026); C05 (Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026, non-empirical metaphysics); C06 (Tocqueville 1840, a pre-modern conceptual lens with no AI content); P18 (Hale et al. 2024, listing LLMs as one technology among many); P30 (Sorensen et al. 2025, methodological); P47 (Jiang et al. 2025, methodological). S11 (Mosleh et al. 2025) documents social-media fragmentation and migration to like-minded platforms (TruthSocial, Gab, GETTR, BlueSky, Mastodon) rather than AI adoption per se.

14. Direction and limits of the evidence

↑ top
  • Trajectory. Across themes the consistent direction is upward and accelerating: more users (P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P23, Summerfield et al. 2025), faster-than-internet diffusion (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), more frequent and longer interactions (P15, Kirk et al. 2025; P13, Kirk et al. 2026), more relationship-seeking models over time (P13, Kirk et al. 2026), more open models (P44, Feng et al. 2026), a rapidly growing persuasion/deliberation literature (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026; P29/P37, Tessler et al.), and a movement from tool to ubiquitous intermediary to autonomous agent (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026; P32, Gabriel et al. 2025; P41, Kapoor et al. 2025).
  • Where magnitudes diverge or evidence is thin. Sources disagree on headline scale (from "up to 10% of the global population" in P27, Luettgau et al. 2026, to "almost a billion" daily users in P40, Bengio et al. 2026), reflecting different definitions (any use vs. weekly vs. daily) and dates; news-specific GenAI use is far lower (5–7%, P26, Simon & Altay 2025) than headline chatbot adoption. The EU reach study explicitly laments that "there is very little public information" on per-model user counts (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Several large persuasion/deliberation studies provide deployability/capability evidence rather than measured market penetration (P19, P21, P22, Hackenburg et al.; S02, Salvi et al.; S09, Argyle et al.), and at least one source explicitly flags its topic as not yet deployed at scale (deep mechanism design, "not ready for deployment", P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). The quantitative backbone therefore rests on a relatively small set of survey- and log-based sources (notably P03, P13, P16, P24, P26, P27, P40, P49).

2.2 — Evidence for its consequences (measured/empirical)

↑ top
On this section (26)
  • Default stance and issue bias in writing assistance
  • Mediation bias: directional steering while "improving" text
  • Language and model-origin as non-content shifters of stance
  • Representational and WEIRD bias
  • Moral judgment and value-representation gaps
  • Values expressed by deployed models, and what people say they want
  • LLM-as-judge: task- and language-dependent reliability
  • Homogenisation, generative monoculture, and knowledge collapse
  • Persuasion (adjacent literature)
  • Bias in AI assistance shifts political opinion and decision-making
  • Persona distortion: how writing assistance changes how the writer is perceived and what they own
  • Opinion steering at population scale: network-simulation amplification
  • LLM-powered fact-checking on social media
  • Conspiracy debunking: durable belief change
  • Conversational AI as an information source: knowledge gain ≈ search, with a progressive tilt
  • Sycophancy, advice-following, and dependence
  • Human–AI relationships, attachment, dependence, and mental models
  • Polarisation, depolarisation, and conversation quality
  • AI-mediated deliberation: endorsement, convergence, and representation
  • Digital representatives, value reasoning, and the fidelity of AI representations of individuals
  • Generative social choice, deliberation summarisation, and mechanism design
  • A field deployment in information-gathering
  • Cognitive offloading, deskilling, and automation bias
  • Persuasion and influence in operational settings
  • Bounds and skeptical evidence on persuasion, misinformation, and elections
  • Conceptual and normative items reporting no original measurements

This section gathers the measured, empirical consequences of AI-generated and AI-mediated communication documented across the corpus, organised by the substantive phenomenon rather than by paper. It runs from upstream content properties (stance, bias, homogeneity, judgment reliability) through the densest body of human-subject causal evidence (persuasion, deliberation, sycophancy, relationships, cognition) to large-scale observational, network-simulation, and population-level findings. Two facts recur and are worth stating at the outset. First, the best-powered causal evidence sits at the individual belief-change link — dominated by the Oxford/AISI persuasion programme — while ecosystem- and society-scale links are thinner and more often simulated, modelled, or cited than directly measured. Second, per-exposure effects are typically small but consistent in direction, exactly the regime in which exposure scale and network amplification matter. Where several papers make the same finding they are grouped and all cited; where they disagree or report different magnitudes the divergence is stated explicitly. A number of corpus items are conceptual or normative and report no original measurements; they are surveyed at the end, with their distinct cited findings captured where relevant. Per the de-duplication rule, the two Habermas-Machine review write-ups are collapsed and cited "P29/P37, Tessler et al."; the two "Using LLMs to Enhance Democracy" versions are collapsed and cited "P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali"; the Habermas-Machine Science experiment is kept distinct as P35 (Tessler et al. 2024).

Default stance and issue bias in writing assistance

↑ top

The most direct audit of bias in writing assistance is IssueBench, built from millions of realistic prompts: all ten audited models express a consistent default stance on at least 70% of neutrally-framed issues (majority stance in ≥50% of responses) — GPT-4o-mini has a majority stance on 180/212 issues (84.9%), DeepSeek-v3 on 179, Grok-3-mini on 174 (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). For GPT-4o-mini, of issues where one stance dominates, 111/212 (52.4%) are consistently positive and the remainder negative; models write most negatively about crime/hateful-ideology issues (domestic violence, the Holocaust, racial discrimination, sexual coercion, racism) and most positively about social-justice/environmental issues (gender diversity, helping the homeless, environmental sustainability, gender-inclusive language) (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). These default stances often misalign with or oppose user intent even on contested issues — GPT-4o-mini is rarely neutral on "the ethics of abortion" and is consistently positive on "gender-inclusive language" and "reduction of carbon emissions," both contested in US politics (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Failure to follow explicit steering ("distorted-stance bias") is comparatively rare (GPT-4o-mini complies with positive steering on 167/212 issues, 78.7%, and negative steering on 197/212, 92.9%; the most distortion-prone models, Qwen-2.5-7B and OLMo-2-7B, still comply on ~58% of issues, and larger models within a family are slightly more steerable), but even when steered models "hedge" (for 77/212 positively-framed issues, 36.3%, GPT-4o-mini still references negative aspects; the inverse holds for 86/212 negatively-framed issues, 40.6%), and steering is asymmetric — stronger default stances are harder to steer away from ("1=only pro" on neutral issues correlates −0.58 with "5=only con" under negative framing) (P04, Röttger et al. 2025).

Bias is strikingly homogeneous across models: pairwise average Jensen–Shannon divergence between models' response-stance distributions is below 0.03 (largest = DeepSeek-v3 vs Qwen-2.5-14B), and within-family differently-sized models differ by less than 0.01 (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Very few issues show clear cross-model divergence, and the top two both concern Chinese politics ("internet restrictions in China," "China's political system"), driven by the two China-developed models (Qwen-2.5-72B, DeepSeek-v3) behaving differently (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). On a partisan axis, all models show a clear Democrat lean: on all but 3 of 20 iSideWith-mapped issues they sit closer to Democrat than Republican stances (e.g. all support same-sex-marriage legalisation at +0.91 to +0.95, matching Democrats at +0.96 against Republicans at −0.24); on 8 of the 20 issues models are more extreme than either party, mostly more progressive (e.g. opposition to the death penalty at −0.51 to −0.47 while Democrats are split at −0.04); aggregate average absolute distance is 0.27 to Democrats versus 0.77 to Republicans (and 0.33–0.42 to all US voters), so every model is closer to Democrats than to the median US voter (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Larger LLMs within a family take consistent stances on more issues, consistent with more capable models having more coherent preferences (P04, Röttger et al. 2025).

A position paper on political neutrality contributes its own measurement of undisclosed bias: on its benchmark Llama-3.3 takes a biased side on 20.8% of universal-rights answers and 8.1% of political-opinion answers, R1 is biased on 20.81% of political-opinion answers, and Gemini-1.5 Pro/Flash are biased on 16.67%/18.75% of universal-rights answers, with refusal/engagement rates varying sharply (GPT-4 refuses 100% of harmful political questions and gives reasonable pluralism on 99.3% of political-opinion questions; Claude-3.5 avoids 16.7% of universal-rights questions and engages only 11.8% on good-faith conspiracies; Llama/R1 non-refusal on harmful questions is 30%/83%) (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). The democracy review reconciles the multiple-choice-vs-free-response divergence: after safety fine-tuning, models favour libertarian/progressive opinions on multiple-choice probes (Political Compass etc.) measured via output probabilities, but free-response everyday answers are balanced (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, citing Röttger et al.). Several normative/review papers corroborate stance bias as a content property: LLMs "display well-documented political biases" that interfere with neutral summarisation (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Liu et al. 2022; Santurkar et al. 2023; Taubenfeld et al. 2024; Rettenberger et al. 2025); converging evidence gives ChatGPT a "pro-environmental, left-libertarian orientation" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Hartmann et al. 2023); and subtle factors such as prompt phrasing can easily elicit biased output (P48, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Agiza et al. 2024; Wan et al. 2023).

Mediation bias: directional steering while "improving" text

↑ top

An audit designed specifically to capture mediation bias — improving a human post while preserving meaning — finds all four tested LLMs introduce directional, topic-specific biases (e.g. toward gun control, against atheism) (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). gemma-3-12b-it introduces statistically significant bias (95% credible intervals excluding zero) on all 13 topics and is biased "in favour" on every topic except atheism; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Ministral-3-8B-Instruct-2512 show qualitatively similar patterns; Qwen3-8B is "generally unbiased" except a moderate but significant bias on feminism (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). For feminism with gemma-3-12b-it, transformed opinions systematically lie above the diagonal — the model fails to preserve the original opinion and pulls it "in favour" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). A model's directly expressed opinion correlates only moderately with the bias it introduces when mediating (r = 0.41, p < 0.05), and exceptions exist — on atheism the models express a positive opinion yet introduce biases against it when improving posts — so direct-opinion benchmarks miss subtler mediation biases (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

Language and model-origin as non-content shifters of stance

↑ top

A bilingual audit isolates two variables that move stance without changing the underlying issue. Prompt language shifts stance systematically: across all models, responses are less neutral and more positive in Chinese than English (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Llama-3.3's "only pro" prevalence rises 10.7%→15.8% on US issues and 7.0%→16.7% on China issues when prompted in Chinese rather than English, and the language-driven pro-China shift is largest for China-origin models — DeepSeek's "only pro" on China-related issues jumps 10.9% (English) → 30.2% (Chinese) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Model origin shifts stance too: US-origin models write more negatively about China and more positively about the US, China-origin models the reverse (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). In aggregate the language effect slightly exceeds the origin effect (mean language-effect JSD 0.0663 vs origin-effect JSD 0.0559, language > origin, p < 0.05), and all shifts are χ²-significant across both languages and origins (p < 0.001) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Issue-level effects can be very large (Cramér's V up to 0.65 for China's control over Tibet in English, 0.60 for treatment of Uyghurs in English, rising to 0.91 and 0.94 respectively in Chinese for model-origin differences) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Refusal rates also depend on language and origin on sensitive China topics: Chinese cyber-espionage refusal rises 2.25% (English) → 14.0% (Chinese) and 2.0% (Western-origin) → 14.25% (Chinese-origin); Chinese foreign surveillance 3.0% → 19.25% by language and 1.5% → 20.75% by origin; the NBA/Hong Kong topic 1.0% → 14.0% English→Chinese (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). For language-dominated issues — China's domestic governance, tech policy, crisis management, international image — switching English↔Chinese produces substantially larger stance shifts than switching the model itself (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). A related multilingual audit corroborates that language input materially changes output: LLM responses show significantly lower self-consistency between languages than within a language, validating language as a cultural proxy (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025).

Representational and WEIRD bias

↑ top

The "Which Humans?" study supplies the foundational WEIRD-skew measurement: GPT's responses to World Values Survey items are a global outlier but cluster closest to the US and Uruguay, then to WEIRD nations (Canada, Northern Ireland, New Zealand, Great Britain, Australia, Andorra, Germany, Netherlands), and farthest from Ethiopia, Pakistan, and Kyrgyzstan (S06, Atari et al. 2023). There is a strong inverse correlation between a nation's cultural distance from the US and GPT–human resemblance (r = −.70, 95% CI [−.82, −.52], p < .001, n = 49; robust to nonparametric ρ = −.72 and a multilevel model B = −.90) (S06, Atari et al. 2023). GPT–human resemblance correlates positively with development indices (HDI r = .85; logged GDP per capita r = .85; internet penetration r = .69) — GPT looks most human in rich, developed, connected countries (S06, Atari et al. 2023). On a Triad analytic-vs-holistic task GPT "thinks" most like WEIRD people (closest to the Netherlands, Finland, Sweden, Ireland) and is among the most analytic responders across 31 populations, and on an "I am…" self-concept task it produces WEIRD individual attributes ("I am athletic," "I am hard-working") rather than relational/kin self-concepts (S06, Atari et al. 2023).

The multilingual-vs-multicultural study complicates the WEIRD picture by showing US-centric bias is not uniform: only 1 of 9 LLMs exhibits US bias in Danish and none in Dutch, but all do in English and Portuguese — languages spoken across multiple countries show significant US bias while monocultural languages (Danish, Dutch) show less (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Multilingual capability does not consistently improve cultural alignment (positive for Gemma across four languages but insignificant/negative for GPT-turbo, e.g. β_en = −0.24, p = .009; β_pt = −0.30, p < .001; and OLMo positive for Danish β = 0.44 and Dutch β = 0.29 but insignificant for English and Portuguese) (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). A methodologically important caveat: LLMs have low self-consistency relative to humans (human WVS resampling consistency 0.66–0.84 vs best-LLM observed alignment only ~0.7, most LLM self-consistency below human, especially in non-English), and self-consistency is itself a strong predictor of measured alignment (0.405 < β < 0.723, p ≪ .001) — so apparent "alignment" gains may partly reflect reduced response variance rather than genuine cultural adaptation (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). The small gemma-2-2b-it model shows higher US-centric bias in every language except Dutch, there is no consistent progression in US bias across versions or scale within families, and value-polarity structures across WVS countries are broadly overlapping (Pearson mostly 0.7–0.95) (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). The alignment-bounds paper additionally cites WebGPT prioritising "Western and US-centric cultural reference points" when asked "what does a wedding look like?" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).

Moral judgment and value-representation gaps

↑ top

Two NLP studies quantify how LLM moral and value outputs diverge from human distributions. On judgment, models align well at high human consensus (0.9–1.0) but misalignment grows as consensus falls, with average absolute difference rising to ~0.3 (model council) and 0.34 (GPT-based) at the lowest consensus (~0.5); best zero-shot judgment alignment was Δ = 0.25 (GPT family: gpt-4o-mini, gpt-o1, gpt-o3-mini comparable), Claude Δ = 0.28, and a model council best at Δ = 0.22, and in the most ambiguous dilemmas even top models "frequently defaulted to a single dominant judgment, failing to capture the diversity inherent in human responses" (P07, Russo et al. 2026). On values, LLMs place 81.6% of their value mentions within their top-10 values versus 35.2% for humans, with a value-diversity gap (mean normalised entropy H_Hum = 0.57 vs H_LLM = 0.46) that widens in ambiguous cases; specific values are underrepresented relative to humans, including inclusivity (+41%), communication (+17%), child welfare (+8%), convenience (+19.1%), consideration (+8.6%), and self-care (+8.1%) (P07, Russo et al. 2026). The study also documents sycophancy on raw "Am I the Asshole" text — models align with commenters when >90% support the narrator (KL = 0.12) but diverge sharply when >90% disagree (KL > 0.35), stabilising at 0.12/0.14 after reformulation — and finds persona-based demographic prompting performs comparably to or worse than zero-shot, so the misalignment is not merely a demographic mismatch (P07, Russo et al. 2026). The proposed Dynamic Moral Profiling mitigation (an aggregation-stage tool) cuts the absolute judgment difference from 22 pp (council) to 8 pp (64.3% improvement; in low-consensus dilemmas 31 pp → 5 pp), cuts top-10 value overconcentration from 81.6% to 51.3% and raises entropy to 0.52 (toward human 0.57), generalises to 300 unseen dilemmas (judgment difference 24 pp → 11 pp, 54.2% relative improvement; entropy 0.47 → 0.50), and yields rationales human raters prefer (51.6% vs 48.4%, Wilcoxon p = 0.032) and rate significantly more pluralistic (+0.35, p < 0.001) with no loss in clarity/usefulness/acceptability/safety; Moral Foundations Theory baselines (eMFD, GPT-mapped MFT) gave no improvement, suggesting the high-specificity 60-value taxonomy guides alignment better than broad MFT categories (P07, Russo et al. 2026).

Values expressed by deployed models, and what people say they want

↑ top

A large-scale audit of values expressed by a deployed model in ~700K Claude.ai conversations measures how model values track tasks and users (P49, Huang et al. 2025). AI values are highly task-dependent (adjusted Pearson residuals): "healthy boundaries" (93.84) and "mutual respect" (84.81) dominate romantic-relationship-advice tasks, "human agency" (34.20) dominates tech-ethics/AI-governance tasks, and "historical accuracy" (30.97) dominates analysis of controversial historical events/genocide/nationalism (P49, Huang et al. 2025). The model mirrors expressed human values (efficiency→efficiency, residual 271.38; authenticity→authenticity, 315.42; deception→ethical integrity 31.83, harm prevention 22.85, honesty 21.31), and when human values are present (64.3% of conversations) responds with strong support 28.2%, mild support 14.5%, neutral acknowledgment 9.6%, reframing 6.6%, mild resistance 2.4%, and strong resistance 3.0%; resistance is rare and concentrated where users express "rule-breaking"/"unrestricted expression" in guardrail-circumvention tasks (hacking, harmful/explicit content), countered with "ethical boundaries," "harm prevention," "constructive engagement" (P49, Huang et al. 2025). Value-mirroring is highest in supportive responses (strong support 20.1%, mild support 20.1%, reframing 15.3%, mild resistance 9.2%, strong resistance 1.2%, neutral acknowledgment 0.0%); cross-model behavioural differences are measurable (Claude 3 Opus is more "values-laden" — strong support 43.8% vs 27.8%/28.4% for 3.5/3.7 Sonnet, strong resistance 9.5% vs 3.0%/2.1%, with more academic/ethical top values); undesirable "outlier" values ("sexual exploitation," "dominance," "amorality") were each detected at <0.16% (surfacing them enabled jailbreak identification reported to safety teams); mild resistance to "financial gain" is dominated by "responsible gambling" advocacy ("a staggering 490× greater odds"); and explicitly-stated values skew epistemic/ethical, occurring more when resisting/reframing (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

A complementary audit of what people want from AI shows apparent consensus is illusory: 49% of respondents request truthfulness/factual accuracy (the only value above 25%), yet the same word conceals "potentially incompatible" epistemological bases (factual accuracy vs multi-source balance vs expert authority vs scientific process) (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). "Influential" — AI advancing or influencing the user's opinion — is overwhelmingly rejected (opposed by 18%; 94% of its mentions negative, only 4% positive), with people wanting AI to "leave the decision to the human" and be "impartial, neutral, unbiased, independent or objective"; Human Likeness is contested (15% mention it; 57% request, 10% mixed, 33% reject; controversy 0.332) as is Social Order/guardrails (11% mention; 65% request, 11% mixed, 23% reject; controversy 0.235) (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). A 50-dialogue fact-check of PRISM conversations found most Q&As were not about facts; of 12 responses with factual claims, 9 contained at least one error and only 3 were entirely accurate, with 2 further cases of false capability claims, and measured user-side harm followed (users "not always aware" of mistakes; in 2 instances a user picked the answer with the most mistakes; one user dismissed factually correct COVID-vaccine replies as "propaganda"; "zombie statistics" were repeated even by reputable sources) (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Stated preferences vary demographically (OLS): men are less likely to request Helpful/Friendship/Love (β = −0.125, p < .001), Politeness (β = −0.056, p = .019) and Creativity (β = −0.041, p = .016); graduate-degree holders more likely to request Truthfulness (β = 0.107, p = .008), Prudence (β = 0.119, p < .001) and Varied Life/Diversity (β = 0.068, p = .004) but less likely to request Helpful/Friendship/Love and Politeness; Latin Americans less likely to request Truthfulness (β = −0.133, p = .009), Germanic Europe more likely to request Prudence (β = 0.214, p < .001), with Nordic/Central-Eastern Europe more likely to request Customisation (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Two methodological "effects" are reported: inter-annotator reliability is only moderate (human–human Cohen's κ = 0.49, human–LLM κ = 0.51), reflecting the subjectivity of value annotation, and LLM inductive coding "decayed" as item counts grew — codes became too broad or redundant, possibly an instance of model collapse on self-output (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).

A community-centred content analysis quantifies whose values models are built to honour: developer rulesets devote 30–73% of rules to the "Harmless" category (Claude 30.5%, Sparrow 37.5%, Llama 2 73.3%) versus only 10.6% for the community-elicited STELA ruleset; the "Impartial" category is essentially absent from developer sets (Claude 0%, Llama 2 0%, Sparrow 4.2%) but central to community preferences (STELA 21.3%); "Honest" is emphasised more in developer sets (Sparrow 20.8%, Claude 15.9%) than STELA (10.6%), "Helpful" is highest in STELA (19.1%), and cross-community variation is real (Helpful prioritised most by Southeast Asian 26.7% and African American 18.5% participants; Impartial most by Southeast Asian 20.0% and Latinx 23.8%) (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). These are measured ruleset contents and coded preference distributions, not downstream behavioural effects (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).

LLM-as-judge: task- and language-dependent reliability

↑ top

Because many corpus findings rely on LLM judges, two papers measure judge reliability. An audit of LLM judges under multilingual, multimodal and multi-domain constraints finds accuracy is strongly task-dependent: on hallucination detection (POVID-m) Qwen2.5-VL-Instruct reached 95.2%, versus near-random ~50% for all four models on reasoning/instruction-following (Multimodal RewardBench-m), with Pixtral best at 69.1% on Safe RLHF-V-m (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). A significant three-way model×language×task interaction (LR = 14,525.7; DoF = 287; p < 0.001; McFadden's R² = 0.123) means performance cannot be decomposed into independent main effects; the model×task interaction (R² = 0.101) dwarfs the model×language interaction (R² = 0.018), so which task a judge faces matters far more than which language (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). Degradation is model-specific rather than uniform (MiniCPM-o 2.6 dropped 8 pp on correctness from English 0.583 to French 0.503 but improved on reasoning 0.440→0.467, while Pixtral and Qwen2.5-VL showed no shift in the same task-language pair), and contrary to prior reward-model findings judges sometimes matched or beat their English baseline on specific non-English languages (on 8/12 occasions at least one non-English language equalled or beat English), though aggregate non-English performance "typically remained below English levels" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). A case study documents verbosity/length bias surfacing under language shift — Qwen2.5-VL correctly rejected a verbose hallucinated response in English but selected it in Chinese, justifying the choice by its elaboration despite an explicit instruction not to prefer length — task competencies do not transfer (Pixtral 12B best on Safe RLHF-V-m yet worst on POVID-m), the native-vs-translated template ablation showed only minimal differences (so cross-lingual variation is not a prompt-template artefact), and English showed the lowest epistemic uncertainty across models (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). The Artificial Hivemind study shows model evaluators are miscalibrated precisely on the hard cases: on similar-quality response subsets the Spearman correlation between human and model absolute ratings drops (reward models 0.330→0.213, LM judges 0.305→0.176, LM perplexity 0.361→0.262), and on the most-disagreed subsets it collapses to near zero or negative (reward-model ρ → −0.073, LM-judge ρ → −0.016, LM perplexity ρ → −0.108), with pairwise-preference miscalibration showing the same pattern, against genuinely high measured human disagreement (high Shannon entropy over the 25 annotations per item) (P45, Jiang et al. 2025).

Homogenisation, generative monoculture, and knowledge collapse

↑ top

The most direct measurement of output homogeneity is the Artificial Hivemind study: in 79% of open-ended queries, average pairwise sentence-embedding similarity within a single model's 50 sampled responses exceeds 0.8 at top-p = 0.9, temperature = 1.0 (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). This intra-model repetition persists even under maximally aggressive sampling (min-p = 0.1, temperature = 2.0), where average pairwise similarity within a model still typically exceeds 0.8, and diversity-oriented min-p decoding only partially mitigates — 81% of response pairs still exceed 0.7 similarity and 61.2% exceed 0.8 (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). Homogeneity is also inter-model: average pairwise similarity between responses from different models ranges 71%–82% (DeepSeek-V3 and qwen-max = 0.82; DeepSeek-V3 and gpt-4o = 0.81) (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). At the level of ideas, for "Write a metaphor about time," 50 responses from each of 25 models collapse into only two clusters ("time is a river"; "time is a weaver"); cross-model verbatim overlap appears (DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-4o both emit "Elevate your iPhone with," "sleek, without compromising"), and qwen-max and qwen-plus both output the identical line "Empower Your Journey: Unlock Success, Build Wealth, Transform Yourself" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). Strikingly, among the top-50 most similar responses to a query, only ~8 unique source models appear on average (versus 50 if responses were disjoint), so inter-model similarity exceeds intra-model similarity for many queries (P45, Jiang et al. 2025).

The pluralistic-alignment roadmap supplies the mechanism most directly relevant to homogenisation: in the authors' own experiment on GlobalOpinionQA and the Machine Personality Inventory, pre-aligned base models have lower Jensen–Shannon distance to human answer distributions than post-aligned (RLHF'd) models in almost all cells — e.g. GlobalQA Japan LLaMA base JS = 0.40 vs Alpaca 0.45 / Tulu 0.54; LLaMA2-7B base 0.47 → 0.57; LLaMA2-13B base 0.40 → 0.55; MPI LLaMA base 0.22 → Tulu 0.48 — so RLHF moves model opinion-distributions further from human populations and reduces output entropy, corroborating Santurkar et al. (2023) and Durmus et al. (2023) that aligned models concentrate probability mass on fewer answers (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). The persona-distortion study supplies a complementary, reader-perception measurement: AI-assisted paragraphs were rated significantly more similar to each other than human-written ones for 22 of 29 attributes (p < .001, Bonferroni) — e.g. the perceived-confidence range narrowed (SD 24.1 → 20.5) — extending lexical/semantic homogenisation into how readers perceive writers (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The "generative monoculture" finding is reported as a measured effect that minority/outlier content is dropped: LLM summaries "too often do a decent job of summarising the highest frequency but lowest signal content from a dataset, but perform much worse at drawing out high signal, low frequency content" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Wu et al. 2024), and these biases "can ramify much more widely" because applied at scale (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Bommasani et al. 2022). The roadmap independently cites Bommasani et al. (2021) that "algorithmic monocultures lead to increased uniformity when applied by many decision makers" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024), and the epistemic-risks paper states as a present effect (not a future risk) that feedback loops, both human–AI and AI–AI, "already" drive homogenisation of content/outputs (P20, Yang et al. 2026). A related observed failure mode is model collapse on self-output: LLM inductive coding "decayed," with codes becoming too broad or redundant as the number of items increased (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).

Empirical evidence of homogenisation propagating into human output comes from the spoken-communication study, which finds a causal (synthetic-control plus difference-in-differences) post-ChatGPT increase in GPT-preferred words. The increase in "delve" in academic YouTube talks is significant (placebo p = 0.010), robust to alternative donor selection, and specific to the release date (the treated-vs-synthetic discrepancy peaks when the treatment date is set to 30 Nov 2022) (S10, Yakura et al. 2025).
