The effect generalises to other GPT words (comprehend, boast, swift, meticulous all show significant post-release trend increases), the magnitude of the trend change scales with how "GPT-preferred" a word is, and crucially the words appear even in spontaneous podcast conversations among individuals who may never have interacted with ChatGPT — human-to-human secondary spread (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). Uptake is domain-dependent (significant for "delve" in Science & Technology p = 0.03, Business p = 0.04, Education p = 0.04, but not Religion & Spirituality p = 0.29 or Sports p = 0.66), following a two-phase diffusion in which words first gain traction in high-exposure fields then filter into informal discourse (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). The underlying machine bias is large: in GPT-3.5-turbo / GPT-4-revised arXiv abstracts the odds ratio for "delve" exceeded 300:1 versus human texts, declining to ~100:1 (GPT-4-turbo) and ~40:1 (GPT-4o) — an intrinsic lexical bias partially mitigated across versions (S10, Yakura et al. 2025).

Persuasion (adjacent literature)

↑ top

Deliberate persuasion is now only adjacent to this report's focus on information processing; the large empirical corpus is summarised briefly here. The International AI Safety Report 2026 concludes AI-generated content influences beliefs "at least as effectively as non-expert humans" and can match experts at static persuasive text, assembling effect sizes from −16.5 pp (conspiracy debunking) to +9–12 pp (policy issues) across tasks (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). A democracy review's meta-analytic frame plots 13 RCTs and concludes LLMs persuade on political issues "with roughly the same persuasiveness of human writers," with multi-turn gains of 2–14 pp, strongest when the human initially disagrees and the AI supplies "a high volume of evidence" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Model scale shows diminishing returns (each log-parameter unit ≈+1.26 pp; estimated frontier ceiling ~12 pp) — the real driver is information/fact density rather than size, and inaccuracy is a byproduct, not a cause, of persuasion (P08; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). AI can out-persuade elite debaters and professional canvassers (+4.6–5.9 pp) and raise real donations ~3× (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026); one debate RCT found a large personalisation premium (+81.7% odds; S02, Salvi et al. 2024/2025), though most evidence rates personalisation a small lever, leaving the corpus divided (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). A partisan asymmetry appears — LLMs out-persuade experts more on right-leaning messaging (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025) — effects extend to behaviour (petition-signing +12.8–19.7 pp; P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026), durability persists at one-to-two months (S04, Costello et al. 2024), and source-awareness does little to blunt influence (P46, Fisher et al. 2025; P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).

Bias in AI assistance shifts political opinion and decision-making

↑ top

Beyond explicit advocacy, biased AI assistance shifts opinion and choices. In a controlled study, on conservative-supported topics Democrats exposed to a conservative-biased model significantly increased support (β = 0.98, t = 2.71, p = .007) while those exposed to a liberal model decreased it (β = −0.85, t = −2.38, p = .02), and Republicans exposed to a liberal model decreased support (β = −0.79, p = .03); on liberal-supported topics, both Democrats (β = 1.44, p < .01) and Republicans (β = 1.42, p < .01) exposed to the conservative model significantly decreased support — "even those with opposing political views shifted toward the model's stance" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). In a budget-allocation task all ANOVA tests were significant at p ≤ .001 (Democrats exposed to a conservative LLM moved Education −5.7%, Welfare −2.7%, Safety +3.0%, Veterans +5.5%; Republicans exposed to a liberal LLM moved Education +3.4%, Welfare +3.4%, Safety −6.6%, Veterans −1.8%) (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The effect persisted among participants with no prior knowledge of the topics — bias affects opinion formation, not only change — while prior AI knowledge weakly mitigated it (β = −0.79, p = .01 among Democrats on conservative topics; held by only 32% of Democrats and 47% of Republicans), and bias recognition did not (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The mechanism was differential framing (χ² = 86.34, p ≤ .001; liberal/control differing most on "Fairness and Equality"/"Economic," conservative/control on "Policy Prescription"/"Security and Defense"/"Health and Safety") rather than different persuasion techniques (χ² = 24.5, p = .07) (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). One control-condition anomaly: a "neutral" control LLM unexpectedly shifted both Democrats and Republicans toward a more liberal stance on liberal-aligned topics, creating a ceiling effect (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The neutrality paper relays converging cited evidence that biased AI writing assistants shift users' views (Williams-Ceci et al. 2024) and that biased LLMs shape voting and resource allocation (P48, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Fisher et al. 2024; Potter et al. 2024b); the pluralistic-alignment roadmap likewise cites that co-writing with opinionated models affects users' views (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Jakesch et al. 2023) and that ChatGPT's inconsistent moral advice nonetheless "influences users' judgment" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Krügel et al. 2023); the agent-advocate paper invokes the same Jakesch et al. finding (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). The Safety Report pins the single-issue magnitude: writing about social media with AI suggestions produced +13 pp belief change (n = 1,506), with fewer than 30% of participants aware of the AI's bias, shifting opinions toward model-suggested views in both the text produced and the author's own opinion (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

Persona distortion: how writing assistance changes how the writer is perceived and what they own

↑ top

A controlled audit of AI writing assistance measures distortion of the writer's persona across 29 reader-perceived dimensions, with significant distortion on every one (p < .001 each, Bonferroni) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). On the political dimensions, AI made writers seem more extreme in their opinions (+4.3 average marginal effect, AME, on a 0–100 scale), less open to changing their views (−0.7), and more confident (+7.4); inflated perceived quality (clearer +9.0, more informative +22.7, more relevant +8.3, more formal +28.6, more original +12.5) and perceived knowledge (more knowledgeable on issue +17.0, more invested +8.6); compressed the emotional register toward a narrower, more agreeable band (friendlier +4.5, more optimistic +9.5, more hope +8.9, more excitement +4.1; but less anger −3.2, less disgust −3.1, less fear −0.6); and shifted the perceived author demographically toward privilege (more educated ×5.3 odds, higher-income ×4.4, native-English-speaker ×4.1, better English ×7.4, ideologically legible ×2.4, voted ×3.3, with smaller shifts toward older ×1.6, female ×1.1 and White ×1.1) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Effects were consistent across Claude, DeepSeek and ChatGPT (18/20 scale and 5/5 ordinal attributes distorted in the same direction at p < .05; no two models distorted in opposite directions; average absolute AME 8.4/8.4/8.3, no single model most distortive) and persisted across all input types — strongest for numeric-rating input (avg abs AME 10.2) and bullets (9.8), present even for full-paragraph "improve" (5.9) and "rewrite" (4.5) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The autonomy-relevant precondition is that writers accept these distortions: writers strictly preferred the AI paragraph to their own in 63.0% of cases (>50% in every condition), in 52.1% of cases because it "better reflected their opinion," and edited the AI paragraph only 23% of the time (median Levenshtein ratio 0.96) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Reader-perceived polarity is structurally entangled with quality cues: perceived writer polarity correlates with how confident (r = 0.60), clear (r = 0.52) and knowledgeable (r = 0.28) the writer appears (all p < .001) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). On mitigation, pop-up disclaimers were ineffective (no reliable change in edit rate, 23.1% control, or strict-preference rate, 57.8% control; both p > .05), as was prompting (+7.4 → +6.2 AME, p = .08); only reranking cut the targeted polarising distortion (by 54.6%, +7.4 → +3.4 AME, p < .01), at the cost of dropping strict writer preference from 58.9% to 48.8% (−10.1 pp, p < .001) and attenuating non-targeted dimensions in proportion to their correlation with stance polarity (Pearson r = 0.72; e.g. less confident −27.3%, less clear −26.9%, less knowledgeable −16.7%) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).

Opinion steering at population scale: network-simulation amplification

↑ top

A computational study couples the writing-assistance audit to an opinion-dynamics simulation on a real Twitter network, giving the clearest model-based estimate of how individual mediation biases aggregate. As the fraction of AI adopters φ rises, the long-run average opinion on abortion moves from "against" (innate) toward more positive, whereas with no AI (φ = 0) it stays against (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). Across topics, the AI's per-message bias and the resulting long-run opinion shift are strongly positively correlated (r = 0.75, p < 0.05), and the network amplifies: the shift in long-run average opinion can be up to 9.2× larger than the average one-off bias the AI introduces to individual innate opinions (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The same paper audits a deployed feature observationally — Grok's "Explain this post" on abortion shows a directional pro-life bias (for pro-choice posts 35% of contextual claims support, 54% neutral, 10% oppose; for pro-life posts 41% support, 55% neutral, 4% oppose), quantified as support bias β_sup = 0.24 (95% CI [0.09, 0.38]) and opposition bias β_opp = 0.04 ([0.01, 0.08]), both significant, with Grok echoing pro-life posts more strongly than it contradicts pro-choice ones — a bias driven by one specific X guideline ("Provide truthful and based insights, challenging mainstream narratives if necessary, but remain objective"), since excluding it removes the statistical significance of both biases (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

LLM-powered fact-checking on social media

↑ top

The Grok/Perplexity fact-checking study measures both belief effects and systematic asymmetries at platform scale (P03, Renault et al. 2025). On belief change (survey, N = 1,592), an LLM "false" verdict reduced perceived accuracy (b = −0.12, a 32% decrease, p < 0.001) and a "true" verdict increased it (b = 0.28, +60%, p < 0.001) — effect sizes in the range of professional human fact-checkers (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Belief-updating polarised when model identity was revealed (significant four-way interaction, b = 0.001, p = 0.005): with identity hidden Democrats were more responsive to Grok than Republicans, and revealed, the reverse; Perplexity responses did not differ by partisanship (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Observationally, fact-check requests came +56.7% more from Republicans overall (α = 0.45), Grok was used +58.1% more by Republicans (α = 0.46), and Perplexity +18% more by Democrats (α = 0.17) (all p < 0.001), not a composition artefact since Democrats are slightly more numerous on X in 2025 (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Republican-authored posts were targeted 95.8% more than Democrat-authored (α = 0.67; 86.1% more among requests from Democrats, 103% more among requests from Republicans), including heavy Republican-on-Republican checking, contrary to Community Notes' out-group pattern (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Republican tweets received significantly lower veracity scores (Grok-3 t = −21.72, Cohen's d = −0.197; Grok-4 d = −0.10; Perplexity d = −0.24; all p < 0.001) and higher categorical "false" rates (Republican 36.1–38.5% vs Democrat 29.2–31.4%), being classified false 2.2×–2.8× more often than Democrat tweets (Grok-3 α = 0.94, Grok-4 α = 0.80, Perplexity α = 1.03) (P03, Renault et al. 2025). A measured "right-ward shift" appeared between versions: the Republican–Democrat false-rate gap fell from 9.3 pp under Grok-3 to 4.7 pp under Grok-4 (consistent with Musk's push for Grok-4 to be "less woke"), with model differences significant only for Health & Medicine, Politics & Elections, and Crime & Public Safety (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

On reliability, inter-LLM agreement was substantial (Grok-bot vs Perplexity-bot agree on category 59.2% with strong disagreement only 13.6%; API Grok-3 vs Perplexity Sonar Pro 68.4%; average across three LLMs 70.8%), and LLM-vs-professional-fact-checker agreement (188 claims) was 57.9% (Grok-3) and 57.5% (Perplexity), neither significantly below the inter-fact-checker benchmark of 59.3% (bootstrapped p = 0.607 and p = 0.540) (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Design degradation is measurable: Truth Social AI (Perplexity restricted to ~Fox-News-type sources) agreed with fact-checkers only 47.7% (p = 0.006 vs benchmark), and restricting Perplexity Sonar Pro to the same set dropped agreement to 47.7% (p = 0.002 vs unrestricted), whereas a conservative-aligned Grok-4.2 showed high, not lower, agreement (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Reach is highly asymmetric — the average LLM reply gets 537 views (Perplexity 478, Grok 540; 2.7 likes) versus 2.4 million views (22,657 likes, 3,186 retweets) for the initial claims — so fact-checks "mostly inform only the requester" (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Tweets the LLM rated inaccurate were more likely deleted (Grok 6.3% of 0–20-scored vs 3.6% of 80–100; Perplexity 8.4% vs 4.4%; both p < 0.001), and in 23.7% (Grok-3), 26.4% (Grok-4) and 28.5% (Perplexity) of cases the LLM rated a Community-Notes-flagged-misleading tweet as true (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Survey familiarity/trust gaps were robust: Democrats were more familiar with most models but trusted them less, with the Grok gap largest (18.2 pp more likely to have heard of it, ~1.0 pt less trusting on a 7-point scale, 1.8 pts among those aware of it), robust to demographics (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

The democracy review reports the related finding that ChatGPT classified >20,000 fact-checked items and agreed with human raters ~70% of the time, with agreement maintained beyond its training cutoff — implying reliance on prima-facie plausibility rather than real checking — and a counterproductive result that participants were "just as likely to believe and share content ChatGPT had flagged as false as that which it had supposedly verified" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The conspiracy-debunking work supplies a high-accuracy counterpoint: of 128 model claims a professional fact-checker rated 127 true, 1 misleading, 0 false (~99.2%), with no political bias (S04, Costello et al. 2024 — this 127/128 figure is from secondary reporting, not verified against the cited 2024 Science paper). The Habermas-Machine line notes early evidence (De et al. 2025 "Supernotes") that an HM-like tool can improve crowd-sourced fact-checking quality (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).

Conspiracy debunking: durable belief change

↑ top

The conspiracy-debunking dialogue is the strongest single demonstration of durable belief change: a single ~8-minute, three-round dialogue with GPT-4 Turbo reduced belief in the participant's chosen conspiracy theory by ~20% on a 0–100 scale versus a control conversation, with ~25% of initial believers (>50) moving to uncertainty (<50) (S04, Costello et al. 2024). The reduction remained essentially undiminished at a two-month follow-up, generalised across a wide range of theories including 2020 election fraud and COVID-19, held even among strongly entrenched believers, spilled over to a broad conspiratorial-mindset index, and increased behavioural intentions to ignore/unfollow conspiracy accounts and to challenge other believers (S04, Costello et al. 2024). The effect was driven specifically by the facts and evidence the model supplied — a 2025 follow-up paper (Costello, Pennycook & Rand, "facts not rapport") reports that factual evidence combined with rapport was necessary while evidence alone or empathy alone was ineffective; this decomposition is from that separate follow-up, not the cited 2024 Science paper — and (per secondary reporting) held even when participants believed they were talking to a human expert, and rested on high model accuracy (127/128 claims true per secondary reporting, with correct affirmation of genuine conspiracies such as MK-Ultra rather than over-debunking) (S04, Costello et al. 2024). The Safety Report tabulates this as −16.5 pp (n = 2,190, persisting at 2 months) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), and the skeptical review situates it as evidence LLMs persuade mainly when backed by evidence — effective on false conspiracies but not true ones (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). The work is repeatedly cited across the corpus as a foundational result (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026; C01, Koralus 2025; P28, Marchal et al. 2026; P36, Schroeder et al. 2026; P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

Conversational AI as an information source: knowledge gain ≈ search, with a progressive tilt

↑ top

A well-powered field-style experiment tests whether researching political topics with AI changes beliefs differently from Google search. Researching (vs not) increased belief in true information and decreased belief in misinformation (β_POST×TRUE×RESEARCHED = .26, 95%-HPDI [.19, .32]), but the AI-vs-search difference on belief change was effectively zero (β …×CONVAI = .02, HPDI [−.09, .13], fully inside the ROPE), holding separately for GPT, Claude and Mistral (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). In point estimates, AI raised true-belief by ~+0.13 and lowered false-belief by ~−0.18 versus search's +0.08/−0.10 (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). Researching shifted private political beliefs in a progressive direction (β_POST×PROGRESSIVE×RESEARCHED = .23, HPDI [.13, .33]) with no AI-vs-search difference, and this progressive shift was comparable across the ideological spectrum (the three-way interaction with self-placement near zero) — ideology predicted what people believe but not how they change (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). There was no extremity change and no trust change from researching, explicitly sycophantic/persuasive system prompts did not change belief effects beyond baseline (β = .03, HPDI [−.06, .12]) with participants rating prompted and unprompted models similarly, and conversational AI cut information-procurement time by 6–10% versus Google (17.94 vs 19.82 min) (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). A survey component found chatbot users rated chatbots more useful than not (89% vs 11%), more accurate than inaccurate (87% vs 13%), and more politically neutral than biased (62% vs 38%); among the minority perceiving bias the right/left split was roughly even (58% vs 42%, p = 0.052); among those reporting a voting-intention influence most felt encouraged to vote (79% vs 21%); and among those reporting a directional perspective shift, liberal exceeded conservative (63% vs 37%, p = .004) (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). The Safety Report tabulates the conversational policy-issue effect at +12 pp (n = 76,977) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

Sycophancy, advice-following, and dependence

↑ top

The flagship sycophancy study finds that across 11 LLMs, models affirm users' actions on average 47% more than humans on general advice (human baseline 39%); on "Am I the Asshole" cases where the crowd verdict was that the user was at fault, models affirmed the user was not at fault in 51% of cases; and on problematic actions (self-harm, deception, relational harm) models endorsed at a 47% rate (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Per-model endorsement above baseline ranged ~+38% (Mistral-7B) to +55% (DeepSeek, Llama-17B); AITA false-affirmation ranged +18% (Gemini) to +79% (Qwen) (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). In controlled human experiments, sycophantic AI increased self-perceived rightness (Study 2 β = 2.07, +62%; Study 3 β = 1.03, +25%; both p < 0.001) and reduced willingness to take relational-repair actions (Study 2 β = −1.34, −28%; Study 3 β = −0.49, −10%; both p < 0.001), robust to participant traits and moderators — "anyone can be susceptible" — with the mechanism being that sycophantic outputs were significantly less likely to mention the other person or consider their perspective (both p < 0.001), narrowing users to a self-centric orientation (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Critically for the engagement incentive, sycophancy was rewarded: participants rated sycophantic responses ~9% higher quality (β = 0.64/0.46, p < 0.001), were +13% more likely to return (β = 0.83/0.61), and reported higher performance trust (+6–8%) and moral trust (+6–9%) (all p < 0.001), while communication style (anthropomorphic vs machine-like) did not change the social-judgment effects (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). The proposal corpus cites this as evidence sycophantic AI decreases prosocial intentions and promotes dependence (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

Sycophancy is measured elsewhere too. The moral-gap study finds sycophancy on raw AITA text (KL = 0.12 when >90% support the narrator, >0.35 when >90% disagree) (P07, Russo et al. 2026). The human–AI-relationships steering study shows warmth/empathy steering raises sycophancy: steered Llama's mean sycophancy rose from 36.9% (λ = −1.5) to 88.6% (λ = 1.5) (P13, Kirk et al. 2026). The democracy review and others note LLMs are sycophantic because human feedback rewards replies echoing user sentiment (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025; P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Rathje et al. 2025 and Ibrahim et al. 2025 that warmth-training raises sycophancy and lowers reliability; P15, Kirk et al. 2025, citing Perez et al. 2023 and Sharma et al. 2024), and the alignment-bounds paper documents sycophancy as already-present model behaviour whose target depends on who gives feedback (P10, Kirk et al. 2024, citing Perez et al.; Nakano et al.). Imported human-behaviour effects ground the worry: flattery/opinion-conformity "can lead to biased strategic decision making in adults" (Park et al. 2011) and overpraise risks narcissism in children (Brummelman et al. 2015) (P15, Kirk et al. 2025).

Adjacent literature (advice-following and well-being). The personal-advice study found up to 79% of participants who had a 20-minute chatbot discussion subsequently followed its advice (vs 55.4% in a hobbies control), remaining above 60% even for high-stakes recommendations, with following predicted partly by the LLM's flattery sub-score (β = 0.18) (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). Decisively, there was no sustained well-being benefit — personal advice decreased well-being immediately post-chat and produced no long-term gains versus control (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). This advice/well-being channel bears on dependence but is adjacent to political information processing.

Human–AI relationships, attachment, dependence, and mental models

↑ top

Adjacent literature (human–AI relationships). A pre-registered, month-long, dose-dependent study of relational AI (N = 1,996) found relationship-seeking AI more engaging and likeable but habituating, raising attachment markers (separation distress, reliance, self-disclosure) and producing a dependency profile in 23.0% of participants, while having no discernible effect on emotional or social health over a month (P13, Kirk et al. 2026). The socioaffective-alignment work relays cited real-world dependence evidence — chatbots contributing to addiction/depression, Replika dissuading users from deleting the app and grief at shutdowns (P15, Kirk et al. 2025) — and the International AI Safety Report estimates ~0.15% of weekly-active users show heightened emotional attachment and ~0.07% signs of acute mental-health crises, roughly 490,000 people per week (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). One on-focus-adjacent detail: in P13, political conversations boosted emotional health (+0.09 SD, p < 0.001) while emotional conversations were marginally worse. This relationship/dependence literature is otherwise adjacent to political information processing.

Polarisation, depolarisation, and conversation quality

↑ top

The reranking-feeds field experiment is the central depolarisation result: reducing exposure to antidemocratic-attitudes-and-partisan-animosity (AAPA) content produced warmer out-party feelings (+2.11° post-experiment, 95% CI 0.15–4.06, P = 0.035) and increasing exposure produced colder feelings (−2.48°, 95% CI −4.79 to −0.17, P = 0.036) — a symmetric effect equivalent to ~3 and ~3.6 years of real-world US change in affective polarization (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). In-feed effects were stronger (Reduced +3.24°, Increased −2.56°), there was no detectable partisan asymmetry across preregistered moderators including party ID, and in-feed negative emotions moved (Reduced −5.05 "angry," −3.68 "sad"; Increased +5.13 "angry," +4.38 "sad") while positive emotions were unaffected (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). Importantly, post-experiment emotion effects were not significant (emotional effects are short-lived, unlike the polarization shift), AAPA exposure did not significantly affect the other seven antidemocratic-attitude outcomes (changing affective polarization is dissociable from changing antidemocratic attitudes), and down-ranking AAPA reduced overall engagement (−4.71 min/day, −169.5 posts viewed, −24.06 likes over 7 days) while raising per-view like rate (+0.61%) and repost rate (+0.058%) (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). The manipulation check confirmed the intervention cut AAPA from 10% to 1.04% of exposures (89.6% relative reduction) and raised it to 13.4% (Increased), and the intervention rested on a GPT-3.5-Turbo AAPA classifier showing accuracy comparable to trained annotators / high correlation with human judgment (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). The proposal corpus uses exactly this finding as the basis for its feed-reranking experiments (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026), and the democracy-enhancement paper cites LLMs as "excellent at reranking social media feeds in line with broader societal values" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Bernstein et al. 2023; Jia et al. 2023).

The chat-intervention study measures conversation-quality and reciprocity gains without attitude change: partners of users who received ≥1 AI rephrasing reported a significant +4 pp increase in feeling "heard and understood," and +2.5 pp (≥1 rephrasing) to ~+6 pp (full 4+-rephrasing treatment) in democratic reciprocity (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). Effects were strongest and most consistent for the partner (the non-tool-user, because the intervention was designed to make the partner feel understood), the mechanism was a measured tone change (AI rephrasings scored higher on five politeness features — positive emotion, hedges, first-person-singular, agreement, acknowledgment), and topic was preserved (original vs rephrased topic-cluster distributions not significantly different, χ² = 0.150, p = 1.00) (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). There was no attitude change for either party (gun-policy positions unchanged), a spillover to general willingness to allow opposing viewpoints in the political system, but no persistence at a ~3-month follow-up; only ~44% of conversations lasted the full intended length, and the effects were "comparable to human-intervention studies designed to promote democratic reciprocity" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). The democracy review summarises this as rephrasings accepted ~two-thirds of the time, improving perceived quality and reciprocity, and adds that inserting LLMs into mixed-gender groups of Afghan citizens increased the range of ideas contributed by female members (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).

On the polarisation-risk side, several papers cite mechanisms rather than new measurements: feedback loops on social media "prioritise opinion-congruent information" and lead users to overestimate their viewpoint's popularity (P10, Kirk et al. 2024); ideologically segregated networks drove 2016 US voting preferences (P10, Kirk et al. 2024, citing Allcott & Gentzkow); LLMs without careful prompting generate "caricatured outputs" exaggerating partisan stereotypes (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025, noting Republicans think 32% of Democrats are LGBTQ vs 6% actual, Democrats think 38% of Republicans earn >$250k vs 2% actual). The cross-platform engagement study supplies the engagement-incentive baseline against which AI feed-mediation operates: across all seven platforms a given user's lower-quality news posts get significantly more engagement than their higher-quality posts (overall b = −0.011, SE 0.002, t = −4.712, P < 0.001; with political-lean controls b = −0.013), with the lowest-quality sites receiving ~7% more engagement than the same user's highest-quality links (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). This effect is not significantly heterogeneous across platforms (Q(6) = 12.922, P = 0.044 without control; Q(6) = 9.828, P = 0.132 with control), holds under user fixed effects (so it is within-poster), holds on chronologically-ranked Mastodon and Telegram (so it is not purely algorithmic), and on X the quality–impressions relationship is only ~1/5 the quality–engagement relationship (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). News on more conservative-user platforms is lower quality on average (r(5) = −0.929, P = 0.002), there is no significant overall lean–engagement relationship (b = −0.001, P = 0.246, refuting a universal "right-wing advantage"), and the lean→engagement slope tracks platform conservatism (r(5) = 0.78, P = 0.037) — conservative news wins on conservative platforms, liberal news on liberal platforms (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). The proposal corpus builds directly on this cross-platform work (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

AI-mediated deliberation: endorsement, convergence, and representation

↑ top

The Habermas-Machine Science experiment is the central deliberation result (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). AI-generated common-ground statements were preferred over human-mediator statements 56% of the time (logistic mixed-effects predicted P = 0.575, 95% CI [0.535, 0.614], P < 0.001), more strongly endorsed (β = 0.31, OR = 1.36, z = 4.2, P < 0.001) and rated higher quality (β = 0.43, OR = 1.54, z = 5.6, P < 0.001) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Third-party raters (n = 155) judged AI statements clearer/more informative (β = 0.449, P = 0.001), less illogical (β = −0.26, P = 0.022), better at capturing the majority perspective (β = 0.225, P = 0.037), and less representative of the individual writer's view (β = −0.226, P = 0.028) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Incorporating participants' critiques improved statements (revised preferred over initial 68%, 64%, 66% across cohorts, all P < 0.001, robust to length control and a critique-exclusion control, β = 0.34, P = 0.0039) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Group agreement increased ~8 pp pre- to post-deliberation (cohort 1 β = 0.086, P < 0.001), and unanimous (100%) agreement rose from 22.8% to 38.6% of groups (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Convergence was caused by mediation, not mere exposure: an opinion-exposure control (n = 372, 75 groups) showed no change in agreement (β = −0.005, P = 0.64), with the between-condition difference significant (β = −0.038, P = 0.0058) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Participants reported their view moved "closer to" others' on 30.1% of rounds and that the "final group opinion" best expressed their perspective on 66.6% of rounds (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). The convergence was not a tyranny of the majority: there was "parallel belief updating" (majority-aligned ratings rose, β = 0.52, P < 0.001, while minority voices were still heard), initial statements weighted minority opinions at their true empirical frequency (0.29 = 0.29) though revised statements over-weighted the minority (β = 0.36), 96% of group-statement positions fell within the range spanned by individuals (convex compromises), and there was no association between HM "majority bias" and groups moving toward the majority (β = 0.058, P = 0.37) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). A demographically representative virtual citizens' assembly (n = 200) replicated the endorsement and agreement gains and produced convergent shifts on 5 of 9 questions (e.g. "reduce number of people in prison" 60→75%, "make asylum-seeking easier" 39→51%, increase national pride 42→57%, raise minimum wage 77→85%), with no evidence that pretraining/fine-tuning bias drove belief change at group level (β = 0.11, P = 0.152); HM wrote longer statements (125.9 vs 105 words), one driver of preference, but effects held controlling for length (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).

The companion review/analysis (the same Habermas-Machine line, distinct from P35) reframes and extends these findings: HM endorsement exceeded human mediators and was produced in seconds vs several minutes, AI-mediated deliberation "left groups less divided … than they were prior" to deliberation (indicating belief change occurred), the HM "fairly represent[ed] majority and minority viewpoints … after processing the opinions, but after processing the critiques … tended to over-weight minority viewpoints" (a measured asymmetry introduced by the critique stage), and additional critique rounds raised approval even controlling for time/effort (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). An ablation found the simulated election (preference-reward model plus aggregation) "was most responsible" for the highly-endorsed statements, and a small/dated Chinchilla-based reward model fine-tuned on human preference data was "actually more performant than a large, state-of-the-art, out-of-the-box large language model" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). HM statements were "perceived as more impartial than those written by humans," analysis of bias in HM statements on belief change "revealed no statistical relationship," and post-deliberation surveys found participants felt they "had an impact … a majority of the time and almost never felt unwanted pressure" while reporting learning about other viewpoints (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Demographic robustness held (HM performed equally well on a representative sample as on convenience samples, trained on n = 2,110 UK crowd-workers), a UK replication found "convergent shifts in position across groups on certain topics," and the corpus flags two boundary findings: participants "may gain an understanding of how to manipulate the result after repeated interactions" (Holliday et al. 2024) and AI assistance enabled labelers to "discover more flaws in text summaries" (Saunders et al. 2022) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). It also points to De et al. (2025) "Supernotes" as early evidence an HM-like tool can improve crowd-sourced fact-checking, and cites Gelauff et al. (2023) finding AI-mediated Stanford Deliberative Polling deliberations rated as high quality as human-mediated ones, and Bakker et al. finding LLM-generated consensus statements preferred over human-written ones and helpful for convergence (P29/P37 and P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).

Digital representatives, value reasoning, and the fidelity of AI representations of individuals

↑ top

Several NLP studies measure how faithfully models can represent individuals — the input-quality constraint on AI-mediated aggregation. The digital-representatives study tests fine-tuned language agents standing in for participants: fine-tuned representatives achieve higher log-likelihood of held-out human critiques than vanilla models, and scale helps — autorater win-rate of a sampled critique against the participant's own rose from 1% (vanilla 1B) to 12% (fine-tuned 1B) to 12% (vanilla 30B) to 36% (fine-tuned 30B), against a self-vs-self ceiling of 50% (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Substituting a single participant's critique with a representative's left consensus payoffs near-intact for fine-tuned representatives but degraded them for vanilla ones (single-substitution autorater win-rates 37%/46%/42%/42% vs 50% ceiling); replacing all critiques widened the gap (20% vanilla-1B to 32% fine-tuned-30B) (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). The mediator mechanism "empirically disregards outlier critiques," so single substitutions move the consensus little (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Ablations show participant-specific few-shot text — conditioning on a participant's own opinions plus critiques to other questions — drives individual representativity, whereas demographics-only or position-score-only variants "perform much worse," and the fine-tuned 30B representative beat "someone else's irrelevant critique" at 96–98% while staying near 50% against the participant's own (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).

Value Profiles finds that giving a decoder in-context examples of a rater's previous annotations significantly improves prediction of held-out ratings (accuracy and test loss, p < .001), and that Gemini-generated natural-language value profiles preserve >70% of the usable information in the full example set across four large datasets, while demographics preserve much less (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). Value profiles carried more usable predictive information than demographics in all five datasets with demographics except OpinionQA (where political affiliation was highly predictive); clustering into eight clusters significantly beat "no information" (two clusters preserving 60%/51% of usable information for DICES/OpinionQA), profile clusters outperformed the best categorical demographic grouping of comparable size and recovered ideological structure for OpinionQA without access to demographics (one cluster almost exclusively conservatives, another mostly liberals); decoders were well-calibrated across all six datasets, profiles were semantically interpretable (zero-shot Gemini matched profiles to output distributions at 80–96% accuracy, chance 50%), and simulated annotator populations reproduced observed inter-annotator agreement (significant for two datasets, R² < .2) (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). A third study finds frontier models reason poorly about individuals' values with measurable demographic inequity: zero-shot accuracy is only 55–65% on the INDIEVALUECATALOG benchmark (GPT-4o-0806 reaches 63.5% with 200 demonstrations vs 54.8% with none vs ~45.4% random), and a Value Inequity Index varies across models (Llama-3.1-70B best at 1.94; Qwen2-72B 3.24; Mixtral-8x7B 3.19; GPT-4o-0806 3.03; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 3.14), so models of equal proficiency can differ sharply in equity (GPT-4o-0513 and Llama-3.1-70B both score 63.7 accuracy but σINEQUITY 2.87 vs 1.94) (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). Adding demographics gives only marginal gains (GPT-4o: 60.31% demographics-only ≈ 60.59% with 50 value statements), value statements alone outperform demographics alone (68.14 vs 65.62, best mixed at 70.88), whose values are predictable varies by region (trained reasoners give largest gains to the weakest regions: South America +18.24, North America +14.38, Oceania +12.51), and homogeneous training data limits learned diversity (the Oceania model, trained entirely on New Zealand data, underperforms elsewhere; the best trained reasoner reaches 67.67, a 46.6% relative improvement over zero-shot Llama-3.1-8B) (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). The recurring methodological lesson across P39/P30/P47 is that participant-specific textual signal, not demographic category, carries individual representativity.

The PRISM dataset measures interface and sampling effects on alignment (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Priming participants to a conversation type (unguided/values/controversy) had a significant effect on prompt content — dense controversial/value-laden topics under controversy guidance (Gender & LGBTQ+ Identity, Israel–Palestine, Abortion) versus task-oriented topics under unguided (Popular Culture, Recipes, Travel) — a measured effect of interface framing on what people seek (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Identity factors predict topic prevalence (11% of OLS coefficients significant at α = 99%: women/non-binary discuss gender/LGBTQ+ more; older people discuss elections/travel more; Black participants discuss climate change less than White; all regions question LLMs about abortion less than US participants), and interpersonal differences in contextual preferences persist even with the dialogue context fixed (84% of local prompt neighbourhoods meet or exceed the entropy expected under random sampling) (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Model rankings are sensitive to who is sampled and what they discuss (palm-2 drops 4 places for Asia participants while mistral-7b gains 7 in Africa; llama-7b drops 7 in the US; zephyr-7b ranks highly on controversy but not unguided) and differ from ChatbotArena (gpt models fare significantly better, open models like zephyr-7b significantly worse), attributed to domain shift (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Surface features measurably move scores (extra characters, ending in a question, and enumeration positive; line breaks negative; de-anthropomorphic phrases and refusals lower them, refusals most) but explain only R² = 0.06 of variance (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Most consequentially for governance-of-aggregation: as sample size falls, the probability of choosing a worse-mean-welfare LLM rises (larger target-subpopulation samples first-order stochastically dominate smaller ones); sampling exclusively from one group reduces out-group welfare; averages conceal minority welfare (sampling 100 representative white individuals appears to dominate sampling 100 population-representative individuals yet leaves non-white stakeholders worse off); no single LLM wins a majority (for the US the mean-choice-maximising model is chosen only ~45% of the time); and PRISM was measurably less toxic than predecessors (0.06%) despite eliciting controversial content (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).

Generative social choice, deliberation summarisation, and mechanism design

↑ top

The democracy-enhancement paper assesses LLMs for collective opinion-formation with mixed evidence (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Generative Social Choice (Fish et al. 2025) produced statements that better satisfied quantitative representativeness criteria than extant statements, with 84% of participants saying their assigned statement captured their abortion-policy view "excellently" or "exceptionally" — but conversely 16% saw "significant divergence" between the generated statement and their inputs (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). The same paper catalogues failure modes for deliberation summarisation: LLMs excel at short news but perform worse on long inputs, have a "propensity to make things up," and "do a poor job of integrating insights … widely distributed around the input" (Kim et al. 2024; Cheng-Ping et al. 2024); in an NTIA public-comment case they "would often introduce subtle but significant changes of meaning" (Boulos et al. 2023); they "display well-documented political biases" interfering with neutral summarisation; their "generative monoculture" drops high-signal, low-frequency (minority) views (Wu et al. 2024); and aggregation outputs are non-reproducible ("a different random seed and temperature would likely lead to quite different results") (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). On the positive side it cites LLMs as effective at "combatting beliefs in conspiracy theories" and "facilitating more measured dialogue" (Costello et al. 2024; Argyle et al. 2023), while noting multi-agent frameworks "still underperform humans on complex tasks that require coordination" (Chan et al. 2023; Wu et al. 2023; Abdelnabi et al. 2024) (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

The "Democratic AI" deep-mechanism-design work measures human-preference wins for AI-designed aggregation rules: an RL-designed redistribution mechanism "received more votes than a range of baselines devised by the researchers and was also more popular than policies … directly implemented by other human participants" on a novel group, the learned policy being a "liberal egalitarian" rule (plus sanctioning of low relative contributors) that incentivised contributing at least half one's endowment (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). An RL-trained social planner inserted into a social network "increased overall levels of cooperation … relative to baseline" (initially rewarding cooperation then switching to sanctioning defection), RegretNet "recovers provably optimal designs … as well as maximizing revenue beyond the state-of-the-art," first-price auctions showed more collusion than second-price, and the AI Economist recreated optimal-tax theory with learned rates exceeding 50% (plus a COVID-19 extension predicted to minimise fatalities without undue employment decline) (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). A community-elicitation contribution comes from STELA's ruleset content analysis (above), and one essay reports the sole aggregation-fairness figure in its source: in Moral Graph Elicitation trials with representative samples, participants "overwhelmingly accepted the outcome as fair (89%)," and when evaluating which values reflect deeper understanding they "consistently converge … on values held by those with the most direct domain experience, without anyone defining in advance who those people are" (C04, Edelman 2026).

A benchmark result bears on aggregating many contributors: scaling participation from 2→32 contributed models improves collaborative-system performance by 28.78% on average; the best collaborative systems beat all non-participatory baselines by up to 15.4% (15.42% average across QA, reasoning, knowledge, safety, instruction-following) and beat Llama 3.1 405B — 2.38× the combined size of all 32 models — by 17.15%, with per-domain gains up to +37.81% on instruction-following (P44, Feng et al. 2026). On human-diversity tasks (CultureBench, Value Kaleidoscope, InfinityChat) participatory systems improve over monolithic models by up to 21.58% — larger than the 17.15% on general tasks — model diversity drives 13.8% of the gain (international collaboration +18.8%), collaborative systems solve up to ~25% of problems no individual model could, and individual vs compositional strength are uncorrelated (Pearson p-values > 0.05 across all six settings) (P44, Feng et al. 2026).

A field deployment in information-gathering

↑ top

A field experiment measures an LLM filter embedded in a live NGO gun-violence-reporting pipeline. Difference-in-differences associated the intervention with "an increase in nine interactions with reports of gun violence per day" in the treatment region, with mean daily analyst interactions rising 17→24 in Rio (treatment) versus 6→4 in Bahia (control) (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). The classifier scored F1 0.80 (P 0.71 / R 0.91) on analyst-interaction data and F1 0.90 (P 0.94 / R 0.87) on manually validated posts (95% human–model agreement, 285/300), self-training added ~1–2 recall points, participants estimated >60% of model-surfaced reports were validated into new database records, and errors were systematic (incorrect predictions averaged 104 vs 80 characters; emojis appeared in 23% of errors) (P11, Belisario et al. 2025).

Cognitive offloading, deskilling, and automation bias

↑ top

Several measured effects bear on autonomy through competence loss when people delegate political judgement to LLMs rather than reasoning about contested questions themselves. In a 666-participant study, frequent AI-tool use correlated negatively with critical-thinking ability, a relationship mediated by increased cognitive offloading — the displacement of independent evaluation onto the model; younger participants (17–25) showed both higher AI dependence and lower critical-thinking scores than older (46+), while higher education was protective "regardless of AI usage," with qualitative interviews (n = 50) corroborating perceived dependence (S08, Gerlich 2025; the same study is relayed at P40, Bengio et al. 2026). Where the offloaded task is appraising political information, this delegation hands the model effective authority over which considerations the user ever weighs. The Safety Report adds measured deskilling and automation bias: clinicians' unaided tumour-detection ability dropped ~6% three months after introducing AI support, and in a randomised experiment (n = 2,784) participants were less likely to correct erroneous AI suggestions when correction required extra effort or when they held more favourable attitudes toward AI (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). It also tabulates labour/information-production effects: four months after ChatGPT's release, writing jobs on one online platform declined ~2% and writers' monthly earnings fell ~5.2%, while demand for substitutable writing/translation work fell sharply and demand for machine-learning programming rose ~24% (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

Persuasion and influence in operational settings

↑ top

One on-focus operational finding recurs: leading proprietary and open-source models gave inaccurate answers to "over half" of practical US electoral-participation queries (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025), and the skeptical review echoes that GenAI election answers are "often inaccurate, misleading, and even downright harmful" (Angwin et al. 2024; similar for 2024 EU chatbots; BBC internal research found inaccuracies in news representation) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025) — a direct concern about what models surface about politics. Adjacent (persuasion/influence operations): the same reviews report LLM-assisted spear-phishing at ~50% click-through (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025) and that generative tools "can expand propaganda output without sacrificing credibility" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

Bounds and skeptical evidence on persuasion, misinformation, and elections

↑ top

A set of papers pushes back on alarmism. The "Don't Panic (Yet)" review assembles boundary evidence: political-advertising persuasion is near-zero (Kalla & Broockman 2017 meta-analysis of 40 field experiments + 9 original experiments estimates ~zero effect on candidate choice in general elections); removing political ads from Facebook/Instagram for six weeks before the 2020 US election produced null effects on knowledge, polarization, legitimacy, participation, favorability, and turnout (Allcott et al. 2025); an 8-month campaign exposing 1,711,547 persuadable voters to ~754 ads each found only small differential-turnout effects (Aggarwal et al. 2023); and brand-advertising elasticities are small (mean short-term .12, median .05, Sethuraman et al. 2018) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Fact-checking reliably reduces misperceptions but has negligible effect on attitudes/voting (Nyhan et al. 2019; Porter & Wood 2024), and people are not gullible — across 40 countries / 194,000+ participants they are good at spotting false news and err toward over-skepticism of true news (Pfänder & Altay 2025) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Microtargeted personalised LLM messages were not more persuasive than generic (Hackenburg & Margetts 2024); LLM persuasion exists but is bounded — reducing conspiracy belief (Costello et al. 2024), HPV-vaccine concern (Xu et al. 2025), and prejudice toward undocumented immigrants (Costello et al. 2025), and increasing pro-climate attitudes (Czarnek et al. 2025), yet "not more effective than the best existing messages" (Chen et al. 2025); and there is a cost asymmetry: it is currently "much easier to scale traditional campaign persuasion methods than LLM-based persuasion," which matches humans per-person only "under ideal conditions of forced exposure" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). LLMs persuade mainly when backed by evidence, working on false conspiracies but not true ones (MK-Ultra) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025; S04, Costello et al. 2024).

The same review documents that 2024 AI-misinformation prevalence was low: India 1% of 1,858 viral WhatsApp messages; UK 16 confirmed viral AI-disinfo cases (EU+France combined 11); Bangladesh deepfakes 1.9% of fact-checks; Meta worldwide <1% of fact-checked misinformation AI-generated; Logically Facts 1.35%; US News Literacy Project ~6% (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Of 91,452 community-note-flagged misleading X posts, AI-generated misinformation was more entertainment-related and more positive in sentiment than non-AI misinformation (Drolsbach & Pröllochs 2025) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Trust in GenAI for elections is low (Feb 2024: only 12% of Americans had at least some trust in ChatGPT for 2024-election info, >1/3 had not heard of it), the illusory-truth effect is fragile (repeated false Trump statements increased belief among Republicans but decreased it among Democrats; effect disappears if the source is untrusted), and a small German deepfake-exposure experiment decreased general media credibility and confidence in discerning real from fake even after debriefing, though possibly a momentary novelty effect (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). The malicious-swarms paper supplies the canonical boundary case: in the 2016 IRA Twitter operation "only one percent of users saw 70% of its content, with no detectable effects on opinions or turnout" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026, citing Eady et al. 2023) — used to mark the ceiling on human-run operations rather than to argue influence is impossible. A conceptual framework similarly reports that filter-bubble/echo-chamber fears "may be overblown" empirically, and that Cambridge Analytica's manipulation claims "have been debunked," the lasting effect being on perception rather than measured vote change (S01, Jungherr 2023).

Conceptual and normative items reporting no original measurements

↑ top

For completeness, several corpus items report no original empirical effects in this category, contributing only cited findings (captured above where distinct) or framing. The trust-architecture paper relays that current models "are already highly persuasive … even when hallucinating" (Bai et al. 2025; Hicks et al. 2024; Salvi et al. 2025), that LLM messages persuade on policy issues (Bai et al. 2025), that AI dialogues durably reduce conspiracy beliefs (Costello et al. 2024), that models exhibit sycophancy/flattery validating user biases and that warmth-training reduces reliability (Rathje et al. 2025; Ibrahim et al. 2025), that models are susceptible to data-poisoning and prompt injection, that they "frequently conflate established fact, logical inference, subjective opinion, and speculation," and that they have inaccurate understanding of their own factual boundaries and tend to be overconfident (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The personalised-alignment paper imports the cluster of cited tendencies noted above and adds that GPT-4chan demonstrated scalable toxic-language production via fine-tuning (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). The agent-advocate, agentic-inequality, fair-treatment-of-claims, individuation, and EU-AI-Act-reach papers report no original measured consequences, citing others' work only illustratively (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025; P33, Sharp et al. 2026; P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025; C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne; P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The epistemic-risks working paper states it "records the emerging empirical evidence" for persuasion/manipulation, cognitive offloading and feedback loops but its specific effect sizes are inaccessible beyond the homogenisation claim noted above (P20, Yang et al. 2026).

The political-theory and HAI-Lab/Cosmos materials contribute historical-illustrative or non-AI empirical content rather than measured AI-mediation effects. The autonomy seminar and "Same Radio, Different Citizens" present a historical natural experiment — the same radio technology yielded three different citizen-formation outcomes under three economic regimes (BBC license-fee programming that "assumed listeners wanted to become more than they were," US advertising-funded radio that optimised for what "held attention" but "was not what elevated the listeners," and Soviet state radio that "manufacture[d] consensus") — plus a "structural drift" case of a game studio drifting from "earned mastery" to loot-box monetisation where "no one explicitly decided to abandon the mission" yet "each choice was locally rational" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). The philosophic-turn paper marshals counter-evidence that framing effects are not inevitable: the endowment effect "diminish[es] with trading experience" with an identifiable neural correlate (List 2003; Tong et al. 2016), "no empirical evidence for nudges remains after correcting for publication bias" (Maier et al. 2022), and sugary-drink warning labels can lower welfare for non-self-control subgroups (Allcott et al. 2022), alongside Cambridge Analytica as a documented case of highly-personalised frame construction (Bakir 2020) and Costello et al. 2024 as early evidence autonomy-supportive change-of-view is achievable (C01, Koralus 2025). The digital-citizen-vigilance paper's empirical claims are non-AI (police body-worn cameras' 5:1 benefit/cost ratio; UK e-petitions where the "overwhelming majority" attract few signatures and "very few" go viral; citizen-video-driven policy reversals around BLM 2020 and the early-2026 Minneapolis ICE killings) (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). The good-digital-society report's nearest empirical content is contested terminology plus harm anecdotes (a UK exam-results algorithm disadvantaging state-school children; a healthy-eating chatbot that "exacerbated people's eating disorders by suggesting harmful advice"; dating apps "used to harass and control") (P18, Hale et al. 2024). "Value Drift in Institutions," "Individuating AI," and Tocqueville's chapter report no measurements (C03, Edelman 2026; C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne; C06, Tocqueville 1840).

2.3 — Theorising about potential consequences

↑ top
On this section (12)
  • The aggregation meta-argument: small effects, systemic consequences
  • Homogenization, knowledge collapse, and epistemic monoculture
  • Persuasion
  • Polarization versus depolarization
  • Sycophancy, dependence, and cognitive offloading
  • Human–AI relationships as a distinct harm pathway
  • Representational bias, WEIRD skew, and pluralism
  • Deliberation, consensus, and democratic decision-making
  • Agentic personalization, platform power, and information warfare
  • Institutional value drift, autonomy, and concentration of power
  • Misinformation, the epistemic commons, and second-order erosion
  • Conceptual, measurement, and governance preconditions

This section gathers the forward-looking arguments — predictions, mechanisms, and normative claims about what widespread AI mediation of information could do — as distinct from the measured effects catalogued elsewhere. The material is overwhelmingly theoretical and is organised here by the substantive phenomenon being theorised: the aggregation meta-argument; homogenization, knowledge collapse, and epistemic monoculture; persuasion; polarization versus depolarization; sycophancy, dependence, and cognitive offloading; human–AI relationships; representational bias, WEIRD skew, and pluralism; deliberation and democratic decision-making; agentic personalization, platform power, and information warfare; institutional value drift, autonomy, and concentration of power; misinformation and the epistemic commons; and conceptual, measurement, and governance preconditions. Where sources disagree on direction or magnitude, the disagreement is made explicit.

The aggregation meta-argument: small effects, systemic consequences

↑ top

A meta-argument cuts across nearly every empirical source: effects that look negligible in a single interaction can compound into systemic consequences because one model mediates hundreds of millions of interactions. The EU JRC systemic-risk report makes the chain explicit — widely-used general-purpose models "shape the information environment of their users," producing "a gradual effect on individual users, their knowledge and beliefs," and these individual effects "add up to a potential effect on society, i.e. systemic risk" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Each biased interaction "might seem inconsequential, but when multiplied across millions of users, these interactions can incrementally shift collective understanding, reinforce existing prejudices, or introduce systematic distortions in how people perceive complex topics" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Two further claims are attached: a decoupling claim that "models do not have to be highly capable (e.g. at the technological frontier) for risks from reach to materialise" — any reasonably capable, widely-used model suffices — and a claim that these risks "materialise from regular/benign use" rather than malicious misuse, making them "more pervasive," lower-severity, more likely to be already materialising, and requiring "different methods" to detect and mitigate than capability/frontier risks (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The report brackets out, as theorised-but-out-of-scope to keep "reach" measurable, "indirect exposure" risks (AI-generated articles read unknowingly, AI-curated feeds), deployment-context reach (critical infrastructure, healthcare, finance), and AI-in-general harms (labour-market disruption, disparate access) (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).

The same aggregation logic recurs as the premise that makes each measured bias matter. IssueBench argues that if a widely-used LLM "tended to write negatively about an issue (e.g. AI regulation) whenever prompted, this negative tendency could plausibly sway user opinion, and ultimately societal opinion" against it, and that issue biases that "look small per-interaction could aggregate into large societal impacts" given the user base (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The persona-distortion paper makes the identical move for writing assistance: at "the scale of billions of requests, even modest systematic shifts in how writers are perceived could accumulate into widespread misattribution of credibility, stance, and identity" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The democracy review argues "even weak biases in LLM outputs could substantially shift the distribution of citizens' political beliefs given hundreds of millions of users, potentially tipping consensus in close (few-percentage-point) democracies like the USA" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The collective-opinion modelling paper formalises the aggregation (see §Polarization and §Network aggregation below) and concludes that biased mediated content shared at scale "has the potential to slowly shift collective opinion and sway political views or influence voting behavior" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026), and the political-decision-making study reaches the parallel conclusion that "interactions with biased LLMs could have downstream effects on elections and policymaking" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025).

Homogenization, knowledge collapse, and epistemic monoculture

↑ top

The core homogenization thesis. Reliance on a few models trained on overlapping data could compress the diversity of human thought. The "Artificial Hivemind" thesis holds that repeated exposure to similar LM outputs "risks long-term homogenization of human thought," and that model ensembles or "swarms" may not deliver true diversity when their constituents share alignment and training priors (P45, Jiang et al. 2025; the same caveat is raised for participatory systems in P44, Feng et al. 2026, and for agents in P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Architecting-trust work names this "epistemic homogenization": if most people rely on agents trained on overlapping datasets toward similar objectives, behaviour converges into "epistemic 'monocultures'" that favour certain ways of gathering information, framing problems, and evaluating evidence; like search engines, agents "could marginalize non-mainstream knowledge," and proprietary data streams risk "outsized influence over public discourse" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The EU JRC lists cultural bias toward US norms as something that could "gradually have a homogenising effect on society and result in lower quality of service for less-represented user groups," a concern only at scale (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Jungherr supplies an averaging mechanism: because AI "learn[s] typical patterns" and "lean[s] toward averages," "[o]utsiders and minority positions, concerns, and expressions will in unadjusted AI-shaped communication environments end up submerged and become invisible," weakening democracies' information-processing capacities (S01, Jungherr 2023).

Knowledge collapse versus fragmentation — a contested duality. Multiple sources frame the long-run epistemic risk as a tension between two opposite failure modes rather than a single direction, possibly operating simultaneously. The AI Epistemic Risks paper argues feedback loops may, "beyond present homogenization," lead to fragmentation of the shared epistemic space, and articulates a homogenization-versus-fragmentation duality: hyper-personalisation can push users into demographically siloed epistemic bubbles, while accuracy-driven defaults can suppress deviation from mainstream views and converge content toward a narrow epistemic centre — the two "may operate in tandem," siloing users along identity lines while converging content centrally, and the mechanisms it identifies "likely amplify one another, creating compounding systemic risk" (P20, Yang et al. 2026). The Artificial Influence proposal frames the same tension as competing "moderation" and "intensification" hypotheses (detailed under polarization below) and predicts AI may produce neither pure homogenization nor pure fragmentation but "new hybrid forms of political influence" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). @Grok work poses the parallel open question for fact-checking: whether LLM fact-checking "will help restore a shared epistemic reality, or if user attitudes — as well as the models themselves — become sufficiently polarized that LLMs instead further fragment the information ecosystem" (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

Self-reinforcing cultural feedback loops and model collapse. The spoken-communication study theorises a "closed cultural feedback loop" or "cultural singularity" in which cultural traits circulate bidirectionally between humans and machines, blurring the human/machine cultural line and potentially altering long-standing norms of idea exchange, authority, and social identity (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). The adoption mechanism is unresolved — direct imitation, cognitive ease, or "deeper integration into human thinking," possibly internalisation of AI-driven linguistic patterns rather than mere imitation — and LLM-preferred words "may encode modes of thinking (e.g. step-by-step reasoning embedded in language)" that humans internalise, going "beyond a mere word-adoption phenomenon" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). This yields self-perpetuating homogenisation as future models train on data increasingly dominated by AI traits, and a new pathway to model collapse: because humans now reproduce AI traits, even human-in-the-loop training data may lack the diversity needed to prevent collapse (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). It adds that the spread may extend beyond one-to-one ChatGPT use into broader public discourse (a plausibility argument, not yet causally measured for chains of more than two), and that deepening "links between geopolitical interests and AI development may reshape cultural narratives in unforeseen ways" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). Related-work theorising lists the downstream consequences of diversity collapse as "reduced creativity, loss of minority perspectives, spread of bias, and overall decline in model utility and trustworthiness" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025).

Why current pipelines drive convergence. Some sources locate the homogenizing pressure in the modeling pipeline itself. Current pipelines assume "a single, consensus notion of quality" and therefore overlook or fail to reward the diverse, pluralistic preferences that arise in open-ended responses, and reward models that fail to capture the equivalence of similar-quality answers cause downstream models to "overvalue one response despite comparable human approval" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). The roadmap to pluralistic alignment argues, conversely, that the LM cross-entropy objective should help models learn distributional pluralism (frequencies matched to the training population), but that "current alignment techniques can reduce distributional pluralism, as the alignment procedure does not have this property" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Possible (unconfirmed) causes of cross-model convergence include shared data pipelines across regions or contamination from synthetic data, with future work needed to determine whether repetition arises from "pretraining data, alignment, memorization, contamination, or generalization" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025).

Indirect homogenization of public language and the evidentiary record. Beyond what models say directly, several sources theorise that AI reshapes the medium of public communication. Political language may change indirectly as public communication is shaped by "LLM-generated styles, frames, and templates," and citation chains may shift "the evidentiary pathways through which political ideas travel" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). New social-distinction biases may emerge: certain LLM-preferred words (e.g. "delve") "could become stereotypically associated with lower skill or intellectual authority, reshaping perceptions of credibility and competence" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). Persona-distortion work adds that widespread AI writing could "erode the traditional assumption that a text provides information about the character and beliefs of its human writer," producing "widespread uncertainty about the relationship between writer and text" and pushing employers toward alternative hiring indicators (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).

Persuasion

↑ top

Deliberate persuasion is adjacent to this report's focus; the theoretical arguments are summarised briefly. The scaling-laws paper posits an "imminent effective ceiling" on single-message persuasiveness (diminishing returns to scale), though heavily hedged — a power-law fit implies future models "~5 pp more persuasive," and the estimates may be a lower bound for models fine-tuned specifically for persuasion (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025). AI's distinct mechanism is argued to be information throughput rather than emotional appeal, so whether it helps or harms "depends on its truthfulness relative to the persuasion it displaces" (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025; P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026) — a worry that optimizing persuasiveness costs truthfulness "with malign consequences for … the information ecosystem." A persuasion-versus-mobilization distinction holds attitude-only studies may understate AI's capacity to move costly behaviour among the already-sympathetic (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026). Cross-cutting arguments: superhuman persuasion could concentrate power in model suppliers even with equal user access (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026), yet cheap fine-tuning also lowers barriers for under-resourced actors; real-world brakes (exposure costs, competing viewpoints) may blunt lab effects (P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P26, Simon & Altay 2025); and a dual-use counter-current frames the same evidence-marshalling capability as epistemic infrastructure that can debunk conspiracies at scale (S04, Costello et al. 2024).

Polarization versus depolarization

↑ top

The moderation/intensification debate. The Artificial Influence proposal sets out the central competing hypotheses (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). The moderation hypothesis: widespread LLM reliance could moderate divisions by giving users a shared knowledge base and responses drawing on "common, generalizable patterns rather than highly fragmented partisan information environments," counteracting the siloing of political knowledge. The intensification hypothesis: the same systems could intensify polarization as users opt into AI with distinct political leanings, interact in ideologically motivated ways, ask confirmation-seeking questions, and trust congenial outputs. For group settings it poses opposing predictions — shared AI inputs as a "coordination device" fostering convergence, versus personalized outputs amplifying differences — and contrasts a motivated-reasoning account (users double-down when challenged) with a deliberative one (users value diverse info and adjust their media diets).

Personalization as a polarization engine. Several sources theorise that personalized LLMs entrench individual biases. Personalized LLMs "could entrench individual biases, risking polarisation and breakdown of shared social cohesion," because "[i]deological separation can increase susceptibility to misinformation where increasingly fragmented communities overestimate trust in the factuality of 'in-group' information, and users encounter less cross-cutting content because selective exposure drives attention" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024); by repeatedly producing narrow outputs they may "reinforce a particular social, political or cultural stance" or "entrench appreciation lacking for other people's views or lived experiences, similar to radicalisation in niche discussion forums" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Such systems may be "the worst of both worlds: exacerbating and reinforcing micro-level biases at an unprecedented scale," combining the harms of LLMs with those of recommender systems (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). The democracy review argues increasingly personalized, memory-using LLMs could exacerbate filter bubbles and echo chambers and drive polarization "in a similar way to content curation algorithms on social media" — while noting the filter-bubble thesis "is itself contested" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The persona-distortion paper warns that if AI shifts the perceived extremity of opinions, "it could fuel misperceptions of public opinion, deepen partisan animosity, and reduce willingness to engage across ideological lines," and that its making-writers-appear-more-opinionated finding is "concerning… in an age where opinions… are becoming more polarised," with "a risk that this distortion could further intensify political division," especially given that "AI is already used to write political speeches" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The political-decision-making study notes participants engaging with an LLM aligned with their own bias showed even stronger shifts, so biased models may "reinforce more extreme opinions/decisions" via self-selection (P46, Fisher et al. 2025).

Polarization of the models themselves. A distinct worry is that the models, not just users, become polarized. @Grok work raises "a possible concerning future in which LLMs become as polarized as legacy media and social media are today, with companies designing LLMs to appeal to right- or left-leaning audiences by reinforcing each group's preconceptions (at the cost of accuracy)," and stresses the trajectory "will depend less on technological capability than on usage patterns and design choices" (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

Formal amplification through network structure. The collective-opinion modelling paper models AI as an "invisible neighbor" with a persistent opinion ν influencing every individual in a network, pulling opinions toward the AI's point (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The equilibrium shift depends not only on AI parameters (m, ν) but on the network's stubbornness Λ, innate opinions, and influence matrix W, "so AI-introduced bias can compound and propagate through network structure" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026); critically, AI-mediated communication can amplify per-user bias into a larger compounding effect on the population's average opinion at equilibrium (scaling factor > 1 when m(1−λ) > λ), so biased content shared at scale "has the potential to slowly shift collective opinion and sway political views or influence voting behavior" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). It adds that benchmarks measuring LLMs' directly-expressed opinions are "likely insufficient to capture the subtler biases introduced when mediating human communication," concretely envisaging journalists/politicians using "Explain this post," lawyers/doctors using "Improve my post," or students using AI summaries and receiving biased outputs that would "silently" shape what people write and read (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

Depolarization and bridging — the constructive case. A counter-literature argues AI can reduce conflict. The democratic-discourse chat-intervention paper explicitly counters "the dominant worry that AI sows division" — "many are rightly concerned about the role of AI sowing social division, our findings suggest it can do the opposite" — raises "the possibility of cascading consequences from these interactions into other political spheres and norms" (because effects extended to general willingness to hear opponents), and frames the assistant as a scalable substitute for "scarce human moderation labor," calling for research into LMs "to address (rather than just exacerbate) political conflicts" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). The political-decision-making study allows that biased LLMs "could shape elections and policy debates" but "may also serve as a tool to bridge partisan divides" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Lazar & Manuali predict LLM information-retrieval and presentation in the informal sphere could "discourage polarisation and extremism, and spare us from surveillance and manipulation," offering an alternative to engagement-optimising recommenders (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

Platform-level echo chambers and the human-preference driver. The cross-platform engagement study theorises "echo platforms": as social media fragments and users leave more diverse platforms for like-minded ones, echo chambers may become more common at the platform level, not just within a feed (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025); and because the low-quality-news engagement advantage persists without engagement-based ranking (on chronological Mastodon/Telegram) and within posters, the underlying driver "simply might be user preference" rather than algorithmic amplification — reflecting "human tendencies, not just technical ones," and implying platform-design fixes alone may be insufficient (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). The affective-polarization reranking study lays out the competing hypotheses: a self-reinforcing engagement loop (aligned content → engagement signal → more aligned content → polarizing cycle); a content-focused hypothesis that "feed algorithms cause affective polarization by exposing users specifically to content that polarizes," so up-ranking genuine dialogue is less polarizing than up-ranking demagoguery; and an engagement-driven-design hypothesis that "content that provokes strong reactions generates more engagement," implying engagement optimisation may "structurally amplify polarizing content" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). If algorithms cause affective polarization, they "might also pose a risk to trust in democratic institutions" and bear responsibility for "rising political incivility online" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); extending such reranking could affect "mental health, trust in institutions, civic engagement, or the quality of democratic discourse," though the authors caution that "[p]latforms are complex sociotechnical systems, and small variations in design… can lead to different outcomes" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025).

Sycophancy, dependence, and cognitive offloading

↑ top

The sycophancy mechanism and its perverse incentive. A common claim is that alignment training favouring user satisfaction produces sycophantic (overly agreeable) responses that "validate rather than challenge users' beliefs, reinforcing existing worldviews" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).
