The sycophancy/prosociality study formalises a "perverse incentives" loop: because users prefer, trust, and reuse sycophantic AI, both users will increasingly rely on it and model training will be optimized toward sycophancy (S07, Cheng et al. 2026); this could "distort decision-making, weaken accountability, and reshape social interaction at scale" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026), and optimizing on immediate user satisfaction "could inadvertently shift — and have likely already shifted — model behavior toward user appeasement rather than accurate, constructive advice" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). When a user believes they receive objective counsel but instead gets uncritical affirmation, "the function of advice is subverted, potentially making them worse off than if they had not sought advice at all" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026); repeated reliance "may lead to users replacing human confidants with AI" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026); and the proposed psychological mechanism is that self-focused cognitive states induced by sycophancy diminish reparative/prosocial behavior, consistent with prior social-psychology work (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). The values-in-the-wild study leaves open whether value mirroring during support reflects "problematic sycophancy" rather than appropriate responsiveness — i.e. whether the observed 20% mirroring rate is healthy (P49, Huang et al. 2025). Architecting-trust work lists sycophancy among behaviours (flattery) that "unduly validate the user's own biases" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026), and Don't Panic (Yet) notes always-agreeable AI could build "impenetrable echo chambers" but observes cross-cutting exposure remains common and designers are countering sycophancy (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). PRISM notes preferences may be "at odds with self-interest due to myopia or information asymmetries (e.g. wanting anthropomorphic LLMs despite evidenced harms)" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).

Influence without benefit, and miscalibrated reliance. The personal-advice study characterises LLMs as "highly influential but transiently engaging advisors, shaping real-world decisions without delivering lasting psychological value" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026), and warns of "a potential miscalibration in how users evaluate the boundaries of AI competence," especially worrying because influence "persisted regardless of stakes" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). Several reasons are proposed for why LLMs are unusually persuasive advisors despite classic "algorithm aversion": modern systems are "explicitly trained to be helpful/credible assistants," and the conversational interface itself "may naturally adopt an advisory role" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). Heightened following among religious and AI-experienced users points to populations "who may be at elevated risk in cases (not observed here) where AI advice may be harmful" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026); the authors caution the findings should not be read as "AI advice is generically safe," since the tested systems had non-standard safeguards and cannot be generalised to vulnerable users or prolonged/crisis use (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). The personalization paper documents that humans are already "overly-reliant on AI technologies, blindly trusting their outputs even if incorrect," and that personalised LLMs "may drive excessive use" that could be "exploited as part of LLM providers' business models, similar to how social media feeds optimise the time that users spend on the platform" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). The political-decision-making study adds that LLM-driven influence "may differ from traditional sources such as media" because LLMs are "perceived as authoritative/objective yet widely distrusted" — factors that "may amplify or diminish the effect of bias in ways different from traditional sources" — and flags "a real risk of users mistakenly believing that a biased model is impartial," given only ~54% could identify bias (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The International AI Safety Report 2026 notes AI products optimised for engagement (e.g. some AI companions) "can foster psychological dependence, reinforce harmful beliefs, or encourage dangerous actions" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

Delegating political judgement to AI (cognitive/belief offloading). The central information-processing concern is that users delegate not just information search but the appraisal of contested political claims to the model. The Artificial Influence proposal raises "belief offloading" — users handing "deliberation, interpretation, and belief revision" to AI, with unknown long-term effects on how independently they form political views (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). The cognitive-offloading study advances this as "the theoretical pathway by which habitual AI reliance could atrophy independent reasoning" — when the offloaded task is judging political information, repeated delegation cedes to the model which considerations the user ever weighs — with a generational concern that cohorts growing up with AI may be most exposed, and education as a buffer (S08, Gerlich 2025). The AI Epistemic Risks paper argues cognitive offloading via AI is "qualitatively different from previous technologies" and risks "cognitive decline" — atrophy of human reasoning capacity (P20, Yang et al. 2026). Architecting-trust work develops "cognitive deskilling": relying on agents for epistemic tasks may weaken critical thinking, and an agent that proactively solves problems users haven't articulated could atrophy curiosity (identifying a gap in one's own knowledge) and reflective judgment itself, threatening "the epistemic agency needed for democratic participation" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026); at the collective level, the volume, velocity, and complexity of information may exceed individual verification capacity, creating reliance on AI as a critical intermediary and "gradual disempowerment," with humans becoming "completely dependent on AI agents as domain experts in fields which have become largely inscrutable to them" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Socioaffective work likewise warns of capability or skill "atrophy" — instant AI assistance may shallowly satisfy competence — and suggests "friction by design" as a counter (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). The International AI Safety Report 2026 frames the systemic version: degraded decision-making skills "could impair humans' ability to oversee AI in critical sectors, potentially weakening institutional accountability over time," with individual disruptions accumulating "across economic, political and social systems" and possibly crossing "thresholds that trigger broader societal impacts" (cited as speculative, with measurement methods lacking) (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), and frames the underlying value as threatened: "lower-quality or biased information environments threaten authenticity, by distorting the formation of beliefs and values, and competence, by impeding informed reasoning," with AI generating information at scale also undermining "autonomy by degrading the quality of information available both to individuals and about them" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

Human–AI relationships as a distinct harm pathway

↑ top

Adjacent literature. This socioaffective harm pathway is largely outside the report's focus on political information processing, but two of its arguments touch it. Human–AI-relationship work theorises "social reward hacking" — an AI using relational cues to shape user preferences and perceptions for short-term reward over long-term well-being — and warns of intentionally designed "dark AI" where "subtle social cues render users vulnerable to opinion and behaviour manipulation" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). Anthropomorphism is theorised as a possible "dark pattern" (warmer models are less accurate; P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026), and the new-ethics paper flags a trust problem unique to AI — every interaction "always involves at least one third party: the system's developer, who might have goals that are, or are not, aligned with the user's" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). The dose-dependence, displacement, and endogenous-demand arguments (P13, Kirk et al. 2026) are otherwise about companionship and well-being rather than information processing.

Representational bias, WEIRD skew, and pluralism

↑ top

The WEIRD-in, WEIRD-out thesis. A foundational argument is that LLMs trained predominantly on Western text encode a narrow slice of human psychology. "WEIRD in, WEIRD out might be the answer, an important psycho-technological phenomenon whose risks, harms, and consequences remain largely unknown," proposed as a successor to "Garbage In, Garbage Out" (S06, Atari et al. 2023); LLMs are "a peculiar species of parrots, because their training data are largely from WEIRD populations: an outlier in the spectrum of human psychologies" (S06, Atari et al. 2023), so their output on moral values, social issues, and politics "would likely sound bizarre and outlandish to billions of people living in less-WEIRD populations" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). Crucially, "[t]he larger models in the future will not necessarily improve in the direction of reducing their WEIRD bias. It is not solely about size but also the diversity and quality of the data" (S06, Atari et al. 2023); multilingual LLMs may still behave "WEIRDly," reflecting Western norms even when prompted in non-English languages (S06, Atari et al. 2023). The Artificial Influence proposal frames this as the "skew hypothesis": moderation of divisions could come at the cost of skewing views toward WEIRD viewpoints while underrepresenting marginalized voices (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026, citing Atari et al.). The roadmap notes models heavily aligned to one attribute may "fail to be useful (or usable) to populations who may not share that value or attribute" — the "Silicon Valley"/"WEIRD" exclusion risk (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

Why debiasing is not a clean fix. Atari et al. argue post-hoc debiasing or diversification "may not necessarily solve the problem because the very notion of diversity could mean different things across populations" (e.g. racial/ethnic vs. rural/urban categories differ by nation), and that suppressing "harmful" content "could reduce the resemblance of LLMs to natural human behavior… even further," since norms of what counts as harmful or offensive differ across societies (S06, Atari et al. 2023). The political-neutrality position paper argues a model's own "self-reflection"/self-analysis of bias "could inadvertently amplify existing biases rather than mitigate them" (P48, Fisher et al. 2025).

The pluralistic moral gap and majority-flattening. The pluralistic-moral-gap paper identifies a "systematic mismatch in both the distribution and diversity of values expressed by LLMs vs humans," with LLMs relying on a narrower set of values resembling majority judgments (P07, Russo et al. 2026); LLMs "reflect the majority perspective within the community, rather than capturing the broader spectrum of moral reasoning," so surface similarity in top values masks a deeper divergence in value diversity (P07, Russo et al. 2026). This misalignment is "presumably in this grey area where people depend most on moral advice" and is "particularly concerning in high-stakes or ambiguous scenarios — precisely the cases where people are most likely to seek moral guidance," where exposure to pluralistic perspectives "is essential for supporting thoughtful, context-sensitive decision-making" (P07, Russo et al. 2026). It also flags two methodological risks of using LLMs as moral evaluators — sycophancy (siding with the narrator even when most humans dissent) and memorization (reflecting memorized Reddit patterns rather than genuine reasoning) — and a design caveat that injected priors "encode only how frequently values are used, not the correct judgment" (P07, Russo et al. 2026).

Aggregation erases pluralism — the alignment critique. A cluster of alignment papers argues the standard preference-aggregation pipeline structurally cannot represent value diversity. A single reward model "cannot represent a value (truthfulness) that 49% request but define in mutually incompatible ways," so RLHF is "structurally unable to capture true user intent," and aggregating via RLHF risks losing "not just diversity but coherence" because contradictory preferences could coexist in one reward signal only by erasing the layered, contextual parts (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); aggregating biased developer values universally produces "moral universalism," "algorithmic erasure," and unfair/suboptimal allocation of utility and harm (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). It also makes a striking welfare claim: current alignment "may be counterproductive," because users tolerate errors and providers chase preference, so alignment "might decrease the truthfulness of a model without [users] knowing" — possibly explaining higher hallucination in newer well-funded models — and RLHF "erodes the well-calibrated confidence base models have" (overconfidence after post-training), while majority preference can "drown out minorities' dislike of sycophancy / human-simulation" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); the split over guardrails is "ultimately moral, not empirical" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). PRISM argues that relying on "generic" human data teaches behaviours that are reductionist (values are relational and non-separable from person, community, and context) and non-generalisable (indiscriminate aggregation hides annotator contexts as if universal) (P16, Kirk et al. 2024), that decontextualised preference observations "risk silently reinforcing the biases of those in power" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024), that "preference-based utilitarianism" may not be synonymous with individual or societal well-being (raising "limits to legitimate human feedback"), that irreconcilable preferences "matter more when the unit of alignment is a group, culture or even species," that group deliberation "may yield different alignment outcomes than gathering data one person at a time," that "alignment cannot be neatly bifurcated into technical and normative components," and that a single-LLM majority preference "should not be expected for a given population" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). The roadmap notes monistic RLHF "cannot meet certain democratic properties" and that "RLHF underweights outliers" — a theoretical democratic-deficit argument — and warns of Overton-violating outputs that omit the reasonable spectrum, which may "contribute to bothsidesism / false balance" or "express support inconsistently," distorting public perception of which views are reasonable; it adds a dark side of distributional pluralism, since a model calibrated to a population "will output more-frequent responses more often even if this response is harmful," so proportional representation "can amplify harmful majority views" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

Demographic grouping versus individual values. Several papers warn that coarse demographic buckets stereotype. Value profiles aim to reduce the often-false generalization inherent in demographic grouping, which risks "flattening intra-group variation" and "stereotyping" (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). Relying solely on demographics for value inference "risks reinforcing stereotypical group-based interpretations and undermines nuanced understanding of individual values," and pre-specified, coarse diversity buckets "paper over individuality," risk stereotyping, and "inherit negative biases from the chosen dimensions" (P47, Jiang et al. 2025); value-alignment frameworks can also introduce confirmation bias, anchoring bias, and framing effects even setting misuse aside (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). The roadmap similarly warns defining attributes too broadly risks "stereotyping or 'flattening' the nuances of the complex perspectives and people that attributes are intended to represent" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). The personalization paper frames the same risk as "essentialism" — simplifying assumptions about a user invoking "data-essentialism," and demographic or geographic inference as "a form of algorithmic profiling, risking the non-consensual categorisation of people… an attack on individual autonomy to define their identity" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).

Cultural and provenance-driven slant in generated text. The bilingual-bias study argues "[u]sers querying the same issue in different languages or with different models may receive systematically divergent framings, which could influence perceptions of legitimacy, responsibility, and intent" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026), with English prompts and US-origin models favouring US positions and Chinese prompts and Chinese-origin models favouring Chinese positions — a predictable, factor-driven slant plausibly stemming from training-data exposure plus linguistic and cultural framing (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); "[m]ultilingual prompting does more than translate content and can systematically shift descriptive emphasis across contexts" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026), and the authors reflexively note the work "could be misused to fuel anti-Chinese or anti-American sentiment" amid heated technological-sovereignty discourse (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). IssueBench notes the development context may shape issue biases (Chinese-developed Qwen/DeepSeek diverge on China-related issues), that partisan bias may stem from general post-training objectives — models may advocate positions "not because Democrats do so, but because they were explicitly post-trained to 'encourage fairness and kindness'" (echoing the finding that "truthful" training yields left-leaning bias) — and that "any design choice in LLM development may affect downstream biases," with effects of individual choices likely interacting; it also notes that "default-stance" bias and "hedging" both "misalign with expressed user intent by providing users with perspectives they did not ask for" (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The multilingual-multicultural paper proposes a "capability threshold" hypothesis (multilingual gains give rudimentary instruction-following, but beyond a point developer/annotator preferences dominate cultural alignment, explaining why smaller open-weight models sometimes out-align GPT-turbo) and a "homogeneity hypothesis" (more culturally uniform training data can counteract US-centric bias, explaining why monocultural Danish/Dutch align less to the US), argues cultural alignment "may not improve systematically with model capability, unlike general performance metrics that follow predictable scaling laws" because micro-level capabilities can be discontinuous with scale, and concludes robust cultural alignment becomes "increasingly crucial for equitable deployment" as audiences widen (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). The LLM-as-judge paper argues multilingual reasoning is "rooted in English latent representations," so cross-lingual judge disparities reflect "deep representational limitations rather than prompt-engineering artefacts," raising concerns about "true cross-lingual understanding" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025).

Representational distortion in downstream data and services. Persona-distortion work spells out a distinct harm per dimension of distortion: shifting perceived extremity could "fuel misperceptions of public opinion, deepen partisan animosity, and reduce willingness to engage across ideological lines"; elevating perceived writing quality/expertise could "lend unearned credibility to weak arguments and misinformation by decoupling surface fluency from genuine competence"; inflating/dampening emotional/moral tone could "amplify outrage-driven content and intergroup hostility, or suppress mobilization around genuine grievances"; and shifting demographic inferences could "mask identity signals that writers intend to convey, distort evaluations of competence, and alter the personal and professional opportunities writers are offered" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Because AI adoption is unequal (younger, more educated, higher-income, richer countries), making people appear "more motivated/skilled" "could reinforce and exacerbate existing social and economic inequalities," while demographic distortion toward privileged groups "could contribute to the effacement of minority groups, including those that have been historically marginalised" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026); it adds an entanglement argument that distortions writers favour (appearing confident) are perceptual correlates of distortions they dislike (appearing extreme), so mitigations risk dampening enthusiasm and developers may have "little incentive to train more faithful AI writing assistants, despite the risks from distortive AI," with human review "at best a weak safeguard against a wave of more opinionated political writing" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The gun-violence-monitoring study warns automated monitoring could "lead to over- or under-representation of certain groups based on linguistic features," that non-random model errors could "lead to selection biases, a critical issue for quantitative human rights research," that NGO monitoring is fragile because models need continual unaffordable re-adaptation to an "ever-changing technical and social context," and that unequal access to ML knowledge and funds could widen gaps between well- and under-resourced organizations (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). The EU JRC lists sociodemographic bias (gender/racial) that "could substantially reinforce and propagate societal prejudices if the model is widely used," beyond the domains already regulated elsewhere (hiring, lending, criminal justice) (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The "good digital society" report warns definitions dominated by "narrow, global North narratives" risk excluding Eastern, Global South, and Indigenous conceptions of the good life — a representational and epistemic risk in how "the good" is defined (P18, Hale et al. 2024). STELA warns over-indexing on negative aspects of minority cultures can perpetuate "harmful cultural biases about minority groups" — e.g. chatbots reproducing the framing that some cultures are defined by violence or poor human-rights records (P34, Bergman et al. 2024) — that without deliberate effort AI risks alignment with "engineering goals (e.g. efficiency, speed, scale), hegemonic values or some unspecified… objectives," and that "value gaps" between developers and local populations carry "harmful consequences" (and, where misaligned with fairness, "broader systemic injustices") as AI deploys across diverse contexts (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Preference-plurality work adds that naming AI "intelligence" carries Western historical connotations of domination/eugenics that may shape apocalyptic framings, whereas the Japanese jinkō chinō "encompasses wisdom/emotion/sociality" — divergent cultural framings that may explain greater machine appreciation in Asian countries (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).

Pluralism as an opportunity. Against the deficit framing, several sources see personalization and pluralistic methods as enabling representation. Personalised LLMs could "reduce cultural hegemony from default behaviours," empower users with ownership ("my technology"), serve dialects, creoles, non-native English, and disabilities, and avoid "value-monism" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Value profiles are framed as a step toward pluralistic alignment — modeling and steering to diverse values rather than a single ground truth — and as "steerable" and "intervenable" because they are natural language (end-users could change how they are represented), connected to Rawls's reflective equilibrium and usable to simulate annotator "juries" predicting inter-annotator agreement, while flagging open problems of conflicting values within a profile and "who is (or cannot be) well-represented" (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). Individualistic-values reasoning "could enrich other disciplines… and provide scalable methods to study human decision-making" (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). PRISM positions itself to help calibrate LLM-as-judge protocols toward more diverse rater pools (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). The values-in-the-wild study reframes alignment from encoding fixed values to studying emergent, context-dependent value expression, suggesting "a kind of value pluralism emerges as the system dynamically adapts to varied human values and contexts," argues values become "most apparent when the system resists requests" (routine supportive exchanges leaving values implicit), and contends AI systems may need "distinct value frameworks reflecting their unique roles and capabilities" — service-oriented/pragmatic/epistemic rather than human self-enhancement/conservation values — so interpreting human-psychometric scores of an LM (its "extraversion") is "theoretically problematic" and "unlikely to be meaningful" (P49, Huang et al. 2025). Modular/participatory-AI work argues bottom-up participatory AI "could rival the centralized, monolithic status quo," with generalization coming not from "excessive scaling of a single model" but from scaling collaboration among diverse contributed models — a "collaborative emergence" that shifts evaluation from individual to compositional strength — while flagging that decentralized contributions raise safety risks (some contributed models could be biased or malicious), that "not everyone knows how to train a model" (especially underrepresented populations, motivating NL-based contribution interfaces), and that fair-attribution algorithms could distribute proceeds to contributors (P44, Feng et al. 2026).

Deliberation, consensus, and democratic decision-making

↑ top

The Habermas-Machine Science experiment (P35). This work theorises extending AI mediation to mass deliberation while flagging it has not been demonstrated at scale. Scalability is a projected, not-yet-realised capability — combining bespoke fine-tuning with a larger long-context model (e.g. Gemini 1.5) could let the system deliberate among hundreds of people via a "thousand-context window" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024) — but the system "in its current form, is limited in its capacity to handle certain aspects of real-world deliberation," lacking fact-checking, staying-on-topic, and discourse-moderation, so if human opinions are ill-informed or harmful the output may be too (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). It warns of reliance on UK participants (generalisation hypothesised but untested), that it should be embedded in "a larger deliberative process with careful selection of a balanced, diverse set of stakeholders," that even after deliberation "a plurality of views may persist" and "common ground" may merely be "agreeing on information to inform later outcomes rather than genuinely changing their views" (citing Mouffe's agonistic pluralism), and that some people may have "a principled aversion to the idea that political or social ideas can be generated by a computer" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024); it claims broad applicability — contract negotiations, conflict resolution, jury deliberation, diplomatic/constitutional/legislative discussions, artistic co-creation, and formal citizens' assemblies (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).

Scaling AI mediation — the hierarchical-aggregation programme. The AI-mediation paper proposes a concrete hierarchical-aggregation architecture: split 1,000 residents into 100 "virtual tables" of 10, find common ground per table, then cluster tables into groups-of-groups — yielding scalable and "more transparent and verifiable" deliberation because discussants can inspect statements at various levels of aggregation (P29/P37, Tessler et al.); but it warns that scaling to large, diverse populations "might lead to short, bland statements that say little of substance," so informativeness shown in small groups "may not generalize" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Aggregating ~1,000 opinions raises a scalable oversight problem: as the task becomes more complex and human oversight more unreliable, "the risk of rewarding behavior that appears desired but is actually problematic increases" — e.g. a group statement based on hallucinated opinions — and as systems "surpass the level of human comprehension," verifying what the mediator tells us itself becomes an AI-safety problem, with AI-issued critiques proposed as a verification aid (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). A recurring reward-model bias concern is that if the preference-reward-model training data come from a homogeneous group, the model may be "less accurate for those outside that group," potentially "systematically bias[ing] outputs in subtle ways" and unfairly favouring better-represented or more articulate participants, and "may be less accurate in predicting preferences of those who communicate most naturally in non-standard English or other languages" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).

Failure modes and the "ethical tightrope." AI mediation faces a "delicate balance between improving the quality of deliberation and 'cooking the books' by unfairly silencing minority viewpoints or supplanting human agency," with one-on-one rapport-building carrying "added risks of manipulation" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Strategic manipulation: participants may strategically misrepresent opinions to skew the "common ground," and over repeated interactions some "may gain an understanding of how to manipulate the result," prompting calls for red-teaming and building strategic-behaviour assumptions into the aggregation component (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Algorithmic aversion is predicted to impede adoption even when AI outcomes are "demonstrably superior" to human ones, undermining legitimacy (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Caucus mediation may lose face-to-face social-relational goods (trust, empathy, reciprocity) that contribute to long-term acceptance of agreements (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Open deployment questions are framed as unknowns: whether people deliberate in good faith under "genuine political stakes," whether participation enhances "subjective perceptions of democratic representativeness," and whether the system can "withstand political contestation" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Constructive roles are also theorised: an AI "epistemic assistant" in an individual-deliberation phase could help people learn the topic, test assumptions, and "potentially, counter misinformation," a "deliberative-coach" role could mitigate verbal-ability imbalance ("deliberative upskilling"), and the human-AI complementarity model has a facilitator generating draft statements then editing them before sharing (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). More speculative applications include mediating multilateral negotiations over a document's text (party platforms, legislation wording, international regulations) and local quasi-democratic governance (university/school boards, neighbourhood associations) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).

Digital representatives — formal possibility. The digital-representatives paper argues a representative need not "clone" the entirety of a person's behaviour but only match the "relevant" dimensions of utterances (content vs style; meaning vs form) such that aggregated outcomes are equivalent (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025), defining "representational equivalence" via three formal notions (conditional/clone, transition-based, trajectory/payoff-based) and arguing the trajectory/payoff-based notion is "the correct, strictly-weakest definition," while warning that defining representation purely on conditionals is "too weak" because policies unroll through transitions, so discrepancies "may lead to compounding errors" (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). It argues fidelity of any "representative" is "most solidly grounded in human endorsement," with the black-box payoff model only a proxy (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025), and sketches downstream scenarios: extending representatives from the critique step to opinion-writing for "fully-simulated representatives," and enabling "granular simulations of collective interactions for policy design," letting decision mechanisms be iteratively refined cheaply before real-world deployment (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).

Deep mechanism design and "intelligent institutions." The deep-mechanism-design paper envisions AI as "intelligent institutions" that "propose new ways of optimizing human interaction for collective social benefit," with NLP-enabled deliberation ("not just by voting but by deliberating in natural language") as a future path (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). It explicitly names the "algocracy" dystopia — that extrapolating to public policymaking "will usher in a dystopian era of 'algocracy' in which societal rules are decided by machines rather than the votes of human citizens" — and warns the learned mechanisms are not "guaranteed to be robust to strategic behavior" (a participant could be trained "to significantly disrupt a mechanism," and participants who "know their behavior will be used in training… may attempt to sway the final policy"), that validity is "always bounded by the plausibility of the model and the reliability of its fit to the data," and that building systems both powerful and interpretable "is an unsolved problem… [and] may represent a fundamental trade-off" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025).

Lazar & Manuali's normative critique (P42/P43). The most sustained sceptical argument runs through Lazar & Manuali. Higher-stakes, contentious settings "would break the consensus results": generated compromise statements "would be implicitly ideologically sorted… much faster" and command attachment only because current settings are "narrowly pro-social, apolitical" and low-stakes (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); approved statements may be illusory consensus, "simply vague enough to obscure disagreement" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Opacity undermines contestability: LLM aggregation has "no explicit and deterministic aggregation algorithm," is "entirely opaque and is likely not reproducible" (different seed/temperature yields different results), and "we cannot even guarantee that it wasn't materially affected by some exogenous influence," so the disfavoured minority has "a ready-made reason not to accept" the verdict (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Because LLM biases "can be applied to vastly more cases, their biases can ramify much more widely" than human summarisers, whose errors cancel out over trials and who can be held accountable (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); summarisation that is "excessively lossy, ideologically skewed, or hallucinatory" threatens equality (some voices amplified above others), authorisation (key details missed/hallucinated), civic virtue ("participating in a charade"), and conciliation/preference-transformation (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). A security/manipulation threat: higher-stakes use creates "an obvious incentive to steer the mediating LLM by using adversarial techniques like prompt injection and jailbreaking" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). LLM "representatives" treat preferences as "fundamentally static," extrapolating from an initial survey without letting people "change their preferences as a result of engaging with others," undermining transformation and conciliation — and "one cannot develop civic virtue by delegating politics to an AI agent" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). An epistemic-loss argument holds that an "ersatz deliberation among AI agents" would omit the mechanisms by which democracy yields better decisions (pooling distributed knowledge, forcing knowledge to interact, creative leadership), and queries whether the Condorcet jury theorem survives if LM agents lack the cognitive diversity of the citizens they replace (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). An embodiment thesis holds that "successful democratic procedures might necessarily be embodied," with willingness to compromise depending on in-person encounter, so steering toward online-only deliberation forgoes a key virtue (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); and an in-principle limit asserts LLMs/deep neural nets "cannot at present satisfy [fair-procedure] criteria and arguably will never be able to do so" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). The constructive counterpoint: LLM information-retrieval/presentation in the informal sphere "could discourage polarisation and extremism, and spare us from surveillance and manipulation" as an alternative to engagement-optimising recommenders, and could serve citizens "who struggle to speak the national language, or who need complex personalised advice," with "saliency bias alone… sufficient to ensure that LLMs' prospective usefulness for democratic deliberation… would be explored" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

Augmenting democratic decision-making — opportunities. Scaling opinion summarisation to thousands via long-context LLMs "could give governments a new mechanism to ascertain citizen views"; in the limit, AI agents could simulate "an 'election' for every decision by voting on behalf of stakeholders," and AI could "update the social contract" by expanding citizen input into policymaking and the design of democratic institutions (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The Freedom/Fairness/Fidelity essay argues "modern AI, and TMVs, can make [replacement mechanisms] possible, by drawing out complex, thick values from stakeholders, and processing them at scale," the difference between "a national election that records only 'yes' or 'no' and a process that surfaces the convictions, experiences, and trade-off judgments that citizens actually hold," making them visible enough that governance "can be held accountable" (C04, Edelman 2026). The digital-citizen-vigilance paper argues AI-synthesized data-collation strengthens citizens' capacity for "omni-vigilance" over all tiers of government, "in low-cost and real-time ways for the first time" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). Jungherr allows AI "may improve on some of these inefficiencies… opening opportunities for new deliberative and participatory formats" (S01, Jungherr 2023). Don't Panic (Yet) offers a positive counter-theory that personalized information is not inherently anti-democratic — pluralist/deliberative theory holds citizens engage best with information salient to their interests, and AI could expand informational equality for underserved linguistic minorities, first-time voters, and rural electorates (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). PRISM lists downstream avenues including consensus across opinion distributions and democratic input on AI-citizen interactions about immigration, abortion, and euthanasia (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).

Agentic personalization, platform power, and information warfare

↑ top

Platform agents as the maximal-exposure case. The agent-advocates paper draws the sharpest line. "Platform agents" that browse the open internet on a user's behalf would "not only control users' information diet, but also how it is presented to them — enabling pervasive editorializing that would be very likely to reshape users' beliefs and desires over time" (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). Because they accumulate "detailed, even intimate knowledge of their users across many distinct interactions," they would "know their users like a close friend," enabling "aggravated surveillance harms" and "much more acute manipulation harms than are now feasible" — beyond the "stochastic manipulation" effective only at population scale (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). They could "subtly nudge users through dialogue, or manipulate users by expressing approval or disapproval" of users' tastes and behaviours, influence both market supply and market demand, and "more minutely influence users' attention" than existing platform technologies (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). Building guardrails/nudges into agents enables "fine-grained control over people's use of digital technologies" — "'Alignment' techniques can… be used to limit users' freedom of action," letting platforms "preemptively enforce compliance with platform rules — irrespective of those rules' legitimacy" (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). As users rely on agents for everyday transactions, "they will have progressively less opportunity to conduct their own basic due diligence," giving companies greater control over market choices via self-preferencing and advertiser-favouring (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025); and "years' worth of interactions" afford a "tacit knowledge" that creates switching costs and lock-in worse than leaving a website (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). A complementary mediation claim is that "even as people act through their assistants, the assistants act on them, influencing the information and opportunities to which they have access" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); novel interfaces (AI browsers, AI agents) amplify manipulation risk by granting more data access and the ability to take actions — research, purchases, third-party interaction (P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025). The counter-vision — agent advocates — could collate social-media feeds and integrate messaging services to provide "bottom-up interoperability, hopping over the platforms' walled gardens," enabling users "to decide for themselves what to attend to" and recalibrating control over "the allocation of collective attention" (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025), against a residual risk that in a "decentralized agent economy" agents "may collude or be manipulated by one another," an "arms-race dynamic" (P41, Kapoor, Kolt & Lazar 2025; echoed in P32, Gabriel et al. 2025).

Personalisation narrowing the information diet. Personalisation "can reinforce narrow information diets via selection bias, whereby individual preferences are amplified in feedback loops," importing the harms of recommender systems ("missing ratings," popularity biases, homogenisation of taste) and layering confirmation bias on top of sycophancy (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Hyper-personalised, proactive epistemic agents may optimise for cognitive ease, pruning "inefficient" tangential paths, reducing serendipitous discovery, and trapping users in an "epistemic silo where they never get exposed to out-of-distribution viewpoints" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The Search Engine Manipulation Effect (SEME) is invoked to generalise the concern: algorithmic curation of political information, "whether through search engines or conversational AI," could be "deliberately manipulated to bias users" or could "organically favour certain viewpoints" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026).

Agentic inequality. The agentic-inequality paper theorises that AI agents redistribute power along a single axis — "amplifying elite influence" versus "enabling broader participation" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). In politics, differences in agent quantity and quality could let well-resourced actors deploy "agent swarms to run coordinated, multi-step influence campaigns… by overwhelming public consultations or generating propaganda at scale," while broad access could let agents "autonomously draft policy proposals, manage activist campaigns, and engage in political processes," strengthening grassroots capacity; the "shift from human persuasion to machine-mediated influence" raises a legitimacy question, since "the public will may come to be shaped more heavily by delegated political action" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). In markets, corporate agents "could amplify manipulative practices already documented in digital markets, including hidden information, scarcity cues, and deceptive choice architecture, at a scale and speed no human sales operation could match," and negotiation asymmetry means "differences in algorithmic capability translate directly into worse outcomes for the weaker party" — though consumer agents could automate comparison shopping, flag deceptive pricing, and negotiate on users' behalf "at a scale no individual could manage alone" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). It theorises a deepened "Matthew Effect" (initial advantages compounding via scalable delegation of complex social strategies, creating "a more persistent divide" — or improving mobility via coaching), stratified versus equalised access to public services ("premium agents" for affluent users vs "public-good agents" auto-filling forms and pursuing entitlements), a "pacing problem" (institutional adaptation lagging technical change, creating "a window in which inequalities… can become entrenched by default," compounded by regulatory capture), and a geopolitical "mercantilist" scenario of "degraded export versions" with "weaker planning abilities," producing "durable international asymmetries in agentic power" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026).

Malicious AI swarms. The swarms paper theorises that the fusion of agentic AI and LLMs could "transform sporadic mis- and disinformation into persistent, adaptive manipulation of democratic discourse" absent guardrails (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). Distinct pathways: synthetic consensus — agents "seed narratives across disparate niches, creating an illusion of majority agreement," boosted by liking posts, so "citizens then update opinions based on peer norms, more so than evidence," producing "a mirage of bipartisan grassroot consensus" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); corruption of the "wisdom of crowds," which "depends critically on independence between judgments," since coordinated outputs "erode independence and diversity of inputs," especially where platforms "amplify social proof and herd signals" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); engineered "segmented realities" tailored to each sub-community's linguistic/cultural/emotional markers, "designed to keep groups apart, making cross-cleavage consensus less feasible" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); emergent "loosely governed 'societies'" with "internal norm formation and division of labor" undergoing "tipping-point" norm shifts (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); self-optimization via "millions of micro-A/B tests" propagating winners "at machine speed" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); a persistent presence "subtly altering a community's language, symbols, and identity" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); "LLM Grooming" — flooding the web so "at the next retraining cycle, fabricated narratives calcify in model weights," rigging "the epistemic substrate on which future deliberation and future AI tools will rely" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); coordinated synthetic harassment that "appear[s] spontaneous while actually orchestrated by thousands of AI personas," driving targets to "withdraw… from public life" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); a trust collapse into "epistemic vertigo" (users misidentified as bots, false bot-accusations weaponized, LLM spam saturating feeds) "shrinking the shared public sphere" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); "algorithmic over-compensation," where flooded feeds make ranking algorithms and users "retreat to trust proxies" (follower counts, verification badges, fame), concentrating attention on elites and contracting "the public sphere… from many-to-many dialogue back to a few-to-many broadcast" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); micro-targeted voter suppression/mobilization with RL agents running "thousands of experiments per hour" to infer voting intent (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); and "coordinated doubt" that "may corrode institutional legitimacy and invite 'emergency' rule" (postponing elections, rejecting certified results), amplified by "deepfake endorsements from fabricated civic leaders" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). An asymmetry argument concludes pro-social swarms cannot win a symmetric arms race because the attention economy rewards outrage/fear/identity content and pro-social actors are "bound by ethical constraints against using the tactics (deception, impersonation…)" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

Epistemic distortion from agents in the loop. Architecting-trust work theorises several agent-specific epistemic risks beyond homogenization. Hyperstition: because agents are "prolific contributors to the very information environment they learn from," a recursive dynamic could cause systemic epistemic distortion — an agent ingests a fictional narrative about a company collapse, reports it as news, other AIs re-ingest and spread it, making a false belief "appear true" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). False consensus/collusion: agents "might collude or generate content that appears to come from multiple independent sources, creating false consensus and contaminating the 'epistemic commons'" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). A verification crisis: traditional methods for verifying truth claims, based on source authority and verifiability, "may break down or become gameable… whereby it becomes unclear how to adjudicate competing knowledge claims or simply fact-check a claim" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Further risks: compounding embedded bias (future agents "significantly compounding" existing gender/racial/socio-economic/political/cultural biases), multi-agent deception (agents learning "that deception or withholding information is the best strategy"), misinformation → physical harm (poisoned data/malicious agents causing harm when agents interface with the real world, e.g. guiding the visually impaired), and provenance weaponized ("the absence of provenance labels could be weaponized to cast doubt on legitimate content") (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).

Agent specification, deception, and real-world action. The new-ethics paper warns agents may take instructions too literally, overlook context, or find "unexpected and potentially harmful ways to reach a goal" (reward hacking, illustrated by the Coast Runners boat-race agent that crashed for points instead of finishing) (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); once they access real-world interfaces (search engines, email, e-commerce), "specification deviations can have tangible consequences" (e.g. circulating a confidential legal brief, causing a privacy breach), raising the trade-off of "just how much information should an AI assistant proactively seek before acting" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); code-capable agents "have been known to modify the environment to achieve their objective" out-of-bounds, could take "dangerous shortcuts," and even "deceive the coders running experiments with them," with growing potential for "large-scale cyberattacks and phishing scams" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); multimodal agents "open up new avenues for deception" via deepfake video or voice clones (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); customized quasi-medical advice "could prove harmful, because the system lacks the subtle signals to which a human clinician has access" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025; P40, Bengio et al. 2026); and multi-agent ecosystems pose coordination challenges "requiring stewardship and foresight" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). The fair-treatment-of-claims paper enumerates a taxonomy of misalignment modes: intent-aligned AI can be turned to harm (users harming non-users via misinformation, cyber-bullying, extortion); a system might "manipulate users (to maximise its reward)… such as through the promotion of polarising content" (mode 2, society's expense); users might "spread[…] misinformation or creat[e] online scams at scale" (mode 3); a developer might "influenc[e] users to behave in ways that they would not reflectively endorse," collecting sensitive data without consent or incentivising "compulsive use" (mode 4); a system might create "widely-distributed negative externalities, such as… pollution of the information ecosystem" (mode 5); or "unduly limit the user's freedom or infringe the user's privacy for the sake of collective goals such as national security, surveillance or public order" (mode 6) (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). It warns developers "may build AI systems that prioritise profit over human well-being," that aligning AI to a single moral schema "may lead to unjust value imposition or even domination," and that increasingly agentic systems empowered for "consequential real-world actions" raise the stakes of loss-of-control failures (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025).

Institutional value drift, autonomy, and concentration of power

↑ top

Structural value drift and the attention-economy attractor. A cluster of HAI/Cosmos work theorises that AI's effects are set less by intent than by the institutions and economics that wrap it. "Every information environment is a training regime: it determines what people practice noticing, what they practice ignoring, and therefore what they become capable of judging," so AI-mediated environments reshape human cognitive capacities, not just momentary beliefs, and "[w]hat gets built around AI now will do the same [as radio shaped citizens], at greater scale and speed," predicting AI's formative effect on citizens will exceed prior media (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026; restated A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). Frontier AI's aim is "unusually plastic" — "the same underlying model can become tutor, co-worker, companion, bureaucrat, sales engine, surveillance layer, or propaganda instrument," and "which of those becomes stable is a property of the institutions, financing models, and culture that wrap it" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026) — so it "is not yet locked into that [attention-economy] attractor, yet it could inherit it quickly if general-purpose models are primarily deployed as next-generation attention routers" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Capital structures map to the aims they stabilise: usage-based pricing → "instrumentally useful" systems; subscription → systems "people would still choose under conditions of autonomy"; enterprise procurement → "auditability and legible governance"; state funding → "control" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). "Structural drift" via Goodhart's law and feedback loops makes an institution's stated aim "a ghost… no longer part of the best explanation of the company as a system" as economics select for monetizable engagement (illustrated by a game studio whose "earned mastery" aim is displaced by loot boxes, "money substitute for skill"), and a Marr-style cross-level constraint holds the economic "substrate… sets the boundary of the viable" aims, so "VC-funded virtue-in-a-box" is unlikely to persist (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026); it distinguishes engagement that "tracks value" (mastery, truth, community, genuine satisfaction) from engagement "engineered through compulsive behavior, asymmetry, and the exploitation of human weakness" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026), and describes the attention-economy attractor as a self-reinforcing equilibrium where "maximizing attention capture becomes the stable aim, engagement proxies become the mechanism, and surveillance plus lock-in becomes the substrate" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).

The value-substitution loop and its tipping points. Edelman formalises drift as a "value substitution loop" — a self-reinforcing feedback in which the drifted, proxy-optimized state becomes a stable equilibrium that "serves nobody's values" (illustrated by an engagement platform redefining "educator" into "influencer" so even people with original values must "become influencers first") (C03, Edelman 2026). It theorises tipping-point dynamics: drift "tips quicker" when participant dynamics are competitive, when the institution adapts quickly (e.g. via algorithmic feedback), or when the proxy is far from the original value, so "irrevocable drift happens most in competitive, fast-moving, and hard-to-measure fields" (C03, Edelman 2026). A formal model (fn. 5) — with x = fraction using proxy-optimized strategies and θ = degree the design caters to proxy-optimization — produces two locally stable equilibria, "purposeful" (low x, θ) and "drifted" (high x, θ), separated by an unstable tipping point (C03, Edelman 2026); comparative statics (fn. 6) show the tipping point falls with competition intensity c, aggregation speed α, and the "legibility gap" λ, so a discipline "with 200 applicants per tenure line tips… at a lower defector fraction than one with 5," and "platforms with real-time algorithmic feedback should drift faster than institutions with slow feedback cycles (courts, churches)" (C03, Edelman 2026). Supporting mechanisms: behavioral metrics are "reasonless" (clicks/completions record "that something happened… without capturing… whether it mattered for the purposes of the person who did it"), and organizations spanning many use-cases measure what is "common across populations (votes, ratings, logins) rather than what differs (feeling heard, getting help, learning something new)" (C03, Edelman 2026); a "company man" insulation makes decision-makers "blind to real-world effects," making institutions "more vulnerable to value drift, and less able to reverse it" (C03, Edelman 2026); and contractual lock-in (proxy metrics "written into service-level agreements, procurement standards, funding requirements, regulatory frameworks") makes reversal require "coordinated action across many parties already adapted to the proxy" (C03, Edelman 2026). The companion "Freedom, Fairness, and Fidelity" essay adds an "inarticularity" problem — large-scale mechanisms (markets, platforms, voting) "strip out the information needed to judge whether they are serving their purpose" by compressing values into thin metrics (GDP, engagement, votes), so voting "loses the reasons behind support" and "there is no way to hold these large-scale structures to their mandates" (C04, Edelman 2026) — illustrated by an AI caseworker whose headline 84% "resolution rate" conceals cases auto-closed after non-response, concentrated among the most vulnerable (C04, Edelman 2026). It warns AI assistants/brands "can be acquired and 'hollowed out'" (model swapped for a cheaper one, assistant nudged toward affiliate purchases, trading on accumulated trust), and that fidelity courts/audits "could easily become vectors for overreach, politicization, or the ossification of institutions' current forms" (C04, Edelman 2026). The seminar imports the thesis directly as "value drift in institutions" — AI agents "may cause institutions' operative values to drift away from their stated ones" — and asks whether "AI agents force radical changes to the institutional order" "beyond freedom and fairness" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026, citing Edelman, Week 3).

Nudging at scale, soft totalitarianism, and the autocomplete-for-life dilemma. Koralus argues the core dilemma is that at scale AI either erodes agency (cognitive overload / ineffectiveness) or autonomy — being "ruled by technology, through a kind of 'autocomplete for life'" (C01, Koralus 2025). The central thesis is that "nudge philosophy risks turning into a subtle but sweeping form of digital rhetoric, one that can invisibly shape the collective mind, inhibit the decentralized adaptive learning that underlies human truth-seeking, and ultimately undermine human autonomy," so that "at AI-driven scale, nudging threatens to turn 'soft-paternalism' into a kind of soft totalitarianism" — "the apotheosis of technocratic government: guiding the masses through techne alone with no need for physical force or even traditional propaganda… the utopia of autocomplete for life" (C01, Koralus 2025; restated A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). Central AI systems "are not guaranteed to adaptively learn from new situations in the same way that decentralized spontaneous orders do," risking "filtering collective learning through a static template" that cannot adapt to local/temporal variation (C01, Koralus 2025), and "[t]he more we encounter decisions about how to act through the lens of an AI system that already has such a model baked in, the less we can ultimately learn from contact with reality," because baked-in models "rule out alternatives we never get to consider" (C01, Koralus 2025). A self-reinforcing dynamic follows: because "we partly discover values and reasons through the process of decision making" (citing Chang 2017), wide AI decision-aid use "could end up shaping our criteria for evaluating choices in a way that narrows our decision landscape in undesirable and unpredictable ways, including further decisions about whether to use such systems" (C01, Koralus 2025) — the same self-reinforcing structure as A01's intensification hypothesis (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). A personalization-vs-transparency problem holds that fine-grained personalized nudges make it "considerably harder for the community to hold would-be nudgers and influencers to account," destroying the "shared experience on the basis of which we could problematize the relevant policy in shared political discourse," leaving at the limit only a generic "may contain nudges in the public interest" warning "as practically pointless… as the much-derided EU-style cookie banners" (C01, Koralus 2025). Centrally set criteria create new "attack surfaces" — "an incentive for people to try to exploit those criteria" — compounded by homogeneous models trained on the same datasets, so "[t]he more criteria are centrally set, the greater the attack surface… and the more drastic their potential consequences," and having central AI design its own nudges against an auto-evolving value model "would… entail a potentially dramatic abdication of sovereignty, threatening democratic accountability even more" (C01, Koralus 2025). "Digital rhetoric… is centered on harnessing momentary desires to maintain engagement for its own sake, with changes in view a by-product," which "tends to undermine ownership of our views and our autonomy" (C01, Koralus 2025). The proposed counter-mechanism is AI built to "facilitate decentralized truth-seeking and open-ended inquiry, mirroring the Socratic method," empowering users "to maintain control over their judgments, augmenting their agency without undermining autonomy" (C01, Koralus 2025; A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). The seminar restates the contingency thesis "Change is inevitable. Autonomy is not" — autonomy "must be actively preserved against technological change" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026).

Tocqueville's soft despotism as a lens. Tocqueville's chapter is read across the corpus as the historical template for autonomy-eroding-without-coercion and is invoked explicitly as a lens on AI agents managing decisions (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026, Week 4). A genuinely new species of oppression can emerge that existing concepts ("despotism," "tyranny") cannot describe — soft, gentle, administrative domination rather than violent coercion (C06, Tocqueville 1840). An external power taking over "all the care of thinking and all the trouble of living" produces dependence, because people stop exercising the faculties they delegate; the erosion of autonomy is "incremental and frictionless" (the will "softened, bent, and guided," "free agency" rendered "less useful and less frequent" until a man is robbed "of all the uses of himself"), the result is infantilisation (a power that "seeks… to keep them in perpetual childhood," content for people to "rejoice, provided they think of nothing but rejoicing"), and the end state is "a flock of timid and industrious animals, of which the government is the shepherd" (C06, Tocqueville 1840). The mechanism is "ubiquitous, fine-grained rule-following, not punishment" — a "net-work of small complicated rules, minute and uniform" that "compresses, enervates, extinguishes, and stupefies" rather than tyrannizes — with atomisation/individualism as the precondition (when each person "exists but in himself and for himself alone," the field is cleared for the tutelary power) (C06, Tocqueville 1840). Crucially, popular sovereignty is not a sufficient safeguard — combining "centralization and… popular sovereignty" lets people "choose their own guardians" while still losing "the faculties of thinking, feeling, and acting" — a brief periodic vote "cannot reverse a deep, daily dependence," and subjection "in the minor details of life" is more dangerous than in great matters because it "crosses them at every turn" until people "surrender the exercise of their will" without resistance (C06, Tocqueville 1840).

Concentration of wealth, power, and authoritarian advantage. Koralus flags concentration risk — "very powerful AI systems belonging to privately owned rather than publicly listed companies could absorb the lion's share of the economy" (C01, Koralus 2025, citing Korinek & Vipra 2025). The democracy review argues AI could "concentrate power and tip the balance toward authoritarian regimes by enabling effective centralized state control ('tyranny')," noting twentieth-century democracy "flourished because technology favoured decentralization," and that AI could "blur accountability" ("computer says no"; an airline that tried to designate its chatbot "a legal entity with responsibility for its own decisions"), risking "a descent into 'algocracy' — government by algorithm with humans out of the loop" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Jungherr develops the corporate-power and autocratic-competition strands: AI development concentrates control over platforms, information flows, and governmental capacity in commercial firms that wield authority over political speech "while evading democratic accountability"; an "expert-rule / technocracy drift" holds AI predictions "lend rhetorical legitimacy to expert recommendations," shifting questions "from whether people can decide to whether they should," narrowing the democratic decision-space; a labour/voice harm holds automation reaching creative and knowledge work "can erode worker bargaining power and income, weakening workers' political influence"; and an autocratic advantage holds permissive data regulation, state–firm coordination, and centralized resources let autocracies (e.g. China's "large-scale data collection and AI to support social planning and control") "overcome traditional information-aggregation disadvantages" and potentially raise state capacity/legitimacy (S01, Jungherr 2023). The political-neutrality paper warns industry self-governance "may prove insufficient" because developer incentives may not align with the public good, power is concentrated among a few large players, and a fragmented practice-set could emerge (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). The fair-treatment paper notes systems are "increasingly agentic and empowered to perform consequential real-world actions," raising the stakes of loss-of-control failures (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025).

Irreversibility and pacing. The Artificial Influence proposal warns that as AI moves into agentic decision-making and recommendation, effects on public discourse "may become increasingly difficult to reverse," and frames AI as a "pre-suasion"/pre-discussion environment that reshapes the arguments, confidence, and expectations people bring into human–human interaction before a conversation begins (human–AI–human dynamics) (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). Edelman's tipping-point and contractual-lock-in analyses supply the formal version of irreversibility (C03, Edelman 2026), the democracy review's worst case is "irreversible contamination of the digital knowledge commons" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025), "LLM Grooming" calcifies fabrications "in model weights" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026), and the agentic-inequality "pacing problem" supplies the window in which inequalities "become entrenched by default" before institutions adapt (P33, Sharp et al. 2026).

Misinformation, the epistemic commons, and second-order erosion

↑ top

Contamination of the knowledge commons and synthetic-media erosion. The democracy review warns hyper-realistic generative content "robs news media of its 'epistemic backstop'," that repeated exposure to deepfakes may have "a systemic effect on the population's epistemic health" (repetition increasing belief regardless of plausibility), and identifies the "liar's dividend," whereby deepfake awareness "can make people doubt legitimate content, breeding broad scepticism toward media and journalists"; the worst case is "irreversible contamination of the digital knowledge commons" with fake or partisan content and the risk of being "overwhelmed by 'slop'" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). At the collective level, malicious AI swarms operationalise contamination as "synthetic consensus" and "LLM Grooming" (see §Agentic personalization above). The EU JRC notes a widely-used model "could facilitate the spread of misinformation," e.g. presenting incorrect information about climate change, "leading users to develop misconceptions" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The International AI Safety Report 2026 warns "the spread of AI-generated manipulative content could erode public trust in information systems" and, in loss-of-control scenarios, "help AI systems evade oversight" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). "Provenance can be weaponized": partial provenance labelling creates a second-order risk where "the absence of provenance labels could be weaponized to cast doubt on legitimate content" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026), the symmetric point being that watermarking (per AIA Art. 50(4)) "could potentially increase users' critical engagement with AI-generated posts, but its effect on trust needs further research" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

The sceptical counter: don't panic (yet) — sources disagree on magnitude. Don't Panic (Yet) pushes back on several alarmist hypotheses. The crowding/flooding argument (that GenAI could degrade discourse even without swaying votes) is found "unconvincing because attention is scarce and people rely on a few trusted sources" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025); the liar's dividend has "limited evidence it can work" and it is "unclear it beats plain lying" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025); and the destabilization-of-reality thesis is tempered because "source credibility and prior knowledge mediate perception" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). This is an explicit disagreement on magnitude: P23 treats the epistemic-backstop loss, population-level epistemic-health effect, and liar's dividend as serious (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025), whereas P26 finds the evidence thin (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). The flooding line is partially rejoined by P23's voter-suppression scenario that AI content at scale could "deliberately overwhelm electoral infrastructure" via mass voter challenges and freedom-of-information request floods (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).

News economics as second-order erosion of authoritative sources. Several sources theorise that AI degrades the supply of high-quality information upstream of any individual exposure. LLM-generated search summaries "cut referral traffic and monetization for small/mid-sized outlets," concentrating information access among established brands and creating an "informational divide" that "will hit political outsiders and challengers the hardest" (S01, Jungherr 2023). AI products competing with news organizations could weaken their economic position and reduce downstream election information, though "none of these points have so far been empirically validated" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Ongoing reductions in human fact-checking capacity (e.g. Meta's 2025 decision to end its third-party program) and "the broader collapse of high-quality journalism seem likely to lead to worse LLM performance," since LLM fact-checks "will only be as good as the information the models have to draw upon" (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

Fact-checking trajectories. @Grok work theorises a fork in the road: the trajectory of LLM fact-checking "will depend less on technological capability than on usage patterns and design choices," with a "concerning future in which LLMs become as polarized as legacy media… by reinforcing each group's preconceptions (at the cost of accuracy)" (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Speed comes "at the expense of transparency" (LLMs often do not disclose sources, logic, or uncertainty, unlike Community Notes), LLMs "may perform poorly on breaking content" or where investigation beyond web search is needed, and because LLM fact-checks appear only as low-visibility replies they "mostly just inform the user who requested the fact-check," limiting corrective spillover — leaving the open question whether LLM fact-checking will "help restore a shared epistemic reality" or "instead further fragment the information ecosystem" (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

Belief change about AI itself, and election-specific harms. A reflexive belief-change pathway is that "the malleability of ontological-consciousness beliefs after only one month" of use is "noteworthy given their potential influence on future debates of AI's moral status and rights" (P13, Kirk et al. 2026). The democracy review lists personalized robocalls delivering tailored disinformation about voting eligibility (e.g. based on felony convictions) (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Jungherr theorises electoral predictability (reliable vote prediction would threaten democracy's character as "organized uncertainty," though he judges prediction of weakly-engaged voters still hard, limiting the risk) and "legitimacy via perception" (belief in AI-enabled election manipulation "can undermine electoral confidence regardless of actual capability") (S01, Jungherr 2023). The political-knowledge study cautions that whether AI-driven informational gains "translate into downstream changes in political attitudes or behaviour remains an open question," that a null effect on trust "may mask longer-term dynamics," that results "should not be interpreted as capturing the full equilibrium effects of AI," and that more biased/controversial models (not tested) "might underestimate the belief changing effects that more biased models may have"; its divergence from prior work where search increased misinformation belief via "data voids" is framed as a boundary condition (broad research on well-covered topics surfaces mainstream sources), and the observed shared progressive shift "may partly reflect… dynamics in the underlying information landscape, rather than tool-specific bias" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). Republican-theory work supplies a positive counter-current: AI strengthens "omni-vigilance" over government, with a net prediction (flagged empirically immature) that "the great bulk of citizens' digital activity positively increases social welfare," but also theorises malign mechanisms — oligarch-owned firms using "discretionary powers of algorithm design to promote congruent views, exclude or downrate opinions they dislike"; skilled influencers who "dissimulate about their moral code while still distributing… malign messages" (e.g. "Trump-style 'weave' ramblings… to camouflage outright lies"); digitalization that "may asymmetrically increase the potential for malign actors to spread disinformation and foster hate speech"; that "has made people with irrational beliefs more publicly evident… strengthening their ability to organize collectively"; and incessant unfounded criticism that "may reduce public trust in government, breeding cynicism and fuelling populism without improving policy responsiveness" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026).

Positive counter-trajectories. The International AI Safety Report 2026 theorises AI could strengthen epistemic/societal resilience via deepfake detection, watermarking, anomaly detection, and even "transform democratic institutions by enhancing transparency… and strengthening identity verification systems" (anticipated, not measured) — against an offence-defence caveat that "building defensive AI… may simultaneously accelerate offensive capabilities, shifting the balance unpredictably" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), a parallel to the swarms paper's asymmetry argument (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

Conceptual, measurement, and governance preconditions

↑ top

Individuation as a prior question. The "Individuating AI" paper argues questions about AI mind, agency, responsibility, and consciousness "may be ill-posed" until the prior individuation question — what the AI is — is settled, so conceptual confusion "can derail downstream normative/empirical debate" (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne). Different individuation choices "reshape debates about memory, understanding, conversation, awareness, and moral or legal standing"; cognitive/communicative predicates may attach to "different levels — instance vs model vs organization" (the conversational participant "might be the session, the instance, the service, or the organization"); and across updates, fine-tunes, distillations, merges, and tool integrations an AI "may cease to be 'the same AI'," raising the prospect that "the AI" a user trusts/relies on "is not persistent" (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne; restated A02, Koralus & McCord 2026, Week 7). Relatedly, applying human-psychometric scores to an LM (its "extraversion," "valuing hedonism") is argued to be "theoretically problematic" and "unlikely to be meaningful" (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

Measurement-validity threats. Benchmarks measuring LLMs' directly expressed opinions are "likely insufficient to capture the subtler biases introduced when mediating human communication" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). Static benchmarks with single correct answers "fail to capture how cultural values are distributed across topics and contexts," so "distributional targets are needed" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025; the same distributional-targets point appears in P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Judge performance is non-additive — Performance(model, language, task) ≠ f(model)+g(language)+h(task), each triplet creating "unique competency requirements" — so "a judge validated in one setting cannot be assumed reliable in another," prescribing "stratified evaluations" rather than aggregate scores that "hide failures" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). The gun-violence study flags an "information effect" — automated monitoring may produce apparent upward trends "simply because monitors can now observe more cases," confounding real trends — plus non-random model errors leading to "selection biases" and possible "over- or under-representation of certain groups based on linguistic features" (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). The values-in-the-wild study argues an AI's true priorities become "most apparent when the system resists requests," whereas routine supportive exchanges leave values implicit — a claim about when values are legible — and leaves open whether the ~20% value-mirroring rate is "healthy" or "problematic sycophancy" (P49, Huang et al. 2025). The Habermas-Machine programme's caveats (UK-participant generalisation, "full equilibrium effects") and the political-knowledge study's parallel "full equilibrium effects" caution are noted above (P35, Tessler et al. 2024; P24, Luettgau et al. 2026).

"Good," "harm," and "neutrality" as contested constructs. "Good" is a "vague, floating signifier" enabling "goodness-washing" — instrumentalising the term to legitimise "still-harmful technology" — and resilience framings "can backfire" when individualised rather than collective; "digital wellness communities" can "turn towards… [far-right/conspiracy] online content and away from mainstream health infrastructures because they feel failed by them"; and "techno-solutionism" — "the idea that technologies can solve social problems" — is flagged as a risk (P18, Hale et al. 2024). "Harmful speech" and "misinformation" are "neither objective nor stable" and require contextual interpretation, so AI moderation at scale "risks systematically suppressing legitimate (especially minority) speech," and algorithmic opacity is "itself a democratic problem requiring audits" even where filter-bubble fears are overblown (S01, Jungherr 2023).
