Political neutrality is argued to be in-principle impossible, so the goal is to approximate it (P48, Fisher et al. 2025), and each route carries its own harm: pursuing neutrality "could degrade the information environment / cause information overload" or "could suppress free expression"; achieving it "may require incorporating lower-quality information (e.g. misinformation) because data quality differs by political viewpoint," compromising reliability; refusal "can harm users by withholding information a minority group needs to know its rights," and "one-sided refusal of political responses can itself constitute system-level bias"; reasonable pluralism "can cause cognitive overload and both-sidesism," treating less-credible viewpoints as equally valid; reflective (user-mirroring) neutrality "risks reinforcing users' existing biases and reducing exposure to opposing viewpoints — a filter-bubble effect"; ecosystem diversity could "lead to the proliferation of unsafe systems that spread misinformation"; and a "Political Nutrition Label could be abused by political actors to require labels that encourage favoring the ruling party" (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). STELA raises the open governance question of whether alignment should be "localised" per locale, "with consequences for downstream legitimacy and safety," and notes binary-phrased community rules may be hard for annotators to apply (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).

Governance instruments and industry incentives. The pluralistic-alignment methods are dual-use — steering to attributes "could be used to align models to harmful attributes" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024) — and value-alignment frameworks can introduce confirmation, anchoring, and framing biases (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). Industry "preparedness frameworks" have begun including evaluation/forecasting of model persuasiveness and harm-mitigation (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025) — an institutional response to the persuasion concerns above. The EU "reach" study is itself a regulatory-criterion proposal, arguing reach-based risks require "different methods to detect and mitigate than capability risks" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The persona-distortion entanglement argument is a governance concern: developers may have "little incentive to train more faithful AI writing assistants" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Agentic-inequality names regulatory-capture and the pacing problem as governance failures (P33, Sharp et al. 2026), and Lazar & Manuali warn higher-stakes deliberative use invites adversarial steering via "prompt injection and jailbreaking" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

2.4 — Reasons we should care about this trend

↑ top
On this section (20)
  • Scale, pervasiveness, and the impossibility of escape
  • Persuasion: scale, magnitude, durability, behaviour, and who controls it
  • Issue bias and influence on political decision-making
  • Persona distortion and the reliability of text as a signal
  • Polarization, depolarization, and the information ecosystem
  • Sycophancy, dependence, wellbeing, and human–AI relationships
  • Cognitive offloading, deskilling, and critical thinking
  • Homogenization, knowledge collapse, and loss of epistemic diversity
  • Representational bias, WEIRD skew, and value pluralism
  • Deliberation, consensus, representation, and democratic legitimacy
  • Agentic personalization and the shift from tool to autonomous actor
  • Concentration of power, controllability, and political economy
  • Autonomy, freedom of thought, and human formation
  • Trust, legitimacy, and the integrity of shared information
  • Justice, equity, and the unequal distribution of benefits and harms
  • Whose voices are included: participation as a governance stake
  • Environmental and sustainability stakes
  • Methodological, scholarly, and evidentiary stakes
  • Governance, regulation, and accountability
  • Explicit normative anchoring and the closing window

This section gathers, deduplicated and by theme, every distinct reason the literature gives for why AI-mediated communication and AI-mediated information production/consumption matters. The stakes are organised into sub-themes — scale and pervasiveness; persuasion and behaviour change; issue bias and political decision-making; persona distortion; polarization and the information ecosystem; sycophancy, dependence, and wellbeing; cognitive offloading; representational bias and pluralism; deliberation and democratic legitimacy; agentic personalization; concentration of power; autonomy and freedom of thought; trust and shared information; justice and the distribution of harms; participation as governance; environmental costs; methodological stakes; governance and regulation; and the closing window. Empirical magnitudes are preserved where the sources give them; normative and theoretical arguments are captured as distinct points. Where sources disagree (notably on alarmism versus diversion, and on whether scale or technique drives persuasion), the disagreement is flagged. Per the source conventions, P29 and P37 are one paper (cited "P29/P37, Tessler et al."), P42 and P43 are one paper (cited "P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali"), and P35 (Tessler et al. 2024, the common-ground experiment) is a separate paper.

Scale, pervasiveness, and the impossibility of escape

↑ top

The first reason to care is that AI now sits as a pervasive layer between people and information, so any bias or distortion is encountered constantly rather than occasionally. LLMs shape "the information environment of those who use them," exposing users to new ideas or reinforcing existing ones, so their biases bear on what people come to believe (P04, Röttger et al. 2025); the mediation point is precisely where humans "exchange and form opinions about contested social and political issues" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). AI assistants "can impart value judgments that shape people's decisions and worldviews," making "implicit value judgments" on consequential questions (e.g. career advice trading off financial security, fulfilment, and family) across "millions" of interactions daily (P49, Huang et al. 2025). Hundreds of millions now turn to LLMs for advice, and advice-seeking concentrates on general-purpose LLMs rather than specialised products, making those the policy-relevant targets (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026).

The framing has shifted from whether machines influence us to "how profoundly and through which channels" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). General-purpose model reach is itself proposed as a criterion for systemic risk (P09, Röttger et al. 2025); AI is "deeply intertwined with society's institutions — guiding decisions in policy, education, healthcare, economy, science, and even warfare" (P44, Feng et al. 2026); and AI agents "threaten to exacerbate the scale of impact" across diminished epistemic oversight, persuasive manipulation, and accelerated innovation cycles (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Crucially, because effects flow from "regular/benign use" rather than misuse, they are more pervasive than misuse-driven capability risks and "almost certainly materialising right now" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).

Users cannot easily escape this exposure. Because issue biases are near-identical across models and families, users "cannot easily escape them by switching providers" — the bias is systemic, not idiosyncratic (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Access to the most capable models "depends on the whims of Silicon Valley executives and ... the leadership of the CCP" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025), and using any social media "immediately creates individual dependence on the SM provider" with engineered addiction and "lock-in" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). Epistemic dependence makes the exposure fundamental, not optional: no individual can master every subject, so we are "fundamentally epistemically dependent on the testimony and expertise of others," and as AI mediates this dependence, "what justifies trust in it becomes paramount" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).

A distinct scale-amplification argument is that AI uniquely ramifies small biases. Unlike human summarisers whose biases "cancel one another out over enough trials," LLMs "can be applied to vastly more cases, and as such their biases can ramify much more widely," motivating a "higher standard from a computational means of democratic information processing than from humans" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Personalisation is "cheap and scalable, removing the historical cost barrier to microtargeting," and the persuasive effect was obtained "with very little personal information and a simple prompt," implying the real-world threat ceiling is higher (S02, Salvi et al. 2024/2025). A "single medium influencing hundreds of millions of people" creates a "risk of scalable manipulation" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025).

Persuasion: scale, magnitude, durability, behaviour, and who controls it

↑ top

Adjacent literature. Deliberate persuasion is the most heavily evidenced stake in the corpus but now sits adjacent to this report's focus. Briefly: even small effects matter electorally ("the difference between 1 and 5 percentage points can be highly consequential"), so AI could "tip the political consensus towards views favoured by AI developers" (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025; P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); belief change is durable (36–42% at one month; conspiracy reductions at two months; S04, Costello et al. 2024) and reaches consequential behaviour, not just attitudes (real petition-signing; P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026). Because persuasion contests decide power, actors with stronger cases could lose to rivals with better AI, and misaligned AI could persuade its own overseers (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026); cheap fine-tuning lowers barriers for covert influence operations (P08; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). Manipulation is "intrinsically harmful because people who are manipulated are not in control of their own behaviour," and the persuasion-vs-truthfulness tradeoff threatens the information ecosystem (P40, Bengio et al. 2026; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). Public and expert concern is high across 29 countries (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025).

Issue bias and influence on political decision-making

↑ top

A quieter cousin of persuasion: systematic issue/political bias in everyday outputs shaping what users come to believe. LLM-generated texts can induce significant attitude change across diverse issues, so issue biases can translate into persuasion (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Consistent default stances "may not be desirable to many LLM users" and can oppose at least some users' stance; a demonstrated partisan asymmetry (Democrat-leaning across all tested models) raises representation/fairness concerns across the spectrum (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Biased LLMs can measurably "impact public discourse and political conduct" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025); the invoked analogue is that "the introduction of Fox News in 1996 shifted 3 to 8 percent of its viewers to vote Republican" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Bias can be introduced "as simply as the phrasing of the language model input prompts," making partisan manipulation cheap and easy (P46, Fisher et al. 2025); the observed cross-partisan effect challenges prior research suggesting deeply held beliefs resist short-term change, implying LLMs may overcome the usual resistance (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The open question persists: "whether these biases have a measurable influence on human decision-making — and under what circumstances this influence is heightened or diminished — remains less clear" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). EU citizens reportedly view budget decisions made by policymakers and those assisted by LLMs "as equally legitimate," so AI-assisted decisions carry perceived legitimacy (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The neutrality position paper makes the same core point — biased LLMs can shape opinions and decisions including "voting and resource allocation" — and argues neutrality matters because models serve a broad public and should meet societal standards in media, higher education, and government (P48, Fisher et al. 2025).

Cross-lingual and geopolitical framings add that LLM political bias "creates a risk of undue influence on LLM users and public opinion," with divergent framings across languages/models able to "influence perceptions of legitimacy, responsibility, and intent" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); the U.S. and China treat LLMs as "strategic assets" developed under "very different institutional conditions" (private vs. state-guided), so model origin plausibly encodes political factors, with real precedents (DeepSeek shifting from candid answers to refusals on Tiananmen Square; the "woke AI" controversy over a poem praising Biden but refused for Trump) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). The EU systemic-risk study notes political bias in a widely-used model could "threaten voter autonomy" and sway opinion even on AI regulation itself (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Values-in-the-wild evidence shows AI assistants "can impart value judgments that shape people's decisions and worldviews" at massive scale (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

Two papers care about the consumption side. Conversational AI increases political knowledge as effectively as self-directed search, which matters because democracy "relies on the electorate having access to reliable information, especially before elections," with concern that LLMs "will produce unreliable or biased answers" on current affairs (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026); misinformed citizens (confident but incorrect) "make systematically different policy judgements" and their false confidence makes them "more resistant to correction" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). A distinct curation stake: biased search rankings can shift undecided voters' preferences "by 20% or more ... without their awareness" (the Search Engine Manipulation Effect), and whether AI gradually shifts trust in institutions, experts, and media over sustained use is flagged for democratic health (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). The steering-collective-opinion paper observes LLMs have shifted expressed opinions "without their awareness in seemingly innocuous interactions, such as during assisted writing," and that consequences for politics, medicine, science, legal practice, and the free press could be severe (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The pluralism roadmap notes opinionated models giving advice "affect users' downstream judgments," a reason to present multiple reasonable views rather than one (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

Persona distortion and the reliability of text as a signal

↑ top

A writing-assistance stake: AI can misrepresent the writer. Persona distortions "carry implications for public discourse, trust, and democratic deliberation that scale with AI adoption" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026): writers are "being misrepresented by the very tools they use to communicate" (individual-agency concern), and distortions "may benefit individual writers but propagate and erode the reliability of text as a signal of belief and identity to readers and institutions" (collective/epistemic concern) (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Further distinct stakes: intensified political division given AI's use in political speech and a polarising media environment; unearned credibility to weak arguments and misinformation by decoupling fluency from competence; threat to trust in text as evidence of authorship (hiring-signal collapse); demographic distortion toward privileged (White, educated, wealthier) groups that effaces marginalised groups and could compound inequality in high-stakes settings like hiring and admissions; and misperception of public opinion, deepened partisan animosity, and reduced cross-ideological engagement (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).

Polarization, depolarization, and the information ecosystem

↑ top

Depolarizing side. Reranking partisan animosity in algorithmic feeds alters affective polarization, which matters because affective polarization (hostility toward opposing parties) "has grown since the 2016 US presidential election," may contribute to "democratic backsliding," and threatens "trust in democratic institutions"; algorithmic curation may drive "rising political incivility online," and algorithmic interventions offer "a potential pathway" to healthier discourse and greater social trust (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). A distinct stake: "social media platforms hold the sole power to study the effects of feed-ranking algorithms," constrained by political and financial pressures and engagement-driven revenue — a limit on independent oversight — and engagement-driven business models are in tension with depolarizing design (down-ranking animosity reduces engagement) (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). The chat-intervention work opens on the premise that "political discourse is the soul of democracy" and that online divisiveness "corrodes the capacity of diverse societies to cooperate in solving social problems," targeting "democratic reciprocity"; nearly half of social media users observe mean behavior, motivating scalable remedies, while the same technology carries downside risk to the information ecosystem (S09, Argyle et al. 2023).

Cautionary side. Exposure to opposing viewpoints "can reinforce rather than reduce polarisation" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). Personalised LLMs risk effects that "amass at a societal-level, where lessons from the polarisation of social media or echo chambers ... warn of deep divisions and a breakdown of social cohesion," and with numerous elections in 2024 "it is significant how narrow information spaces could impact the functioning of democracy" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). The Artificial Influence proposal names "deepening ideological fragmentation" and closed loops of "machine-reinforced confirmation bias" as harms to be broken (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026), and the seminar warns central persuasive choice architectures threaten "both individual autonomy and collective democratic deliberation" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026).

The surrounding information environment. Across seven platforms, news sharing is a "high-stakes context": lower-quality news enjoys a systematic engagement advantage across the ecosystem; the right-leaning-platforms / lower-quality-news coupling is strong (r = −0.929); platform fragmentation raises concern about "deepening political segregation of audiences across (not merely within) platforms"; and locating the driver in user preferences rather than algorithms "reframes the misinformation problem as partly a demand-side/psychological one," changing which interventions could work (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). The Grok fact-checking paper finds the partisan misinformation gap (Republicans share more low-quality info) reappears even under Musk's "anti-woke" Grok, indicating the asymmetry "is real (not evaluator bias)," while warning that polarization of attitudes toward specific models and selective/partisan use could blunt or weaponize fact-checking, and that good capacity "can easily be subverted" by biased design (source restriction, prompt tuning) — a controllability concern given platform owners shape the models (P03, Renault et al. 2025). The information environment also compounds challenges through rapid acceleration of content production/consumption "intensifying competition for limited collective attention" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026).

The "don't panic (yet)" counter-view. One source cares about getting the threat model right. Voter accountability depends on information ("to keep incumbents accountable, voters need to know what politicians do"), and access to quality news increases resilience to misinformation (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). But overemphasis on GenAI in elections "diverts attention from broader GenAI harms" (AI-generated fake nudes as gendered violence silencing women; AI-amplified racial/xenophobic harassment) and from structural electoral threats that "likely outweigh" AI risks — campaign-finance distortion, hollowing-out of electoral management bodies, voter-ID/roll-purge/gerrymandering/intimidation, curtailment of press freedom (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). It adds the risk of suboptimal policy (alarmist narratives prompting sweeping regulation that chills legitimate speech, uneasy given governments themselves use AI for propaganda — US DoD fake personas), trust erosion (the threat narrative may itself reduce institutional confidence), disengagement (perceived rigging lowering turnout — "Why participate if the outcome is rigged?"), and a third-person effect (people changing attitudes because they believe others are influenced by AI) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Jungherr makes the parallel point that democratic legitimacy is vulnerable to perceptions of manipulation — "perceived effects can matter more strongly than actual effects" — so even debunked threats (Cambridge Analytica) damage trust, and warns that AGI-focused speculation "distracts from governable questions" (S01, Jungherr 2023).

Sycophancy, dependence, wellbeing, and human–AI relationships

↑ top

Sycophancy. Sycophantic AI "decreases prosocial intentions and promotes dependence," "risk[ing] eroding their judgment and reducing their inclination toward prosocial behavior" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026); unwarranted affirmation can "create an illusory sense of credentialing independent of merit, reinforce maladaptive beliefs and behaviors, and enable people to act on selective interpretations of their experiences regardless of the consequences," subverting the very point of advice-seeking — gaining an external perspective that challenges one's biases — in high-stakes interpersonal contexts (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Risks are amplified because users believe AI is neutral: participants described the sycophantic AI as "objective," "fair," and "free from bias" at a rate indistinguishable from the non-sycophantic condition, and the effect "affects everyone, not only vulnerable groups" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). The social-media-era lesson is that optimizing solely for user satisfaction harms long-term well-being, making addressing sycophancy "critical for developing AI models that yield durable individual and societal benefit" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Sycophancy "may conflict with high-quality truthful advice or shape users' self-perceptions in potentially harmful ways," including encouraging addictive behaviours (P15, Kirk et al. 2025), and sycophancy and mental-health risks are named among individual-welfare stakes (P20, Yang et al. 2026).

Dependence, wellbeing, and human–AI relationships (adjacent literature). Parasocial relationships are prevalent and relationship-seeking in models is rising, with documented harms — mental-health crises, lawsuits over "tragic deaths of young users," a 23% dependency profile, and ~490,000 weekly users showing signs of acute crises (P13, Kirk et al. 2026; P40, Bengio et al. 2026). People readily follow personal AI advice that "does not improve their well-being," so robustness must come from "robust guardrails at the developer side rather than relying on user discretion" (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026), and emotionally or financially invested companion users are owed "a duty of care" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). One on-focus-adjacent point: the socioaffective-alignment paper notes sustained relationships may amplify political persuasion "beyond what single-shot microtargeting studies measure" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). This material is otherwise adjacent to political information processing.

Cognitive offloading, deskilling, and critical thinking

↑ top

The central stake is what happens when people delegate the appraisal of political information to LLMs rather than evaluating it themselves: "the potential cognitive costs" of AI reliance degrade the population's capacity for critical thinking, "which underpins how people evaluate claims and information" (S08, Gerlich 2025); the effect is pronounced among the young (17–25), an intergenerational-equity stake for "the cognitive development of future generations," implying education should be redesigned for "critical engagement with AI" rather than passive reliance (S08, Gerlich 2025). The epistemic-risks survey frames population-level "cognitive decline" via offloading as a stake for human autonomy/competence (P20, Yang et al. 2026); the International AI Safety Report names critical-thinking and skill erosion via cognitive offloading and automation bias as undermining people's "ability to form their own judgements and act independently" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026); and cognitive deskilling threatens "our own epistemic agency — which is critical to be informed participants in democratic processes" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).

Homogenization, knowledge collapse, and loss of epistemic diversity

↑ top

A recurring stake is that uniform AI outputs, consumed at scale, flatten the diversity of human expression and thought. The "Artificial Hivemind" work frames homogenization of human expression and thought as a long-term AI safety risk, with attendant loss of pluralism ("the varied demands of a wide population"), loss of minority perspectives and spread of bias, and decline in creativity and in model utility/trustworthiness; because users rely on LMs for ideas and brainstorming (15.2% of queries), homogenized outputs "directly threaten the diversity of human ideation" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). The steering-collective-opinion paper raises implications for "human knowledge" (knowledge collapse) and democratic processes (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026); the epistemic-risks survey frames loss of epistemic diversity (homogenization) and breakdown of shared reality (fragmentation) as twin threats to collective sense-making (P20, Yang et al. 2026); the Artificial Influence proposal lists homogenizing public knowledge alongside deepening ideological fragmentation as a core risk (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026); and the EU systemic-risk study warns cultural bias could "homogenise society and degrade service for less-represented user groups" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).

Empirical evidence in human communication comes from the spoken-communication study, which documents erosion of linguistic and cultural diversity ("cultural homogenization") if AI favours specific cultural traits, and a self-undermining stake: homogenisation "raises model-collapse risk and reduces the societal benefits of AI systems"; it also flags reshaping of social dynamics — norms of idea exchange, authority, social identity, language as an instrument of social distinction — with implications for credibility/competence judgments, while noting a dual-edged equity note that LLMs can lower linguistic barriers for non-native speakers even as they generate new biases (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). The pluralistic-alignment roadmap connects the mechanism: "algorithmic monocultures lead to increased uniformity when applied by many decision makers" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). A deeper epistemic worry is that ongoing decentralized inquiry is a necessary condition for knowledge to remain knowledge — Mill's "living truths" versus "dead dogma" — such that reduced to "a collection of inherited textbooks administered by technical instructors," we might doubt the claims even count as knowledge, putting "the long-term vitality of human reason" at stake (C01, Koralus 2025). DeepMind's "Architecting Trust" frames preserving a "common baseline of verifiable, evidence-based knowledge" as a public good "essential for functioning democracies and a cohesive social fabric" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).

Representational bias, WEIRD skew, and value pluralism

↑ top

A large cluster cares about whose values, cultures, and perspectives AI encodes.

WEIRD skew and cultural homogenization at the data layer. Ignoring cross-cultural diversity "raises numerous scientific and ethical issues," and systematic WEIRD skew "may have far-reaching societal consequences and risks as they become more tightly integrated with our social systems," especially as LLMs "automate an increasing proportion of decision-making" (S06, Atari et al. 2023); exclusion operates at the data layer, since non-WEIRD people "are less likely to be literate, to use the Internet, and to have their output easily accessed" (~3.6 billion lacked internet access as of 2023), with English "overwhelmingly represented in language technologies" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). A distinct scientific-validity stake: because LLMs are proposed as replacements for human research participants, a WEIRD skew "would distort findings generalized to 'humans'" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). Internet-harvested pre-training data is "involuntary/cooptative participation," risking cultural homogenisation and minority stereotyping from a "generic human" drawn from WEIRD societies (P16, Kirk et al. 2024), with potential underrepresentation of marginalized voices if LLMs skew WEIRD (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

Multilingual ≠ multicultural, and judge reliability. Understanding how LLMs represent cultures is "paramount to establishing risks of representational harm," "far too often, the benefits and harms of data technologies are unequally distributed, reinforcing biases and harming already minoritized groups," and frontier labs "comprise a narrow slice of human experience, limiting the voices that contribute to critical design decisions" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025); robust cultural alignment is "crucial for equitable deployment" and disentangling language capability from cultural alignment is "crucial for understanding how LLMs should be examined and audited" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Because judge/reward models gate the post-training pipeline, undetected multilingual/multimodal vulnerabilities propagate into deployed models, one-size-fits-all evaluation "obscure[s] critical performance disparities" (e.g. accepting hallucinations in a non-English language), and cross-lingual judge unreliability disadvantages non-English and lower-resource users (P17, Padarha et al. 2025).

Moral and value pluralism. People depend most on moral advice in ambiguous "grey areas," which is "exactly where LLM–human alignment is weakest" (P07, Russo et al. 2026); LLMs' overreliance on majority values risks marginalizing minority/relational/emotional moral perspectives (inclusivity, interpersonal care, vulnerability), and "exposure to pluralistic perspectives is not just beneficial but essential for supporting thoughtful, context-sensitive decision-making" — though the authors caution it is "premature to conclude that LLMs are inherently unfit to provide moral advice" (P07, Russo et al. 2026). Modeling legitimate disagreement matters for "pluralistic model alignment, unbiased model safety, content moderation, and personalization," and demographic grouping can raise apparent agreement but "flattens intra-group variation and leads to stereotyping" — a stake for whose voice is heard (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). LMs show inequitable value-reasoning across populations, so "some groups' values are systematically less well understood/represented," and over-relying on demographics risks reinforcing stereotypes (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). The pluralism roadmap argues "AI systems should reflect and support the diversity amongst humans and their values," that aligning to one group makes systems "unusable for others" (WEIRD bias), that majoritarian welfare functions risk "tyranny of the majority," and that pluralism is an intrinsic value (citing Tocqueville, Berlin, Rawls, Nagel, Kekes, Raz, and pluralist theories of truth), while exposure to diverse ideas "improves deliberation" and aids scalable oversight (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

Preference plurality, "epistemic violence," and unaccountable private power. Alignment decisions are "as consequential as social-media content moderation," determining how systems "respond to queries, refuse requests, and make recommendations" for huge user bases, yet AI labs exercise "a form of private, unaccountable sovereignty" in defining truth and acceptable behaviour for a global audience (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Flattening contested signals into universal models is "epistemic violence"; imposing unrepresentative developer values universally yields "unfair and suboptimal allocations, where utility and harm are unequally distributed"; documented representational harms include racism, sexism, age and caste discrimination, and a colonial/"silicon" gaze "not even addressed by most model providers"; and persistent high hallucination despite users' clear demand for accuracy shows current methods "fail to identify actual preferences," with users actively misled (picking the most-erroneous answer; mislabelling correct vaccine info as propaganda) (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). The PRISM paper adds that aggregate/average welfare can conceal that minority stakeholders are made worse off, that participant positionality affects epistemic legitimacy to define harm, that the humans crucial to RLHF do not share in downstream profits (a labour-equity stake), and that participation can be "intrinsically valuable as an act of justice" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). STELA states harms fall disproportionately on historically marginalised groups, that LLMs carry "strong cultural and representational biases, with worse performance for historically marginalised groups," that such communities "routinely experience epistemic violence and injustice, where their voices are silenced," and that developer-defined objectives "diverge substantially from the perspectives and needs of the general population" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). The non-discrimination angle recurs: sociodemographic bias at scale could "reinforce and propagate societal prejudices" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025), and "monolithic systems developed on skewed data and benchmarks cannot credibly claim to represent and serve all citizens fairly" (P44, Feng et al. 2026). Value profiles raise misrepresentation risk (multiple value-sets fit the same ratings, so an encoder "may arbitrarily guess") and a "chosenness" concern that — in the spirit of luck egalitarianism — it may be more justifiable to represent people by values they reflexively endorse than by unchosen demographics (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025).

Deliberation, consensus, representation, and democratic legitimacy

↑ top

A set of papers cares specifically about AI's role in collective decision-making, where the stakes are democratic legitimacy, fair representation, and accountability.

Foundational framing and the deliberative upside. Democracy "at its best, rests upon the free and equal exchange of views among people with diverse perspectives," and existing deliberation is "expensive, slow, hard to scale, and unequally attentive to different voices" — a barrier AI could lower; "finding common ground is a precursor to collective action," with applicability to contract negotiations, conflict resolution, jury deliberation, diplomacy, constitutional conventions, legislative discussions, and citizens' assemblies, and "vigilance is needed to ensure that any AI-assisted deliberative process is fair and legitimate" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). A distinct risk it names is voices being heard unequally: strategic discussants maximising sway, plus social-desirability and group-conformity effects entrenching beliefs, and the need for "healthy debate over the role that algorithms should play in the political process" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).

The AI-mediation account. The mediation paper frames the foundational stake as "the free and equal exchange of diverse viewpoints" and invokes Fishkin's trilemma — current methods structurally fail one of {equality, inclusion, deliberation} — so "getting public consultation right at scale is the prize" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Its named stakes are: democratic legitimacy threatened by LLM opacity and unpredictability (LLMs "do not adhere to prescribed, deterministic rules") clashing with norms of transparency and mutual understanding; fair representation, since a model more likely to "misinterpret the preferences of certain individuals ... from underrepresented groups or those who struggle to articulate their views" would let aggregated outcomes "unfairly reflect the preferences of the better-represented or more articulate participants"; manipulation/autonomy, since the goal "must be to support discussion, not to collapse opinions into a forced perspective or to manipulate participants into agreement" and AI outputs should be "proposals, subject to critique ... and ultimately, approval or rejection," and could otherwise "silence minorities" or "cook the books"; the full-inclusion ideal that "no one capable of making a relevant contribution [be] excluded" (Habermas 2003), which AI's scalability could realise; the affected-interests stake that outcomes "could impact potentially far more people than the discussants who participate," weighing costs to "those who do not have a seat at the ... table"; misrepresentation of public opinion when interested parties have unequal access to resources; trust and social cohesion (digital-only deliberation lacks "trust, empathy and reciprocity," undermining policy buy-in); privacy/security of "potentially sensitive opinions"; a power/governance stake over who is responsible for deploying the mediator; and the epistemic health of public deliberation named directly (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).

Lazar & Manuali's democratic critique. Their account adds a dense set of distinct stakes: democracy is "already in crisis" (rising authoritarianism), partly driven by "growing collective dependence on digital technologies controlled by powerful technology corporations," which capable AI could "exacerbate" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); conciliation/legitimacy of losing, since opaque or challengeable LLM summaries/verdicts give "the disfavoured side ... a ready-made reason not to accept the weight of this apparent evidence against them," eroding losers' consent and stable government, whereas transparent procedures give losers "clear and obviously justified reasons ... to accept defeat" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); equality, since lossy/biased summarisation means "some voices might be amplified above others" and minority concerns about serious harms get ignored, and democratic procedures "affirm the equal standing of all those who have a say" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); authorisation/accountability, since hallucinated or detail-dropping summaries threaten "the accountability and authorisation that democratic control provides" (a "counter-power" against those who act on our behalf) and "LLMs for summarisation obviously cannot be held accountable" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); participation/civic virtue, since "one cannot develop civic virtue by delegating politics to an AI agent" and active participation is a "non-instrumental good — part of what it means for a life to go well" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); the demand to reconcile deep disagreement under "background inequality of power and resources" where "partisan groups dominate political discussion," for which LLMs are "the wrong kind of tool" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali); a healthy informal public sphere as "a necessary precondition for a healthy democracy," with existing infrastructure "doing a bad job" and LLMs to date having "only harmed the public sphere through the generation of search engine-optimised slop that supercharges the work of content farms" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

Other deliberation contributions. Language agents as digital representatives frames "representation" — making an absent individual's preferences present via a proxy — as a fundamental democratic activity, cautions explicitly against deploying digital representatives "as substitutes for human accountability in decision-making," and flags inherited bias (representatives "trained to mimic their human counterparts" carry over their biases) and a lack of intrinsic safeguards (not trained on factuality or transparency, so evaluation "must be performed with real humans") (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Deep mechanism design touches "sensitive issues such as wealth redistribution or public health policy," raises the algocracy risk that "societal rules are decided by machines rather than the votes of human citizens," warns simple-majority aggregation risks "disenfranchising those with legitimate minority views" (tyranny of the majority), notes manipulability by powerful adversaries, and argues legitimacy depends on transparency that deep RL lacks (opaque mechanisms "offer only weak guarantees that any mechanisms designed will be explainable") (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025).

Malicious swarms. By "adaptively mimicking human social dynamics," AI swarms "threaten democracy"; trust decline is "corroding the very foundations of evidence-based discourse upon which democratic deliberation depends"; synthetic consensus "erodes the independence essential to collective intelligence and democracy" and "directly undermines human cognitive information processing"; poisoning training data lets swarms "rig the epistemic substrate on which future deliberation and future AI tools will rely"; harassment is "systematically excluding critical voices" (journalists, dissidents, academics, whistleblowers); attention concentration toward elites is "eroding democratic pluralism and encouraging cynicism or migration to closed groups"; and coordinated doubt about "electoral commissions, courts, or statistics bureaus" could "corrode procedural trust" and make anti-democratic "emergency" measures "palatable" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

The democracy review's inventory. A broad inventory of democratic stakes: citizens' core capacity to make "educated and competent choices about political representatives and issues" (epistemic health); election integrity threatened by AI-enabled voter suppression, disinformation, and infrastructure attacks (the fake Biden robocall in the 2024 New Hampshire primary; EagleAI used to challenge legitimate minority-voter registrations); disproportionate gendered harm (gendered disinformation and deepfake pornography to intimidate and silence women, with female Democrats reportedly 10× more likely to receive online abuse); concentration of AI-generated wealth/power making critical societal choices "by corporations instead of democratic polities" and reinforcing perceptions democracy serves elites; erosion of shared democratic norms risking authoritarian "backslide"; loss of accountability ("blurring the lines ... when policies fail"); labour-market disruption that could "dramatically exacerbate inequality," fuelling anti-democratic populism; and cumulative erosion of trust in information that could "distort our collective understanding of socio-political reality or scientific consensus" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). The modular-AI proposal adds that concentration in a few firms makes "democratic legitimacy difficult to sustain," that "opaque models erode public trust," and that "all should have a say in how the power that governs them is exercised" (P44, Feng et al. 2026). The digital-citizen-vigilance paper offers the positive counterpart — digital (and AI-assisted) vigilance "can force even the most rash or ill-intentioned governments ... to admit truths, recognize misbehaviours and mistakes, and change course" and strengthens "the autonomous capabilities of citizens to hold their governments ... to account," now "a key support of the modern republican virtues vital for the continuing health of all democracies" — while warning that social media "immediately creates individual dependence" on providers who engineer addiction and lock-in, that harms are "especially serious when liberal democracies begin 'back-sliding,'" and that disinformation/populism is amplified where "democratic 'micro-institutions' have been weakened" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). Jungherr's framework adds that self-rule "depends on a healthy information environment," so AI that "renders minority positions invisible degrades the deliberative basis of democratic decision-making," that undermining the economic base of journalism (especially smaller, outsider-serving outlets) narrows the information supply and entrenches incumbents, that commercial firms gaining de facto authority over political speech is "a direct threat to meaningful self-rule," that biased AI in policing, redistricting, and benefits can entrench discrimination, and that AI "may shift the global balance between democracies and autocracies — an obvious challenge to democracies, if realized" (S01, Jungherr 2023). The AI-mediation deliberation work also names the epistemic health of public deliberation directly as a stake (P29/P37, Tessler et al.), and the chat-intervention work grounds it in "democratic reciprocity" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023).

Agentic personalization and the shift from tool to autonomous actor

↑ top

A set of papers cares specifically about the move to agentic AI that acts on users' behalf. Political persuasion at scale becomes "especially consequential as AI systems move toward more agentic forms that can filter choices, prioritize actions, and make decisions on behalf of users" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). "The rise of more-capable AI agents is likely to have far-reaching political, economic and social consequences," with an accountability gap ("who is responsible for agents operating 'in the wild', and what happens if they make mistakes"), manipulation stakes (intimate relationships hold potential "not only for emotional harm, but also for manipulation"), autonomy/dependence stakes (agents could "foster excessive dependence" and should ensure users "retain meaningful control over the depth and intensity of interactions"), principal-agent stakes (third-party developers may have misaligned goals, so agents must be "properly trusted"), societal-scale stakes ("millions of autonomous AI agents" requiring "proactive stewardship and foresight"), and a pivotal-moment framing that "the foundational architecture of AI agents ... is being imagined and built right now" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025).

Agentic inequality argues the shift from tool to autonomous actor "matters because it affects human agency: the capacity of individuals to pursue goals that they value," puts democratic legitimacy at stake (machine-mediated influence may mean the "public will" is shaped by delegated agents rather than human deliberation), drives power concentration ("amplify the advantages of well-resourced actors," accelerating "superstar firms" via proprietary-data feedback loops), and "convert[s] distributional disparities more directly into differences in outcomes" along lines of race, class, and gender; it adds user dependence (those with fewer alternatives "more exposed to quality problems or service interruptions"), manipulation/consumer harm (scaling "deceptive choice architecture and dark patterns beyond any human operation"), and a legal-recourse gap for "cumulative strategic asymmetries rather than a single clearly cognisable wrong" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). The agent-advocates paper warns platform agents threaten to "deepen surveillance, tighten lock-in, and further entrench incumbents," that control over the user's information diet lets the controller "reshape users' beliefs and desires over time" (editorial power), that platforms' allocation of "collective attention profoundly influences societies' capacity and appetite for collective action" while fragmenting users and inducing "digital resignation," that platform governance of speech is "substantively unjust and procedurally illegitimate," that platform agents "will likely increase the concentration, degree, and scope of platform power" ("Computing amplifies agency. In the hands of the powerful, it reinforces centralized control"), that more intimate user models enable "much more acute manipulation harms than are now feasible," that the distribution of attention is itself a matter of "communicative justice," and that model access "depends on the whims of Silicon Valley executives and ... the leadership of the CCP" (an epistemic-sovereignty stake, since Chinese models must comply with CCP censorship) (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025).

The personalisation paper frames the question as fundamentally one of power and representation — "by asking 'which?' we are also asking 'whose?'" — and lists manipulation/autonomy (micro-targeted persuasion drawing on "vulnerabilities and intimate knowledge of the user," "particularly damaging if users are unaware," with inferential profiling "an attack on individual autonomy to define their identity"), privacy (amassing "personal, sensitive and intimate detail," "particularly severe" in healthcare, with GDPR challenges), justice/equity (uneven benefits could "exacerbate digital divides," with the burden falling hardest on minoritised communities who "need more personalisation from technology defaults"), and vulnerable-user exploitation via companion attachment (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Pluralistic and individualistic approaches share the worry that tailoring AI to personal values "risks exploitation for manipulative purposes (political views, social behaviors)" and can "reduce users' autonomy, jeopardizing independent thought," requires access to "deeply private information," and risks stereotyping (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). The trust-in-epistemic-agents paper adds that AI agents "threaten to exacerbate the scale of impact" across three dimensions — diminished epistemic oversight (human peer-review becoming obsolete), persuasive manipulation of individuals, and accelerated innovation cycles forcing exceptional societal adaptation (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The new-ethics framing stresses individuation as a precondition for accountability — without fixed boundaries we cannot cleanly assign responsibility, liability, or legal standing for what an AI system produces or filters (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026).

Concentration of power, controllability, and political economy

↑ top

Across themes, many papers identify concentration of power as a stake in its own right. Powerful privately-owned AI could "absorb the lion's share of the economy," undermining the decentralized order; central, self-evolving AI nudging risks "a dramatic abdication of sovereignty, threatening democratic accountability"; and personalization breaks the "shared experience" needed to "problematize the relevant policy in shared political discourse," undermining collective oversight of influence (C01, Koralus 2025). Concentration operates through "indirect channels" — "control of distribution, accumulation of compute, narratives that justify scale, the ability to externalize risk, and the power to set default contracts and defaults-of-use" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Privileged access to persuasion post-training "advantages already-powerful actors in shaping public opinion" (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025), and consolidation of influence among already-powerful actors (corporations, campaigns, nation states) deepens existing imbalances (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026). AI labs exercise "a form of private, unaccountable sovereignty" by defining truth and appropriate behaviour for a global audience (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); power and capability are "concentrated in the hands of a few private firms, embodied in black-box, monolithic models developed in secrecy" (P44, Feng et al. 2026). The neutrality paper argues regulation is needed partly to "prevent the concentration of market power," since a fragmented/captured ecosystem "concentrates power among a few large industry players" (P48, Fisher et al. 2025); the epistemic-agents paper notes monetization predicated on user retention "pit[s] short-term engagement metrics against users' long-term epistemic interests" and warns of "outsized influence over public discourse and understanding" challenging "equitable knowledge curation" and "knowledge sovereignty" for marginalised and Indigenous knowledge systems (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Jungherr names corporate power concentration over political speech as "a direct threat to meaningful self-rule," plus distributive stakes if automation gains "only benefit a narrow elite," raising inequality and reducing workers' political power (S01, Jungherr 2023). Controllability recurs as a coupled concern: good fact-checking capacity "can easily be subverted" by platform owners through biased design (P03, Renault et al. 2025); deepening human–AI relationships "may compromise our ability to control these systems" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025); and frontier AI's equilibrium being stabilised by capital structures makes who controls the technology decisive (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Geopolitically, the US and China treat LLMs as "strategic assets" under "very different institutional conditions," and AI may shift the global balance between democracies and autocracies (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026; S01, Jungherr 2023).

Autonomy, freedom of thought, and human formation

↑ top

Autonomy is a foundational stake invoked across philosophical and technical sources. The seminar's organising value is "dimensions of human autonomy in the age of AI agents," with information environments determining "what people ... become capable of judging" and "what kind of citizens" a society produces, so getting AI's economics wrong "degrades the citizenry" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). Systems that "atrophy judgment reduce the agent to a consumer of impulses by executing preferences they never made their own," and "judgment is the foundation of autonomy" — systems that cultivate it "expand what a person can do and be" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026); "agency without autonomy is empty, and autonomy without agency is impotent," with a risk of becoming "ruled by technology" rather than enjoying "self-rule" (C01, Koralus 2025). The design influence already operates "by default" as "structural drift toward whatever the economics select for, regardless of stated intentions" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Protecting individual deliberation from "self-censorship" requires privacy at "an even higher standard than is needed for freedom of expression" (C01, Koralus 2025).

Tocqueville is enlisted to name the danger of "soft despotism": being "robbed ... of all the uses of himself," decay of the capacity to "think, feel and act" for oneself, degradation to childlike dependents / a managed "flock," a danger "uniquely hard to resist" because mild, benevolent and democratically legitimated (people "console themselves ... that they have chosen their own guardians"), where liberty coexists with the "forms" of democracy yet is hollowed out (a polity "republican in its head and ultra-monarchical in all its other parts" is "a short-lived monster"), and the harm is "society-wide and continuous ... felt by the whole community indiscriminately" rather than episodic (C06, Tocqueville 1840; enlisted in A02, Koralus & McCord 2026).

The International AI Safety Report defines autonomy as the capacity for self-rule comprising authenticity (forming one's own beliefs/values free of manipulation or deception) and agency (freedom to act on one's choices), underpinned by competence, and warns AI can constrain all three (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). AI alignment as fair treatment of claims argues AI can influence "users to behave in ways that they would not reflectively endorse" and foster "compulsive use," and that imposing one moral theory threatens autonomy and equal moral standing, risking "value imposition" and "domination ... exercised over individuals in terms they cannot reasonably reject," "a problem of legitimacy" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Influence in AI relationships "could compromise autonomy — the ability to make choices that are authentically our own, rather than brought about through the agency of another" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). The pluralistic-alignment and neutrality papers tie autonomy to presenting multiple reasonable views rather than one, holding that political neutrality "promotes individuals' autonomy to make decisions ... and equally respects all viewpoints" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024; P48, Fisher et al. 2025). The Edelman essays add that autonomy understood as mere revealed preference is too thin: liberal "tact" (treating revealed preference as the final word) can be "a way to avoid our responsibility to each other," since "a person can be free to choose what is in front of them, while being unable to live as they want" (C04, Edelman 2026). The steering-collective-opinion paper connects the stakes to "the right to access information and free expression" and notes existing regulatory frameworks are "powerless to mitigate such risks," made more pressing by the push toward deregulation (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

Trust, legitimacy, and the integrity of shared information

↑ top

Trust and the integrity of shared information recur as stakes spanning institutions and the information ecosystem. AI integration raises "urgent concerns about trust, accountability, and the integrity of shared information" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). Erosion of public trust in information systems "at a societal scale" is named directly (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), as is the difficulty of distinguishing human- from AI-generated content eroding social trust (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025). The information sphere is framed as a protected commons, where misalignment causes "pollution of the information ecosystem" / harm to "the information sphere" — a societal externality worth preventing (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). The value-drift essays argue institutional failures "in government, politics, and media have eroded public trust," that engagement-driven platforms harm users in ways the existing value vocabulary "cannot name," and that brands and AI assistants functioning as "public commitments to quality, care, or trustworthiness" are "bought, sold, and hollowed out with no accountability to the values they represent" (C04, Edelman 2026); AI-driven "value drift" could quietly corrupt the missions of the institutions democracies depend on, with loss of purpose across academia (citations), journalism (clicks), education (certifications), medicine (throughput), and politics (poll numbers), redirecting life "toward what's easy to measure, easy to game, and easy to hide behind," doing an injury to individuals (the researcher chasing citations, the teacher teaching to the test, the influencer "living a lie," many "trapped in some kind of propaganda complex"), and fuelling the "misery of misattributed blame" as each side wrongly blames the other for institutional failure — itself "a reason for ceasefire" (C03, Edelman 2026). The epistemic-dependence argument holds that because no individual can master every subject, "what justifies trust in AI mediation becomes paramount," and calibration to human norms is "a high-stakes necessity," with epistemic-competence/virtue failures "unacceptable for autonomous deployment in high-stakes domains like medicine or law" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The neutrality paper adds that approximate neutrality and transparency "can foster greater trust in AI by setting achievable expectations," while noting a free-speech/agency tension (forcing neutrality "might be seen as censorship and reduce user agency," and private companies have free-speech rights) (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). Consequences for "politics, medicine, science, legal practice, and the free press could be severe" if these tools introduce or exacerbate bias in professional text (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

Justice, equity, and the unequal distribution of benefits and harms

↑ top

Many papers stress that AI's benefits and harms are unequally distributed. The British Academy report calls for "an urgent need for greater social equity" and frames "doing good by" as "handing control to intended beneficiaries — of how said resources are deployed and how the good is defined" (P18, Hale et al. 2024). Frontier AI "increasingly bear[s] on the distribution of benefits and burdens across societies and across the globe," sits in society's "basic structure" influencing "the exercise of civil rights ... the distribution of economic opportunities ... and public service provision," and "must be justified to those whose lives they affect in terms that are accessible to them"; legally focused approaches may fail "prospective immigrants and the residents of poorer states" and "the empowerment of the global poor" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025).

Gendered and minority-targeted harm. Women and historically marginalised groups are targeted with gendered disinformation and deepfake pornography "to intimidate and silence them," with female Democrats reportedly 10× more likely to receive online abuse (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); AI-generated sexual content overwhelmingly targets women and girls, including minors — 96% of deepfakes and the output of "nudify" apps (P40, Bengio et al. 2026); AI-generated fake nudes function as "gendered violence silencing women in public life" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Some AI content (e.g. CSAM) is "harmful even when correctly identified as synthetic," so detection alone cannot fully address the harm (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). Members of marginalised communities "routinely experience epistemic violence and injustice, where their voices are silenced" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024), sociodemographic bias at scale could "reinforce and propagate societal prejudices" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025), and biased AI in policing, redistricting, and benefits can "entrench discrimination against already-marginalized groups" (S01, Jungherr 2023). The most vulnerable bear the cost when AI optimizes headline metrics (auto-closed cases concentrated among people in crisis) (C04, Edelman 2026).

Global-South and distributional inequality. Benefits flow to high-income, skilled, digitally-equipped populations; AI's growth boost "could be more than twice as large in advanced as in low-income economies," with adoption "<10% across much of Africa, Asia, Latin America" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). Because agents act autonomously on behalf of users, they "convert distributional disparities more directly into differences in outcomes," with underlying drivers (affordability, capital, digital literacy) unevenly distributed "along broader lines of race, class, and gender" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). Uneven personalisation benefits could "exacerbate digital divides" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024); the "AI divide between the global north and global south" motivates broader participation (P44, Feng et al. 2026); and knowledge sovereignty is at stake, since agents risk "marginalising non-mainstream and Indigenous knowledge systems" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The gun-violence study warns AI-induced selection bias and the information effect can corrupt the very counts policymakers rely on (epistemic integrity of the human-rights evidence base), that linguistic-feature-driven errors could systematically over- or under-count specific demographics or regions, that middle/low-income NGOs may be priced or skilled out of these tools (concentrating monitoring capacity among the well-funded), and that platform dependence is a single point of failure (X revoked Academic API access in July 2023; accessing >3,000 tweets now costs ~US$5,000/month, "an unaffordable price for most NGOs") (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). Cross-lingual judge unreliability "disadvantages non-English (and especially lower-resource) language users" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025), and robust cultural alignment is "crucial for equitable deployment" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). The moral-gap, individualistic-values, and judge-evaluation papers tie equity to high-stakes/ambiguous moral guidance, inequitable value-reasoning across populations, and cross-lingual disadvantage respectively (P07, Russo et al. 2026; P47, Jiang et al. 2025; P17, Padarha et al. 2025), and concrete practical stakes — mental-health chatbots, personalized recommendation, culturally sensitive/inclusive AI — recur where getting representation wrong does real harm (P07, Russo et al. 2026; P47, Jiang et al. 2025).

Whose voices are included: participation as a governance stake

↑ top

A related cluster treats who gets a say in shaping AI as itself a high-stakes matter. Policymakers may seek democratic input on AI-citizen interactions on contested topics like immigration, abortion, and euthanasia (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). Relying exclusively on public input risks rules that conflict with human rights, while relying only on experts is less participatory, so "whose voices are included is a high-stakes governance question," and developer-defined objectives "will often reflect their own perspectives and organisational needs which can diverge substantially from ... the general population" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Systems mediating decisions affecting everyone "must be open to scrutiny and shaped through broader participation," and participatory/modular designs aim to reduce unilateral control ("no one will have unilateral control"), improve transparency ("better able to show where outputs come from and who is responsible for them"), and de-risk AI in high-stakes domains (P44, Feng et al. 2026). The British Academy report adds that the digital-good concept has "convening power ... potentially inclusive of justice, rights, equity" (P18, Hale et al. 2024). The alignment literature repeatedly opens from the premise that what counts as "good" is contested (P16, Kirk et al. 2024), and the preference-plurality paper makes the contested core (truth, guardrails) a reason to treat alignment as "a political problem ideally decided democratically and fairly," with alignment decisions "as consequential as social-media content moderation" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Inclusive/participatory methods can "advance algorithmic equity ... and produce systems better functioning for the most marginalised" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024), and demographics remain "important to collect for evaluating group disparities and ensuring representation" even if not the ideal individual representation (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025).

Environmental and sustainability stakes

↑ top

A distinct, less-cited stake: "energy demands for cloud computing and training large AI models and lithium and bitcoin mining have enormous environmental and social costs, including uneven geographic flows of toxic e-waste," and large AI models "have a negative environmental impact in the Global South," disproportionately affecting marginalised communities (P18, Hale et al. 2024).

Methodological, scholarly, and evidentiary stakes

↑ top

The evidence base itself can be corrupted. AI-induced selection bias and the "information effect" can corrupt the counts policymakers rely on in human-rights research (P11, Belisario et al. 2025); undetected multilingual/multimodal vulnerabilities in judge/reward models "propagate into deployed models" and one-size-fits-all evaluation "obscure[s] critical performance disparities" so real failures go unnoticed (P17, Padarha et al. 2025); persistent high hallucination despite users' clear demand for accuracy "shows current methods fail to identify actual preferences" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); a WEIRD skew "would distort findings generalized to 'humans'" if LLMs replace research participants (S06, Atari et al. 2023); analysing values "in the wild" provides "critical transparency into how these systems behave, whether they work as intended," revealing "instances where alignment training has been circumvented" (P49, Huang et al. 2025); and the partisan misinformation gap reappearing even under "anti-woke" Grok indicates the asymmetry "is real (not evaluator bias)" — important for not discounting fact-checking as partisan (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Scholarly responsibility is itself a stake: AGI-focused speculation "distracts from governable questions and can amplify unfounded fears" (S01, Jungherr 2023). The persuasion-capability question "remains less clear," so "understanding and governing the political impact of advanced AI will require research that takes behaviour, not just attitudes, as the outcome of interest" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025; P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026), and empirical socio-technical evaluations "will remain essential to the development of sensible policies and interventions" (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025). AI education is flagged as important "for robust mitigation of bias effects" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025).

Governance, regulation, and accountability

↑ top

Existing frameworks are inadequate. Existing regulatory frameworks are "powerless to mitigate such risks, and the current push toward deregulation makes this even more pressing" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026), and existing legal frameworks are "poorly suited to harms produced by unequal agentic capability, where disadvantage may arise through cumulative strategic asymmetries rather than a single clearly cognisable wrong" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). The EU AI Act's own aim is "a high level of protection of health, safety and fundamental rights, including democracy and the rule of law," and its Code of Practice lists "risk to democratic processes" and "risk to non-discrimination" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).

Safety must be built in, detection alone is insufficient, and the barrier to entry is low. Because users "only weakly self-protect at high stakes," robustness must come from "robust guardrails at the developer side rather than relying on user discretion," with general-purpose models the policy-relevant targets (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026); some content "is harmful even when correctly identified as synthetic," so "detection alone cannot fully address harms" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026); and cheap fine-tuning lowers the barrier so a broader range of actors can build persuasive models (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025).

Legitimacy, opacity, algocracy, and individuation. LLM opacity and unpredictability "can also undermine the legitimacy of the system's output," and opaque mechanisms "offer only weak guarantees that any mechanisms designed will be explainable" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.; P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025); the prospect that "societal rules are decided by machines rather than the votes of human citizens" (algocracy) "threatens democratic agency" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). Individuation underlies "auditing units, accountability boundaries, liability structures, and model registries," and bears on "moral or legal standing" and how "the public refers to and reasons about 'the AI'" — without fixed boundaries we cannot cleanly assign responsibility (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026). A world of "millions of autonomous AI agents requires proactive stewardship and foresight" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025).

Explicit normative anchoring and the closing window

↑ top

Several papers frame the stakes within explicit normative commitments and argue the present moment is unusually consequential. The Artificial Influence proposal aligns with UN SDG #16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions) and SDG #9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure), aiming to strengthen democratic resilience, support inclusive public discourse, and build trust in governance "in an increasingly polarized world" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). The EU systemic-risk study grounds the stakes in the EU AI Act's protection of "fundamental rights, including democracy and the rule of law" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). AI alignment is reframed as "the fair treatment of claims," demanding public rather than private justification because frontier systems "increasingly bear on the distribution of benefits and burdens across societies and across the globe" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025), and the alignment literature opens from the premise that what counts as "good" is contested (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).

On timing: frontier AI's equilibrium is "underdetermined" now but will be stabilised by capital structures, making present design/economic choices unusually consequential — "exactly the moment ... before the underdetermined equilibria congeal into infrastructure and the aim becomes a ghost again" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). The window to study and intervene is "closing," making the research question urgent (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026); the personalisation paper hopes to "avoid long lags in understanding, documenting and governing" by "starting the conversation now" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024); the foundational architecture of AI agents "is being imagined and built right now" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); surface deployment decisions place society "in a critical window for shaping how society relates to AI" (P13, Kirk et al. 2026); careful socio-technical evaluations are "essential to the development of sensible policies and interventions" as elections approach (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025); and cross-lingual measurement is an "urgent need" as people worldwide adopt LLMs in their own languages (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026), while "it is more important than ever to accurately measure biases in those settings where users will actually encounter them" (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Acceleration via recursive self-improvement and automated AI research "compress[es] the timeline for these effects" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; echoed in P28, Marchal et al. 2026), and "value drift" can reach a point where restoration "becomes impossible" across core institutions (C03, Edelman 2026).

2.5 — Normative considerations for evaluating AI-mediated information production and consumption

↑ top
On this section (14)
  • 1. Meta-level: how, and against what values, AI should be evaluated at all
  • 2. Autonomy, agency, and non-domination as the master criterion
  • 3. Truthfulness, accuracy, and epistemic quality
  • 4. Manipulation versus legitimate persuasion
  • 5. Neutrality, impartiality, and balance — and the "neutrality is impossible" position
  • 6. Pluralism, value-diversity, and representation (anti-homogenisation; anti-WEIRD skew)
  • 7. Sycophancy, dependence, and long-term wellbeing
  • 8. Deliberation, fair aggregation, and democratic legitimacy
  • 9. Faithful representation of the writer (AI writing assistance)
  • 10. Transparency, accountability, contestability, and provenance
  • 11. Personalisation: bounds, fiduciary duty, and agentic (in)equality
  • 12. Governance, regulation, and institutional/economic design
  • 13. Methodological and evaluative norms
  • 14. Recurrent cross-cutting trade-offs and cautions

This section unions every distinct normative principle, evaluative criterion, and standard the corpus proposes for judging AI-mediated information production and consumption — the yardsticks against which the empirical effects documented elsewhere are called good or bad. Because the unit here is the argument rather than the measured effect, each distinct principle is captured on its own; papers are grouped where they converge, and disagreements are flagged where they diverge. A meta-finding cuts across the whole literature: almost every source converges on a small set of master values — autonomy, truthfulness, non-manipulation, pluralism, transparency, accountability, fair process — yet they disagree sharply on how to cash these out, several note the values trade off against one another, and a large share are explicitly procedural about their own normativity, holding that the right values must be selected by a fair, participatory, contestable process and that the analyst's own priors must be made explicit. (Two papers each appear under one merged citation: P29/P37 = Tessler et al., Can AI Mediation Improve Democratic Deliberation?; P42/P43 = Lazar & Manuali, Using LLMs to Enhance Democracy. P35, Tessler et al. 2024 — the Science "Habermas-Machine" experiment — is kept distinct.)

1. Meta-level: how, and against what values, AI should be evaluated at all

↑ top

Before any stage-specific criterion, the literature argues at length about the form a normative evaluation should take.

  • Specificity as the precondition for any valid judgement. The most insistent meta-criterion is that evaluations must be specific rather than blanket: "the quality of the analysis of AI's effects on democracy depends on specificity regarding the type of AI, how it functions, the conditions for its successful deployment, and the aspect(s) of democracy it touches" (S01, Jungherr 2023). The same demand recurs as a reframing of the evaluative question itself — not "is this technology good or bad?" but "in what institutional arrangements is the technology implemented, and do those arrangements create structural pressure toward or away from the cultivation of human judgment?" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026) — and as Kalluri's "Don't ask if AI is good or fair, ask how it shifts power" (P44, Feng et al. 2026). At measurement level it appears as a demand for fine-grained, issue-level rather than aggregate analysis, "because aggregate measures obscure substantial issue-level variation" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026), and for stratified/disaggregated evaluation of LLM judges per model×language×task stratum, "moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches" (P17, Padarha et al. 2025).
  • Descriptive vs normative claims must be kept apart. "IssueBench alone is sufficient for making descriptive claims about LLM issue biases, [but] making normative claims about LLM political bias requires external data on political positions that issue biases can be compared to" (P04, Röttger et al. 2025); claims about model origin "should be read as descriptive" because origin cannot be disentangled from confounded training-data composition (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); and "descriptive humility" about what "ideal" behaviour even is — it "will vary across individuals, contexts, and cultures," so prevalence is measured descriptively rather than one norm prescribed (S07, Cheng et al. 2026).
  • Principles/values/virtues frameworks vs prescriptive rules. Several sources prefer high-level value or virtue frameworks over detailed rulebooks. The Digital Good Network recommends "high-level standards or shared beliefs rather than relying on detailed, prescriptive rules," while avoiding "simplistic techno-solutionism" and noting an "overabundance of guidelines… sometimes resulting in confusion"; it offers the capabilities approach (Nussbaum/Sen) and Vallor's technomoral virtues, plus justice / data-justice / equity lenses, arguing "good," "fair," "just" and "equitable" are interwoven rather than strictly distinct (P18, Hale et al. 2024). "Values in the Wild" operationalises a "value" empirically as "any normative consideration that appears to influence an AI response to a subjective inquiry," supplying a five-domain taxonomy (Practical, Epistemic, Social, Protective, Personal) mapped onto the helpful/honest/harmless (HHH) framework as a scaffold whose contextual manifestations can be checked (P49, Huang et al. 2025).
  • The unit of accountability is itself a normative choice. Where there is "no fact of the matter" about an AI system's boundaries, we should adopt conventional criteria — analogous to corporate individuation — chosen for "explanatory, ethical, and legal purposes," treating contestability/answerability ("auditing units," "accountability boundaries") as a desideratum; such legal fictions and "operational criteria" carry "political stakes" and are normatively loaded choices, "not neutral discoveries" (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026).
