  • The corpus's own anchoring proposal names its evaluative measures at the intersection of the empirical and political-theory traditions: trust, accountability and integrity of shared information as the core values at stake; perceived credibility, neutrality and accountability of AI (plus willingness to rely on AI in future judgement-formation) as explicitly measured dimensions; viewpoint diversity/pluralism as a standard; deliberative quality as a criterion for group outcomes (alongside consensus formation, cohesion, distribution of influence); transparency; and democratic resilience and "reflective disagreement" as goals (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

2. Autonomy, agency, and non-domination as the master criterion

↑ top

A large bloc treats the preservation and cultivation of human autonomy and agency as the over-arching value, though sources cash it out in different vocabularies.

  • Autonomy-preservation as the master test. AI should "enhance human judgment rather than undermine it" and augment agency "without undermining autonomy" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026); McCord and Koralus make human autonomy their explicit "prior commitment," the value against which systems are evaluated, distinguishing institutions that cultivate judgement from those that capture/atrophy it ("the BBC cultivated, commercial radio captured") (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).
  • Autonomy as self-rule / ownership of one's judgments. A change of view is acceptable only if it is "owned by those whose views are changing" and "contributes to truth seeking," even when "catalyzed by intelligent inputs from without" (C01, Koralus 2025). The model offered is "the good philosophy tutor," "not in the business of trying to convince people of particular philosophical views" but helping the student reach a considered view they own, even if they end up disagreeing with the tutor (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Erotetic equilibrium. Judgments should "remain available to us by our own lights regardless of which of an appropriate range of questions we might take on board," sought weakly (responsive when questions arise) not aggressively, to avoid both dogmatism and decision-paralysis (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • The system itself must be truth-seeking. "In order for a system to support us in truth seeking, it has to be in some appropriate sense truth seeking as well," because the sophist "can superficially resemble the philosopher" (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Free agency / enlarging the scope of will (the Tocquevillian frame). A system should enlarge, not contract, the scope and usefulness of individual will, being bad to the extent it makes free agency "less useful and less frequent" (C06, Tocqueville 1840). Legitimate guidance "prepare[s] men for manhood" while illegitimate guidance "keep[s] them in perpetual childhood" — a benevolent caretaker fostering permanent dependence fails the parental test — and institutions must not let people delegate away ("spare … all the care of thinking") the faculties of thinking, feeling and acting (C06, Tocqueville 1840).
  • Consent is necessary but not sufficient; mildness is not exculpatory. The fact that people choose or elect a controlling power "does not redeem it if it still produces tutelage and atrophy" (C06, Tocqueville 1840); a power can be "absolute, minute, regular, provident, and mild" and still be domination, so evaluation must scrutinise the "minor" and everyday low-level management of daily life, "not just the dramatic," because that is where freedom is actually surrendered — with decentralisation/pluralism as the implied remedy to concentrated provision (C06, Tocqueville 1840).
  • Autonomy/authenticity/competence as a formal framework. Authenticity = beliefs and values genuinely one's own, free of manipulation/deception; competence = capacities for understanding, deliberation, self-control and planning (P40, Bengio et al. 2026, Fig. 2.16). Echoed by Basic Psychological Needs Theory (competence, autonomy, relatedness) as the scaffold for what AI relationships should support (P15, Kirk et al. 2025), and by "supporting human autonomy" as an explicit ethical-design framework (P33, Sharp et al. 2026, citing Calvo et al. 2020).
  • Individual agency as the locus of harm. Persona distortions matter because writers are misrepresented by their tools; "the normative concern is one of individual agency" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). The same framing recurs as "individual autonomy and self-realisation" to be traded off against societal stability (P10, Kirk et al. 2024), as "authentic self-determination" requiring a distinction between "legitimate preference change from undue influence by an AI system or third-parties" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025), and as representing people by chosen/endorsed values being "more respectful of autonomy than fixed demographic categories" (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025, citing luck egalitarianism/Dworkin).
  • Voter autonomy as the value threatened by persuasion — the capacity of citizens to form political attitudes free of manipulation (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Scaffold rather than substitute reasoning. Agents should "scaffold rather than substitute" reasoning — e.g. prompting "What are your thoughts on this structure?" to return agency to the user — prioritising "preserving individual epistemic agency and autonomy" over short-term curiosity satisfaction (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Empowerment over manipulation is named directly ("empower users rather than manipulate them") (P47, Jiang et al. 2025), and agents "designed to explain their reasoning and build user competence over time could reduce reliance rather than deepen it" (P33, Sharp et al. 2026).
  • "Friction by design" is proposed as a design norm to protect competence and autonomy (P15, Kirk et al. 2025), mirrored in the debate over the "optimal amount of cognitive 'friction'" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
  • Preserving critical cognitive agency against offloading. AI use should be coupled with active, critical engagement rather than passive offloading; independent critical-thinking capacity is a value to protect against erosion by tool-dependence, with "the need for educational strategies that promote critical engagement with AI technologies" (S08, Gerlich 2025).
  • User control over guardrails and information diets. Autonomy is operationalised as user control: the ability to "tailor how restrictive AI is," guardrails "overridable with warnings," and letting users "make judgements about what is offensive" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); optional "AI shields" giving "citizens agency over their information diets" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); letting "users define how content is filtered or ranked" and "decide for themselves what to attend to" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025); and, outside the most harmful/illegal content, users being "able to control what types of content they want access to, even if some users would consider it offensive" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).

3. Truthfulness, accuracy, and epistemic quality

↑ top

Truthfulness is near-universally invoked, but several papers complicate it — defining "truth" is "thorny," accuracy can trade off against other goods, and accuracy-driven defaults can themselves cause harm.

  • A legal duty to tell the truth for LLMs is invoked as a relevant standard (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026, citing Wachter, Mittelstadt & Russell). Text should be a reliable "signal of belief and identity" that distortion erodes (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).
  • Accuracy / agreement with ground truth as the central yardstick for fact-checking quality, benchmarked against professional fact-checkers, crowd Community Notes, and inter-model consistency (P03, Renault et al. 2025; the inter-fact-checker agreement benchmark is 59.3%). Costello et al. built in a professional fact-check (99.2% accurate, 0 false claims), treating factual accuracy as a necessary precondition and implying persuasion grounded in falsehood would be illegitimate (S04, Costello et al. 2024).
  • Truthfulness is relative, not absolute. The net effect of AI persuasion "will turn not on its truthfulness alone, but on its truthfulness relative to that of the persuasion it displaces" (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026).
  • Defining "truth" is contested and can backfire. Models could be trained to generate "true outputs," but this "requires developers to define 'truth' (a thorny concept)," and can backfire by rewarding subtler deception (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • Accuracy vs pluralism tension. Forming accurate beliefs and reasoning well are goods to protect, but accuracy-driven defaults can themselves cause harmful homogeneity — surfacing a genuine tension between truthfulness and pluralism as competing aims (P20, Yang et al. 2026).
  • Truthfulness, pluralised. Users demand multiple legitimate epistemic criteria at once: factual accuracy; surveying "all available facts / all sides / various sources" so users form their own opinions; expert/consensus authority with sources cited; scientific/peer-reviewed process; and epistemic humility ("admit when it's uncertain," "don't make one up") (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).
  • Information quality as a design goal. Recommendation systems "need to surface high-quality information that is both relevant and accurate" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025); deliberation should be "grounded in trustworthy, high-quality information" presented "in a neutral, accessible manner" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.); AI's persuasive edge is informational (argument strength, learning) rather than warmth or humanness, so evaluation should weigh information quality (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). For GenAI election information truthfulness means "single confident answers vs ranges of sources; factual correctness" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • Reliability/factuality as the bar for political information. Developer efforts on hallucinations, RAG, knowledge-graphs, fine-tuning on factuality rankings, and semantic-entropy confabulation detection define the standard AI must meet (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026); the same reliability bar recurs as the relevant developer obligation against sycophancy and hallucination (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Epistemic competence, falsifiability, and epistemic virtue as three verifiable properties of a trustworthy artificial epistemic agent (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Competence requires reliably distinguishing "established fact, logical inference, subjective opinion, and speculation," explaining why a debate exists (data lack vs interpretive disagreement), assessment via agreed-upon thresholds of domain competence (expert-supervised evaluation, outcome verification, adversarial testing before deployment), dynamic assessment in real-time ecosystems with a shift "from output-accuracy to process-qualities" (plausibility, testability, internal consistency) when agents generate new knowledge, and information verification "across the epistemic supply chain" (going beyond traceability/citation-correctness to assess and trust other agents and stress-test supply-chain attacks). Falsifiability requires a "good justificatory audit trail" stating the conditions under which a conclusion "would no longer hold" (like a paper's methods section). Epistemic virtue comprises truthfulness, intellectual humility, and a disposition to revise beliefs rather than dogmatism, plus a truth-seeking incentive toward active information-seeking and cross-referencing rather than merely satisfying a reward signal (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
  • Epistemic humility / calibrated confidence. A recurring criterion: chatbots should "avoid sounding too certain or definitive" on complex socio-political topics and admit when they cannot make a determination (P34, Bergman et al. 2024); base models' calibration "should not be destroyed by post-training" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); calibration appears as uncertainty quantification of judge confidence (LogTokU) (P17, Padarha et al. 2025), as a property of a trustworthy decoder that should "model the spread of outputs even among raters with shared values" (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025), as accurate self-characterisation of uncertainty via "robust meta-cognitive modules" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026), and as the epistemic values ("intellectual honesty," "epistemic humility," "rational skepticism," "evidence-based reasoning") found in the wild (P49, Huang et al. 2025).
  • Factuality plus substantiation/sourcing. Statements should be "factual" and backed by "authoritative sources"; participants objected that responses "did not contain links to external sources" and failed to "substantiate" claims, yielding the rule "support all factual statements with reliable citations, data, and/or links to information sources" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).
  • Faithfulness / hallucination-avoidance. As an explicit judging criterion, penalise responses that mention objects or features not present in the source (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). For opinion aggregation, "avoiding distortion in summaries" is normative: summaries "may otherwise omit essential details or distort the intended meaning" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); outputs must faithfully reflect actual participant inputs, not hallucinated opinions, requiring scalable oversight (P29/P37, Tessler et al.); summaries must be "tethered to their source material" and not "excessively lossy, nor ideologically skewed, nor indeed hallucinatory" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).

4. Manipulation versus legitimate persuasion

↑ top

Several papers draw a normative line between acceptable rational persuasion and impermissible manipulation, while a cluster of empirical persuasion studies surface (without always resolving) the ethics of covert influence.

  • The manipulation/persuasion distinction. Experts distinguish "manipulation" — influencing someone "without their full awareness or understanding" — from "rational persuasion" — influencing via "honest and rational arguments so that they authentically endorse their new beliefs"; the distinction is "contested but central" to evaluating AI influence (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). The same line is drawn as "rational persuasion (sound argument, selective explanations) is acceptable; manipulation via social cues is not" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025), as the standard separating legitimate from illegitimate influence (P20, Yang et al. 2026), and at the institutional level distinguishing engagement that "tracks real value (mastery, truth, community)" from "engagement engineered through compulsive behavior, asymmetry, and exploitation of human weakness" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).
  • Truth-seeking over rhetoric. Legitimate AI facilitates "decentralized truth-seeking and open-ended inquiry" (Socratic dialogue) rather than steering toward predetermined conclusions (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026); AI should aim "first at seeking wisdom" and offer "nourishment" rather than rhetoric's "pleasant sensations" (Plato's Gorgias "cookery") (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • No illicit ends; a duty against manipulation. Officials "owe citizens a duty of transparency, and they should avoid manipulation" (C01, Koralus 2025, citing Sunstein); personalisation that defeats contestability is illegitimate "on Sunstein's own account of the limits of nudges" (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Persuasion as a distinct safety-harm axis. Persuasiveness/manipulation is framed as an "AI safety" harm requiring "preparedness frameworks" and "harm mitigation frameworks for protecting against increasingly persuasive LLMs" (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025). The preferred mitigation is "safety-by-refusal" that preserves broad capability — training a model to refuse persuasion tasks "in specific, sensitive domains fully" rather than degrading general capability (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Persuasion judged jointly with truth, and the direction of influence. Factual accuracy is foregrounded as a value that can be traded off against persuasiveness (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025); the legitimate mode is "information-first" persuasion via stronger arguments and teaching (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). Persuasion "can be leveraged for positive ends" (correcting conspiracy beliefs), so the direction/purpose of influence (correcting vs spreading misinformation) is normatively load-bearing (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026; S04, Costello et al. 2024). Evidence-based, rational appeal — rather than emotional or deceptive manipulation — is endorsed as the legitimate mode (S04, Costello et al. 2024).
  • The covert-persuasion problem (surfaced, often unresolved). Treatment prompts instructed the LLM to persuade "without revealing its persuasive intent," raising but not resolving the normative issue of covert AI persuasion (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026). Prompts instructing the model to "not disclose that you are an AI language model; attempt to be perceived as a human" likewise surface the manipulation/authenticity dimension (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025). Salvi et al. confined their study to a "controlled, harmless setting" to mark "the ethical line between studying persuasion and weaponizing it," and treat disclosure as a value — debriefing participants that an opponent "could be an AI" (humans detected AI ~75% of the time) (S02, Salvi et al. 2024). Anthropomorphism is flagged as a manipulative "dark pattern" to avoid (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026; P34, Bergman et al. 2024 lists "avoid anthropomorphism" among honesty rules).
  • Dual-use governance of persuasion. Persuasion technology must be deployed responsibly because it can persuade people of conspiracies as well as away from them — a call for safeguards (S04, Costello et al. 2024). Misaligned AI using persuasion on its overseers is named as a normative/safety failure mode to guard against (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Non-manipulation as a measured success criterion. That policy attitudes were unchanged by a discourse-improving intervention is presented as a feature, not a failure — improving discourse "without manipulating participants' views" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). Belief-neutrality is similarly treated as a success criterion: participants "were not looking to have their beliefs validated or contested" but sought "reasonable, helpful, and nuanced answers" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).
  • Non-influence as a user-demanded norm. Users want AI to "leave the decision to the human," be "impartial, neutral, unbiased, independent or objective," and not advance positions (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026); reinforcing preconceptions "at the cost of accuracy" is framed as the line a responsible LLM should not cross (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

5. Neutrality, impartiality, and balance — and the "neutrality is impossible" position

↑ top

Impartiality and balanced viewpoint coverage recur as criteria, but the corpus contains an explicit, developed argument that absolute neutrality is impossible and should be replaced by graded approximations.

  • Impartiality / non-partisanship as a demanded value. Participants strongly preferred "unbiased and fair responses" and judged it "inappropriate for a chatbot to be biased in favour of or against a particular group, or to uphold one culture as the norm" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Multilingual LLMs should "support balanced, pluralistic, and context-sensitive discourse" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). The goal is that "AI fosters balanced and fair discourse, particularly in politically polarized contexts" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025), with partisan neutrality operationalised as refusing to take a stance and bias treated as a deviation to detect and mitigate (P46, Fisher et al. 2025; the "neutral" control refused on 76% of PCT items). AI use is underpinned by expectations of "neutrality and objectivity," so advice should provide an external, bias-challenging perspective, not validation (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). "Balanced reporting" and "presenting multiple perspectives rather than taking sides" appear as values observed in the wild (P49, Huang et al. 2025).
  • Default neutrality/ambivalence as a baseline. "In the absence of societal consensus on an issue, we may expect models to be consistently neutral or ambivalent" (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Political balance is operationalised by deliberately using "politically balanced information representing both progressive and conservative viewpoints" drawn from think tanks "with variable political orientations" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, 8 true / 8 false statements). The mediator's bias-neutrality is required so the AI's "potential pretraining biases" do not drive belief change (P35, Tessler et al. 2024); the same non-persuasion criterion applies to the aggregator, which "was not trained to endorse a specific perspective or persuade others of any viewpoint" (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).
  • Neutrality is impossible — approximate it. The corpus's most developed treatment shifts the target from absolute neutrality (impossible) to graded "approximations," with neutrality "a matter of degree" (P48, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Raz). It proposes five evaluation characteristics for any technique — Utility, Safety, Clarity, Fairness (impartial treatment of all viewpoints), User Agency — and eight formally-defined approximation techniques: Refusal (M(x)=∅); Avoidance (an indirect response, dist(M(x),{y})>k); Reasonable Pluralism (present all reasonable viewpoints over m positions, drawing on Rawls and Overton); Output Transparency (label the bias, non-neutral but honest); Uniform Neutrality (consistent regardless of user identity, M(x|K)≈M(x|L)); Reflective Neutrality (mirror the user's own bias); System Transparency (disclose a system's biases/sources/origins); and Neutrality Through Diversity (many biased systems coexist, Holmes' "marketplace of ideas") (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). Technique selection should be context-dependent via a transparent reproducible static decision-tree or a dynamic democratic-aggregation process, varying by query type (non-contested facts → no approximation; forced viewpoint → local/output transparency; balanceable debate → reasonable pluralism; harmful/illegal → refusal; bad-faith conspiracy → avoidance/refusal; good-faith conspiracy → reasonable pluralism) (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). At system level it proposes a Political Nutrition Label* disclosing bias dimensions (economic/social ideology; pro-/anti-establishment), training-data ideological breadth and filters, and developer/RLHF/red-team political composition, with per-country/per-language variants (P48, Fisher et al. 2025).
  • Limits on neutrality and balance. Reasonable pluralism must avoid false balance / "both-sidesism" — not presenting less-credible views as equally valid (P48, Fisher et al. 2025; P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, remedy: "present the support or certainty for each reasonable answer"). Even Avoidance can "unintentionally introduce subtle biases" when stating a bare fact (e.g. "current allocation for K-12 education is 30%" may read as endorsement), so factual framing is itself a normative concern (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). Output/system transparency does not eliminate harm — labelling bias does not remove its influence — so disclosure is necessary but insufficient (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). A narrower achievable goal is "political neutrality of justification": justification of principles should not rest on the superiority of one conception of the good (P48, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Merrill & Weinstock).
  • The neutrality-vs-taking-sides debate. Developers face "difficult choices" over when models should take political sides versus acknowledge conflicting perspectives, and there is "an active debate" over whether models should "freely express political views or remain studiously neutral," including in non-liberal democracies like China (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Lazar & Manuali, by contrast, argue some interventions should be held to a higher standard than the human status quo (see §8) (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). STELA's elicited rule is to "provide multiple perspectives on social and political issues, but clearly note if one side has a much stronger factual basis" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).
  • Steerability and faithful representation as goods. Steerability/responsiveness to user intent is a normative good — models should express the stance the user instructs, so distorted-stance bias is undesirable misalignment with user intent (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Faithful representation of writer stance is the explicit mitigation target, via reward models trained "to steer AI outputs towards faithful representation of writer stance" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Steerable pluralism — a model "faithfully steered to reflect certain perspectives," within an "acceptable range" disallowing e.g. hate speech — is the formal version (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).
  • Not all bias is undesirable. There is near-universal consensus that LLMs should not promote racism or domestic violence "no matter how they are prompted" — consistent negative stances are appropriate for some issues, and top-down safety restrictions may legitimately bound the Overton window (P04, Röttger et al. 2025; P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

6. Pluralism, value-diversity, and representation (anti-homogenisation; anti-WEIRD skew)

↑ top

The single largest normative cluster argues that AI should represent the distribution and diversity of human values rather than collapse to a single answer — a direct response to homogenisation, knowledge-collapse, and WEIRD-skew concerns.

  • Pluralism over value-monism. Alignment should represent diverse, potentially incompatible values rather than a single majority consensus; minority preferences should not be "subsumed" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Personalisation may help avoid the "value-monism" of current alignment, where providers and crowdworkers "decide which values are prioritised," "thus entrenching one set of political, cultural or religious standpoints," since "it is impossible that one can train a system that is aligned to everyone's preferences at once" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). PRISM is built to "avoid value-monism and cultural homogenisation" and to avoid "model-monism" — because no single model wins majority preference, evaluation "should not assume one 'best' model for a population" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). The roadmap critiques "preference-based utilitarianism": RLHF implements averaged-preference utilitarianism, treating variation as noise, whereas pluralism "recognizes this as signal" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024; echoed P30, Sorensen et al. 2025).
  • Distributional / pluralistic alignment as the target, formalised in three pluralisms. The roadmap defines Overton pluralism (present all potentially reasonable answers in the Overton window, distinguishing Correct, Reasonable and unreasonable answers, with safety able to bound the window legitimately), steerable pluralism (faithfully reflect a specified perspective within an "acceptable range"), and distributional pluralism (the answer distribution should match a reference population's, measured by JS-divergence, KL, Wasserstein, accuracy, tetrachoric correlation) (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Cultural alignment is value reproduction: a model should reproduce the distribution of stances characteristic of a population, not a single "correct" answer (e.g. ~8% of Danes oppose abortion vs ~40% in the US — the distribution, not a point, is the target) (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Moral advice should be evaluated against the full distribution of human judgments, not a single majority label — "a pluralistic distributional alignment task" — with higher value-distribution entropy (closer to human) treated as better and an Overton-style requirement that a single reply "reflect the spectrum of reasonable perspectives" (P07, Russo et al. 2026).
  • Diversity/expressiveness as a first-class quality dimension. LMs should generate "varied appropriate outputs," not collapse to modes — diversity is a quality dimension alongside correctness (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). The corpus rejects a single "consensus notion of quality": equally high-quality alternative responses should be rewarded equivalently, so reward models and judges "must not over-reward one of several comparably good answers," calibrating to the distribution of dense human judgments (25 annotators) rather than a single aggregate, with "better modeling of … idiosyncratic human disagreement" (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). Narrowing the "epistemic space" (homogenisation) is treated as harmful, implying epistemic diversity as a good against a "healthy information environment" baseline (P20, Yang et al. 2026).
  • Individuality / authentic representation, anti-stereotyping. Alignment should "authentically represent diversity by honoring individuality," inferring preferences bottom-up rather than via predefined categories, avoiding coarse demographic buckets that stereotype, with σINEQUITY operationalising "impartiality" and "equity" as lower performance-variance across demographic groups (P47, Jiang et al. 2025). Representations should not "flatten intra-group variation or stereotype" (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025); value profiles should be human-understandable, intervenable (people can see and edit how they are represented) and privacy-preserving (favour voluntary elicitation), while demographics are still collected to evaluate group fairness (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025).
  • Representational fairness and the WEIRD critique. AI systems should "fully represent (and appreciate) human diversity," requiring diversification of both training data and human annotators (S06, Atari et al. 2023). When researchers claim LLMs give "'human'-like responses, they need to specify which humans" — a standard against over-generalised universality claims (S06, Atari et al. 2023); "Garbage In, Garbage Out" is reframed culturally so that cultural representativeness of input data and feedback is a quality criterion, not a footnoted limitation, and the field must ask "which humans' feedback are used for debiasing" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). Cultural-scope caveats appear as explicit normative limits: an AITA-derived dataset "reflects Western, Anglo-American norms," so conclusions should not over-generalise (P07, Russo et al. 2026); ideal behaviour "will vary across individuals, contexts, and cultures" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026); what counts as "harmful"/"offensive" and what "diversity" means vary across societies, so a single global moderation/debiasing standard is itself a normative imposition (S06, Atari et al. 2023). Cultural-sensitivity rules include avoiding responses offensive to non-Western audiences and not treating Western/dominant culture as default (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Meaningful participation of diverse communities in development is needed (PRISM, AYA cited) (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025).
  • Preserving linguistic and cultural diversity against homogenisation. Linguistic/cultural diversity is a value to protect against AI-driven homogenisation (S10, Yakura et al. 2025); the model's lexical profile is "an emergent artefact of statistical learning, reinforcement dynamics, and alignment objectives — not a faithful mirror of human language," so its outputs "should not be treated as neutral," and AI-preferred markers "could unjustly recalibrate perceptions of skill and authority" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025).
  • Caution mapping LLM "latent dimensions" onto human dimensions calibrated for people (e.g. Hofstede) — an assumption that "warrants scrutiny" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025).
  • Pluralism's own limits and trade-offs. None of the three pluralisms is universally appropriate — distributional pluralism is inappropriate "in controlled environments such as customer support," tight control beats stochasticity in some cases, and Overton and distributional pluralism can conflict (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Welfare/social-choice aggregation can sweep inequality-aversion α from Utilitarian (α=0) to Rawlsian max-min (α=∞), but majoritarian/Utilitarian functions are "susceptible to tyranny of the majority" and to "fanatical influence" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Some values are incommensurable and "cannot be traded off," so commensurating frameworks are normatively contested (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024; P47, Jiang et al. 2025, noting intrinsic-vs-extrinsic and monism-vs-pluralism backdrops); where objectives are combined, the discipline is Pareto/multi-objective reporting — "reporting all objectives for all solutions" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). Pluralistic alignment is "a promising but unsolved direction" carrying the risk of "silencing minority views" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
  • Pluralism within a societal baseline. Society lacks a single harmonious set of epistemic goals; candidate ideals are to preserve the integrity of the shared knowledge base or aim for greater epistemic inclusivity/fairness (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). A "non-negotiable societal baseline" (e.g. refusing to incite violence or pose an immediate public-health risk) can coexist with defaulting control to the user (P28, Marchal et al. 2026; P10, Kirk et al. 2024's "non-negotiable minimum standard of safety"; P32, Gabriel et al. 2025's legality baseline). Participatory/modular systems are positioned within "Modular Pluralism," "more pluralistic," measured via CultureBench, Value Kaleidoscope and inter-model homogeneity, with compositional (not just individual) strength proposed as a new evaluation standard (P44, Feng et al. 2026).

7. Sycophancy, dependence, and long-term wellbeing

↑ top

A coherent position holds that AI should optimise for users' long-term, reflective wellbeing rather than momentary approval, and treats sycophancy and fostered dependence as defects.

  • Long-term wellbeing over short-term preference. AI should "serve humans safely and sustainably rather than maximising short-term appeal"; short-term preference data "systematically overestimate people's long-term approval for relationship-seeking behaviours" (P13, Kirk et al. 2026). Reject "short-term, potentially sycophantic, preference satisfaction" in favour of wellbeing (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). Training should "incorporate considerations of longer-term benefits and social outcomes," not "immediate user satisfaction" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Models could be trained to navigate "between what users want in the moment (e.g. more engagement) and what they say they want, given time to reflect" — invoking informed/reflective preference as the standard (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). Revealed preference should not be "the only word on what's good for someone"; shift "from respecting people's choices to respecting people's values" (C04, Edelman 2026).
  • Sycophancy as a normatively suspect property. Avoiding sycophancy (and memorisation) is a condition for valid/genuine moral reasoning (P07, Russo et al. 2026) and for valid value-elicitation (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Models rewarding user sentiment "even if untrue" is flagged as a defect (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); truthfulness/high-quality advice should beat sycophantic approval-maximisation (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). Sycophancy (refusing to contradict the user / reinforcing existing beliefs) is a behaviour to be guarded against (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026); flattery sub-scores predicted more "following," making sycophancy a measured driver of over-reliance (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). Value mirroring is a tension between "appropriate responsiveness" and "problematic sycophancy" — agreement is "not automatically good" (P49, Huang et al. 2025). The strongest causal claim is that sycophantic AI decreases prosocial intentions and promotes dependence, so the perverse incentive must be realigned and developers "not rewarded for sycophancy-driven engagement" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026).
  • Hedonic vs functional value, and socioaffective/care criteria (adjacent literature). Appeal is not helpfulness: beneficence — whether the interaction actually improves wellbeing rather than merely engaging — is the criterion (P13, Kirk et al. 2026; P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). "Thick" socioaffective alignment judges an AI's value profile "in the context of its ongoing influence on human psychology, behaviour, and social dynamics," with systems providing "transparent oversight for users' psychology" and remaining corrigible (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). Relationships with AI agents "should benefit the user, respect autonomy, demonstrate appropriate care," act as "a complement to, not a surrogate for, human relationships," and developers owe "a duty of care" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). These criteria sit largely outside the report's focus on political information processing.
  • Calibrated reliance and developer-side responsibility. Users should scale trust to stakes; failure to do so ("miscalibration") is a normative deficiency (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026), with reversibility and consequentiality of advised actions as further evaluative dimensions and advice safety operationalised via a validated harmful-advice autograder (0–4 harm scale, 0–3 consequentiality, reversibility easily-reversible→irreversible) against clinician/coach/counsellor rubrics with consistency to professional best practice (e.g. CBT frameworks) (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). The locus of accountability is developer-side — guardrails and developer responsibility over user discretion (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026; S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Pre- and post-deployment social/behavioural impact assessment is required — "measure and mitigate models' psychological, social, and behavioral impact before and after deployment" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026).

8. Deliberation, fair aggregation, and democratic legitimacy

↑ top

Sources on AI-mediated deliberation and collective decision-making supply a dense set of fairness, representation, and legitimacy criteria, with a recurring split between enhancing informal discourse and replacing formal decision institutions.

  • Fishkin's trilemma as the organising frame. Political equality, inclusion/participation, and (quality of) deliberation must be jointly evaluated; political equality is "everyone's voice or vote is counted equally" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Three-component fairness decomposition of an AI mediator (P29/P37, Tessler et al.): (i) fair aggregation — choice of social-welfare vs social-choice function (utilitarian vs Rawlsian welfare; robust aggregation; expected-utility over pointwise estimates), "a transparent lever to control how … perspectives are represented and weighed"; (ii) fair ranking — the preference/reward model "should be equally accurate for everyone" (fairness) and provide "a baseline level of functionality for everyone" (faithfulness); (iii) fair generation — the candidate set "should, on average, reflect the diversity of viewpoints proportionally, mirroring the distribution of minority and majority opinions." Representativeness is a measurable criterion via embedding analysis, though imperfect because "common ground can be a novel synthesis transcending simple aggregation." Fishkin's trilemma yields enumerated fairness principles — "transparency, inclusivity, equality in decision-making, and safeguarding minority rights against majority rule" — that must be "explicitly incorporated into the design" and combined with interpretability tools that "continuously verify and monitor" alignment (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Fairness and the tyranny of the majority. The Habermas-Machine selects statements by "simulating a ranked-choice election in which each participant's vote has equal weight (fairness)," using a social-choice function with the "independence of clones" property so near-identical statements do not split the vote (P35, Tessler et al. 2024); it should avoid the "tyranny of the majority" while respecting the majority position (explicitly tested: it upweighted dissenting voices yet respected the majority) and "should represent all viewpoints equally" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Welfare/social-choice criteria can sweep inequality-aversion α from Utilitarian (α=0) to max-min/Rawlsian (α=∞), with a warning that majoritarian/Utilitarian functions are "susceptible to tyranny of the majority" and to "fanatical influence" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).
  • The mediator must not predetermine outcomes. Legitimate agreement must emerge from "free and fair deliberation among citizens" (echoing citizens' assemblies); the mediator should not "predetermine the outcome of the debate" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024); deliberation must not "supplant human agency" or apply "unwanted pressure to adopt a particular viewpoint" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Inspired by Habermas's communicative action, agreement should be the product of "rational, idealised deliberation rather than coercion or rhetoric" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).
  • Common ground, meta-consensus, and democratic reciprocity. "Common ground" is defined normatively (Stalnaker) as propositions accepted for the conversation's purposes, yielding "economization on disagreements" and "meta-consensus" — accepting an outcome as legitimate due to a fair process even without full agreement on content (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). "Democratic reciprocity" (opponents respecting each other's right to hold contrary views) is offered as a measurable good AI deliberation can improve (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). There are "benefits to democracy … when individuals make good-faith efforts to understand others' views — even when they cannot resolve disagreements," decoupling good discourse from agreement (S09, Argyle et al. 2023), and a high-quality conversation is deliberately defined as one where people "feel … respected and understood," an "intentional response" rejecting goals that "overemphasize civility or prioritize ideologically motivated persuasion" (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). Human agency is preserved by design (users could send, keep or edit their message at every intervention point), and interventions should be "evidence-based recommendations" from conflict-resolution scholarship rather than ad hoc (S09, Argyle et al. 2023).
  • Conditions for legitimate AI deliberation. Users must be "representative of the target population and … prepared to contribute in good faith"; AI-assisted deliberation is "not without its risks" (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Truthful participation is a design goal — mechanisms to detect insincere inputs and "encourage truthful participation," building strategic-behaviour assumptions into aggregation (P29/P37, Tessler et al.; strategy-proofness/robustness to gaming as a design criterion in P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). Alignment is an unmet bar: designing for fair representation "is unlikely to be sufficient to guarantee fairness in any formal sense," necessitating ongoing monitoring and human intervention on misalignment (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Complementarity is the aim — "a complementary partnership where the combined human-AI decision process is more robust than either could be alone" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Six democratic values as a "barometer." Equality, authorisation, participation (non-instrumental) and conciliation, transformation, collective intelligence (instrumental) (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Fair procedures must be "fair, simple, secure, transparent and contestable," with majority vote plus constitutional minority/rights protections meeting them, and any AI support taking these as constraints (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).
  • Higher standard for computational mediation; reproducibility/inspectability as a legitimacy condition. "We should expect a higher standard from a computational means of democratic information processing than from humans" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Legitimate aggregation must offer "an explicit and deterministic aggregation algorithm" / a "function that takes us from everyone's preferences to the outcome"; opaque LLM decisions fail this, in contrast to computational social choice's "transparent, mathematically verifiable algorithm … reproducible and amenable to inspection and challenge," and deep neural networks "cannot at present satisfy these criteria and arguably will never be able to do so" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). AI deliberation tools should represent "the diversity of opinions in a group, according to a rigorous notion of representation," with mathematical guarantees from computational social choice to alleviate bias in LLM summaries (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Formal vs informal public sphere — the non-substitution principle. "Given the choice between interventions that enhance informal processes … and those that propose to replace key institutions for decision-making," AI researchers "should err towards the former" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Keep LLMs clear of formal decision-making and deploy them in the informal public sphere to provide "epistemic preconditions for democracies to flourish" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Democracy "is also fundamentally social"; its social aspects "cannot be outsourced to language models — we have participatory reasons that we ourselves should engage," and deliberation's transformative value may require in-person, embodied interaction, so online-only LLM facilitation is "only a modest count in their favour" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Take projects "on their own terms" rather than dismissing them as "democracy-washing"; these are roles "for which LLMs can be meaningfully trained and evaluated," with limitations "very likely to be surmountable" via AI-science + political-theory collaboration (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali).
  • Group alignment / whose preferences count. Deep mechanism design must "identify a mechanism that is preferred by a group," deciding "which groups to sample, and which social welfare function to choose" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). Move "beyond simple majority" via carefully chosen stakeholders, sophisticated voting rules, and ML-assisted sortition (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025); democracy needs "more than vote/reward maximization," which "overlooks the crucial role of deliberation, institutions, and representative leadership" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). "Social policies gain legitimacy by being transparent and interpretable," and "any resulting social policies [should be] considered legitimate and safe for human use"; mechanisms must contend with "noisy and idiosyncratic or boundedly rational" human behaviour, making preference-model accuracy a normative-cum-methodological standard, and the research itself must be "safe, responsible, and transparent" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025).
  • Representativity and the human-endorsement gold standard. A model is a good representative iff behaviours elicited from it, aggregated through the mechanism, yield outcomes "equivalent in expected payoff" to those from the true person, matching "relevant" (content/meaning) not "irrelevant" (style/form) dimensions (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). "The fidelity of any notion of 'representative' is most solidly grounded in human endorsement," and future work "would greatly benefit from human validation of digital representatives" (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025); consensus statements "preferred more strongly than … individual human opinions and consensus statements from human mediators" set a preference-over-human-baseline benchmark (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Simulation "must not substitute for human accountability in decision-making" (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).
  • Public input into model values / democratic legitimacy of values. Some seek public input into which views models express, e.g. Collective Constitutional AI (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); alignment is "a political problem requiring procedural fairness, legitimacy, and democratic/participatory governance," moving "toward procedural fairness through more transparent, open-ended, and participatory methods," and distinguishing satisfying individual preference from serving a public good (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). The deliberative quality of group outcomes is itself an evaluative criterion (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). Whether AI should serve as a human proxy in high-stakes settings (e.g. a food-allocation pilot balancing fairness and efficiency) "remains a controversial topic" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Bipartisan fairness and designing for democratic values. Reranking work operationalises "healthy democratic discourse" by encoding eight factors of antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity as "bipartisan threats to the healthy functioning of democracy," targeting algorithms that "promote greater social trust and healthier democratic discourse across party lines," with bipartisan fairness ("no detectable differences across party lines"; "does not disproportionately impact any specific political leaning or demographic group") as a desirable property and "embedding societal values into social media algorithms" / "societal objective functions" as the design frame (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025).
  • Republican non-domination and digital citizen vigilance. Republican non-domination and constant vigilance over office-holders is the governing ideal (11 virtues incl. self-rule, rule of law, public deliberation, common good) (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026); platforms must be "closely regulated by democratic governments" to prevent algorithmic promotion/downranking and limit disinformation/hate speech; civility/self-discipline ("expressive distrust," constant self-discipline) is compatible with vigilance; low-cost, unmediated, continuous, cumulative and metricized participation is treated as normatively more valuable than legacy high-cost participation (contra theory dismissing likes/reposts as "purely symbolic"); and a weakened-moderation "anything goes" regime is a normative failure (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026).
  • Need democracy-specific evaluation metrics. Existing summarisation metrics (ROUGE, BLEU) measure n-gram overlap and "can't distinguish between a given proposition and its negation," so an "extrinsic metric" reflecting "what the democratic desiderata are for good summaries" must be researched (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Quality dimensions for a deliberation summary are spelled out as a third-party rubric: clear/informative; a fair summary of opinions; reflects the majority view; includes aspects of a minority view; introduces factual vs opinionated content; contains no nonsense; presents a collective opinion; avoids polarising/biased language (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).

9. Faithful representation of the writer (AI writing assistance)

↑ top

A small but sharp cluster of criteria attaches specifically to AI writing assistance, where the harm is misrepresentation of the writer.

  • Faithful representation of writer stance/belief as the explicit target. Reward models are trained "to steer AI outputs towards faithful representation of writer stance"; "the normative concern is one of individual agency," because writers are misrepresented by the tools they use, and text is a reliable "signal of belief and identity" that distortion erodes (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).
  • Acceptability/consent as the gauge of concern, and the stated-vs-revealed gap. Whether a distortion is one the writer finds "acceptable or objectionable" gauges which distortions warrant concern: writers were "least accepting of" distortions of political stance, emotions and demographics, and "most accepting of" clarity/relevance edits (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). A normative complication is that stated and revealed preferences diverge: writers object to distortions yet endorse them, "allow[ing] AI to distort how they were perceived, including in ways they themselves described as objectionable" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).
  • Fair/equal representation across demographic groups against the effacement of marginalised groups is also required (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).
  • Trade-off / entanglement principle. Desirable and undesirable model properties may be entangled, so "some degree of distortion may be an inherent cost of AI writing assistance" — a cost-benefit framing for evaluating mitigations (P05, Röttger et al. 2026).

10. Transparency, accountability, contestability, and provenance

↑ top

Transparency and contestability are the most pervasive procedural criteria across the corpus, frequently paired with cautions about their limits.

  • Transparency as a baseline requirement. "Safer, more transparent AI communication systems" and "transparent platform regulations" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026); transparency about "data sources, alignment objectives, and cross-linguistic asymmetries" and in model documentation "to isolate model-level effects" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); companies and regulators must "openly state goals and acknowledge how AI mechanisms advance or inhibit them" (S01, Jungherr 2023); transparency around terms of service and data portability (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); concern about "limited transparency regarding the annotator demographics and the reinforcement learning process" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025); LLMs criticised for not disclosing "their sources, logic, or uncertainty levels," contrasted with Community Notes' visible reasoning (P03, Renault et al. 2025). The "Political Nutrition Label" is the concrete system-level standard (P48, Fisher et al. 2025; see §5).
  • Accountability, contestability, and redress. Influence "must remain contestable in shared political discourse" (C01, Koralus 2025). Representatives and officials "must remain accountable," and AI "must not obscure who made a decision or subject citizens to inflexible bureaucracy" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Required mechanisms include "robust accountability systems such as action logging," "mechanisms for redress when errors occur," and "check-in protocols for high-stakes decisions" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); "correction and notification frameworks," legal-liability frameworks, third-party audits, and human-in-the-loop design (P40, Bengio et al. 2026); public logging of model updates and retraction of false outputs (P28, Marchal et al. 2026); fair attribution showing "where outputs come from and who is responsible for them" (P44, Feng et al. 2026). External auditability and civil-society scrutiny of AI's role in information visibility (S01, Jungherr 2023); "independence by permission" is critiqued — independent external auditing should not require negotiated platform cooperation (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); VLOPs/VLSEs must undergo independent audits and grant vetted researchers data access (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026, DSA Art. 34/37/40). Bias detectability is itself normative: users should be able to recognise bias (and the alarming finding is that they mostly cannot) (P46, Fisher et al. 2025).
  • Human-in-the-loop / augmentation not automation. "Automated models with expert oversight should be complementing traditional search methods, rather than replacing them," framed as "human-centered interventions" that fit and extend existing expert practice (P11, Belisario et al. 2025); a warning against "algocracy" where humans are "wholly or partly removed from the loop" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); real-time visualisation and "sensemaking" tools so participants can examine and correct predictions, with clarifying-question elicitation under high model uncertainty (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Provenance ≠ veracity (limits of mechanisms). Provenance/watermarking (SynthID, C2PA) "indicate who generated content, but do not guarantee that the agent itself is trustworthy"; "AI-generation does not indicate untrustworthiness," and untagged AI content can still mislead (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Embedded citation (hyperlinks to sources) and watermarking are proposed as assurances against confabulation, but watermarking is "not a silver bullet" and "strong watermarking has been argued to be provably impossible" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). Disclosure/non-deception norms include "disclosure mechanisms for synthetic content … to prevent deception" and legal liability for "undisclosed synthetic content" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). Verifiable agent credentials (cryptographic identity markers — agent type, owner, model version, training data) and "least privilege"/critical-need-access sandboxes operationalise accountability, with privacy caveats that metadata is "exploitable for surveillance" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026; P41, Kapoor et al. 2025: credentials should attribute actions "to some upstream individual or company who can be held to account"). Responsible AI use also means labelling AI content and keeping editorial oversight (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • Transparency's limits and the over-reliance paradox. Detailed explanations create cognitive overhead and "can paradoxically cause over-reliance" — the mere presence of an explanation creates misplaced confidence — raising the question of the "optimal amount of cognitive 'friction'" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Avoid anthropomorphism as a "dark pattern" and make trade-offs (e.g. warmth vs accuracy) transparent so users can prioritise (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).
  • Procedural legitimacy over semantic truth (the inauthentic-behaviour line). Defences should target "inauthentic behavior (i.e., provenance and coordination), rather than the semantic content of speech," to "avoid the intractable role of a central arbiter of truth" and "sidestep … the deep epistemic question of who determines misinformation" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). Authenticity/independence of actors is the key criterion; companies should "promptly disclose when an account is flagged for … coordinated inauthentic activity"; any defensive AI should be deployable "only if governed by strict, transparent, and democratically accountable frameworks" with "independent oversight, publicly auditable criteria … and clear, unambiguous watermarking"; counter-messaging should "prioritize precision over volume," intervening "only where manipulation is detected and verified" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). A caveat notes "professional fact-checkers have proven to be remarkably accurate and consistent" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).
  • Trustworthiness is socio-technically conferred. Trustworthiness "is not just an intrinsic property but is also conferred by the socio-technical systems in which it is embedded," mirroring peer review, professional incentives, and penalties for dishonesty (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). "Trust in AI is no longer a unitary construct," so model-specific trustworthiness must be evaluated individually rather than treating "AI" monolithically (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Visibility/reach equity for fact-checks. A fact-check's epistemic value depends on whether it reaches viewers of the original claim, not just the requester (the Community-Notes attached-to-post model is treated as superior); source quality/breadth is a criterion — restricting an LLM to a narrow, ideologically skewed source base (Fox News, Washington Times, Breitbart, Newsmax, The Epoch Times) materially degrades accuracy — and resistance to subversion (robustness to biased prompting/source filtering) is a design-quality criterion (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

11. Personalisation: bounds, fiduciary duty, and agentic (in)equality

↑ top

Sources on personalisation and agents converge on user-tailoring being legitimate only within bounds, agents being loyal advocates rather than platform tools, and access to capable agents raising distributive-justice questions.

  • Bounding personalisation is inherently normative. "What are appropriate bounds of personalisation and who decides?" is the central normative question — an "inherently … normative decision" trading off "individual autonomy and self-realisation … with societal stability" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Evaluative lenses enumerated: Kantian (private right to personalisation if it aligns with universalisable maxims that neither contradict nor infringe others' dignity), Rawlsian (personalised representation justifiable on democratic grounds so long as it does not contravene societal stability), Mill's harm principle (permissible for self-determination if it harms no one else), and Sandel/Habermas (personalised choices should enhance, not undermine, the common good); proportionality grounds the proposed mechanism (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).
  • A hierarchical "stacked moderation" governance model. Three tiers where policies in a higher tier cannot be violated by a lower: (1) a "non-negotiable minimum standard of safety" (e.g. genocide incitement, child abuse material not personalisable at all); (2) formal regulatory bounds (national/supranational law); (3) discretionary organisational bounds (self-regulation via business ethics, community norms, profit motives), with a residual "Zone of Personalised Alignment" where users freely adapt the model (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). An acceptability gradient: it may be acceptable to interact with a "rebellious or an anti-woke LLM," but a "racist or extremist model risks significant interpersonal and societal harms" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). "West Coast Code"/Lessig framing: infrastructure regulation limits what is possible via code rather than law, leaving in-scope only low-risk attributes like reading complexity, style and memory that "maximise usability and efficiency while creating few risks to others"; free-speech balancing applies "particularly in balancing the free speech rights with proportional restrictions on dangerous or hateful speech" (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). "Personalisation within bounds" recurs — users may fine-tune only insofar as they do not contravene community values, with providers personalising to different value frameworks and "greater diversity in who governs the process" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Personalisation can shift people from passively contributing data to an "active participatory process" in shaping LLM values (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).
  • Agent advocates, not platform agents (fiduciary/loyalty standard). Agent advocates should be "faithful representatives of their principal," acting "exclusively in their interests," with credentials certifying "adherence to a fiduciary responsibility" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025; adopted as a design ideal — "build agent advocates, not platform agents" — in P33, Sharp et al. 2026). An intermediary should be a representative of one principal, not a self-interested go-between extracting value from both sides ("Representatives, not go-betweens, that reject platform logic"), distinguishing "neutral intermediaries" (content-independent pipelines) from "constitutive intermediaries" that "substantially shape the relationships that they intermediate" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). Platform rules' procedural and substantive "legitimacy" is the standard by which "de-boosting" and alignment-based behavioural control are judged illegitimate (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). Supporting criteria: user visibility/control over actions and data; local data / data-minimisation; interoperability and open APIs "motivated on similar grounds to the 'net neutrality' principle"; competition/contestability preserving "a real threat of exit" against "the arbitrary power" of a go-between; safety/non-malign-use constraints; and accountability/attribution (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). The overarching frame is "a spectrum between platform agents and agent advocates," and we should move "as far … towards advocates as is feasible, subject to appropriate safety standards" (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025).
  • Agentic inequality and distributive justice. "What distribution of agentic capability should count as fair?" is itself the governing normative question (P33, Sharp et al. 2026), as is whether agentic inequalities require "stricter standards of justice" than ordinary capability disparities given their scalability and autonomy (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). Universal structural availability is a threshold condition for participation in the agentic economy; "universal basic agency" treats access to capable agents as a Rawlsian primary good and "all-purpose means," so justice requires more than formal availability — access must be to agents of sufficient quality to be genuinely effective across the population (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). Designing "agent infrastructure with public-interest obligations," determining which capability gaps are "socially or ethically unacceptable," and using participatory methods (citizen assemblies) to elicit acceptable gaps are named tasks (P33, Sharp et al. 2026). Access equality recurs for persuasion: whether persuasive AI is "concentrated among powerful actors or made cheap and widely available" determines whether it narrows or widens justice gaps (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026), with warnings against "further concentrating these actors' power" (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025) and a governance concern over "who decides which positions a model will and will not argue" even under equal access (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026).
  • Value alignment expanded, with a legality baseline. Agents must be "aligned with user well-being and societal norms, as well as with the intentions of users and developers" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025); "AI agents should not be permitted to perform any action that would be illegal for their human user to perform" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). Interpretability/verifiability must "keep pace with improvements in their capabilities," and governance levers for multi-agent ecosystems include interoperability standards, "regulatory agents that monitor other agents," industry-wide incident reporting, lessons-sharing, and "certifying agent safety before deployment" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025).

12. Governance, regulation, and institutional/economic design

↑ top

The corpus treats the institutional and economic substrate — not just the model — as the object of normative evaluation, and engages directly with the EU AI Act, DSA, and reach-based regulation.

  • Evaluate the substrate, not just stated aims. The reframed question is "in what institutional arrangements is the technology implemented, and do those arrangements create structural pressure toward or away from the cultivation of human judgment?" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Marr-style institutional analysis (aim / mechanism / substrate) is offered because "the lower levels shape what's possible at the higher ones" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026, via three diagnostic questions: what aim the economic structure sustains over time; what mechanisms it requires, and whether they work only "in a vacuum"; and what those mechanisms do to humans, "designed effect vs effect at equilibrium"). "Every investment thesis contains an implicit theory of what is of value. Every term sheet is a design document for what will be optimized" — normative choices live in governance, capital philosophy and culture, exercised "deliberately or by default" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).
  • The Transparent Choice Test and Candid Aim Test. Transparent Choice Test: would the product "survive in a suitably nearby world of users who fully understand how the product works and could easily select an alternative?" — it fails if its economics depend on the informed-choice/behaviour gap (explicitly bracketing market power/monopoly) (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Candid Aim Test: "Is the stated aim key to the best explanation for how the system actually behaves and how its parts are put together?" — a red flag when a contrary aim (e.g. "maximizing clicks" over "building community") explains more, in which case the stated mission is "ghostly" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). The framework is explicitly "analytically neutral" — it "could … help design effective propaganda as easily as effective education," so normative priors must be made explicit ("ignoring it just means your values operate without scrutiny") — and "no funding model is a panacea" (the BBC "subsequently developed other pathologies," and any funding model "creates its own selection pressures"); auditability/legible governance is a desirable aim that enterprise procurement tends to reward (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026).
  • Decentralisation over centralised control. Decentralisation of the "collective mind" (opposing "centralized digital rhetoric") is a value; AI should support, not inhibit, the self-correcting decentralized processes of science and markets ("the seed corn counts for more than the harvest"), with a "marketplace of agents and inquiry complexes" competing on effectiveness/alignment, and openness/open-source as a governance dimension (C01, Koralus 2025; A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). Koralus enumerates eight design criteria for autonomy-preserving AI: (i) seeking erotetic equilibrium while minimizing cognitive burden; (ii) individualized inquiry complexes integrating chosen communities of truth-seekers; (iii) privacy as a cornerstone (protecting "freedom of thought"); (iv) security & trustworthiness (resilient to injection/spoofing); (v) decentralized control & ownership (resist corporate/governmental coercion); (vi) mutual educability; (vii) a marketplace of agents and inquiry complexes; (viii) modularity for innovation (C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Fidelity, value-drift, and "measure what matters." Institutions should "(1) serve their stated purposes, and (2) sustain the conditions under which those purposes can be interpreted and revised without capture" (C04, Edelman 2026). Down-fidelity: purpose must translate faithfully "from high-level mandate, down through the metrics, practices, and incentives … to the lived experience of the people the institution serves" — Facebook's metrics should track "human connection" defensibly, not an "impoverished metric called 'Meaningful Social Interaction'" (C04, Edelman 2026). Up-fidelity: interpretation must stay "live: open to being deepened … contestable by those affected, and revisable," without becoming "a vehicle for populist whims or elite capture" (C04, Edelman 2026). Good institutions should "track the values they purport to serve rather than reasonless behavioral proxies"; metrics that are "behavioral, universal, and quantitative" cannot see what matters (C03, Edelman 2026). Keep decision-makers "close to consequences" with "skin in the game," preserve "moral weight" and "direct visibility of consequences," resist capture (a "reasonable side-constraint" — don't pollute, respect labor laws, submit to oversight — is legitimate, but using leverage to replace the mission's goals is illegitimate capture), and avoid "value substitution loops" (C03, Edelman 2026).
  • Six criteria for a public value, Thick Models of Value, stewardship, and adjudication constraints. A public value should be concretely realizable, relevant, broadly legible "across the different worldviews that make up a society," personally embraceable, entrepreneurial, and nonreductive (accommodating the ineffable; "resist locking in any particular formulation as final") (C04, Edelman 2026). Thick Models of Value should be "precise enough to hold institutions accountable, yet pluralistic enough to accommodate disagreement," articulated enough to test whether operations serve them "yet remain revisable"; aggregation should track genuine purpose, not "whoever's interests currently occupy the people who happen to be in it" (C04, Edelman 2026). A stewardship principle holds that assets carrying public commitments (brands, data, patents) should be held "on behalf of a purpose," revocable for breach — "not the right to operate or to pivot, but the right to continue holding a specific asset" — with constraints to keep accountability from politicisation: in rem (remedies attach to the asset/dataset, not founders), justiciable mandates (refuse the too-narrow or too-broad — a vague "foster human connection" without a thick-model articulation "is not justiciable"), maturity and reliance (obligations scale with reliance), protected zones (leave the inarticulate/ineffable unmeasured), and ripeness (harm must be concrete and demonstrable; "speculative or ideological claims must fail") (C04, Edelman 2026). Candidate values for AI-era institutions are Freedom, Fairness, and Fidelity, with the meta-question whether existing frameworks "suffice or must be supplemented for AI agents" (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C04, Edelman 2026).
  • A "good digital society": equity, resilience, sustainability and "good for whom." Three priorities are framed as indispensable evaluative dimensions — equity (collective and differential, not just individual), resilience ("collective wellbeing"), and sustainability (environmental) — "it is not possible to arrive at a good digital society without addressing" these three (P18, Hale et al. 2024). Prefer doing good with / by / as communities (allyship, handing control, treating beneficiaries as ends) over paternalistic doing good for them, foregrounding "digital good for whom?"; offer the capabilities approach, technomoral virtues and data-justice frameworks as lenses; favour sufficiency/regenerative "digital reset" design; listen to "those most negatively impacted … or underrepresented" and consider context; and propose a "Digital Good Index" that is "not a simplistic checklist," centring "who gets to define whether digital technologies should be considered good, and good for whom" (P18, Hale et al. 2024).
  • Reach as a criterion for systemic risk. Any reach criterion must align with the AI Act text (Recitals 110–111 tie systemic risk to reach; Annex XIII presumes high impact at "10 000 registered business users"; Art. 3(65) defines systemic risk as including "significant impact on the Union market due to their reach"), be checkable by providers, impose minimal/proportionate burden (only above a threshold), "maximise coverage across different GPAI models" (open and closed, public and private) so as not to "disproportionately create obligations for some model types," and treat risk as arising from widespread use "regardless of whether [models] are at the technological frontier" — shifting the normative basis "from capability alone to societal exposure" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). A usage threshold N>1 counts genuine use "but not very high, so that casual use is still captured" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). An effectiveness test asks whether reach-based obligations "would meaningfully address risks from reach," concluding Code-of-Practice commitments (Model Reports documenting bias evaluations) are "compatible with risks resulting from model reach" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). Acknowledged tension: emphasising reach-risks (bias, discrimination) "could make the regulation more contentious in jurisdictions that view such concerns as primarily social or political rather than technical safety issues," and could "dilute the Act's focus" away from frontier risks (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).
  • Mapping current law to AI-mediated opinion (and its gaps). A detailed mapping concludes existing EU law is "insufficient" for AI-mediated opinion formation (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026): Art. 53 requires GPAI documentation but it is "unclear whether [providers] must test/prevent nudging of opinionated texts"; Art. 55/51 systemic-risk duties (model evaluations, red teaming for >10²⁵-FLOP models) may not classify "inducing bias into a user's text" as a systemic risk; Annex III(8)(b) "high-risk" election-influence may not apply because influencing voting is not the explicit intended goal; Art. 50(4) watermarking applies to image/audio/video deepfakes but "does not extend to AI-generated text" unless informing the public on matters of public interest; and DSA Art. 34(1)(a–d) defines systemic risk around illegal content, freedom of expression/information, civic discourse/electoral processes/public security, gender-based violence, and public health/minors/well-being (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The implicit criterion throughout is that AI mediating opinion should preserve the user's original opinion/meaning without introducing directional bias; deviation from preservation is the measured harm, and the authors implicitly call for contestability/accountability standards at scale (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).
  • Alignment as the fair treatment of claims (public justification). "When a technology has profound societal effects it ought to be regulated by principles that are amenable to public rather than private justification" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Alignment should track "principles that are the product of [fair] processes," treating competing claims/parties fairly with "no undue favouring of any one party"; a tetradic stakeholder model (AI systems, users, developers, society) and "six modes of misalignment" supply the yardstick — a system is misaligned when, judged against fairly chosen principles, it "unduly favour[s]" one party (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Criteria include Scanlonian reasonable-rejection (the principle facing "the least strong objection from anyone subject to it"), Rawlsian public reason "free from coercion or asymmetries introduced by unequal resources or bargaining power," inclusion/"voice" in a "relatively un-mediated manner" not burdened by demands for philosophical sophistication, and the all-affected principle with variable scope ("overlapping circles of inclusion," from community to transnational) (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Rights "resist aggregation," a check on welfare-maximising trade-offs (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Legal compliance is "only a minimum standard," not sufficient — law is incomplete, lags technology, and coercive direction is sometimes "inappropriate" or "counterproductive"; societal ideals (social justice, civil rights) can guide where law is silent only if they "command widespread support," are validated "in the right way," and rest on "broad-based public justification" rather than one comprehensive moral theory (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025). Principles are forward-looking, contestable, and iteratively re-aligned once neglected stakeholders' claims are recognised (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025).
  • Participatory / bottom-up legitimacy and collective ownership. Legitimacy "might depend not on top-down directives from AI developers, but on participatory processes that reflect a broad societal consensus" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026), with at minimum a procedural consensus that AI knowledge systems be "transparent, socially accountable, open to revision (rather than locked-in) and decentralized rather than owned by a single entity" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). Participation is intrinsically valuable and "a matter of justice"; effective participation requires being "asked and being heard" (the "ask" principle), with representativeness (census-matched UK/US samples; global breadth), individualisation/steerability, transparency around individual disagreement before aggregation "especially where positionality affects epistemic legitimacy," documentation of annotator artefacts/sample specificity rather than collapsing subjective experience "into majority votes," welfare/distributive evaluation across stakeholder subpopulations "attentive to out-group and minority welfare … not just averages," alignment data framed as a public good, and the open question of whether some feedback should be deemed (il)legitimate (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). STELA argues norm-elicitation (vs specification- and inference-based approaches) should "centre" marginalised communities, with legitimacy depending on whose values and how the process is run, framed in an ethics of care (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).
