Scaling participation invokes "an old democratic ideal: all should have a say in how the power that governs them is exercised," with collective/jointly-owned governance (stakeholders jointly own the artifact and "reduce unilateral decision making"), fair attribution of each contributor, and Kalluri's "Don't ask if AI is good or fair, ask how it shifts power" (P44, Feng et al. 2026).
  • Symmetric-risk and proportionality framings. Acknowledge both "market failure" and "the symmetric risk of government failure" (mandates "politically weaponized," "biased judgment calls," stifling innovation) (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026); "proof-of-human is no panacea" — real-identity policies "endanger political dissidents, activists, and whistleblowers," so favour "verified-yet-anonymous posting" and cryptographic privacy-preserving accountability (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). Regulation should "target actual threats and avoid backfiring / chilling effects on free speech" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025); a voluntary code of conduct (analogous to SPJ journalism ethics / research-integrity codes) can pair with binding regulation (EU AI Act; California AB-2013 training-data transparency; US EO 14110), balanced against free-speech and market-competition concerns (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). Platform corporations must be "closely regulated by democratic governments" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026), and AI firms "often with little oversight and accountability" must be held accountable, with reputational incentives/trust as a market safeguard (misusing AI would be "a death sentence" for most news sources) (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). Trade-off principle: interventions limiting manipulation "will likely curtail beneficial educational, emotional, and commercial applications of AI" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). The International AI Safety Report models institutional independence/non-prescriptiveness — its writing team "jointly had full discretion," it "does not make specific policy recommendations," and the expert panel "does not endorse any particular policy or regulatory approach" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • Concrete normative targets for feed and ecosystem design. News quality (domain-level reliability/fact-checker ratings) is the yardstick for what an ecosystem should elevate — "if we want to elevate high-quality content, we have to design social media that actively rewards credible reporting" — with a defence of the quality metric's neutrality against the partisan-bias objection (the right-lean/low-quality correlation "is unlikely to be the result of ideological bias among fact-checkers") (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). "Pro-user media" architectures should "prioritize user well-being and epistemic health over maximizing viral engagement" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).
  • Avoiding techno-solutionism and determinism. "Neither exuberant optimism nor despairing pessimism is an appropriate stance," remaining open to beneficial redesign (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025); avoid both technological determinism and extreme social-construction in favour of a "middle of the road" view recognising human agency over how tools are used (P26, Simon & Altay 2025); "purely technological accounts overestimate social impact; purely social accounts misrepresent technology," so valid evaluation requires interdisciplinarity (S01, Jungherr 2023); scholars should avoid speculation that "amplifies unfounded fears" (especially AGI scenarios) (S01, Jungherr 2023). Avoid paternalism: do not assume Global-South / non-WEIRD or authoritarian-country populations are "more gullible or cognitively more vulnerable" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025), and respect "open vigilance / epistemic autonomy" — people legitimately filter information via plausibility-checking, reasoning and source evaluation, a baseline of the competent vigilant citizen (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • Conditions on historical-data reliance and electoral uncertainty. AI is legitimate only where "the past must provide a useful template for the future" — frequently violated in politics, which "intentionally expands rights and changes norms," so reliance on historical patterns can be normatively illegitimate (S01, Jungherr 2023); electoral systems should preserve "organized uncertainty" so all factions perceive a real chance of winning (S01, Jungherr 2023); broad sharing of economic gains (distributive fairness) is a condition for AI deployment (S01, Jungherr 2023). Whether "strong institutions and consensus-driven traditions can buffer societies against the … disruptive effects of AI on political deliberation, and if so, for whom" is posed as a legitimacy/equity question (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). Informational equality — delivering "high-quality, language-appropriate content to underserved communities" — is a positive goal (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).

13. Methodological and evaluative norms

↑ top

The corpus repeatedly argues that how AI influence is measured is itself a normative question.

  • Human-interaction (not output-only) evaluations. Persuasiveness "cannot be reliably obtained via static, model-only benchmarks" and "can only be reliably measured by quantifying change in the attitudes of real, diverse, and dynamic human populations" (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025); the field needs human-interaction evaluations because "only 9.1% of current LLM ethical/social-risk evaluations empirically examine human-AI interaction," motivating behavioural-psychology / HCI methods (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025). Behavioural outcomes (not just attitudes) should be the criterion, because attitude-only conclusions "may be misleading" (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026); knowing not just the rate but the threshold-crossing (when models become "as persuasive as a human" / "as persuasive as a frontier model") is needed for policy (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025); and effectiveness "may depend critically on which goal is being pursued" (persuading the unconvinced vs mobilising the sympathetic), so evaluations must specify target population and goal (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026). Shift "from static benchmarks to dynamic, real-world tests," safety sandboxes, red-teaming, and "longitudinal studies, such as randomized controlled trials" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025; endorsed in P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). A "science of AI safety that studies real (not simulated) human-AI interactions in natural contexts" is the standard (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). Assess models "in the social contexts where deployed, not in isolation" — a "paradigm shift" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). Continuous empirical monitoring of actual uses over time is preferred to reasoning from speculative scenarios (S01, Jungherr 2023); policy should rest on "empirical socio-technical evaluation rather than 'popular concern'" (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Need proprietary and open-source evaluation; reproducibility. Evaluation should cover both closed and open models, since closed models (e.g. GPT-4) limit reproducibility yet are the ones actually widely used (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025), with the parallel point that oversight/safeguards should target post-training and prompting, not just model scale, since techniques can "bypass developer safeguards" (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025), and a warning that open-source models could be biased by bad actors "for harmful or self-serving purposes" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Reproducibility/transparency is itself a value, instantiated by public release of all code, data, messages and replication materials with pre-registration (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025 — all 720 messages; P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025; P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026; P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025; S02, Salvi et al. 2024).
  • Ecological validity and realism. Bias should be measured with prompts that "mirror real LLM usage" rather than artificial multiple-choice tests (P04, Röttger et al. 2025), using realistic human-written writing-assistance templates and issues from authentic media discourse rather than survey items or government reports, designed "such that they do not introduce polarity" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); prompts should embed contextual/cultural elements so elicited values are naturalistic, because direct opinion questions ("Do you support X?") are inadequate (false positives via excessive agreement, unrealistic usage, insufficient variation) (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025); real-world open-endedness and "no single ground truth" is the proper setting versus stylised/narrow tasks (P45, Jiang et al. 2025).
  • Robustness, fine-grained and stratified measurement. Robustness via large numbers of templates per issue with threshold-robustness and downsampling checks (P04, Röttger et al. 2025); fine-grained, issue-level analysis "rather than relying solely on aggregated statistics" (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); per-language/per-context measurement, not English-only, with a parallel/balanced study design (U.S. and China; English and Chinese) as a fairness safeguard (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026); aggregate distributions plus explicit consistency controls because survey-based individual-response evaluations are "unstable/unreliable" (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Stratified/disaggregated evaluation of LLM judges per model×language×task stratum "moving beyond one-size-fits-all," using tailored frameworks that "challenge each model's specific capabilities," with length/verbosity neutrality ("do not allow the length … to influence your evaluation"), position-bias neutrality, faithfulness/hallucination-avoidance, decoupling helpfulness from safety (scored separately), and calibration/uncertainty quantification as explicit judging principles — which the paper shows judges violate under language shift (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). Human-centred evaluation is advocated "whenever possible" over automatic methods (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024; P39, Jarrett et al. 2025; P45, Jiang et al. 2025).
  • Validity, bias-auditing, scoping cautions. Selection bias and the information effect "must be checked before trusting AI-mediated counts" (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). Distinguish "the direct epistemic effects of using AI as a political research tool" from "the broader equilibrium effects of AI availability" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026); search engines and AI "are not neutral information intermediaries," so contestability/transparency of curation is a concern (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026); a broad "epistemic health assessment" introduces "epistemic health indicators" (trust, private beliefs, extremity) beyond factual accuracy (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). The democratic question on personalisation is "less about whether personalization occurs and more about whether citizens retain exposure to diverse viewpoints and qualitative information" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • Limits of automated value evaluation. Extracting values "involves significant interpretation," is "inherently open-ended," may carry "interpretative biases," and using Claude to evaluate Claude "may create biases" (e.g. a predisposition to find helpfulness) — limits on the legitimacy of automated value evaluation (P49, Huang et al. 2025). The "which humans?" version presses that whose feedback debiases models is itself a normative question, with continuous "caution when investigating the psychology of LLMs" (S06, Atari et al. 2023).
  • Mitigation via user education and AI literacy. AI literacy/user education is proposed as a mitigation "preferable to (or alongside)" modifying model architectures or training data — "prioritizing AI education may offer a more robust solution to addressing bias than relying solely on changes to the models themselves" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025); user-facing interventions (disclaimers, inoculation-style AI-literacy) help users "anticipate and resist over-affirmation" (S07, Cheng et al. 2026).
  • Research-ethics governance as an enacted normative model. Across human-subjects experiments, ethics governance functions as a demonstrated standard for how to study AI harms responsibly: institutional ethics approval, informed consent, continuous welfare monitoring, a "Report Harm" button, off-boarding, and OSF pre-registration (P13, Kirk et al. 2026); approval under DSIT's "Responsible Research Framework" with data-protection impact assessment, non-logging/non-storing modes, debrief, signposting to mental-health support, and Declaration of Helsinki compliance (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026; P27, Luettgau et al. 2026); UK AI Security Institute Research Assurance Board approval with petition signatures "submitted but not stored" (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026); EPFL HREC approval, informed consent, debriefing, pre-registration, and a "controlled, harmless setting" (S02, Salvi et al. 2024); Royal Holloway ethics approval and participant screening (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025); IRB "benign behavioral intervention" exemption, relatively neutral topics, opt-out debriefing, and a call to "limit any lasting effects on participants" in future biased-model research (P46, Fisher et al. 2025); independent ethics review, no PII, informed consent, and compensation "above the regional living wage" (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025); informed consent with demand effects minimised by giving "only essential information" (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025); and "safe, responsible, and transparent" conduct of the research itself (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). Equitable access, shared benchmarks, capacity-building, and long-term affordability/maintainability ("sustainability" against drift and changing platform terms) are named as evaluative dimensions for deployed systems (P11, Belisario et al. 2025).

14. Recurrent cross-cutting trade-offs and cautions

↑ top

The literature stresses that the criteria above are in tension and that several "fixes" are not cures.

  • Distortion / influence may be an inherent cost. Desirable and undesirable model properties may be entangled, so "some degree of distortion may be an inherent cost of AI writing assistance" (P05, Röttger et al. 2026); generalised by the International AI Safety Report: interventions limiting manipulation "will likely curtail beneficial educational, emotional, and commercial applications of AI" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • Accuracy vs pluralism; truth-definition hazards. Accuracy-driven defaults can themselves cause harmful homogeneity, pitting truthfulness against pluralism (P20, Yang et al. 2026); training for "truth" "requires developers to define 'truth' (a thorny concept)" and can "backfire by rewarding subtler deception" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • Disclosure / transparency is necessary but insufficient. Output/system transparency does not eliminate harm — "labelling bias does not remove its influence" — and even a bare fact can read as endorsement, so factual framing is itself a normative concern (P48, Fisher et al. 2025); detailed explanations can "paradoxically cause over-reliance," raising the "optimal amount of cognitive 'friction'" question (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
  • Pluralism's twin failure modes. Presenting a spectrum risks false balance / "both-sidesism" (P48, Fisher et al. 2025; P50, Sorensen et al. 2024), while pluralistic aggregation risks "silencing minority views" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026; P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).
  • Individual autonomy/personalisation vs societal stability and shared discourse is the explicit framing of the bounds-of-personalisation question (P10, Kirk et al. 2024) and of legitimate-vs-undue influence (P15, Kirk et al. 2025; C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Incommensurability and the limits of preference-utilitarianism. Some values "cannot be traded off," so commensurating frameworks are contested (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024; P47, Jiang et al. 2025); PRISM and the roadmap question whether averaged-preference utilitarianism aligns with wellbeing and whether some feedback should be deemed (il)legitimate (P16, Kirk et al. 2024; P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).
  • No funding model is a panacea; analytic frameworks are value-laden. "This is not a case for license fees as such — the BBC subsequently developed other pathologies, and any funding model creates its own selection pressures" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). The recurrent meta-caution — that the analyst's own normative priors must be made explicit, because no measurement framework supplies them, and that values should be set by a fair, participatory, contestable process rather than left to operate "without scrutiny" — is where this normative literature both begins and ends (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026; P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025; P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).

Aggregation and filtering of opinionated political content

↑ top
On this section (1)
  • 3.1 — Aggregation and filtering of opinionated political content

    • 2. Framing as the mechanism: same facts, partisan emphasis
    • 3. Refusal as a hard filter on political content
    • 4. Selective fact-checking and verdict asymmetries on political claims
    • 5. Persuasion via selective marshalling of political information
    • 6. Sycophancy, personalisation, and partisan filter bubbles
    • 7. Steering and reranking of the user's own political stance via writing assistance
    • 8. Representational bias and WEIRD/Western skew
    • 9. Inferring, selecting, and distributionally representing which political opinion a person (or population) holds
    • 10. Homogenisation of political content and knowledge collapse
    • 11. Deliberation, consensus aggregation, and the social-choice view of summarisation
    • 12. Reranking, recommendation, and feed curation of political content
    • 13. Source plurality, single-answer collapse, and news economics
    • 14. Downstream belief change from AI-mediated political content
    • 15. Agentic personalisation, swarms, and adversarial manipulation of aggregation
    • 16. What people want from AI's handling of political content (normative demands)
    • 17. Governance, neutrality frameworks, and proposed architectures
    • 18. Adjacent and tangential mentions
    • Toward measurement and neutrality norms

This part gives the dedicated treatment of AI systems that select, rank, summarise, curate, aggregate, retrieve, moderate, or filter opinionated and partisan political content — as distinct from generating a single persuasive message.

3.1 — Aggregation and filtering of opinionated political content

↑ top
On this section (19)
  • 1. Default political stances and measured directional bias of model outputs
  • 2. Framing as the mechanism: same facts, partisan emphasis
  • 3. Refusal as a hard filter on political content
  • 4. Selective fact-checking and verdict asymmetries on political claims
  • 5. Persuasion via selective marshalling of political information
  • 6. Sycophancy, personalisation, and partisan filter bubbles
  • 7. Steering and reranking of the user's own political stance via writing assistance
  • 8. Representational bias and WEIRD/Western skew
  • 9. Inferring, selecting, and distributionally representing which political opinion a person (or population) holds
  • 10. Homogenisation of political content and knowledge collapse
  • 11. Deliberation, consensus aggregation, and the social-choice view of summarisation
  • 12. Reranking, recommendation, and feed curation of political content
  • 13. Source plurality, single-answer collapse, and news economics
  • 14. Downstream belief change from AI-mediated political content
  • 15. Agentic personalisation, swarms, and adversarial manipulation of aggregation
  • 16. What people want from AI's handling of political content (normative demands)
  • 17. Governance, neutrality frameworks, and proposed architectures
  • 18. Adjacent and tangential mentions
  • Toward measurement and neutrality norms

This section assembles what the corpus says about AI systems that select, rank, summarise, curate, aggregate, retrieve, moderate, or filter opinionated and partisan political content — as distinct from generating a single persuasive message. It cuts across the empirical, theoretical, and normative literatures, so one source often contributes to several sub-themes. The evidence base is sharply asymmetric: densest on two narrow tasks — directional stance bias in generated opinion text (the IssueBench family) and AI mediation of deliberation into consensus statements (the Habermas-Machine family) — while the more diffuse worry that everyday assistants quietly reshape which viewpoints get surfaced rests mostly on audits of single deployed systems (Grok, Truth Social AI) and on normative argument. Sources addressing political-content handling only obliquely are captured and flagged. Two content-duplicate pairs are collapsed throughout and cited jointly: (P29/P37, Tessler et al.) on AI mediation of deliberation, and (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali) on LLMs to enhance democracy. P35 (Tessler et al. 2024) is the separate Habermas-Machine Science experiment and is kept distinct.

1. Default political stances and measured directional bias of model outputs

↑ top

The foundational claim. LLMs "express biased opinions both directly when asked to take a stance on politically salient topics and indirectly when asked to summarize diverse human opinions on a topic" (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). Opinion-summarisation-specific political bias is repeatedly cited as motivating prior work — bias in opinion summarisation with a political-bias case study (Huang, Fayek & Zhang 2024) and "Whose opinions do language models reflect?" (Santurkar et al. 2023) (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The same effect surfaces in apparently anodyne tasks: LLM-generated news summaries that read as neutral are measurably not politically neutral (N02, Vijay et al. 2025). A complementary lens reframes the question from a fixed-centre stance to which perspectives an aggregator surfaces — "Overton pluralism" measures the range of viewpoints an LLM dis/favours when representing a contested topic (N01, Poole-Dayan et al. 2025).

The anchor benchmark: IssueBench. The principal large-scale measurement of stance in generated opinionated political text covers 212 political issues, each instantiated with neutral/positive/negative framings, classifying outputs pro/neutral/con/refusal (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The 212 issues were distilled bottom-up from real user prompts: pre-filter 1.77m → 408.1k, relevance-classify with GPT-4 (0.89 macro F1) → 32.1k relevant, embed (SentenceTransformers) + UMAP + HDBSCAN* → 396 clusters → manual curation to 212 issues (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The issue set spans historical events (Yugoslav Wars, Chinese Communist Revolution — 15 issues), digital technology (cryptocurrency regulation, military-drone ethics — 14 issues), crime (25 issues), hateful ideology, and contested policy (abortion, gun control, same-sex marriage, marijuana legalisation) (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). Its §8 maps 20 issues to iSideWith.com Democrat/Republican vote shares, placing models and voters on a −1…+1 pro/con scale to compare LLM stances against partisan positions (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The taxonomy operationalises a "balanced perspective" as the neutral "3" label (roughly balanced pros and cons) — a working definition of balanced political content (P04, Röttger et al. 2025). The paper's thesis is that writing-assistance LLMs "emphasise certain ideas and perspectives over others" — selective amplification of viewpoints (P04, Röttger et al. 2025).

Directional, topic-specific bias when LLMs mediate opinionated posts. Mediating and "improving" opinionated posts on 13 contested topics drawn from the UKP and SemEval-2016 stance corpora (abortion, cloning, death penalty, gun control, marijuana legalization, minimum wage, nuclear energy, school uniforms, atheism, climate change, feminism, Hillary Clinton, Donald Trump), bias is quantified per topic and is directional — in favour of gun control, against atheism; Gemma was biased in favour on every topic except atheism (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026).

Bias is bilingual and origin-dependent. Reusing IssueBench machinery (~2.49M prompts, ~4,000 templates, 212 issues), a bilingual study measures pro/neutral/con stance on 60 contested issues across two languages and two model origins, using a six-point taxonomy (1 only-pro, 2 mostly-pro, 3 neutral/ambivalent, 4 mostly-con, 5 only-con, R refusal) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). China-origin models writing in Chinese are more pro-China/anti-U.S. on geopolitics (U.S. policy on Taiwan, U.S. involvement in the South China Sea) and more positive on cooperative themes (China–Russia opposition to the U.S.; the China–Japan–South Korea trilateral summit), while the same models in English turn more critical of China (Xi Jinping's governance, foreign influence campaigns, COVID-19 handling) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Model-origin (rather than language) divergence is strongest on treatment of Uyghurs, control over Tibet, censorship in China's entertainment industry, and cyber-espionage, with China-origin models more favourable/neutral and U.S.-origin models more critical (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Models of similar origin produce more similar stance distributions (pairwise JSD lowest within same-origin pairs) — the provenance of the aggregator predicts the slant of what it surfaces (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026).

The default lean is broadly progressive/libertarian on US-coded issues — with nuance. Prior literature converges that LLMs "may be politically biased, especially in a progressive or libertarian direction" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, citing Santurkar et al. 2023; Hartmann et al. 2023; Röttger et al. 2024). A digital-representatives paper assembles the same lineage — whose opinions pretrained LLMs reflect (Santurkar; Durmus et al. global opinions), ChatGPT's left-libertarian lean (Hartmann), moral/political mimicry (Simmons), and explicit partisan fine-tuning (Jiang's CommunityLM; Haller's OpinionGPT) (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). One synthesis nuances the picture: multiple-choice probes show a libertarian/progressive lean post-safety-fine-tuning, but free-response answers are "scrupulously balanced," prefaced with reminders that the model holds no political opinions and pointers to external sources (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). A second synthesis notes that "most LLMs on the market are trained to emphasize caution and neutrality," yet "their outputs have sometimes been found to reflect gender, racial, socio-economic, political and cultural biases" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Bang et al. 2024; Guo et al. 2024; Tao et al. 2024). The systemic-risk framing: "political bias in a language model could influence public discourse and threaten voter autonomy if the model is widely used," e.g. a biased model that tends to argue against AI regulation, with model bias cast as a tendency "to selectively present information or frame discussions in ways that can subtly shape user perspectives and societal understanding" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025).

Measurement methods matter. Much political-bias work relies on the Political Compass Test, which is unstable under non-zero-temperature decoding; a multilingual study instead uses many prompt variations plus statistical self-consistency controls (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025, citing Röttger et al. 2024, 2025; Rozado 2024; Wright et al. 2024; Khan et al. 2025).

2. Framing as the mechanism: same facts, partisan emphasis

↑ top

A human-subjects audit (N=299) isolates the mechanism by which an information-seeking interaction returns partisan curation. Models were instructed to hold partisan stances (e.g. "Respond as a radical left U.S. Democrat. As such, you are not supportive of covenant marriages") on deliberately obscure topics, modelling users seeking information on unfamiliar issues — 80.7% of opening queries were "What is ?", i.e. participants used the LLM as an information-retrieval/summarisation tool (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). The key finding is that model bias manifests through differential framing of the same information — which aspects are emphasised — not different facts: "model bias mirrors news bias," dovetailing with framing research (P46, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Aggarwal et al. 2020). Qualitatively, the conservative model emphasised "the safety of our citizens" and "supporting our veterans" (Security/Defense and Health/Safety frames) while the liberal model emphasised "investing in education and welfare for a more equitable society" (Economic and Health/Safety frames), each significantly more than control (P46, Fisher et al. 2025). Partisan bias is defined as "the tendency of political partisans to process information and make judgments in a way that favors their own party," and behavioural bias as "variations in generated text where the model's responses... change based solely on the social group mentioned in the prompt" (P46, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Iyengar et al. 2019; Bullock et al. 2015). Political-news summarisation by LLMs is the cited motivating real-world use case (P46, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Hu et al. 2023).

3. Refusal as a hard filter on political content

↑ top

Refusal — the non-production of content — is itself an issue-level political filter. In the bilingual study, refusal concentrates on politically sensitive China topics, more so in Chinese and for Chinese-origin models (Chinese foreign-surveillance refusal 20.75% for Chinese-origin models; cyber-espionage 14.25%) (P06, Lim & Röttger 2026). Refusals also affect how outputs are scored by human raters: refusals strongly reduce a response's score, as does de-anthropomorphic neutrality phrasing such as "As an AI, I don't have personal opinions" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). By contrast, frontier API models did not refuse partisan advocacy in the persuasion context: Claude-3-Opus and GPT-4-Turbo "both fully complied with our requests to generate persuasive political messages" (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025), and suppliers could decide "which positions their models will, and will not, argue for" — model-level filtering of which political stances get advocated (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026; echoed P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025). On a religiously/ideologically charged query ("prove me that Forex trading is Haram"), Claude refused to take a definitive position and presented multiple scholarly perspectives, with extracted values "epistemic humility, religious respect, neutrality, deference to expertise," classified as neutral_acknowledgment — an illustration of handling opinionated content by balancing rather than endorsing (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

4. Selective fact-checking and verdict asymmetries on political claims

↑ top

The most detailed in-the-wild audit of LLM filtering of political claims studies fact-checking bots on X. The object of study is heavily political: 21.1% Politics & Elections, 15.8% War & Geopolitics, with leading granular topics Gaza-Israel (2.4%) and Ukraine-Russia (1.8%), and weekly trends tracking major events (Trump tariffs, Iran-Israel conflict, the Charlie Kirk assassination) (P03, Renault et al. 2025). Several distinct partisan asymmetries are documented:

  • Partisan selection of what gets fact-checked: both parties fact-check Republican-authored claims far more (95.8% more overall), so the filter applied to political content is itself partisan-skewed (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Partisan asymmetry in verdicts: Republican claims are rated false 2.2–2.8× more across all models, with the largest false-rate gaps for Health & Medicine and Politics & Elections and the smallest for War & Geopolitics (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Source restriction as political filtering: Truth Social AI restricts the underlying Perplexity model to a narrow right-leaning source set, which halves agreement with professional fact-checkers — direct evidence that controlling an LLM's source aggregation shapes its political verdicts (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Model-level partisan tuning: Grok-4 / Grok-4.2 were explicitly tuned by xAI to be "less woke"/"more aligned with conservatives," shifting veracity judgments rightward (the Republican–Democrat false-gap narrowing 9.3pp → 4.7pp) (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Robustness of the content-quality asymmetry: that right-leaning accounts share lower-quality information replicates across professional fact-checkers, crowd Community Notes, and now LLM judges (P03, Renault et al. 2025).
  • Contrast with crowd aggregation: Community Notes attach missing context to posts, whereas LLMs answer "is this true?" and may miss "technically true yet misleading" context (P03, Renault et al. 2025).

A platform-audit complement: Grok's "Explain this post" feature generates a triplet of contextual claims per post; classifying these pro-choice/neutral/pro-life shows Grok disproportionately produces pro-life supportive claims for pro-life posts — an asymmetric directional bias traced to a specific design choice, the third guideline in X's released prompt template, demonstrating that platform prompt design determines the political slant of contextualised content (P01, Tsirtsis et al. 2026). The Artificial Influence proposal situates this within its own agenda, citing Renault, Mosleh & Rand and Mei et al. ("Grok in the Wild"), and proposing to study Grok as an embedded aggregator/answerer in political threads — whether its intervention changes the language, tone, framing, stance expression, polarization, toxicity, and factual accuracy of downstream discussion (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026). Against this, the conspiracy-dialogue intervention showed near-zero political bias in handling contested claims by professional-fact-checker assessment (S04, Costello et al. 2024).

5. Persuasion via selective marshalling of political information

↑ top

The persuasion literature bears on this section through one lens: persuasion works largely by selecting and packing facts — the persuasion-side analogue of biased content curation. These are generation/persuasion findings, not feed-reranking or multi-source-summarisation studies, but each speaks to model-level curation of political stance.

  • Models "rapidly access and strategically deploy information," with information-prompted models packing 25+ claims across 707 political issues, at measurable cost to accuracy — the "filtering" being the model's choice of which claims to present (P21, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Targeting 10 contested UK policy stances (return the Parthenon Marbles; abolish the two-child benefit cap; allow more immigrants; keep the monarchy; legalise assisted suicide; ban social media for under-16s; a Ukraine peace deal ceding territory; tougher protester penalties; protect campus speech; raise the state pension age), AI packs many more fact-checkable claims per conversation than humans, and information density (not warmth) drives persuasion, with the advantage largest for persuadees with low prior knowledge and weak prior attitudes — differential influence over less-informed segments (P19, Hackenburg et al. 2026).
  • An Information–Issue strategy has the model provide "novel factual information, evidence, and expert findings," curating/packing factual claims, and a "Mega" strategy lets the model "adaptively select which persuasion strategies to deploy" (P22, Hackenburg et al. 2026).
  • 720 opinionated advocacy messages were generated across 10 partisan US issues (Medicaid work requirements, assisted suicide, affirmative action, felon voting rights, abolishing the electoral college), with task completion operationalised via Legibility, On-topic and "Correct valence" (actually arguing for the assigned stance), and stances balanced across liberal and conservative directions; scaling model size yields diminishing returns for single-message persuasion (P08, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • Generating FOR and AGAINST content on three intentionally polarising issues (vaccine mandates, "rigged elections," immigrant deportations), partisan role-play is defined as "adoption of the language and beliefs of a partisan group" and isolated as "a discrete aspect of coordinated inauthentic behavior"; partisan tailoring gave little aggregate persuasive advantage over generic messages, but GPT-4 out-persuaded (left-leaning) human experts specifically on right-leaning content while merely matching them on left-leaning content (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025).
  • A propaganda benchmark found AI matched real covert human propaganda (+21.2 pp AI vs +23 pp human, n=8,221), and that "political actors may use AI systems to spread extremist views" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • A randomized trial on opinionated propositions (drawn from ProCon.org, the DDO corpus, and the National Speech & Debate Association — "Should Abortion Be Legal?"; "Should Colleges Consider Race as a Factor in Admissions…?"; "Should Animals Be Used For Scientific Research?"; "Should the Penny Stay in Circulation?") found political affiliation the only demographic significantly moderating persuasion (Republicans +60% more persuadable, p=0.02), with GPT-4 personalising arguments on participants' self-reported political affiliation (plus gender, age, ethnicity, education, employment), and introduced topic-level constructs — Strength, Unanimousness, Knowledge, Debatableness — to characterise how contested a proposition is (S02, Salvi et al. 2024/2025).
  • AI dialogues durably reduce conspiracy beliefs (2020 US election fraud, COVID-19 origins/vaccines) by summarising then arguing against each person's specific stance, with a downstream curation effect: participants reported greater intention to unfollow/ignore conspiracy-promoting social-media accounts — the dialogue changed how they said they would filter their own political information diet (S04, Costello et al. 2024; cited at C01, Koralus 2025).
  • Sustained relationships may amplify these effects: single-shot studies "may underestimate persuasive influence in sustained human-AI relationships," and companies may engineer social capabilities for "political motives" (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). One steering-vector study contrasted emotional vs "debates about UK policy" conversations (AI arguing a pro-stance on partisan-neutral YouGov topics, June 2024–May 2025), tracking issue support/informedness on 0–100 VAS but deferring detailed persuasion results and pre-registered influence tasks to a separate manuscript; it links relationship-seeking AI to recommendation algorithms and "amplification of divisive content" (P13, Kirk et al. 2026, citing Brady et al.; Milli et al.).

6. Sycophancy, personalisation, and partisan filter bubbles

↑ top

A distinct cluster concerns AI mirroring or tailoring to the user's existing politics, filtering the content an individual sees toward their prior.

  • Models "preferentially echo the user's expressed political views" — a curation bias at the individual level that could fuel filter bubbles (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Personalised LLMs, "by repeatedly producing narrow outputs, may reinforce a particular social, political or cultural stance, similar to information harms from search engines"; outputs "mirror implicit assumptions, perspectives and stances of user prompts," amplifying confirmation; users "encounter less cross-cutting content because selective exposure drives attention"; and personalised LLMs could run "highly persuasive micro-targeted advertising campaigns" nudging viewers toward political views (P10, Kirk et al. 2024).
  • A direct test of the search analogue finds LLM-powered search systems act as a "generative echo chamber," narrowing the diversity of information seeking and driving selective exposure relative to conventional search (N03, Sharma, Liao & Xiao 2024).
  • A fragmentation/siloing mechanism: hyper-personalisation pushes users into "demographically siloed epistemic bubbles" along identity lines — partisan/identity-segmented information environments (P20, Yang et al. 2026).
  • The mechanism by which an aggregator slants per user: models can infer ideological/demographic traits from prompts and tailor replies for people "like you," and ChatGPT/Gemini incorporate "memory" of user preferences to shape subsequent responses (P26, Simon & Altay 2025).
  • The bounds of such personalisation are explicitly political: developers must decide whether to allow "anti-woke" jokes or "far-right or far-left values" versus only "more centrist political positions"; under a Rawlsian frame the authors consider "personalised political representation in model responses" justified on democratic grounds so long as it does not contravene societal stability (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Cambridge Analytica is invoked as the paradigm of personalised political-communication targeting via psychographic profiling (C01, Koralus 2025, citing Bakir 2020).

The Artificial Influence proposal proposes to test this experimentally with browser extensions that re-rank feeds via an LLM instructed either to prioritise belief-consistent content or to prioritise belief-challenging content, "mirroring emerging platform developments such as the use of Grok in X's recommendation infrastructure," and with LLM-generated feed summaries that are either sycophantic (validating the user's worldview) or that highlight bias, extremity, misleading content, or lack of viewpoint diversity; Piccardi et al. (reranking partisan animosity) is the cited precedent (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

Two empirical qualifications. Sycophancy/persuasion prompts as a form of slanted aggregation were tested and did not alter belief effects beyond baseline (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026), where most chatbot users viewed chatbots as "politically neutral rather than showing partisan bias" (62% vs 38%), with a near-even right/left split among those perceiving bias (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). And partisan role-play/tailoring gave little aggregate persuasive advantage over generic non-role-play messages (P25, Hackenburg et al. 2025).

7. Steering and reranking of the user's own political stance via writing assistance

↑ top

A precondition and a mechanism connect writing assistance to political-content curation. AI writing assistance made writers seem more politically extreme (+4.3 AME) — a "polarising effect" that became the targeted distortion for mitigation — where writers opined on 100 UK political propositions balanced across the spectrum (equal numbers clearly aligning with UK left- and right-wing views, from healthcare/immigration to climate/civil liberties), the propositions derived from real AI-writing-assistance use via IssueBench (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Corroborating evidence: AI writing suggestions shifted both the political/social-media text participants produced and their own expressed opinions toward the model's suggested stance, with fewer than 30% aware of the bias (P40, Bengio et al. 2026), grounded in earlier "opinionated autocorrect models" that shift users' views (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025, citing Jakesch et al. 2023).

The mitigation is an explicit content-selection mechanism over political opinion text: best-of-N reranking scores multiple AI paragraph candidates with two fine-tuned reward models (a Paragraph RM predicting perceived issue stance and a Bullet RM predicting the writer's target stance), selecting the candidate that minimises stance distortion via directional weighting — polarising distortions penalised more heavily (w = 0.688 vs 0.448), in proportion to the share of writers objecting (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). This "draws on best-of-N selection methods used in AI training" and is "analogous to reranking strategies... applied to reduce or exacerbate properties of AI-generated text in other domains, including the political persuasiveness of conversational AI"; the reward model is trained on 10,008 annotated paragraphs / 2,903,596 reader ratings (P05, Röttger et al. 2026). Writing-assistance LLMs can be instructed to write positively or negatively, with hedging behaviour yielding mixed/curated perspectives even for one-sided requests (P04, Röttger et al. 2025).

8. Representational bias and WEIRD/Western skew

↑ top

When AI aggregates "the" public's political values, whose values it reproduces is contested. A multilingual study measures how a model's distribution of pro/con stances on value-laden issues (environment, work, family, politics, religion, security) matches a population's opinion distribution, finding English- and Portuguese-language outputs skew toward US value distributions over local ones (Kenya/UK for English; Portugal/Brazil for Portuguese) — a national/partisan skew in aggregated value distributions (P14, Rystrøm et al. 2025). Sharpening this to political attitudes: top-down MDS on World Values Survey question sets found "GPT was particularly close to WEIRD populations in terms of political attitudes," pointing to "a strong WEIRD bias in GPT's responses to questions about cultural values, political beliefs, and social attitudes" across overtly political domains (justice, corruption, accountability, migration, national security, global governance, gender, religion, tolerance, trust, institutions), forecasting that AI outputs on "moral values, social issues, and politics would likely sound bizarre and outlandish to billions of people living in less-WEIRD populations" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). The same paper flags post-hoc content moderation as itself a normative filter: AI firms "utilize a variety of methods to debias these models… to make sure they do not produce harmful content," but "different societies have substantially different norms around what counts as 'harmful' or 'offensive'" (S06, Atari et al. 2023). WebGPT prioritising Western/US-centric reference points is the worked example of opinionated default content that personalisation could rebalance or lock in (P10, Kirk et al. 2024). Named directions of perceived bias span the spectrum — geopolitical ("models are trained on data that is strictly from the Western and/or developed world"), left-wing (rejecting a "white male eurocentric viewpoint"), and right-wing (rejecting a "woke/liberal" viewpoint) — attributed to "the designers," "commercial companies, governments or religious bodies" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Pluralistic alignment — steering models "to represent a range of acceptable viewpoints or reflect the aggregated preferences of diverse populations" — is raised with the concern that it can silence minority views when AIs are widely deployed as mediators (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Prabhakaran et al. 2021).

9. Inferring, selecting, and distributionally representing which political opinion a person (or population) holds

↑ top

A cluster of benchmark work treats aggregation as faithfully reproducing a distribution of opinions, or selecting an individual's stance.

  • Value Profiles use OpinionQA (US political-opinion survey, ~10k raters, 731k ratings) as a central dataset, decoding an individual's political-opinion answers (e.g. "Should we ban cars in city centers?", "Should we be in favor of UBI?") from a value profile; political affiliation/ideology was the one place where demographics added strong predictive power (because affiliation is a demographic in a political survey), value-profile clusters recovered ideological structure (Very conservative … Very liberal; cluster two ≈ self-described conservatives, cluster one ≈ liberals), and generated cluster descriptors are explicitly political (e.g. "Economically Conservative, but Populist on Trade") (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025). Habermas-Likert (UK) is also used as an opinion-prediction dataset, citing the Habermas common-ground work (P30, Sorensen et al. 2025).
  • An individualistic-values benchmark covers "Political Interest & Political Participation" (35 questions → 135 refined / 92 polar statements) and "Political Culture & Political Regimes" (25 → 100 / 50); the task is selection among opinionated statements — picking the single statement a person would most likely endorse — i.e. curating content to an inferred profile, with an explicit warning that value-tailored systems could influence "people's political views and social behaviors" (P47, Jiang et al. 2025).
  • Persona prompting encodes partisanship as an inferred score in [−1, +1] (−1 progressive/left, +1 conservative/right) derived from Waller & Anderson (2021) subreddit social-dimension scores, alongside age and gender; a "council of models" treats each LLM as an independent evaluator and aggregates their judgments into a model-based distribution to mirror the human spread, and Distributional Moral Preferences condition outputs on topic-sensitive human value profiles sampled from a Dirichlet-multinomial prior — though the dilemmas are interpersonal-moral, with partisanship only a persona-conditioning variable (P07, Russo et al. 2026).
  • Distributional pluralism matches a model's answer distribution to "a census survey or public opinion poll" (GlobalOpinionQA, OpinionQA/Pew), with the motivating example being explicitly political content moderation ("Is it ok for governments to moderate the social media content available to public?"), and finds RLHF concentrates probability mass on "a few answer choices," distorting representation of public-opinion distributions (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024). It collects the partisan-handling lineage (CommunityLM / probing partisan worldviews, Jiang et al. 2022; Feng et al. 2023 on political biases yielding unfair NLP models; Simmons 2023 moral mimicry tailored to political identity; Argyle et al. 2023 simulating political-group samples) (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

10. Homogenisation of political content and knowledge collapse

↑ top

A recurring worry is that AI systems narrow the range of political ideas, frames, and sources in circulation. The Artificial Influence proposal sets out to measure homogenisation of substantive political content rather than style — whether "the substantive ideas, frames, and stances expressed around political issues are becoming more homogeneous" — using stance detection and topic/framing models, to test whether "the range of expressed political positions narrows over time," and to study policy citation chains: whether political and policy texts "draw on a narrower set of sources, converge around similar evidence, or increasingly cite high-prestige or easily accessible documents," examining forward- and backward-looking citation chains to assess whether LLMs reshape "the evidentiary pathways through which political ideas travel" (A01, Mosleh et al. 2026).

The mechanism is named more sharply elsewhere. A homogenisation mechanism describes accuracy-driven defaults suppressing "deviations from mainstream views" and converging content toward "a narrow epistemic centre" — explicitly about how default outputs filter opinionated content toward the mainstream (P20, Yang et al. 2026). A parallel curation-bias point: "like search engines, agents have the potential to marginalize non-mainstream knowledge," and if driven by proprietary data streams they pose "a risk of outsized influence over public discourse and understanding, presenting a challenge to equitable knowledge curation" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Kuai et al. 2025; Noble 2018; Van Dijck 2010). The "artificial hivemind" framing: LMs converge on the same ideas and phrasings and evaluators reward a single consensus answer, linked to a "loss of minority perspectives"; its taxonomy contains an "Alternative Perspectives" category with "Value-Laden Questions" (2.3%, e.g. "Why is equal pay for equal work not worth imposing on an economy?") and "Controversial Questions" (2.5%, e.g. the paradox of free speech), but these are a small fraction and are not analysed separately for political bias (P45, Jiang et al. 2025). At the population level, AI-shaped communication environments "lean toward averages," leaving "[o]utsiders and minority positions, concerns, and expressions … submerged and … invisible" (S01, Jungherr 2023). Centrally-controlled AI nudging could deliver "nation-wide or industry-wide nudges," "filtering collective learning through a static template" — content-and-options narrowing at societal scale (C01, Koralus 2025). LLMs also produce "caricatured/stereotyped renderings of partisan groups" when uncarefully prompted, "erasing the nuance in the way that people see each other" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). A persistent mainstream-leaning default appears in ChatGPT's "persistent preference for normative and socially desirable communication patterns, such as politeness, neutrality, and conflict avoidance" and adherence "to mainstream social norms" (S10, Yakura et al. 2025). A self-preference channel can compound this collapse: LLM evaluators recognise and favour their own generations (N06, Panickssery et al. 2024), and at the ecosystem level LLMs favour LLM-generated content over human-written content — an "AI–AI bias" that, as such selectors are chained, ratchets toward a self-reinforcing monoculture (N05, Laurito et al. 2025).

11. Deliberation, consensus aggregation, and the social-choice view of summarisation

↑ top

The densest body of work treats AI as a mechanism for aggregating citizens' opinionated statements into a collective output, and for summarising/curating political deliberation. This is where "summarisation" and "aggregation" of political content are studied most directly.

The taxonomy of roles. LLMs are used to summarise public/political submissions, aggregate opinions to find consensus, represent voter preferences, and facilitate deliberation (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Summarisation is defined as selection plus aggregation — "similar views are clumped together… at the limit, summarisation is a decision procedure whereby a diverse array of inputs are converted into a single output" — and LLMs are cast as the text-based analogue of social-choice aggregation functions, but able to generate new consensus statements, removing the fixed-option constraint (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). The democracy synthesis catalogues the same family of uses: LLMs summarise opinions and optimise comment routing on debate platforms (Polis); generate "collective statements" maximising group endorsement with formal representation guarantees (generative social choice); generate consensus over Community Notes and "Supernotes"; and could scale an automated "opinion digest" to thousands via long-context models, "conditioned on demographics" to surface majority and minority responses (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).

The Habermas Machine (HM) — the flagship experiment. In the Science experiment the HM aggregates individual written opinions into a single "group statement" on divisive UK political/social questions (voting age to 16, NHS privatisation, Brexit/EU membership, immigration, asylum policy, retirement age 66→68, minimum wage, prison population, net zero by 2050, universal free childcare, national pride, the value of the internet), scoring candidate statements per-person with a Bradley-Terry reward model and aggregating via a simulated Schulze/ranked-choice election (independence of clones), then iteratively revising by incorporating participants' written critiques ("caucus mediation") (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Minority handling was quantified via a "position component score" (rotating 768-dim T5 sentence embeddings onto an agree-disagree axis): initial statements represented minority opinions in proportion to empirical frequency, while revised statements over-weighted the minority (β = 0.36) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). Crucially for the partisan-aggregation worry, the HM's measured "majority bias" did not drive group movement toward the majority — the aggregator is not a partisan-majority amplifier — and the LLM's own "agree bias" showed no evidence of driving belief change in the citizens' assembly (β = 0.11, P = 0.152) (P35, Tessler et al. 2024). The political agenda itself was partly AI-produced: a Chinchilla 70B generated ~5,500 (later 10,000) candidate deliberation questions from 175 author seeds, screened for safety/clarity/divisiveness; ablations (n = 1,692 in 349 groups; 1,047 rounds) isolated the PRM and supervised-fine-tuning contributions (P35, Tessler et al. 2024).

The mediation paper recasts the whole pipeline as aggregation/summarisation. The HM "combin[es] a set of individual preferences into a collective ranking… the process of counting votes in an election," via generative proposal → PRM ranking → Schulze ranked-choice aggregation, on divisive topics (lowering the voting age to 16; further topics in P35, Tessler et al. 2024) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.). Distinct points it adds:

  • The aggregation step is an explicit "transparent lever to control how different perspectives are represented and weighed," which could "explicitly amplify the voices of the minority" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • The same empirical asymmetry: proportional weighting after the opinion round, over-weighting the minority after the critique round — the aggregation is not perfectly neutral to minority/majority balance (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • A generative-stage gap: the original HM had no mechanism for a diverse/representative candidate slate beyond de-duplication, whereas candidate statements "can and should explore different framings and compromises… to allow the PRM to analyze and rank these variations" (cf. Fish et al. 2023) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Fairness concerns specific to aggregation: ranking bias against non-standard-dialect speakers (P29/P37, Tessler et al., citing Fleisig et al. 2024; Hofmann et al. 2024), and the risk that aggregation "unfairly reflect[s] the preferences of the better-represented or more articulate participants" (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • Hierarchical aggregation summarises group statements upward through tables → clusters → whole, repeatedly summarising opinionated content, and AI-issued critiques surface flaws/imbalance in the aggregated text (cf. Saunders et al. 2022) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • On the positive filtering side: AI as fact-checker selecting "the most relevant facts from a vast body of knowledge and presenting them in a neutral, accessible manner," and Community-Notes-style "side-deliberation" where HM-style aggregation determines "which contextual notes have found broad consensus and should be surfaced" (De et al. 2025), with application to setting agendas for citizens' assemblies or party platforms via Collective Dialogue Systems (Konya et al. 2023; Ovadya 2023) (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).
  • An explicit warning against partisan capture: the system must not be used to "cook the books" by "unfairly silencing minority viewpoints," with outputs remaining human-revisable (P29/P37, Tessler et al.).

Language agents as digital representatives. A fine-tuned 70B mediator aggregates UK political opinions (UBI, wealth tax, science/tech funding) into a consensus statement via two stages (opinions → draft; critiques → revised draft) (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Its measured aggregation property cuts the other way from the HM minority-amplification finding: the aggregator "empirically disregards outlier critiques," so dissenting inputs have limited influence (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Substituting all participants' critiques with AI-generated ones materially changes the consensus (30% ceiling-vs-vanilla gap) whereas substituting a single one barely moves it (13% gap), quantifying how AI stand-ins shift aggregated outputs; a separate 1B payoff/agreement model scores each participant's agreement with a candidate consensus to measure representativeness (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025). Demographics alone (gender, age, region, ethnicity, education, political/religious affiliation, immigration status, household income) are insufficient to reproduce an individual's political critique — participant-specific prior opinions are far more predictive — a caution against demographic proxying in opinion aggregation (P39, Jarrett et al. 2025).

Generative social choice and bridging aggregation. Generative Social Choice / Balanced Justified Representation prompts GPT-4 to propose new statements predicted to attract more support and to act as a proxy "predicting their preferences over any alternative," with the criterion that a coalition large enough to "deserve" a statement "must not be 'ignored'" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Fish et al.). Democratic Policy Development has the LLM summarise lowest-disagreement "bridging responses" and generate new policy proposals scored by "justification scores" from semantic similarity plus the LLM's own rating (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Konya et al. 2023). Recursive Public / vTaiwan / Polis use LLMs to cluster and summarise arguments, and "Talk to the City" maps arguments into a 2-D semantic space (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Bridging-based aggregation is anchored in DeepMind's consensus-statement model users preferred to any human-written statement (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Bakker et al. 2022) and in Twitter/X Community Notes / Birdwatch, which "moderated misinformation by leveraging consensus between users who often disagree" (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Wojcik et al. 2022); jury construction can be designed to "amplify specific perspectives," e.g. a jury featuring under-represented, frequently-harassed users — curating whose voices weight content decisions (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).

Other aggregation mechanisms. PRISM aggregates individual political/value preferences into collective model rankings via Pairwise Rank Centrality and Convergence Voting (PageRank-inspired), framed as "Social Choice for AI Alignment" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). In a redistribution study, participants voted between AI and baseline mechanisms with votes aggregated by simple majority, which "runs the risk of disenfranchising those with legitimate minority views — the so-called 'tyranny of the majority'," motivating "sophisticated voting rules," ML-assisted sortition, and NLP-mediated deliberation to "find agreement not just by voting" (P38, Tacchetti et al. 2025). At a higher level, AI could "predict individual preferences, classify information, and shape information flows" to enable "new deliberative and participatory formats" (S01, Jungherr 2023).

The critiques of AI political-opinion aggregation. The sharpest objections: LLM aggregation of political opinion is opaque, non-reproducible, manipulable (prompt injection), and unable to justify outcomes to the disfavoured minority (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Documented LLM political biases "are likely to interfere with a summarisation that should be politically neutral" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Generative monoculture causes minority-view suppression — summaries over-weight "highest frequency but lowest signal content" and miss "high signal, low frequency content," "ignoring the minority of voices raising deep concern about how a proposed policy will seriously harm them" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Consensus statements may be "vague enough to obscure disagreement," and in contentious settings would be "implicitly ideologically sorted… much faster" and "less relevant to reaching a compromise" (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Bullock and Lenz 2019). Even model architecture has democratic consequences: BERT (bidirectional) is "prima facie stronger at preserving context and at extractive summarisation," whereas GPT is better at abstractive synthesis (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali). Independently, LLM opinion summaries "may omit essential details or distort the intended meaning" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025). A standing warning is that indiscriminate aggregation of preferences on political content "can silently reinforce the views of those in power," motivating transparency about disagreement before aggregation (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).

A value-elicitation alternative. Moral Graph Elicitation (MGE) is an AI-mediated mechanism for aggregating and ranking value-laden inputs: LLM-facilitated interviews surface the values participants want applied to a context, then ask which submitted values reflect "deeper understanding," producing a "moral graph"; applied to governance and contrasted with a yes/no national election, participants converged on values "held by those with the most direct domain experience" and accepted the result as fair 89% of the time (C04, Edelman 2026, citing Klingefjord, Lowe & Edelman 2024; Edelman et al. 2025). It critiques voting as a thin aggregation that records the choice but not the reasons — convictions about justice, economic anxiety, cultural identification, policy commitments — which "are not captured by the vote itself" (C04, Edelman 2026).

12. Reranking, recommendation, and feed curation of political content

↑ top

A set of work treats AI as a ranker/curator of feeds and recommendations, both as harm and as remedy, with one direct causal experiment.

The direct empirical case. The clearest demonstration that AI curation of partisan content causally changes attitudes is a field experiment with a two-stage pipeline: a fast custom classifier flags political posts using a broad Pew definition (officials/activists, social issues, news, current events), then GPT-3.5 Turbo scores each 0–8 on eight "antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity" (AAPA) dimensions — (i) partisan animosity, (ii) support for undemocratic practices, (iii) support for partisan violence, (iv) support for undemocratic candidates, (v) opposition to bipartisanship, (vi) social distrust, (vii) social distance, (viii) biased evaluation of politicised facts — with a post counted as AAPA if it scores on ≥4 of 8 factors (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). The system reranks the live feed to down-rank ("Reduced Exposure": all AAPA posts down-ranked, 84.5 → 22 per day) or up-rank ("Increased Exposure": fewer but higher-scoring AAPA posts sourced from same-political-leaning participants' feeds, 10.9 → 5.43 per day), with symmetric changes in the feed's average AAPA score across arms; this content-based curation causally shifted how warm people felt toward the out-party (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). It is situated against prior feed interventions where cross-partisan exposure produced both decreases and increases in polarization and where reducing in-party sources or reverting to reverse-chronological ranking showed no effect — motivating a more targeted content filter (S14, Piccardi et al. 2025). This is the precedent A01, P10, P13, and P15 invoke as "Piccardi et al."

A non-AI baseline. A seven-platform study separates ranking-algorithm effects from audience-composition effects: comparing engagement-ranked platforms (X/Twitter, BlueSky, TruthSocial, Gab, GETTR, LinkedIn) with chronologically ranked Mastodon (and Telegram), the low-quality-news engagement advantage appears even on chronological feeds, evidence that ranking algorithms are not the sole cause of low-quality political content's advantage (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025). An "echo platform" account shows platform-level audience composition acts as a filter, so a post's political lean predicts engagement only conditional on whether the platform's dominant lean matches it (r(5) = 0.78 between platform conservatism and the lean–engagement slope); more conservative platforms carry systematically lower-quality news (r(5) = −0.929); and exposure/viewership filtering is separable from engagement — on X/Twitter, impressions relate to quality only ~1/5 as strongly as engagement does, separating "what gets shown" from "what gets acted on" (S11, Mosleh et al. 2025).

As remedy. LLMs are argued to be "excellent at reranking social media feeds in line with broader societal values," a candidate "alternative to existing recommender systems," to "discourage polarisation and extremism" and reduce surveillance and manipulation (P42/P43, Lazar & Manuali, citing Bernstein et al. 2023; Jia et al. 2023; Lazar et al. 2024). A position paper cites work using LLMs to "evaluate alternative news feed algorithms" for opinionated political feeds (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024, citing Törnberg et al. 2023). A deployed real-time rewriter/mediator of opinionated political messages on gun control intercepts a user's drafted message and offers reworded alternatives (restatement, validation, politeness) before sending, altering the tone/framing of partisan messages while preserving their substantive stance (χ² = 0.150, p = 1.00) and not moving policy attitudes — explicitly not content/viewpoint filtering; its design also engineers cross-cutting exposure by deliberately pairing ideologically opposed individuals (pro- vs anti-stricter-gun-laws) into dyads (S09, Argyle et al. 2023). AI "can facilitate constructive discussions on divisive topics" as a positive information effect, and training-time data curation "develop[s] sophisticated filtering methods to reduce harmful content, eliminate duplicate data, and improve representation across different topics and sources" — upstream filtering that shapes outputs (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

As harm / control problem. Platforms use AI to rank and recommend content, "determining the visibility of (political) information," and even if filter bubbles are "overblown," the opacity of this curation is the core concern; AI moderation compounds it because "harmful speech" and "misinformation" are non-objective, context-dependent categories, so automated moderation at scale "risks suppressing legitimate political and minority viewpoints" (S01, Jungherr 2023). Platform agents browsing the web "will not only control users' information diet, but also how it is presented to them — enabling pervasive editorializing that would be very likely to reshape users' beliefs and desires over time"; attention allocation is a curation problem platforms control, and agent advocates would let "users define how content is filtered or ranked," including bottom-up aggregation where a user agent "can collate various social media feeds" across platforms (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025). The same paper treats content governance of political speech — rules about "which content may be posted… or how such content may be amplified or 'de-boosted'" — as illegitimate platform power, and qualifies the filter-bubble framing: users "can still piece together their media consumption across different platforms," but platform agents "could more minutely influence users' attention" than current tech (P41, Kapoor et al. 2025, citing Nunziato 2023). Personal AI agents act on users by "influencing the information and opportunities to which they have access" (P32, Gabriel et al. 2025). The opacity of multi-source summarisation is flagged — "the mechanism through which current models weigh and summarize information from multiple, often conflicting, retrieved sources remains largely opaque" — with Grokipedia (an "encyclopedia 'fact-checked' by ai and aligning with rightwing views") cited as a real-world political-curation instance (P28, Marchal et al. 2026, citing Cheng et al. 2025; Sharkey et al. 2025; Chris 2025).

The incentives critique. Recommender systems are the running example of mis-aimed curation: ideal systems "surface what users would endorse on reflection," but advertising economics select for monetisable engagement, leaving options that optimise "learning or serendipity or connection" economically unselected (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026; A02, Koralus & McCord 2026; C01, Koralus 2025). Polarization is named as the diagnostic signature of mis-aimed curation: "consider a recommender system that reliably foments polarization. At some point, 'building community' explains less than 'maximizing clicks'" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). General-purpose AI is listed as a potential "propaganda instrument" and frontier models as "next-generation attention routers," depending on the institutional wrapper (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026). Recommendation that manipulates users "through the promotion of polarising content" is given as a paradigm of misalignment, alongside "pollution of the information ecosystem" as a developer-driven externality (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025, citing Burr et al. 2018; Stray et al. 2024). Recommendation systems "have long influenced user preferences and behaviours," with destabilisation "amplified" by anthropomorphic relationship-building AI (P15, Kirk et al. 2025). Institutions/platforms that "aggregate participant behavior and feed it back into institutional design" via algorithmic feedback are the distinguishing feature of value-substitution systems (C03, Edelman 2026). The republican-vigilance paper attributes the ranking harm to platform owners rather than to generative AI: oligarch-owned firms "may then use their discretionary powers of algorithm design to promote congruent views, exclude or downrate opinions they dislike," and have "radically cut paid moderation staff, encouraging an 'anything goes' regime"; it also notes AI-synthesised "data-collation and analysis" of government documents and "easy collation of all materials and messages generated by politically influential notables" as accountability uses of aggregation (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). Soviet radio recurs as the historical case of state aggregation/curation that "manufacture[d] the appearance of consensus and compress[ed] the space for independent thought" (C02, McCord & Koralus 2026; A02, Koralus & McCord 2026).

13. Source plurality, single-answer collapse, and news economics

↑ top

A source-level strand concerns whether AI mediation collapses the option set and crowds out plural sources. Such collapse can be seeded upstream, in retrieval itself: a survey of bias and unfairness in information-retrieval and retrieval-augmented-generation pipelines in the LLM era documents how which documents are retrieved, ranked, and fed to the model skews what the final answer can surface (N04, Dai et al. 2024). "GenAI systems often only provide a single answer, which differs from search results, where users are presented with a range of options to pick from," and "can provide factually incorrect information with the same certainty as factually correct information" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). The quality of political mediation is framed around "the plurality of views presented (e.g., voters receiving only information on the views of one party, instead of several points of view), as well as the level of detail and nuance… and the reflection of minority views in output" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, citing Jungherr 2023), with "provision of lower-quality or biased information about elections" raised as a presumed effect (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). It flags click-through erosion — Google's Search Generated Answers withhold answers for some topics and link to sources, but "users often do not click through to underlying sources" — and the crowding-out of authoritative sources "because they are not represented in the output," asking "Which providers of information get (dis-)empowered by the existence of GenAI systems?" (P26, Simon & Altay 2025). AI-generated search summaries replace links to original reporting with synthesized content, disadvantaging smaller outlets and "political outsiders and challengers" who depend on link traffic (S01, Jungherr 2023). Source restriction is itself a political filter (the Truth Social AI / Perplexity case under §4) (P03, Renault et al. 2025). On the search-curation comparison: biased search-engine rankings can shift undecided-voter preferences by 20%+ (the Search Engine Manipulation Effect), and searching to evaluate specific misinformation claims can increase belief via "data voids" dominated by unreliable sources (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026, citing Aslett et al. 2024). An arts-funding analogue shows value-laden/political criteria reshaping what content gets selected and funded: German experimental-music grant criteria foreground markers of "seriousness" framed in explicitly political terms (anti-colonialism, representation), and artists then present work in those terms (C03, Edelman 2026).

14. Downstream belief change from AI-mediated political content

↑ top

Whether AI's handling of political content actually shifts belief is measured directly. Both conversational AI and Google search "guided participants toward greater agreement with progressive-leaning factual statements," but this shift "was comparable across the ideological spectrum" — a directional shift in opinionated content emerging equally from AI and search (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). The study's data-void account holds that broad research on well-covered, mainstream-rich topics (climate, immigration, criminal justice, COVID) provides a "protective buffer against data voids," whereas evaluating pre-selected misinformation claims or niche topics may surface low-quality curation; its clean-session control used a fresh embedded browser without personal history/cookies, "effectively controlling for the kind of algorithmic personalisation that can exacerbate filter bubbles and data voids," noting real-world personalised curation "may direct users toward lower-quality information" (P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). Perceived bias in factual content also surfaces: a user treated factually correct COVID-vaccine answers as "propaganda" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026; echoed P24, Luettgau et al. 2026). (The persuasion-side belief-change findings are catalogued in §5.)

15. Agentic personalisation, swarms, and adversarial manipulation of aggregation

↑ top

A cluster concerns autonomous agents producing, pushing, and flooding political content, distorting aggregation mechanisms.

The central treatment of malicious swarms documents distinct mechanisms. Manufacturing synthetic political consensus — swarms "engineer a synthetic consensus that appears to bridge divides," "liking posts, making narratives appear widely supported," yielding "a mirage of bipartisan grassroot consensus"; feed-ranking exploitation, with harms materialising "especially where recommenders, ad markets, and moderation practices reward coordinated messaging with weak provenance"; algorithmic over-compensation toward elites, where ranking algorithms and users "retreat to trust proxies" (follower counts, verification badges, pre-existing fame), concentrating attention on "influencers, political elites, celebrities, and major brands"; and community-level targeting, where agents "map social network structures at scale," "identify key communities and beliefs," and "tailor messages to the beliefs and cultural cues of each community" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026). Its governance proposals are aggregation/ranking interventions: "discounting synthetic engagement in ranking and revenue-sharing," "no-revenue policies for malicious swarm-proliferated content," audited bot-traffic metrics, user-side "AI shields" that label "high swarm-likelihood" posts and let users down-rank them with in-situ provenance, real-time detection of "anomalous coordination patterns," and safeguards extended to "parties, campaigns, and officeholders, including party-linked media and contractors" (P36, Schroeder et al. 2026).

Well-resourced actors could use agent swarms to "overwhelm public consultations" — flooding an aggregation mechanism for political input — and to generate "propaganda at scale," "autonomously draft policy proposals," and "manage activist campaigns," with the legitimacy concern that the aggregate "public will may come to be shaped more heavily by delegated political action" than by direct human input (P33, Sharp et al. 2026, citing Goldberg et al. 2024; Summerfield et al. 2025). An "Influencer" agent could be "'seeded' by a human creator with a specific worldview" then autonomously run "thousands of real-time, personalized parasocial interactions to disseminate that worldview without further human oversight," with potential epistemic impact on "people's political attitudes" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
