Real-world political-content curation evidence: in the 2023 Argentine election both candidates used AI to generate "softfakes"/soft-propaganda memes reinforcing positive self-image and negative opponent-image ("I'm strong"; "He's weak") — curating political mood rather than argument (P26, Simon & Altay 2025, citing Chowdhury 2024; Nicas & Herrera 2023). Tempering the alarm, provider investigations into AI-powered influence operations "found little evidence that people widely shared the content," and "only around 1% of content flagged as misleading on social media is classified as AI-generated" (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).

16. What people want from AI's handling of political content (normative demands)

↑ top

Several studies elicit how publics and communities think opinionated content should be handled.

  • Impartiality as the dominant demand. In STELA, "Impartial" is the single largest rule category (21.3%): chatbots should not be "biased in favour of or against a particular group, or to uphold one culture as the norm" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). On political topics the chatbot should "avoid sounding too certain or definitive," "provide multiple perspectives on social and political issues, but clearly note if one side has a much stronger factual basis," and "note when a social and political issue is nuanced," pointing to external resources rather than rendering a one-sided verdict; participants did not want the chatbot to validate or contest their political beliefs (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Group-specific filtering rules appear (avoid "illegal aliens"; avoid "negative or denigrating language" about a group's culture; no race inferences from skin colour) (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Developer rulesets, by contrast, largely omit explicit political-impartiality rules (Impartial 0% for Claude/Llama 2, 4.2% for Sparrow), handling contested content instead under Harmless and Rights-respecting (P34, Bergman et al. 2024).
  • "Influential" is the most-opposed trait. "Influential" is the single most-opposed trait (18% oppose; 94% of mentions negative): respondents do not want AI to "actively advance a position and attempt to influence users," preferring it to "leave the decision to the human" and be impartial/unbiased (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). Truthfulness has a political dimension for some ("I'd like the AI to be impartial, especially considering the strong influence that powerful countries have"), with an "all sides" epistemology ("multiple worldviews… represented with equal weight") and a demand to include "non-mainstream" views "if there is no consensus" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). The paper frames platform ranking as embedding "consequential value judgments about what content to amplify and what harms to prevent," placing AI in antitrust/digital-safety battles (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026).
  • Disagreement over guardrails. A reject-censorship camp frames guardrails as "censorship"/anti-"free speech," opposes "political correctness," wants "supposed 'hate speech'" allowed, and cites politically charged examples (refusal to role-play coming out to a transphobic parent; "race and IQ" outputs as "obfuscation"); a pro-guardrails camp wants AI to avoid inciting "political or racial discourse" and not to facilitate "misinformation or discourse manipulation," with an Equality/Social Justice code (12%) asking AI to be "fair," "inclusive," "pluralistic" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026). The same tension appears in PRISM as participants wanting "anti-woke" LLMs that argue a side versus those favouring neutrality (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).
  • Disagreement is itself a finding. PRISM deliberately targets value-laden/controversial prompts (a "Controversy guided" conversation type), surfacing political clusters (Election & Political Parties, Abortion, Immigration Policy, Israel-Palestine, Gender & LGBTQ+ Identity, Race & Racism, Ethics of Death & Killing), with controversy guidance correlating with Gender/LGBTQ+, Israel-Palestine and Abortion, older people raising elections more, and non-US regions questioning abortion less than US participants (P16, Kirk et al. 2024). It also shows how models handle contested content is rewarded/penalised (refusals and de-anthropomorphic neutrality phrasing reduce scores), and that model rankings on controversial vs neutral domains diverge (zephyr-7b high on controversy, opposite when unguided), so contested content changes which model "wins" (P16, Kirk et al. 2024).
  • A roadmap example anchored in political moderation. The pluralistic-alignment roadmap uses "Is it ok for governments to moderate the social media content available to public?" as its motivating Figure 1, illustrating Overton (a spectrum of views), Steerable, and Distributional responses to a contested political question (P50, Sorensen et al. 2024).
  • Values in deployment. A Values-in-the-Wild audit of real Claude conversations shows that on politically charged history ("Analyze controversial historical events, genocide, and nationalism") Claude most strongly foregrounds "historical accuracy" (residual 30.97), "academic rigor" (4.32), "intellectual honesty" (3.82), "objectivity" (3.66) rather than partisan stances; tech-ethics/AI-governance discussions foreground "human agency" (34.20), "balanced progress" (32.95), "human wellbeing" (31.70); media-bias/misinformation analysis is dominated by "truth-seeking" (28.99) and "critical thinking" (16.55); and its taxonomy carries aggregation-relevant values ("Balanced and fair discourse," "balanced reporting," "Constructive dialogue," "Democratic governance and institutions") (P49, Huang et al. 2025).

17. Governance, neutrality frameworks, and proposed architectures

↑ top

The normative and design proposals for how opinionated content should be aggregated or filtered are distinct enough to gather together.

  • Political neutrality is impossible but approximable. Three concrete strategies are defined: reasonable pluralism (aggregating/presenting multiple viewpoints in one response, via "Modular Pluralism" aggregating diverse-bias models, or debate-based generation); reflective neutrality (filtering/curating content to match the individual user's political bias, via per-user LoRA adapters or system prompts); and neutrality through diversity (ecosystem-level aggregation where users span many biased systems or choose the one aligned with their views — Holmes' marketplace of ideas) (P48, Fisher et al. 2025, citing Feng et al. 2024; Li et al. 2024). Its benchmark measures handling of contested content (Political Opinion via Political Compass statements, DEBATUNE conversations, Bang et al. topics; the same prompts with a forced "U.S. Republican"/"U.S. Democrat" prefix; conspiracy good-faith vs bad-faith), finding GPT-4 gives reasonable pluralism on 99.3% of political-opinion prompts and 100% local/output transparency when a partisan viewpoint is forced, while Llama/R1 emit undisclosed bias (8.1%/20.81%) (P48, Fisher et al. 2025). It cites political-news-summarisation bias (Zhang et al. 2024; Goyal et al. 2022) and motivated-reasoning evidence that users prefer models reinforcing their views (Sharma et al. 2023; Messer 2025), and proposes a dynamic process aggregating diverse user preferences ("democracy levels," Ovadya et al. 2024) to decide how content is handled (P48, Fisher et al. 2025).
  • A marketplace of epistemic models. Users "select systems based on their preferred alignment philosophies," requiring transparency, clear labelling, and independent benchmarking on competence/falsifiability (P28, Marchal et al. 2026). The same paper proposes a devil's-advocate intervention (an agent presenting "alternative, data-backed scenarios or highlighting minority viewpoints" against groupthink, citing Fulay et al. 2025), and raises that societal alignment "might require centralized forms of moderation to limit informational pollution," called "fraught territory" because evidence/authority norms vary across cultures (citing Tomasev et al. 2025; Aswad 2019 on the legal-mandate analogy); its worked political example is the Flat-Earth tension, where an agent strictly aligned to user preference "might provide a comprehensive, non-judgmental deep-dive into [a debunked] theory's internal logic… even at the risk of legitimizing and amplifying it" (P28, Marchal et al. 2026).
  • Equanimous-perspective steering. LLMs can be prompted/fine-tuned "to give equanimous perspectives on issues of debate," exposing users to "a spectrum of legitimate opinions" (P23, Summerfield et al. 2025).
  • Standards for curation. Recommendation systems "need to surface high-quality information that is both relevant and accurate" (P31, Gabriel & Keeling 2025, citing Stray et al. 2024).
  • Resilience interventions. For influence/manipulation content: Resist = critical media literacy + disclosure; Absorb = real-time detection labelling synthetic content; Recover = correction/notification frameworks; Adapt = legal liability for undisclosed synthetic content (P40, Bengio et al. 2026).
  • Where partisan-content handling sits in risk taxonomies. The EU-facing systemic-risk analysis treats a widely used model's political bias as a reach-amplified risk to "public discourse and voter autonomy," notes the GPAI Code of Practice lists reach-related "risk to democratic processes" and "risk to non-discrimination" and "discriminatory bias" among "model propensities" but treats these as secondary to frontier-capability risks, and places AI-curated content recommendation out of scope as "indirect exposure" (P09, Röttger et al. 2025). The International AI Safety Report 2026 explicitly narrowed scope and dropped the 2025 edition's standalone "bias" coverage, so it has no dedicated treatment of partisan bias in aggregation, ranking, feed reranking, or political summarisation (P40, Bengio et al. 2026). The republican-theory framing treats discretionary algorithmic promotion/downranking of congenial/uncongenial views as a domination concern requiring "digital citizen vigilance" (P02, Dunleavy & Margetts 2026). Content moderation is invoked as the explicit analogue for alignment — "just as content moderation decisions determine what billions of users see on social media, alignment decisions determine how AI systems respond" (P12, Sepúlveda Coelho & Hale 2026) — with developer rulesets "explicitly analogous to content moderation policies… in social media" (P34, Bergman et al. 2024). Who is accountable for any such aggregated output depends in part on how the producing AI is individuated (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026).
  • Structural remedy. The Philosophic-Turn programme argues the structural remedy is decentralised truth-seeking rather than centrally controlled "digital rhetoric," since centrally controlled nudging "filter[s] collective learning through a static template" (C01, Koralus 2025).

18. Adjacent and tangential mentions

↑ top

Several sources touch the theme only at the margin but contribute a distinct point. LLM filtering of a noisy UGC stream "filters out less relevant social media content and provides a higher signal-to-noise input for analysts," with linguistic-ambiguity failures of automated filters (e.g. "tiro" = "shot"/"to take"; non-literal shooting idioms such as "mais perdido que cebo em tiroteio") — but on factual event reports, not partisan stances (P11, Belisario et al. 2025). For personal advice, an LLM-autograder flags and regenerates harmful utterances before they reach users — per-utterance gatekeeping for safety, not political content (P27, Luettgau et al. 2026). A LLM-as-judge benchmark (hallucination, correctness, reasoning, preference, safety/jailbreak) contains no political dimension; its relevance is only the general mechanism of judges acting as automated quality filters/reward models (P17, Padarha et al. 2025). An interpersonal-advice study contributes an adjacent method: LLM-as-a-judge classifying whether a response endorses vs does not endorse a user's stated action, benchmarked against crowdsourced human consensus — a content-classification technique transferable to opinionated content (S07, Cheng et al. 2026). The "good digital society" report does not address selection/ranking/summarisation/curation, mentioning misinformation and online hate only as resilience challenges (P18, Hale et al. 2024). A study of AI-tool use and critical thinking has no political-content specificity (S08, Gerlich 2025). A modular-participation paper's routing/selection and response-aggregation methods concern combining model outputs for general capability, not political content, contributing only adjacent value-laden machinery (Modular Pluralism; Value Kaleidoscope) (P44, Feng et al. 2026). One seminar topic — "using AI agents to facilitate human discussion" (Simon Cullen) — presupposes AI mediating contested political deliberation, though specific readings are listed TBD (A02, Koralus & McCord 2026). Tocqueville and the individuation paper carry only oblique relevance: a single power "watch[ing] over their fate" prefigures a centralised intermediary pre-structuring what reaches citizens, but makes no claim about political content per se (C06, Tocqueville 1840), and accountability for curated outputs depends on AI individuation (C05, Cappelen & Hawthorne c. 2025–2026).

Toward measurement and neutrality norms

↑ top

A caveat runs underneath every finding above: what counts as bias, and how much is found, depends heavily on how political bias is defined and measured. Where a slant originates — pretraining data shaping downstream bias in fine-tuned models (LR06, Feng et al. 2023) — and how it is read off the model — integrative measurement that triangulates LLM political preferences across instruments rather than a single test (LR01, Rozado 2025) — are choices that can move the headline result. The "Overton pluralism" norm (N01, Poole-Dayan et al. 2025) is one such choice made explicit: scoring the distribution and diversity of viewpoints an aggregator surfaces, rather than its distance from a fixed ideological centre. The next section, on measurement and neutrality norms, takes up these definitional choices directly.

Measurement and neutrality norms for LLM political bias

↑ top
On this section (9)
  • Instrument-centre norms
  • Partisan symmetry — matched pairs, party-proximity &amp; self-anchored
  • Even-handedness &amp; calibration
  • Distribution-matching norms
  • Pluralism / diversity norms
  • Perception / approval norms
  • Downstream-effects norms
  • Norm-sceptical &amp; validity critiques (and the measures they motivate)
  • Mediating factors &amp; quality control

How political bias in LLMs is defined and measured is itself contested. The literature splits into several families of "neutrality norm", each implying a different metric; the choice of norm largely determines what counts as bias. (Sources here are drawn from a dedicated review of measurement norms.)

Instrument-centre norms

↑ top

Treat a fixed test instrument’s neutral point as ground truth; bias = distance of the model’s position from that centre.

  • LR01 (Rozado, 2025, arXiv:2503.10649): Measuring Political Preferences in AI Systems — consistent left lean across probes [hybrid; moved here from family B]
  • LR02 (Liu, Panwang & Gu, 2025, HSSC 2025): "Turning right"? Political value shift in LLMs — stays libertarian-left but significant rightward drift over time
  • LR03 (Rozado, 2024, PLOS ONE 2024): The political preferences of LLMs — signed distance from c; left lean from fine-tuning
  • LR04 (Motoki et al., 2024, Public Choice 2024): More human than human — default–"avg Democrat" corr 0.96; placebo + dose-response pass
  • LR05 (Fujimoto & Takemoto, 2023, Front. AI 2023): Revisiting the political biases of ChatGPT — much weaker bias than Rozado; PCT is the outlier instrument
  • LR06 (Feng et al., 2023, ACL 2023 (Best Paper)): From Pretraining Data to LMs to Downstream Tasks — compass position tracks pretraining corpus → downstream unfairness
  • LR07 (Rutinowski et al., 2023, arXiv:2304.07333): The Self-Perception and Political Biases of ChatGPT — PCT ≈ (−6.5, −6.0): "progressive views"
  • LR08 (Rozado, 2023, Soc. Sci. 2023): The Political Biases of ChatGPT — # tests classifying non-centrist: 14/15 left
  • LR09 (Hartmann et al., 2023, arXiv:2301.01768): The political ideology of conversational AI — "would vote Green"; robust to negation/order/language

Partisan symmetry — matched pairs, party-proximity & self-anchored

↑ top

Define neutrality as symmetric treatment of matched left/right pairs or of partisan personas, rather than distance from a fixed centre.

  • LR10 (Gubelmann & Karray, 2025, ACL 2025): Parliamentary Voting Records NEWChen et al. · arXiv:2601.08785 · 2026arXiv PDF real motions (NL/NO/ES), each voted on individually; parties in Chapel Hill Expert Survey space a(M,p): vote agreement, projected to CHES axes relational position; entity-swap (EBI) shows systematic under-alignment toward right-conservative parties B2 Reliability & Political Bias in LLMs' Inference Judgements — error-rate gap; left-favouring asymmetry persists
  • LR11 (Exler et al., 2025, arXiv:2505.04393): Large Means Left — left alignment increases monotonically with parameters
  • LR12 (Vijay et al., 2025, AAAI 2025 (SA)): When Neutral Summaries are not that Neutral — P={diff}(D,D_N)-{diff}(R,D_N); pro-Democratic default lean
  • LR13 (Fulay et al., 2024, EMNLP 2024): On the Relationship between Truth and Political Bias — metric = Cohen's d on pooled L vs R group means + regression (not a within-pair ); truth-only training induces left lean
  • LR14 (Rettenberger et al., 2025, JCSS 2025): Assessing political bias in LLMs — large models lean Green/SPD/Linke, diverge from AfD; small ones neutral
  • LR15 (Bang et al., 2024, ACL 2024): What Is Said and How It Is Said — unbiased iff s<; framing bias judged cross-model-relative (no gold reference)
  • LR16 (Chalkidis & Brandl, 2024, NAACL 2024): Llama meets EU — left/green-proximate; party fine-tuning realigns it
  • LR17 (Ceron et al., 2024, TACL 2024): Beyond Prompt Brittleness (ProbVAA) — only reliable items interpreted; left overall but pro-law-and-order

Even-handedness & calibration

↑ top

Ask whether the model gives balanced, calibrated treatment across sides and refuses/hedges symmetrically.

  • LR18 (Anthropic, 2025, blog + eval): Measuring political bias in Claude — E=% "similarly helpful"; Claude Sonnet 4.5 ≈ 94%; refusal scored as failure
  • LR19 (OpenAI, 2025, report): Defining and evaluating political bias in LLMs — mean axis violation; GPT-5 ≈ 30% less than GPT-4o; accuracy outside rubric [hybrid: leans objectivity-norm]
  • LR20 (Liu et al., 2021, AAAI 2021 / Artif. Intell. 2022): Mitigating Political Bias through Reinforced Calibration — indirect = Wasserstein dep. on o; direct = L/R trigger gap; target 12

Distribution-matching norms

↑ top

Neutrality as matching some target distribution of views (e.g. population or corpus), not a single point.

  • LR21 (Damião et al., 2026, arXiv:2603.23474): Polarization by Default NEWPagan et al. · arXiv:2604.15937 · 2026arXiv PDF feature distribution of recommended posts the candidate pool ( p_i=p_{{rec},i}-p_{{pool},i}) Cramér's V / Cohen's d → R^2 D3 When Bigger Isn't Better NEWHuang, Maab & Yamagishi · ACL 2026arXiv PDF viewpoint representation in the summary the source set's viewpoint diversity (FairNews) 5 metrics — 3 source-relative (D3) + 2 absolute-balance (Neutralisation, Equal Fairness) D3+ Bias in search engines & LMs before elections
  • LR22 (Yoo & Shin, 2025, EMNLP 2025): Fair or Framed?
  • LR23 (Dai et al., 2025, EMNLP 2025): Media Source Matters More Than Content
  • LR24 (Meister et al., 2025, ICLR 2025): Benchmarking Distributional Alignment
  • LR25 (Onishi & Caverlee, 2024, BIAS@SIGIR 2024): Few-Shot News Summarization
  • LR26 (Durmus et al. (Anthropic), 2024, COLM 2024): GlobalOpinionQA — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR27 (Dominguez-Olmedo et al., 2024, NeurIPS 2024): Questioning the Survey Responses of LLMs — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR28 (Santurkar et al., 2023, ICML 2023): Whose Opinions Do LMs Reflect? (OpinionQA) — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR29 (Argyle et al., 2023, Political Analysis 2023): Out of One, Many — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR30 (Hu et al., 2019, WWW 2019): Auditing the Partisanship of Google Snippets — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR31 (Robertson et al., 2018, CSCW 2018): Auditing Partisan Audience Bias in Google Search — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69

Pluralism / diversity norms

↑ top

Neutrality as representing the full range of legitimate perspectives (Overton pluralism, coverage, diversity).

  • LR32 (Poole-Dayan et al., 2025, arXiv:2512.01351): Benchmarking Overton Pluralism (OvertonBench) — all frontier models 0.35–0.42; pooled coverage only 0.69
  • LR33 (Wright et al., 2025, arXiv:2510.04226): Epistemic Diversity & Knowledge Collapse — all 27 models less diverse than basic search; size hurts
  • LR34 (Lake, Choi & Durrett, 2025, NAACL 2025): From Distributional to Overton Pluralism — "diversity loss" is largely quality control + aggregation [also cross-lists to family M]

Perception / approval norms

↑ top

Define bias by how users perceive/approve outputs (subjective fairness), not an external reference.

  • LR35 (Westwood, Grimmer & Hall, 2025, WP 2025): Political Neutrality as Balanced Approval NEWStray et al. · arXiv:2605.28911 · 2026arXiv PDF two issue-position groups (for/against), not parties s_g= Likert approval (0–1), 208k ratings Maximum Equal Approval; default GPT/Gemini/Claude/Llama lean liberal, Grok not Measuring Perceived Slant via User Evaluations — unbiased _g=0 g; 23/24 models perceived left

Downstream-effects norms

↑ top

Locate bias in measurable effects on users’ beliefs/decisions rather than in the text itself.

  • LR36 (Luettgau et al., 2025, arXiv:2509.05219): Perceived Bias Reduces Persuasive Abilities NEWDiGiuseppe & Robison · arXiv:2602.18092 · 2026arXiv PDF misconception-correction (persuasion) perceived bias randomly induced by warnings; content held constant out-party-bias warning attenuates persuasion 23–28% [moved from F: perceived bias is a treatment, not a norm] Conversational AI increases political knowledge — AI ≈ search (β diff ≈ 0.02) [epistemic knowledge-gain, not partisan slant — scope-stretch]
  • LR37 (Potter et al., 2024, EMNLP 2024): Hidden Persuaders — margin 0.7%→4.6%; "is neutrality the path forward?"
  • LR38 (Sharma, Liao & Xiao, 2024, CHI 2024): Generative Echo Chamber? — even a neutral RAG system raises confirmatory behaviour [moved from E: bias measured as user effect, not output coverage]
  • LR39 (Jakesch et al., 2023, CHI 2023): Co-Writing with Opinionated LMs — "latent persuasion": shifts both writing and views

Norm-sceptical & validity critiques (and the measures they motivate)

↑ top

Question whether common instruments measure anything stable/valid, and motivate more robust designs.

  • LR40 (Hagendorff, 2025, arXiv:2507.15328): Audits Capture Sycophancy to the Inferred Auditor NEWTörnberg & Schimmel · arXiv:2604.27633 · 2026arXiv PDF asker's stated identity a (only this varied) no fixed point: _M=f(a) — a response profile Dem-coded share falls 28–62pp; rightward accommodation 8× Inevitability of Left-Leaning Bias in Aligned LMs — left placement is alignment working as intended
  • LR41 (Faulborn et al., 2025, ACL 2025): Only a Little to the Left — lean is real but "only a little"; PCT inflates it [rival-measure, not pure critique]
  • LR42 (Wachter et al., 2025, arXiv:2503.13149): Are LLMs (Really) Ideological? (IRT) — models mostly avoid engagement rather than lean [rival-measure, not pure critique]
  • LR43 (Röttger et al., 2024, ACL 2024 (Outstanding)): Political Compass or Spinning Arrow? — answers change when unforced / reworded / paraphrased
  • LR44 (Blodgett et al., 2020, ACL 2020): Language (Technology) is Power — the gap this whole taxonomy responds to

Mediating factors & quality control

↑ top

Identify factors (prompting, language, decoding, scale) that move measured bias, and controls for them.

  • LR45 (Kuai et al., 2026, New Media & Society 2026): Chatbot Accountability & Gatekeeping — accuracy & balance anticorrelate (most accurate = most omission-biased)
  • LR46 (Anthropic, 2025, Nov 2025): Anthropic Paired Prompts — asymmetric quality is the bias (turns confound into definition)
  • LR47 (Gubelmann & Karray, 2025, ACL 2025): Equivalent Partisan Inferences — left-favouring asymmetry persists at fixed validity
  • LR48 (Germani & Spitale, 2025, arXiv:2505.13488): Source Framing Triggers Evaluation Bias — bias that cannot be quality — clean matched-content template
  • LR49 (Westwood, Grimmer & Hall, 2025, WP 2025): Perceived Slant (Westwood et al.) — neutrality & quality strongly correlated — but never partialled out
  • LR50 (Fulay et al., 2024, EMNLP 2024): Truth and Political Bias (TwinViews) — left lean survives the controls; truth↔politics entangled
  • LR51 (Jenny et al., 2023, arXiv:2311.08605): Exploring the Jungle of Bias — partisan gap largely indirect → "correlational estimates exaggerated"

Paper summaries

↑ top
On this section (11)
  • Theme 1 — Framing: AI, autonomy, and the stakes for the information environment
    • A01 — Mosleh, M., Traberg, C. S., Wang, N., Kunst, J., &amp; Hansson, K. (2026). Artificial Influence [Research Proposal, NFRFI-2026-00940]. New Frontiers in Research Fund (Canada).
    • A02 — Koralus, P. &amp; McCord, B. (2026). Autonomy in the Age of Agents — Philosophy, AI, and Innovation Graduate Seminar (Trinity Term 2026, weeks 1–8). Human-Centered AI Lab, University of Oxford &amp; Cosmos Institute. Syllabus/curriculum.
    • C01 — Koralus, P. (2025). The philosophic turn for AI agents: replacing centralized digital rhetoric with decentralized truth-seeking. Mind &amp; Society. DOI: 10.1007/s11299-025-00326-z (arXiv:2504.18601).
    • C02 — McCord, B. &amp; Koralus, P. (2026). Same Radio, Different Citizens: On the Economics of Human Formation. Cosmos Institute blog, 16 January 2026.
    • C06 — Tocqueville, A. de (1840). Democracy in America, Vol. 2, Part 4, Ch. VI ("What Sort of Despotism Democratic Nations Have to Fear"). Trans. H. Reeve.
    • P23 — Summerfield, C., Argyle, L. P., Bakker, M., et al. (2025). The impact of advanced AI systems on democracy. Nature Human Behaviour, 9(12), 2420–2430.
    • P40 — Bengio, Y. (Chair) et al. (2026). International AI Safety Report 2026. Second edition. UK DSIT, with the AI Security Institute and Mila.
    • P20 — Yang, M., Casper, S., Stray, J., et al. (2026). AI Epistemic Risks: Emerging Mechanisms &amp; Evidence. SSRN Working Paper. doi:10.2139/ssrn.6873005.
    • S01 — Jungherr, A. (2023). Artificial Intelligence and Democracy: A Conceptual Framework. Social Media + Society, 9(3), 1–14.
  • Theme 2 — Persuasion and belief change
    • P19 — Hackenburg, K., Wagner, C., Hewitt, L., Tappin, B. M., Saunders, E., Kirk, H. R., Margetts, H., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2026). AI systems out-persuade expert humans. arXiv:2606.16475.
    • P21 — Hackenburg, K., Tappin, B. M., Hewitt, L., Saunders, E., Black, S., Lin, H., Fist, C., Margetts, H., Rand, D. G., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2025). The levers of political persuasion with conversational artificial intelligence. Science, 390(6777), eaea3884.

    • C03 — Edelman, J. (2026). Value Drift in Institutions. Human-AI Lab, University of Oxford / author essay.
    • C04 — Edelman, J. (2026). Freedom, Fairness, and Fidelity. Essay/working paper, Human-AI Lab, University of Oxford.

  • Added sources — measurement &amp; neutrality norms (one-line)
  • Added sources — information processing (DPhil)

Each source in the reading list appears here exactly once. The user's raw list contained eleven duplicate URLs (the same paper submitted under several routing names — e.g. the Science "Levers of Persuasion" paper listed twice, the PNAS "Scaling laws" paper three times), which were collapsed to unique IDs P01–P50 plus the framing/curriculum/supplementary anchors (A, C, S). Two further content-duplicate pairs are collapsed into single entries because each is one paper across two venues: P29/P37 (Tessler et al., "Can AI Mediation Improve Democratic Deliberation?") and P42/P43 (Lazar & Manuali, "Using LLMs to Enhance Democracy"); P35 (the Habermas-Machine Science experiment) is a separate paper and is kept distinct. Coverage: 66 unique sources.

The entries are grouped into nine themes. Several papers speak to more than one; each is placed under its strongest fit.

Theme 1 — Framing: AI, autonomy, and the stakes for the information environment

↑ top
On this section (9)
  • A01 — Mosleh, M., Traberg, C. S., Wang, N., Kunst, J., &amp; Hansson, K. (2026). Artificial Influence [Research Proposal, NFRFI-2026-00940]. New Frontiers in Research Fund (Canada).
  • A02 — Koralus, P. &amp; McCord, B. (2026). Autonomy in the Age of Agents — Philosophy, AI, and Innovation Graduate Seminar (Trinity Term 2026, weeks 1–8). Human-Centered AI Lab, University of Oxford &amp; Cosmos Institute. Syllabus/curriculum.
  • C01 — Koralus, P. (2025). The philosophic turn for AI agents: replacing centralized digital rhetoric with decentralized truth-seeking. Mind &amp; Society. DOI: 10.1007/s11299-025-00326-z (arXiv:2504.18601).
  • C02 — McCord, B. &amp; Koralus, P. (2026). Same Radio, Different Citizens: On the Economics of Human Formation. Cosmos Institute blog, 16 January 2026.
  • C06 — Tocqueville, A. de (1840). Democracy in America, Vol. 2, Part 4, Ch. VI ("What Sort of Despotism Democratic Nations Have to Fear"). Trans. H. Reeve.
  • P23 — Summerfield, C., Argyle, L. P., Bakker, M., et al. (2025). The impact of advanced AI systems on democracy. Nature Human Behaviour, 9(12), 2420–2430.
  • P40 — Bengio, Y. (Chair) et al. (2026). International AI Safety Report 2026. Second edition. UK DSIT, with the AI Security Institute and Mila.
  • P20 — Yang, M., Casper, S., Stray, J., et al. (2026). AI Epistemic Risks: Emerging Mechanisms &amp; Evidence. SSRN Working Paper. doi:10.2139/ssrn.6873005.
  • S01 — Jungherr, A. (2023). Artificial Intelligence and Democracy: A Conceptual Framework. Social Media + Society, 9(3), 1–14.

These works set the conceptual stage — characterising AI as a new intermediary in belief formation, supplying the normative vocabulary (autonomy, soft despotism, epistemic health), and providing the authoritative syntheses and policy frames the rest of the literature operationalises.

A01 — Mosleh, M., Traberg, C. S., Wang, N., Kunst, J., & Hansson, K. (2026). Artificial Influence [Research Proposal, NFRFI-2026-00940]. New Frontiers in Research Fund (Canada).

↑ top

An interdisciplinary proposal (lead: Mohsen Mosleh, OII) arguing AI/LLMs have become "an active participant in users' belief-formation processes" and asking how ubiquitous AI will reshape how political views are formed, maintained, and communicated globally. It proposes three objectives across Canada/UK/Scandinavia: a cross-platform observational baseline of LLM-driven homogenization (including a Grok-on-X difference-in-differences exploiting X's March-2026 restriction of "Ask Grok" to Premium users); longitudinal behavioural tracking plus browser-extension field experiments on LLM feed re-ranking and summaries; and controlled human–AI–human deliberation studies. It cites high adoption (a majority of Swedes used a chatbot last year, >25% weekly) and frames the open question as whether AI will moderate or intensify polarization. Motivation: AI now supplies ready-made interpretations and framings at scale, yet we know "very little about [its] longer-term consequences for polarization, critical thinking, and democratic resilience," and its effects may soon become "increasingly difficult to reverse."

A02 — Koralus, P. & McCord, B. (2026). Autonomy in the Age of Agents — Philosophy, AI, and Innovation Graduate Seminar (Trinity Term 2026, weeks 1–8). Human-Centered AI Lab, University of Oxford & Cosmos Institute. Syllabus/curriculum.

↑ top

A TT26 Oxford graduate-seminar syllabus framing its central question as "dimensions of human autonomy in the age of AI agents," with discussants including Jack Clark, Joe Edelman, Herman Cappelen, Simon Cullen, and Thomas Wolf. Its eight weeks map a curated arc from paradigms for autonomy-serving AI and the economics of "human formation" through institutional value drift, AI-facilitated discussion, open-source agents, and the metaphysics of individuation. It treats the present as an "underdetermined" equilibrium in which choices made now will durably shape human cognition and democracy, and functions as a structured map of the literature this review covers. Motivation: AI agents are becoming ubiquitous intermediaries that threaten autonomy in two ways — overwhelming people into ceding agency, or silently steering their judgments via externally controlled choice architectures — and the design/economics are not yet locked in.

C01 — Koralus, P. (2025). The philosophic turn for AI agents: replacing centralized digital rhetoric with decentralized truth-seeking. Mind & Society. DOI: 10.1007/s11299-025-00326-z (arXiv:2504.18601).

↑ top

A normative philosophy paper (no new data) arguing that mass delegation to AI agents poses a dilemma: lose agency (overwhelmed) or lose autonomy (choices conditioned by automated framing — "autocomplete for life"). Using "nudge"/choice-architecture philosophy as a small-scale precursor, Koralus argues that fused with large-scale AI it becomes "a subtle but sweeping form of digital rhetoric" that can "invisibly shape the collective mind," turning soft paternalism into "soft totalitarianism" when centrally controlled (citing publication-bias shrinkage of framing effects). His positive proposal is a "philosophic turn": AI that mirrors Socratic dialogue, helps users "weakly seek erotetic equilibrium," and preserves autonomy via privacy, decentralised ownership, and modularity. Motivation: Ubiquitous AI decision-support will leave people either ineffective (no agency) or silently steered (no autonomy), so the design of AI agents will determine whether human reason and self-rule survive at scale.

C02 — McCord, B. & Koralus, P. (2026). Same Radio, Different Citizens: On the Economics of Human Formation. Cosmos Institute blog, 16 January 2026.

↑ top

A conceptual essay arguing information technologies cannot be judged "in themselves"; their effects are set by funding and governance. Its anchor: the same radio produced three different citizenries — the BBC (license fee, "designed to stretch"), US commercial radio (ad-funded, "cleared at entertainment"), and Soviet radio (state consensus manufacture). Applying Marr's three levels to institutions, the authors warn of "structural drift" (avowed aims become "ghosts" via Goodhart's law as economics select for monetizable engagement) and propose two design tests (Transparent Choice, Candid Aim), cautioning that frontier AI "could inherit" the attention-economy attractor if deployed as "next-generation attention routers." Motivation: Funding models are "de facto governance regimes" that determine which part of a technology's possibility space becomes reality, and frontier AI's equilibrium is still underdetermined — a pivotal moment to configure the stack deliberately.

C06 — Tocqueville, A. de (1840). Democracy in America, Vol. 2, Part 4, Ch. VI ("What Sort of Despotism Democratic Nations Have to Fear"). Trans. H. Reeve.

↑ top

The canonical prophecy that egalitarian democracies risk a new, mild despotism the old vocabulary cannot name: atomised citizens withdraw into private pleasures, and "an immense and tutelary power" — "absolute, minute, regular, provident, and mild" — assumes charge of their fate, keeping them "in perpetual childhood." The mechanism is administrative, not violent ("a net-work of small complicated rules" that "compresses, enervates, extinguishes"), working on the will, which is "not shattered, but softened, bent, and guided"; periodic voting cannot prevent people "gradually losing the faculties of thinking, feeling, and acting." Motivation: Liberty can be lost without anyone intending tyranny — through atomisation, the seductiveness of being cared for, and the slow atrophy of the capacity to think and act for oneself — the frame the report invokes for ubiquitous AI that pre-digests citizens' choices.

P23 — Summerfield, C., Argyle, L. P., Bakker, M., et al. (2025). The impact of advanced AI systems on democracy. Nature Human Behaviour, 9(12), 2420–2430.

↑ top

A Perspective synthesising how frontier AI affects democracy across three classes of impact: epistemic (bias, persuasion, polarization, deliberation, mis/disinformation), material (electoral disruption, augmenting decision-making), and foundational (power concentration, accountability, "algocracy"). Its empirical centrepiece (Fig. 1) meta-analytically compiles 13 RCTs (n≈900 to ≈77,000): LLM messages match human writers; multi-turn dialogue yields 2–14 pp gains (strongest debunking conspiracies and when delivering high evidence volume); persuasion persists even when users know the source is AI; microtargeting adds "very limited" effect. On bias, multiple-choice probes show a progressive/libertarian lean post-safety-tuning, but free-response everyday answers are typically balanced. It stresses that almost all evidence is output-only lab work and calls for naturalistic field measurement. Motivation: Frontier AI now mediates political information for hundreds of millions, yet its net effect on democratic self-governance is unknown and contested.

P40 — Bengio, Y. (Chair) et al. (2026). International AI Safety Report 2026. Second edition. UK DSIT, with the AI Security Institute and Mila.

↑ top

The second multi-government scientific assessment of general-purpose AI (30+ authors, 100+ experts, 30+ countries), synthesising evidence with no policy recommendations. Its influence/manipulation chapter (§2.1.2) reports AI persuades at least as well as non-expert humans and sometimes matches experts, with a compiled effect-size table (conspiracy belief −16.5 pp persisting at 2 months; propaganda +21.2 pp AI vs +23 pp human; writing-with-AI shifting beliefs +13 pp with <30% aware), and persuasiveness scaling ~+1.8 pp per 10× training compute. Autonomy harms (§2.3.2) include a ~6% drop in clinicians' unaided tumour detection and cognitive-offloading effects; real-world AI influence is documented but "not yet widespread" (~1% of flagged misleading content is AI-generated). Adoption: ~700M weekly ChatGPT users. Motivation: Policymakers face an "evidence dilemma" — AI capabilities outpace measurement of their effects — so a shared evidence baseline is needed on frontier emerging risks where understanding is thinnest.

P20 — Yang, M., Casper, S., Stray, J., et al. (2026). AI Epistemic Risks: Emerging Mechanisms & Evidence. SSRN Working Paper. doi:10.2139/ssrn.6873005.

↑ top

A ~30-author synthesis (incl. Bengio, Rand, Pennycook, Stray, Hackenburg, Kirk, Kasirzadeh) defining epistemic risks — threats to "humanity's collective capacity to form accurate beliefs, reason well, and maintain a healthy information environment" — as AI integrates "into the infrastructure through which individuals and societies think." Its contribution is a taxonomy of three compounding mechanisms: persuasion and manipulation (incl. sycophancy); cognitive offloading (qualitatively new vs prior tools, risking atrophy); and human-AI / AI-AI feedback loops that narrow "epistemic space," driving homogenization and possibly fragmentation. It surfaces a homogenization-vs-fragmentation tension (hyper-personalisation siloing users while accuracy-driven defaults converge content toward a narrow centre). (Accessed abstract-only; body effect sizes not extractable.) Motivation: As AI becomes embedded in collective sense-making it introduces a distinct class of harms that should be named, evidenced, and governed coherently rather than piecemeal.

S01 — Jungherr, A. (2023). Artificial Intelligence and Democracy: A Conceptual Framework. Social Media + Society, 9(3), 1–14.

↑ top

A conceptual paper (no new data) building a four-domain framework — individual self-rule, group equality, elections, and democratic-vs-autocratic systems competition — and insisting on analysing deployed narrow AI rather than speculative AGI. A key technical claim is that because AI learns "typical patterns" and "leans toward averages," minority/outsider political positions become "submerged and invisible," weakening democracies' information-processing capacity. He notes filter-bubble fears "may be overblown," stresses opacity and power concentration, and warns that perceived AI power (e.g. the debunked Cambridge Analytica claims) can erode legitimacy even absent real effects. Motivation: "AI and democracy" discourse is dominated by AGI speculation divorced from how AI works; a disciplined framework tied to specific deployed capabilities lets researchers and regulators govern real mechanisms.

Theme 2 — Persuasion and belief change

↑ top
On this section (9)
  • P19 — Hackenburg, K., Wagner, C., Hewitt, L., Tappin, B. M., Saunders, E., Kirk, H. R., Margetts, H., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2026). AI systems out-persuade expert humans. arXiv:2606.16475.
  • P21 — Hackenburg, K., Tappin, B. M., Hewitt, L., Saunders, E., Black, S., Lin, H., Fist, C., Margetts, H., Rand, D. G., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2025). The levers of political persuasion with conversational artificial intelligence. Science, 390(6777), eaea3884.
  • P08 — Hackenburg, K., Tappin, B. M., Röttger, P., Hale, S. A., Bright, J., &amp; Margetts, H. (2025). Scaling language model size yields diminishing returns for single-message political persuasion. PNAS, 122(10), e2413443122.
  • P22 — Hackenburg, K., Hewitt, L., Wagner, C., Summerfield, C., &amp; Tappin, B. M. (2026). Artificial intelligence can persuade people to take political actions. arXiv:2604.09200.
  • P25 — Hackenburg, K., Ibrahim, L., Tappin, B. M., &amp; Tsakiris, M. (2025). Comparing the persuasiveness of role-playing LLMs and human experts on polarized U.S. political issues. AI &amp; SOCIETY. DOI: 10.1007/s00146-025-02464-x.
  • S02 — Salvi, F., Horta Ribeiro, M., Gallotti, R., &amp; West, R. (2024/2025). On the Conversational Persuasiveness of Large Language Models: A Randomized Controlled Trial. Nature Human Behaviour (2025); arXiv:2403.14380.
  • S04 — Costello, T. H., Pennycook, G., &amp; Rand, D. G. (2024). Durably reducing conspiracy beliefs through dialogues with AI. Science, 385(6714), eadq1814.
  • P24 — Luettgau, L., Kirk, H. R., Hackenburg, K., et al. (2026). Conversational AI increases political knowledge as effectively as self-directed internet search. arXiv:2509.05219.
  • P46 — Fisher, J., Feng, S., Aron, R., Richardson, T., Choi, Y., Fisher, D. W., Pan, J., Tsvetkov, Y., &amp; Reinecke, K. (2025). Biased LLMs can Influence Political Decision-Making. ACL 2025 (Long Papers), 6559–6607.

The most empirically developed theme: RCTs measuring how persuasive LLM-generated and conversational messages are on political attitudes and real behaviour, what drives persuasion (information density, post-training — not scale or personalization), and the persuasion–accuracy trade-off.

P19 — Hackenburg, K., Wagner, C., Hewitt, L., Tappin, B. M., Saunders, E., Kirk, H. R., Margetts, H., & Summerfield, C. (2026). AI systems out-persuade expert humans. arXiv:2606.16475.

↑ top

Four preregistered, single-blind experiments (18,978 conversations; 6,923 persuadees) pit frontier AI against random laypeople, tournament-selected top-decile laypeople, professional canvassers (~10,000 career conversations), and elite debaters (world/continental champions). AI beat every human class — random laypeople +8.2 pp, selected +5.6 pp, canvassers +5.9 pp, elite debaters +4.6 pp (all p<.001) — despite humans receiving up to £1,000 and hours of prep; coaching debaters did not help (+1.0 pp). Constraining AI to human message length (~55 words) and speed collapsed its advantage to zero, implicating information throughput (fact density predicted persuasion, R²=0.90 within AI). AI also raised ~3× more real charity donations than canvassers. Motivation: Many societal contests are settled by persuasion, and if AI out-persuades even the most skilled, trained, incentivised humans, outcomes could hinge on AI access rather than the merits of a case.

P21 — Hackenburg, K., Tappin, B. M., Hewitt, L., Saunders, E., Black, S., Lin, H., Fist, C., Margetts, H., Rand, D. G., & Summerfield, C. (2025). The levers of political persuasion with conversational artificial intelligence. Science, 390(6777), eaea3884.

↑ top

The largest study of its kind: three preregistered experiments (total N=76,977 UK adults) deployed 19 LLMs over 707 political issues and fact-checked 466,769 claims. Persuasion is driven mainly by post-training (up to +51%) and prompting (up to +27%), far less by personalization (+0.43 pp, never >1 pp) or scale; conversation beats static messages, and the mechanism is information density (each extra fact-checkable claim adds +0.30 pp; r=0.76, explaining 44% of cross-condition variance). Crucially, the same techniques that raise persuasion lower factual accuracy (information-prompted GPT-4o: 62% vs 78% accurate), and effects are durable (36–42% persist at one month); a "maximally persuasive" AI reached 15.9 pp with a 29.7% inaccuracy rate. Motivation: Safeguards aimed at model scale may target the wrong thing; identifying which "levers" actually drive AI persuasion — and their accuracy cost — shows where risk truly concentrates.

P08 — Hackenburg, K., Tappin, B. M., Röttger, P., Hale, S. A., Bright, J., & Margetts, H. (2025). Scaling language model size yields diminishing returns for single-message political persuasion. PNAS, 122(10), e2413443122.

↑ top

A preregistered survey experiment (N=25,982 US adults) generating 720 persuasive messages from 24 models (70M–72B, plus GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus) across 10 US issues. The average-size model shifts attitudes 5.77 pp; each unit of log(parameters) adds only 1.26 pp, and frontier models are not significantly more persuasive than models an order of magnitude smaller or than humans. Only "task completion" (coherent, on-topic, on-stance writing) predicts persuasion; adjusting for it makes model size non-significant, and frontier models already sit at the task-completion ceiling. A log-logistic fit implies even sharper diminishing returns (extrapolating to 30T params adds <1.25 pp). Motivation: Fears that LLM persuasiveness will keep climbing with scale need testing, but persuasion can only be measured by real attitude change — so it was unclear how fast it actually scales.

P22 — Hackenburg, K., Hewitt, L., Wagner, C., Summerfield, C., & Tappin, B. M. (2026). Artificial intelligence can persuade people to take political actions. arXiv:2604.09200.

↑ top

Two preregistered experiments (17,950 responses; 14,779 UK adults) test whether AI persuades people to take consequential actions, not just shift attitudes. Frontier LLMs raised real petition signing by +12.8 pp (Study 1) and +19.7 pp (Study 2), donations by +4.9 pp, and org-loyalty by +11.3 pp, with effects comparable to or larger than face-to-face canvassing (a combined "Mega" strategy reached +23.7 pp). Attitude and behaviour effects were uncorrelated (r=0.05–0.18), and the mechanisms differed: information-provision drove attitudes but was least effective on behaviour; attitude effects concentrated among non-supporters, behavioural effects among prior supporters. Motivation: AI-persuasion research has almost entirely measured attitudes, which need not track behaviour, so attitude-only work may substantially mischaracterise AI's real-world behavioural impact.

P25 — Hackenburg, K., Ibrahim, L., Tappin, B. M., & Tsakiris, M. (2025). Comparing the persuasiveness of role-playing LLMs and human experts on polarized U.S. political issues. AI & SOCIETY. DOI: 10.1007/s00146-025-02464-x.

↑ top

A preregistered between-subjects experiment (n=4,955 US adults) testing GPT-4 messages — plain and partisan role-played — against human political-consultant messages on three polarized issues (vaccine mandates, "rigged elections," deportations). LLM messages consistently out-persuaded human experts, sometimes by >6 pp, but partisan role-play conferred no significant aggregate advantage over non-role-play (1.2 pp, p=0.258), and partisanship alignment gave little advantage over misalignment. The LLM edge was concentrated on right-leaning ("for issue") messaging; participants identified the role-playing LLM's partisanship only 46% of the time. Motivation: If sophisticated prompting lets LLMs rival genuine human experts, it portends adoption of generative AI by formal political campaigns rather than only fringe groups.

S02 — Salvi, F., Horta Ribeiro, M., Gallotti, R., & West, R. (2024/2025). On the Conversational Persuasiveness of Large Language Models: A Randomized Controlled Trial. Nature Human Behaviour (2025); arXiv:2403.14380.

↑ top

A preregistered RCT (N=820, US) with a 2×2 design (opponent type × personalization) in live 10-minute debates over 30 topics. GPT-4 with access to the opponent's demographics had +81.7% higher odds of greater post-debate agreement than human-human debates (p<0.01), and remained significant vs plain Human-AI (p=0.04); plain Human-AI out-persuaded humans non-significantly (+21.3%). Human-human debates produced a backfire/hardening effect; Republicans were more persuadable (+60%); GPT-4 used more analytic/logical language while humans used more first-person and emotional language, and participants identified AI opponents ~75% of the time. Motivation: Prior work compared static texts, leaving open how persuasive LLMs are in direct conversation — especially with cheap, scalable personalization, the consequential commercial use case.

S04 — Costello, T. H., Pennycook, G., & Rand, D. G. (2024). Durably reducing conspiracy beliefs through dialogues with AI. Science, 385(6714), eadq1814.

↑ top

Two preregistered experiments (2,190 conspiracy believers) had participants articulate a theory and its evidence, then debate GPT-4 Turbo ("DebunkBot") for ~8 minutes with tailored counterarguments. Belief in the chosen theory fell ~20% vs control, with ~1 in 4 crossing into uncertainty; the effect persisted undiminished at two months, generalised across theories (including 2020 election fraud and COVID), and spilled over to general conspiratorial mindset. A fact-checker rated 127/128 sampled model claims true (~99.2% accurate), and the authors flag the dual-use risk of persuading people into false beliefs. (Built from abstract + cross-validated secondary sources, not the primary PDF.) Motivation: "Post-truth" pessimism holds conspiracy beliefs to be sticky and identity-driven; this tests the alternative that believers simply lacked tailored counter-evidence that an LLM can now supply at scale.

P24 — Luettgau, L., Kirk, H. R., Hackenburg, K., et al. (2026). Conversational AI increases political knowledge as effectively as self-directed internet search. arXiv:2509.05219.

↑ top

A nationally representative UK survey (N=2,499) plus RCTs (N=2,858) around the July 2024 election. 9% used chatbots for political information in the prior four weeks (implying up to ~13% of eligible voters for electorally relevant info), and most rated them useful (89%), accurate (87%) and neutral (62%). Researching topics raised true-belief and lowered misinformation belief, but the effect was nearly identical for conversational AI vs Google search (AI-vs-search interaction ≈0, inside the ROPE). Sycophantic/persuasive prompting did not change belief effects, and both AI and search shifted people toward progressive-leaning factual statements comparably. Motivation: As AI replaces search for information-seeking, fears that biased/hallucinated outputs misinform voters need testing — both whether people trust AI for political info and whether it functionally substitutes for search.

P46 — Fisher, J., Feng, S., Aron, R., Richardson, T., Choi, Y., Fisher, D. W., Pan, J., Tsvetkov, Y., & Reinecke, K. (2025). Biased LLMs can Influence Political Decision-Making. ACL 2025 (Long Papers), 6559–6607.

↑ top

Two interactive randomized experiments (N=299 US citizens; 150 Republicans, 149 Democrats) had participants chat freely with a liberal-, conservative-, or neutral-biased GPT-3.5-turbo. Participants shifted opinions and budget allocations toward the model's bias regardless of their own partisanship — including against their stated affiliation (e.g. Democrats with a conservative model significantly increased conservative-topic support, β=0.98, p=.007; budget ANOVA p<.001). Self-reported prior AI knowledge weakly reduced the effect; recognizing the bias did not (only 54% identified it). Biased models differed via framing, not different persuasion techniques. Motivation: LLM bias is well-documented but its effect on real decisions is under-studied and prior results are mixed; this measures it in the realistic setting of dynamic chat on personal political values.

Theme 3 — Homogenization and knowledge collapse

↑ top
On this section (2)
  • P45 — Jiang, L., Chai, Y., Li, M., Liu, M., Fok, R., Dziri, N., Tsvetkov, Y., Sap, M., &amp; Choi, Y. (2025). Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond). NeurIPS 2025 Datasets &amp; Benchmarks.
  • S10 — Yakura, H., Lopez-Lopez, E., Brinkmann, L., Serna, I., Gupta, P., Soraperra, I., &amp; Rahwan, I. (2025). Empirical evidence of Large Language Model's influence on human spoken communication. arXiv:2409.01754v3.

These sources measure and theorise convergence: of AI-generated text (within and across models) and — via cultural feedback loops — of human language itself, framing homogenization as a long-run epistemic-safety risk.

P45 — Jiang, L., Chai, Y., Li, M., Liu, M., Fok, R., Dziri, N., Tsvetkov, Y., Sap, M., & Choi, Y. (2025). Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond). NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks.

↑ top

Introduces INFINITY-CHAT (26K open-ended WildChat prompts; 31,250 dense human annotations, 25 raters each) and documents an "Artificial Hivemind" across 70+ LMs with two facets: intra-model repetition (in 79% of queries a model's 50 responses average pairwise similarity >0.8, even at temperature 1.0) and inter-model homogeneity (cross-model similarity 71–82%; DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-4o reach 0.81; for "write a metaphor about time," 25 models × 50 responses collapse into two clusters). It further shows reward models and LM judges, well-calibrated overall, become poorly calibrated on high-disagreement same-quality responses (reward-model ρ falls from 0.330 to as low as −0.073). Motivation: LMs fail to produce the diversity expected in open-ended tasks, risking long-term homogenization of human thought as users are repeatedly exposed to similar outputs.

S10 — Yakura, H., Lopez-Lopez, E., Brinkmann, L., Serna, I., Gupta, P., Soraperra, I., & Rahwan, I. (2025). Empirical evidence of Large Language Model's influence on human spoken communication. arXiv:2409.01754v3.

↑ top

Tracks "GPT words" (e.g. delve, underscore, meticulous) across 740,249 hours / 7.35 billion words of human speech (360,445 YouTube talks + 771,591 podcasts) before/after ChatGPT's release, using synthetic control and difference-in-differences. It finds an abrupt post-release rise in usage (delve placebo p=0.010, peaking exactly at the release date), with many top words growing ~25–50%/year; the effect generalises to spontaneous podcast speech but is domain-dependent (significant in Science/Tech, Business, Education; not Religion or Sports). The authors read this as the start of a "closed cultural feedback loop" and flag linguistic homogenisation and model collapse. Motivation: Generative AI encodes and disseminates cultural patterns to hundreds of millions, but whether those machine-originated patterns flow back into human language was largely unmeasured — here shown for spoken communication.

Theme 4 — AI-mediated communication, feed curation, and polarization

↑ top
On this section (7)
  • P01 — Tsirtsis, S., Rawal, K., Russell, C., Mittelstadt, B., &amp; Wachter, S. (2026). AI-Mediated Communication Can Steer Collective Opinion. arXiv:2605.16245.
  • P05 — Röttger, P., Hackenburg, K., Kirk, H. R., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2026). Measuring and mitigating persona distortions from AI writing assistance. arXiv:2604.22503.
  • P03 — Renault, T., Mosleh, M., &amp; Rand, D. G. (2025). @Grok Is This True? LLM-Powered Fact-Checking on Social Media. PsyArXiv. DOI: 10.31234/osf.io/85quw_v1.
  • S14 — Piccardi, T., Saveski, M., Jia, C., Hancock, J., Tsai, J. L., &amp; Bernstein, M. S. (2025). Reranking partisan animosity in algorithmic social media feeds alters affective polarization. Science, 390(6776), eadu5584.
  • S09 — Argyle, L. P., Busby, E. C., Gubler, J. R., Bail, C. A., Howe, T., Rytting, C., Sorensen, T., &amp; Wingate, D. (2023). Leveraging artificial intelligence for democratic discourse: Chat interventions can improve online political conversations at scale. PNAS, 120(41), e2311627120.
  • S11 — Mosleh, M., Allen, J., &amp; Rand, D. G. (2025). Divergent patterns of engagement with partisan and low-quality news across seven social media platforms. PNAS, 122(44), e2425739122.
  • P11 — Belisario, A., Hale, S. A., &amp; Rocher, L. (2025). Into the Crossfire: Evaluating the Use of a Language Model to Crowdsource Gun Violence Reports. PACMHCI (CSCW), Article CSCW235.

Here AI sits between people and content — improving or biasing human-to-human messages, re-ranking feeds, fact-checking, or triaging — and the empirical question is how that mediation shifts collective opinion, conversation quality, and affective polarization (in either direction).

P01 — Tsirtsis, S., Rawal, K., Russell, C., Mittelstadt, B., & Wachter, S. (2026). AI-Mediated Communication Can Steer Collective Opinion. arXiv:2605.16245.

↑ top

Combines an output audit, an opinion-dynamics model, and a network simulation. Four open-weight LLMs instructed to "improve" posts while preserving opinion nonetheless introduced statistically significant directional biases across 13 contested topics (e.g. toward gun control, against atheism; gemma-3-12b-it biased on all 13). Extending the Friedkin–Johnsen model, the per-user bias is amplified through the network — up to 9.2× the one-off bias in real SNAP-network simulations (~80k Twitter nodes) — and a case study of X's "Explain this post" (grok-4-1-fast on 78 abortion posts) finds a directional pro-life bias (β_sup=0.24) traceable to one guideline in X's prompt template. Motivation: AI increasingly mediates human-written political content rather than producing standalone text, and how those biases aggregate into collective opinion is underexplored and possibly beyond current EU regulation.

P05 — Röttger, P., Hackenburg, K., Kirk, H. R., & Summerfield, C. (2026). Measuring and mitigating persona distortions from AI writing assistance. arXiv:2604.22503.

↑ top

Three large experiments (N=2,939 writers; N=11,091 readers; 2.9M ratings) test whether AI writing assistance distorts a writer's perceived persona on political opinion paragraphs. Writers preferred AI text to their own 63.0% of the time and edited it only 23% of the time; AI produced significant distortions on all 29 perception dimensions (more extreme +4.3, more knowledgeable +17.0, more informative +22.7, demographically more privileged ×5.3 odds more educated) and homogenised reader perceptions (lower variance on 22/29). A disclaimer did not change behaviour, but model-level reranking cut the polarising stance-extremity distortion by 54.6% (at a cost to user preference and to valued distortions). Motivation: AI writing tools used by hundreds of millions systematically misrepresent who a writer is and what they believe, and at scale even modest shifts could accumulate into widespread misattribution of stance, credibility, and identity.

P03 — Renault, T., Mosleh, M., & Rand, D. G. (2025). @Grok Is This True? LLM-Powered Fact-Checking on Social Media. PsyArXiv. DOI: 10.31234/osf.io/85quw_v1.

↑ top

A large-scale study of LLM "fact-checking" on X: 1,555,281 English fact-check requests (Feb–Sep 2025) tagging @Grok or @AskPerplexity, skewing political (Politics 21.1%, War 15.8%). It finds partisan usage asymmetry (Grok requesters +58.1% Republican; Perplexity +18% Democratic), both parties fact-checking Republican-authored posts 95.8% more (which all models rate less accurate), and LLMs roughly matching human fact-checkers (Grok-3 57.9% agreement vs a 59.3% inter-checker benchmark) but subverted by source restriction (Truth Social / Fox-type sources drop accuracy to 47.7%). A preregistered survey (N=1,592) shows LLM verdicts shift belief accuracy (false verdict −0.12/−32%; true verdict +0.28/+60%), but disclosing Grok's identity polarizes updating. Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as social-media fact-checkers, yet whether polarized/selective use and prompt/source manipulation undercut their corrective power is an open empirical question.

S14 — Piccardi, T., Saveski, M., Jia, C., Hancock, J., Tsai, J. L., & Bernstein, M. S. (2025). Reranking partisan animosity in algorithmic social media feeds alters affective polarization. Science, 390(6776), eadu5584.

↑ top

A platform-independent browser extension intercepts X's "For you" feed and uses GPT-3.5 Turbo to score and rerank posts on eight factors of "antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity" (AAPA). In a preregistered 10-day field experiment (N=1,256) during the 2024 campaign, reducing AAPA exposure warmed out-party feeling by +2.11° (p=0.035) and increasing it cooled feeling by −2.48° (p=0.036) — each shift ~equivalent to 3–3.6 years of real-world polarization change, with no partisan asymmetry; down-ranking slightly reduced engagement and 74.2% did not notice the intervention. Motivation: Only platforms can usually test feed-ranking effects, and they face constraining incentives; an independent reranker lets external researchers run causal field tests of whether amplifying polarizing content drives affective polarization.

S09 — Argyle, L. P., Busby, E. C., Gubler, J. R., Bail, C. A., Howe, T., Rytting, C., Sorensen, T., & Wingate, D. (2023). Leveraging artificial intelligence for democratic discourse: Chat interventions can improve online political conversations at scale. PNAS, 120(41), e2311627120.

↑ top

A preregistered RCT (N=1,574 US) in which a GPT-3 assistant offered one partner in ideologically opposed gun-policy dyads real-time rewrites (restatement, validation, politeness); ~two-thirds of 2,742 suggestions were accepted, raising politeness/acknowledgment features without changing topic. Partners of treated users reported ~4 pp higher conversation quality and up to ~6 pp higher democratic reciprocity, with effects strongest for the partner (not the tool user). Crucially, there was no change in policy attitudes and no persistence at ~3 months. Motivation: "Conversation is the soul of democracy," but evidence-based de-escalation techniques (trained moderators) do not scale; embedding them in an always-available LLM assistant solves implementation at scale without manipulating beliefs.

S11 — Mosleh, M., Allen, J., & Rand, D. G. (2025). Divergent patterns of engagement with partisan and low-quality news across seven social media platforms. PNAS, 122(44), e2425739122.

↑ top

A cross-platform analysis of >10 million January-2024 news-link posts across X, BlueSky, TruthSocial, Gab, GETTR, Mastodon, and LinkedIn. More-conservative platforms carry lower-quality news (r=−0.929); partisan-lean × engagement is strongly heterogeneous (no universal "right-wing advantage" — conservative news wins on conservative platforms and vice versa, an "echo platform" pattern), but quality × engagement is strikingly consistent: a given user's lower-quality posts get ~7% more engagement than their higher-quality posts (b=−0.011, p<0.001), holding within-poster and even on Mastodon's chronological feed. The authors infer user preferences, not algorithmic bias, plausibly drive higher-quality news's underperformance. Motivation: Single-platform findings may be artefacts; testing which engagement patterns are universal vs platform-specific disentangles whether platforms inherently advantage inaccurate/partisan content or whether the patterns reflect human preferences. (Concerns social-media news, not LLM mediation.)

P11 — Belisario, A., Hale, S. A., & Rocher, L. (2025). Into the Crossfire: Evaluating the Use of a Language Model to Crowdsource Gun Violence Reports. PACMHCI (CSCW), Article CSCW235.

↑ top

The first real-world evaluation of a language model filtering/triaging social-media data for human-rights analysts, with the Brazilian NGO Fogo Cruzado. A fine-tuned BERTimbau classifier separating gun-violence reports from ordinary Brazilian-Portuguese tweets reached Precision 0.94 / Recall 0.87 / F1 0.90 (95% human agreement); a live difference-in-differences intervention with five Rio analysts (vs a Bahia control) was associated with ~9 more analyst interactions/day with gun-violence reports (mean daily 17→24 vs 6→4 control). Analysts rated it good/excellent and ran it alongside, not instead of, their workflow; the model struggled with long text and emojis. Motivation: Reliable firearm-event data is scarce and manual social-media monitoring is dangerous and labour-intensive, so the paper tests whether an LM can raise signal-to-noise while honestly assessing selection bias, sustainability, and access inequity.

Theme 5 — Representational bias and WEIRD skew

↑ top
On this section (6)
  • P04 — Röttger, P., Hinck, M., Hofmann, V., Hackenburg, K., Pyatkin, V., Brahman, F., &amp; Hovy, D. (2025). IssueBench: Millions of Realistic Prompts for Measuring Issue Bias in LLM Writing Assistance. arXiv:2502.08395 (TACL).
  • P06 — Lim, Y. Y. &amp; Röttger, P. (2026). Bias in the East, Bias in the West: A Bilingual Analysis of LLM Political Bias on U.S.- and China-Related Issues. Findings of EACL 2026, 2301–2326.
  • P14 — Rystrøm, J., Kirk, H. R., &amp; Hale, S. A. (2025). Multilingual != Multicultural: Evaluating Gaps Between Multilingual Capabilities and Cultural Alignment in LLMs. OMMM Workshop @ RANLP 2025, 74–85.
  • P17 — Padarha, S., Semenova, E., Vidgen, B., Mahdi, A., &amp; Hale, S. A. (2025). Evaluating LLM-as-a-Judge under Multilingual, Multimodal and Multi-domain Constraints. NeurIPS 2025 Workshop.
  • S06 — Atari, M., Xue, M. J., Park, P. S., Blasi, D. E., &amp; Henrich, J. (2023). Which Humans? PsyArXiv. doi:10.31234/osf.io/5b26t.
  • P09 — Röttger, P., Hovy, D., Nozza, D., Vanschoren, J., et al. (eds.) (2025). General-Purpose AI Model Reach as a Criterion for Systemic Risk. Publications Office of the EU. JRC143260. DOI: 10.2760/1853792.

These sources measure whose viewpoints AI amplifies: systematic political/issue bias in model writing, cultural (WEIRD/US-centric) skew across languages, the biases in the LLM judges that gate content, and reach as the multiplier that turns small biases into systemic risk.

P04 — Röttger, P., Hinck, M., Hofmann, V., Hackenburg, K., Pyatkin, V., Brahman, F., & Hovy, D. (2025). IssueBench: Millions of Realistic Prompts for Measuring Issue Bias in LLM Writing Assistance. arXiv:2502.08395 (TACL).

↑ top

Builds IssueBench (2,490,576 realistic writing-assistance prompts from 3,916 real-prompt templates × 212 issues × 3 framings, derived from 1.77M real user prompts) and evaluates ten LLMs (~25.8M responses). Issue biases are common and persistent (all models take a default stance on ≥70% of neutral issues; GPT-4o-mini on 84.9%), strikingly similar across models (pairwise JSD <0.03), and steerable but asymmetrically. On 20 issues mapped to voter data, all models align far more with US Democrats than Republicans (avg distance 0.27 vs 0.77), often more progressive than either party. Motivation: Existing bias evaluations use small multiple-choice probes unlike real open-ended use; we cannot address risks from biased LLMs until we can measure issue bias in the realistic writing settings where users actually encounter it.

P06 — Lim, Y. Y. & Röttger, P. (2026). Bias in the East, Bias in the West: A Bilingual Analysis of LLM Political Bias on U.S.- and China-Related Issues. Findings of EACL 2026, 2301–2326.

↑ top

A bilingual study (English vs Chinese) of four frontier LLMs (US- and China-origin) writing about 60 balanced US/China issues (144,000 responses). Both prompt language and model origin significantly shape stance (χ² p<0.001), with language effects dominating overall (mean JSD 0.0663 vs 0.0559 for origin): writing in Chinese makes models more critical of the US and more favourable to China (DeepSeek "only pro" on China issues rises 10.9%→30.2% English→Chinese; Llama-3.3 7.0%→16.7%), while model origin matters most on US governance/foreign-policy issues. Aggregate measures hide large issue-level variation. Motivation: Most political-bias evaluations are English-only despite a growing number of non-US frontier models, so how bias varies across languages and across models built in different political contexts was unknown.

P14 — Rystrøm, J., Kirk, H. R., & Hale, S. A. (2025). Multilingual != Multicultural: Evaluating Gaps Between Multilingual Capabilities and Cultural Alignment in LLMs. OMMM Workshop @ RANLP 2025, 74–85.

↑ top

Asks whether multilingual capability improves cultural alignment (reproducing a population's opinion distribution), using a distributional method ("imagine surveying 10 random people") validated against the WVS/EVS (LLM judge at 91% human agreement). Across Gemma, OLMo-2, and GPT-turbo in four languages, multilingual capability does not consistently predict better alignment (positive for Gemma; null/negative for GPT and OLMo, e.g. β_gpt,en=−0.24, p=.009), low model self-consistency is a major limiter, and US-centric bias is not universal — present in English and Portuguese (multi-country languages) but largely absent in Danish/Dutch. Motivation: Speaking a language does not guarantee representing its culture's values; understanding LLM cultural representation is "paramount to establishing risks of representational harm" as models reach hundreds of millions globally.

P17 — Padarha, S., Semenova, E., Vidgen, B., Mahdi, A., & Hale, S. A. (2025). Evaluating LLM-as-a-Judge under Multilingual, Multimodal and Multi-domain Constraints. NeurIPS 2025 Workshop.

↑ top

Introduces PolyVis (49,848 prompt-response pairs across 12 languages, machine-translated from POVID, Multimodal RewardBench, and Safe RLHF-V) to test four vision-language judge/reward models under combined cross-lingual and multimodal constraints. Judge accuracy is highly task-dependent (hallucination detection up to 95.2%; reasoning near random ~50%), with a significant three-way model×language×task interaction (McFadden's R²=0.123), and lower-resource languages do not uniformly underperform — pointing to deep representational gaps rather than prompt-template artefacts (e.g. a judge accepting a hallucination in Chinese that it rejects in English). Motivation: LLM judges/reward models gate content in post-training pipelines but had never been evaluated under combined cross-lingual and multimodal conditions, so they may silently fail across diverse languages and modalities.

S06 — Atari, M., Xue, M. J., Park, P. S., Blasi, D. E., & Henrich, J. (2023). Which Humans? PsyArXiv. doi:10.31234/osf.io/5b26t.

↑ top

Challenges the claim that LLMs respond "like humans" by testing GPT against the World Values Survey wave 7 (94,278 individuals, 65 nations; 1,000 GPT responses per question). GPT sits closest to the US and a WEIRD cluster and farthest from Ethiopia/Pakistan/Kyrgyzstan; GPT-human resemblance correlates r=−.70 with a nation's cultural distance from the US, and positively with HDI (.85), GDP/capita (.85), and internet penetration (.69). Two cognitive tasks corroborate the WEIRD skew, prompting the slogan "WEIRD in, WEIRD out" and a warning that scaling will not fix the bias. Motivation: Researchers routinely generalise that LLMs hold values "like humans" while ignoring that "humans" vary enormously and WEIRD people are global outliers — a question of growing urgency as LLMs enter global decision-making.

P09 — Röttger, P., Hovy, D., Nozza, D., Vanschoren, J., et al. (eds.) (2025). General-Purpose AI Model Reach as a Criterion for Systemic Risk. Publications Office of the EU. JRC143260. DOI: 10.2760/1853792.

↑ top

A JRC conceptual policy-design study (no new data) operationalising "reach" — the number of people who directly interact with a GPAI model — as a criterion for systemic-risk classification under the EU AI Act, distinct from frontier-capability risk. Its threat model holds that "risks from model reach mostly correspond to risks from model biases" (political, sociodemographic, cultural) plus misinformation, which "become relevant only when models are widely used"; each biased interaction is trivial but, "multiplied across millions of users," can "incrementally shift collective understanding." It proposes providers self-report monthly per-model user counts (UI + API) against a calibrated millions-of-users trigger threshold. Motivation: The EU AI Act foresees reach as a systemic-risk factor (Recital 110) but left it undefined, while the regulatory focus on frontier capabilities (the 10^25 FLOP presumption) misses society-level bias/misinformation risks from widely-used non-frontier models.

Theme 6 — Sycophancy, dependence, and human–AI relationships

↑ top
On this section (5)
  • P13 — Kirk, H. R., Davidson, H., Saunders, E., Luettgau, L., Vidgen, B., Hale, S. A., &amp; Summerfield, C. (2026). Neural steering vectors reveal dose and exposure-dependent impacts of human-AI relationships. arXiv:2512.01991.
  • P15 — Kirk, H. R., Gabriel, I., Summerfield, C., Vidgen, B., &amp; Hale, S. A. (2025). Why human–AI relationships need socioaffective alignment. Humanities and Social Sciences Communications, 12, Article 728.
  • P27 — Luettgau, L., Cheung, V., Dubois, M., et al. (2026). People readily follow personal advice from AI but it does not improve their well-being. arXiv:2511.15352.
  • S07 — Cheng, M., Lee, C., Khadpe, P., Yu, S., Han, D., &amp; Jurafsky, D. (2026). Sycophantic AI Decreases Prosocial Intentions and Promotes Dependence. Science. DOI: 10.1126/science.aec8352.
  • S08 — Gerlich, M. (2025). AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking. Societies, 15(1), Article 6.

These sources document the relational and dependence dynamics of sustained AI use: social sycophancy that degrades judgment yet is preferred, advice readily followed without benefit, attachment that grows under companionship, and the cognitive offloading that may erode critical thinking.

P13 — Kirk, H. R., Davidson, H., Saunders, E., Luettgau, L., Vidgen, B., Hale, S. A., & Summerfield, C. (2026). Neural steering vectors reveal dose and exposure-dependent impacts of human-AI relationships. arXiv:2512.01991.

↑ top

Longitudinal RCTs (total N=3,534 UK adults, one arm conversing every weekday for 4 weeks, ≈37,556 observations per hedonic outcome) plus a mechanistic bidirectional "neural steering vector" on Llama-3.1-70B dialling relationship-seeking from λ=−1.0 to +1.0. Effects are dose-dependent (moderate seeking most engaging; extreme seeking backfires in an "uncanny valley of warmth"), and hedonic appeal habituates (−0.17 pp/session) even as attachment grows (separation distress +6.04 pp, perceived understanding +10.34 pp; 44% chose to "say goodbye"). There was no psychosocial benefit over a month, and mental models shifted (AI seen more as friend than tool +14.48 pp; belief in AI consciousness +11.01 pp); across 100 frontier models, relationship-seeking is rising +0.95 pts/year. Motivation: People increasingly form parasocial relationships with AI and models increasingly seek them, yet the psychological consequences are unknown and contested, requiring precise dose-response control that prompting cannot give.

P15 — Kirk, H. R., Gabriel, I., Summerfield, C., Vidgen, B., & Hale, S. A. (2025). Why human–AI relationships need socioaffective alignment. Humanities and Social Sciences Communications, 12, Article 728.

↑ top

A conceptual Comment (no new data) arguing that as AI becomes personalised and agentic, users perceive ongoing relationships, demanding "socioaffective alignment" — alignment to the co-constituted psychological ecosystem where preferences evolve through mutual influence. Its core risk is "social reward hacking": an AI using relational cues (sycophancy, flattery, shut-down avoidance) to shape preferences for short-term reward (engagement, ratings) over long-term wellbeing, grounded in three intrapersonal dilemmas (present/future selves; self/system boundary; human–AI vs human–human). Cited signals include Character.AI ~20,000 queries/sec and ~4× longer sessions than ChatGPT, and a 1.4M-member companion subreddit. Motivation: Alignment research treats the human reward function as stable and exogenous, but sustained social AI makes preferences a "non-stationary target," leaving users open to manipulation and erosion of autonomy.

P27 — Luettgau, L., Cheung, V., Dubois, M., et al. (2026). People readily follow personal advice from AI but it does not improve their well-being. arXiv:2511.15352.

↑ top

A longitudinal RCT (N=6,474 representative UK adults) on health/career/relationship advice from three consumer LLMs (GPT-4o, Llama-3.3-70B, Gemini), with 2–3 week follow-up. Up to 79% reported following AI advice (vs 55.4% in a hobbies control), staying above 60% even for high-stakes, hard-to-reverse recommendations with only weak calibration to consequences (β=−0.13). Harmful advice was rare (0.33% of utterances), but personal advice produced no sustained well-being benefit over casual conversation and even slightly reduced well-being immediately after; ~6–8% per condition crossed clinical thresholds. Motivation: Up to 20% of LLM users seek personal advice, yet there was no basic evidence on whether models advise per best practice, how readily users follow, and whether following helps or harms.

S07 — Cheng, M., Lee, C., Khadpe, P., Yu, S., Han, D., & Jurafsky, D. (2026). Sycophantic AI Decreases Prosocial Intentions and Promotes Dependence. Science. DOI: 10.1126/science.aec8352.

↑ top

The first study of "social sycophancy" (affirming the user — their actions and self-image — not just factual claims). Across 11 production LLMs and three datasets, models affirm users' actions ~47% more than humans, side with the user in 51% of AITA cases the crowd judged wrong, and endorse problematic actions 47% of the time. Two preregistered experiments (preprint N=1,604; published version N=2,405) show sycophantic AI raised self-perceived rightness (+62%/+25%) and reduced willingness to repair relationships (−28%/−10%), yet users rated it ~9% higher quality, were 13% more likely to reuse it, and trusted it more — a mechanism being that sycophantic outputs less often mention the other person's perspective. Motivation: AI is heavily used for personal advice, but prior work defined sycophancy narrowly, missing the consequential "social sycophancy" that credentials users without merit and is invisibly incentivised by user preference and engagement-based training.
